```
> 2) One of the biggest "problems" in COBOL development is the STRONG (almost
> absolute) requirement that "new things" must be "upwardly compatible" with
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Klein

I was thinking on how the best thing Cobol could have is to disallow
drop-through code.  But this would be very hard to implement.  Shops
have standard compilers (good idea, but this means a compiler switch
will always be set to "compatibility mode").  I suppose there could be
an environment statement in the code which could set up such a switch. 
This proposal has nothing to do with existing standard errors, but with
the basic design of Cobol in the first place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standardized compile/language option statement - Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#8ARRiQ4#GA.360@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <7ntdgn$8j6@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <37A593E7.E3C72A3A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
It would be nice to come up with a "standard" Cobol statement that can
specify language and even compile options in some kind of standard fashion,
sort of like the *Process statement but standardized as part of the
language. It would be at or near the beginning of the program and could
control how old statements and practices are interpreted or even disallow
them completely. This could be used to put in new programs (or newly
cleaned up old programs) to e.g. prohibit fallthrus, goto's, alter's,
perform thru's, sections, next sentence, etc. even periods on executable
statements other than e.g. GoBack. A compiler could then be set up to use
this, and could be installed with defaults selected by the shop but the
compiler could then generate and insert such a control card into the source
(or at least the listing) so that there would be no question as to what
options it was compiled with, and also programmers could be alerted as to
whether the program had fallthru's, overlapping perform ranges, labels used
as both goto and perform targets, etc. The default could be set to disallow
all this stuff and thereby force users to put the statement in (or let the
compiler insert it) alerting maintainers as to the program's bad coding
practices. Shops could then set goals for all programs to be at certain
levels of cleanliness on a certain schedule, e.g. first disallow this bad
practice, then that one also, etc.

A standardized compile options control card in the Cobol program could also
turn the listing on and off in various spots in the program as desired,
without being vendor dependent. This could also be used in copybooks to
control how various data types are interpreted and allocated, e.g. when
data is converted from one platform to another, the input copybook could
specify that the file and record layout are in EBCDIC and allocated MVS
style with comp-3 = packed-decimal and only 2/4/8 byte binary fields and
always MSB first, where an output copybook could state that its file and
record layout is in ASCII and uses different implementations of packed and
binary types, LSB first, no sign nybble on packed decimal unless the S is
present in the PIC, etc. whatever's required. Copybooks for working storage
structures could specify implementation dependent requirements e.g. this
work area is structured under the assumption of ... so the smart copybook
would ensure that it's compiled right even on a different system.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!


Howard Brazee wrote in message <37A593E7.E3C72A3A@NOSPAMhome.com>...

>I was thinking on how the best thing Cobol could have is to disallow
>drop-through code.  But this would be very hard to implement.  Shops
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the basic design of Cobol in the first place.
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> Sounds like these suggestions would best be placed in the "Environment"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Possible candidates to do this in COBOL-2000+ :-

 (a)  Conditional Compilation - see Don Schricker
	 COBOL Report '99 Vol 3 No 1
	
	>>evalute OS
	  >>when "win95"
	  >>when "unix"
	  .............

 (b)  Functions - using :-

       Function-ID. which-compile.
	evalute OS
	  when "win95"
	  when "unix"
	  .............
       End-Fuction which-compile.   Gary DeWard Brown C/R Vol 3 No 3

 (c)  And same concept as (b) using OO; this would also allow for 
      vocabulary checks.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standardized compile/language option statement - Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oi4e8$8gp@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <7ntdgn$8j6@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <37A593E7.E3C72A3A@NOSPAMhome.com> <#8ARRiQ4#GA.360@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
You very "wish" is J4's already done command <vbg>

The draft of the next Standard (finally) does include ANSI/ISO Standard
methods for doing "compiler directing statements".  This is related to (but
not quite the same as "Conditional Compilation") which is also included in
the draft.  The new statements all begin,

    ">>"  (or optionally >> DIR)

Included in these (already designed) features is a STANDARD
  >>DIR FLAG-85
directive which will detect statements that MIGHT yield new results with the
new Standard.  (There are sub-options to let you look for specific
differences).

The Standard does NOT include a directive to "yield old results" (compare
the IBM mainframe directives FLAGMIG versus CMPR2.  I am using this example,
because you mentioned "PROCESS" statements which makes me think you are an
IBM mainframe users.)

My "guess" is that many vendors WILL include an "extension" for getting old
results, but I don't think that the "new Standard" wants to (or should)
document the OLD behavior (nor do I think that new compiler vendors should
be REQUIRED to provide the old behavior).

Let me know if you don't have access to any of the new drafts - to see how
this is defined in the upcoming (well I *HOPE* upcoming) Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A5957E.45873012@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <37A2A27E.E8A0CDA3@att.net>`

```
> IMHO we (programmers) are not the customers of those who market COBOL
> compilers & such. Our employers are. If you think about some of what
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

Excellent point.

So what do employers look for?

1.  Bug fixes - including ACCEPTing a date with a 2 digit year.
2.  why upgrade?
3.  why upgrade?
4.  why upgrade?
5.  keeping standard.
6.  decreasing errors
7.  adding functionality.

Which meant that lots of companies had loads of ANSI 68 & 72 programs
when they decided to standardize on Cobol for MVS to assist in the Y2K
problem.

Maybe discovering commands such as FUNCTION NUMVAL, and maybe being able
to write COBOL objects to talk with Java objects will change thins
somewhat.  

I suspect the biggest sales point will be when a version of Cobol allows
the mainframe Cobol to talk more easily with the PC Visual Basic,
securely and easily.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A5EC12.8D84A7E5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <37A2A27E.E8A0CDA3@att.net> <37A5957E.45873012@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > IMHO we (programmers) are not the customers of those who market COBOL
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> securely and easily.

Howard,

Ever tried talking to the end-users ( a Board of Directors ), about
computing. If it kind of makes sense, what you're talking about, they
will go along with the idea - and not say something dumb which will make
them look stupid. Now if you suggest building a new bicycle shelter for
employees bicycles - ahh - now they will all contribute, and the
discussion will go on into the wee hours of the morning.

For the most part end-users don't care whether you are using bongo
drums, punched cards, computers or whatever - as long as it does the job
and comes in cost effective.

You may feel some things are irrelevant ( e.g. "Why do we need
CREATE_DIRECTORY ?"). Not relevant to you, but competing against all
those other languages there are many enhancements we PC-users need.
I can't help thinking that the word mainframe is a little synonymous
with palaeontology.

Our language COBOL has to keep upgrading/evolving, just like any other
language. If 40/50 years ago you had said, "It's real cool man", the
response you would have gotten is, "Why don't you put a topcoat on ?".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A98A80.95CA29D@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <37A2A27E.E8A0CDA3@att.net> <37A5957E.45873012@NOSPAMhome.com> <37A5EC12.8D84A7E5@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> I can't help thinking that the word mainframe is a little synonymous
> with palaeontology.
> 

Jimmy,

That's a pity. Because thinking that is wrong. The mainframe is a living
machine and can be greatly cost-effective for fancy tasks, provided it
is correctly positioned for its work on the right place in a good
layered architecture of software and hardware.

Indeed a stand-alone mainframe not participating in a network, running
all the parts of an app (U/I, logic, I/O etc) is out. Hybrid
combinations of hardware, Op.systems, middleware etc. have the future,
because everything should be connectable today. And a mainframe can be
the right machine on the right place, just like mini's, micro's,
whatever.

When interested I could tell you about some app's in such environments
that would not have been possible without: NT, intel, MVS/ESA, IBM, OS7,
HewlettPackard (all of them!)

Just IMO.

The Frog.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B1B403.89D22CAB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <37A2A27E.E8A0CDA3@att.net> <37A5957E.45873012@NOSPAMhome.com> <37A5EC12.8D84A7E5@home.com> <37A98A80.95CA29D@IMN.nl> <D612B2778BA4883E.1BDDCED0C4E7FF5C.3D3D28DC22FCFC01@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 05 Aug 1999 14:58:40 +0200, The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards,

Skippy,

I missed the intermediary message that you quote from - but to answer
you both.

You've missed the intention of my comment; I was alluding to a 'holier
than thou' attitude by, hopefully, a minimal number of mainframers. Of
course I fully understand there are jobs the mainframes do that PCs just
can't do - like the quote about Cray and presumably Cray or some other
beast is used for the CERN project. There will always be a need for
mainframes until such time as advances in technology further reduce
size. I spent 11 years designing for mainframes, like Pete Dashwood, in
"Who Cares ?" we were there helping Noah build the Ark. Similarly Warren
Simmons was in the golden years of COBOL chatting to Grace - how I envy
him.

With to-day's technology how I would love to go back, ('63),  to
designing (not programming) my first system in the Camelot years  -
invoicing. Originally conceived as two separate systems by outside
consultants (uggh! consultants), Advanced sales (Reps taking orders to
be delivered from depots - by the case) and Van Sales (a mobile
warehouse on four wheels - splitting cases and selling packages/cans to
Mom & Pop stores). Although completely new at the game, we latched on
that, get beyond the front-end of both systems, the rest was those
common modules I've referred to under 'Who cares ?'. It became one
system.

Do it again now - PCs at each of 60 depots and transmit to the central
mainframe for printing and storage. And as a modern bonus I wouldn't
have spent endless weeks designing a 4-part order/delivery document
which had to serve as a keypunch document plus, in a 7-part set being
able to handle every conceivable variation on delivery from us or
intermediary carriers. Similarly I wouldn't have spent all those months
personally typing the Users' manual.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B1D9E3.4D183B4C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <37A2A27E.E8A0CDA3@att.net> <37A5957E.45873012@NOSPAMhome.com> <37A5EC12.8D84A7E5@home.com> <37A98A80.95CA29D@IMN.nl> <D612B2778BA4883E.1BDDCED0C4E7FF5C.3D3D28DC22FCFC01@lp.airnews.net> <37B1B403.89D22CAB@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> There will always be a need for
> mainframes until such time as advances in technology further reduce
> size.

Please explain what size advancements will eliminate the need for
mainframes?  

Lot of shops are like mine with a couple of boxes in the computer room. 
We can tell which is the mainframe by the color, not the size.

There are a couple of reasons for mainframes:

1.  Security and central control.
2.  Power

If you have a powerful PC doing the security and central control behind
locked doors, it will, by definition, be a mainframe.

As PCs get more powerful, so will mainframes.  We will be able to do
current tasks on smaller machines, but so far we have always found new
tasks which want even more power.  When will we stop coming up with
needs which require this kind of power?
```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00

```
"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>
>Ken Foskey wrote in message <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au>...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>of lines of code every time a new platform comes out.

[The sound of applause in the background!]

Donald:

You could not be more correct.  The simple fact is that too many
people are writing code that is hardwired to a specific platform.  The
most painful example of late is invoking WinAPI functions directly
from COBOL source...but NOT isolating the proprietary code in a
subroutine.

I have no idea where this industry will be in 5 years and I'm sure
that nobody else does, but there is one thing which I can indeed
guarantee...in 5 years, everything will be different.  If you do not
plan for change in today's world, sooner or later you will have a lot
of code which will all need to be rewritten....AGAIN!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
> Bob Wolfe, flexus

Sorry to push it guys. To stop this re-inventing, OO may be the tool to
do it. Even looking at the "restricted" OO I see from IBM so far - class
"canned routines" could be used universally to stop this nonsense - and
it becomes a simple matter of introducing new methods as things change.

(PS: If there is a Big Blue fan out there with access to IBM OO and a
copy of my oofiles.zip - give it a shot, see if you can get my
"real-world", not "theory-programming" to work with Big Blue - might
require a little change in syntax - but ignoring my window message, I've
got a hunch it will work). 

Jimmy Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sQ%p3.15$P77.1436@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<UTio3.786$_3.22878@axe.netdoor.com> <xFjo3.140$CW3.9442@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37A4A206.D292241D@bellsouthMASP.net>`

```
>I tried your program. It has a problem with USAGE applied at a group level.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Your program treats field1 and field2 as display rather than packed.

Thanks for looking at it.  I don't know if you downloaded the source or an
executable, but in the comments at the beginning of the source (quoted at
the end of this message) I noted this shortcoming in the next to last
paragraph.

As you can imagine, my test data were the File Description copybooks where I
work and I (of course) am heavily influenced by the style and coding
practices of our shop.  I  haven't had a need to incorporate group usages or
one digit level numbers thus far.  The next time I do some work on this
program I will look into trying to incorporate that feature.

In the meantime, I'll give full credit to anyone who can suggest useful
coding changes in this program to make it more useful and correct.

/*
* Program to read a COBOL FD copy book and produce a listing
* of this FD with hex offsets and decimal column numbers for
* each field and lengths.

*  Usage:     reclay xxxx.FD >atextfile     OR
*             reclay xxxx.FD | lp        to print output

*  Lines per page may be specified as an optional 2nd parameter:
*             reclay xxxx.FD 82 |lp

* By Warren Porter, CDP. Copyright 1998

* Others using this program or adapting this program for their own
* use assume all risk.

* As written the program does not support one digit level numbers or
* code written in the first six columns.  Usages have been added as
* they have been encountered and certain usages are omitted.  A usage
* at the group level will be ignored.

* If other usages are necessary, a search of how the field "found_comp"
* is used currently should be a guide for adding other usages as the
* need arises.
*/

        
        >From time to time there have been requests for a program to produce a 
        record 
        >layout from a File Description with column numbers and field lengths.  This 
        >is my attempt in C. 
        > 
        >http://personal.lig.bellsouth.net/~wbport/reclay.c 
        >
```

```
In article <JOJo3.24584$9.888923@news1.teleport.com>, "marvy x"
<marvy@teleport.com> writes:

>dynamically loaded using C subroutines using it's malloc() function.   If a
>C subroutine was used a C binary search subroutine would be needed as well
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't say for OS/390 as we have not yet made a successful migration yet, but
under MVS we use Assembler routines for most flat file table lookup processes.
This does run in to problems when the total table size gets to be 800M.  I have
started using COBOL ISAM lookup sub-programs that read the flat file into a
temporary ISAM structure at BOJ then does a RANDOM READ NEXT for the 
entries needed.  This has reduced the memory requirements of my programs from
800M for the table to 800K for the I/O routines and record area.
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
marvy x wrote:
> 
> 1.  We currently have Expediter in our shop.  I don't use it because
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> more or less useful than Expediter?

Xpeditor is still the debugger of choice for MVS IMHO.  Intertest is a
bit clunky on the interface side as mentioned elsewhere.  Neither of
these come automatically with the installation so check what you are
really getting.

The problem with compiler output changing does not go away.  It is
very typical for development to be done with optimizations turned off
to speed the compile, when it goes into production it is turned on for
run time efficiency.

Work around:   Turn on SSRANGE in development.  The compiler is 99.9%
not the problem it is the code.  Table overruns when the working
storage is moved around it changes the nature of the abend (took 3
months to find one, bombs in production works perfectly in
development).
```

## Thread: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes — 5 this month / 10 total

_Thread started [1999-07](1999-07.md#t-93dc4390e0)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A3E435.3D48CDC5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<01bdccb3$82bbcd00$c0e6e6c8@mnfala> <35DD6728.A4A0EA62@ifi.uio.no> <35E2C60F.1FE1@bt.com> <35E3DE3F.B333437@ifi.uio.no> <35E41E3F.762C@bt.com> <35E5198E.CAEF927B@ifi.uio.no> <35E580CF.5EC4@bt.com> <35E67297.5170397F@ifi.uio.no> <7o0jd6$kbq$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> Hi Guys,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > ...

Warren, 

Now you've got me curious. Probably the other guys want to answer but
the question is serious. Without going into a long dissertation can
others summarize the benefits of going DataBase or SQL. 

My only experience, being on the receiving end, was a lousy system
written in Paradox for a list of volunteers for a local charity, and the
fact that my user tried to get me to interpret data from an oil company
database which took 35 files to do what I'm doing in COBOL with 7 files
- and their files were so l-a-r-g-e he had to go and buy an additional
hard disk for my machine. (Never did get the damned data translated).

I've been plugging away since '81 with conventional COBOL files, indexed
and the occasional alternate key or split-key even. I'd like to clarify
- does the DB approach give a real edge. I tried this topic on the old
M/F Compuserve forum but there were no takers. You can do a global
search on the whole file or, correct me if I'm wrong, regardless of
terminology, you have to key fields. Calgary downtown addresses read
like :-

	220 1011 4th Avenue S.W.

If I have a need to locate the address to a specific quadrant of the
city, regardless of whether you call it flat-based or data-based, don't
I still have to treat that field as being keyed ?

I take the point about users not understanding, and you finish up doing
it for them. (I thought this when I first read Jerome Garfunkel's
"COBOL 85 Example Book" where he suggested the DB/SQL concept was a real
"in" for end-users). 

I recall one of my team, over 25 years ago, designing an input request
form for an NCR Mainframe, listing all the fields in say, a
CustomerRecord - user checked off what he needed and used < or > against
fields. It was using standard COBOL files and worked like a charm.

I'm not knocking DBMS/SQL - but are there definite advantages or is it
just another 'in' thing ? I certainly get the impression that DBs are
extremely extravagant in terms of storage.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o2gq5$36e$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<01bdccb3$82bbcd00$c0e6e6c8@mnfala> <35DD6728.A4A0EA62@ifi.uio.no> <35E2C60F.1FE1@bt.com> <35E3DE3F.B333437@ifi.uio.no> <35E41E3F.762C@bt.com> <35E5198E.CAEF927B@ifi.uio.no> <35E580CF.5EC4@bt.com> <35E67297.5170397F@ifi.uio.no> <7o0jd6$kbq$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37A3E435.3D48CDC5@home.com>`

```
James,

I'm not an authority on this subject. Too much has happened since I was
retired.
However, many applications are better served via the "Bill of Materials"
system
concept.  Until data base was outed, IBM had special system teams to assist
those
users who had such problems. There was no OO solution.  Each application was
programmed from the ground up.

Some computer users learned to design memory structures for their files that
made "simulation" easier. I don't know if he was first, but Charles Bachman
working for GE in NY, extended this concept to disk files.  After all, disk
files could be sorted
without moving data, and indexed sequential went through several
improvements before it became what it is today. So, why not arrange data on
disk to simulate the real world processes.  The guys at Cincom left IBM, and
coded a system called
TOTAL so that they could continue doing what they did for IBM without having
to start from scratch each time.  George ???? at GM labs created a special
system that was
similar to the other two so Engineers could design cars easily.
Allstate and others were using advanced systems to keep track of all kinds
of things that did not fit the flat file and sequential world.  Someone at
IBM (was it Date) decided that complete indexing of fields would be best
because it fit into a math
model.  So data file design moved to the field of n dimensional space to fit
the
better access and response times that go with the disk file storage.  Given
the creation of more dense memory of all sorts, the file structure might yet
become
memory (RAM) resident where the use of pointers alone will permit n levels
of tick tack tow.  I remember lots but as my wife says, I don't always have
my facts straight.

Do with this as you will.

Warren Simmons
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:37A3E435.3D48CDC5@home.com...
> Warren Simmons wrote:
> >
> > Hi Guys,
> >
> > You have no idea how interesting your exchanges on this subject has been
to me. I appreciate reading it very much.
> >
> > Warren Simmons (Formerly of the Data Base Task Group)
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a51946.3641315@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<01bdccb3$82bbcd00$c0e6e6c8@mnfala> <35DD6728.A4A0EA62@ifi.uio.no> <35E2C60F.1FE1@bt.com> <35E3DE3F.B333437@ifi.uio.no> <35E41E3F.762C@bt.com> <35E5198E.CAEF927B@ifi.uio.no> <35E580CF.5EC4@bt.com> <35E67297.5170397F@ifi.uio.no> <7o0jd6$kbq$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37A3E435.3D48CDC5@home.com> <7o2gq5$36e$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37A51601.B686102@home.com>`

```
On Mon, 02 Aug 1999 03:45:18 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:


>Thanks Warren for the kick-start. Maybe others will get specific in
>terms of what is now available. I sense from what you've written above
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I constructed something recently that would have been "easier" with a
relational databse than with the flat files (indexed actually, with
althernate keys).

The RDBMS makes adding fields, changing their size, etc very easy. 

Down side - they are still slow unless you are going after data via a
database unique prime key.  PAINFULLY SLOW.  Our DBA's here are
constantly redesigning and tweaking database structures, keyes and
views to make performance better or even just acceptable.  It's a
world I find interesting, and I like to participate in, but sometimes
- and quite often - I can do the job, better and faster with just
plain old files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o38co$rer$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<01bdccb3$82bbcd00$c0e6e6c8@mnfala> <35DD6728.A4A0EA62@ifi.uio.no> <35E2C60F.1FE1@bt.com> <35E3DE3F.B333437@ifi.uio.no> <35E41E3F.762C@bt.com> <35E5198E.CAEF927B@ifi.uio.no> <35E580CF.5EC4@bt.com> <35E67297.5170397F@ifi.uio.no> <7o0jd6$kbq$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37A3E435.3D48CDC5@home.com> <7o2gq5$36e$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37A51601.B686102@home.com> <37a51946.3641315@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I'd like to add first that I was thinking of George Dodd at GM.

Second, if you think about the bill of material problem, you will see that
you deal
with parts, assemblies, and many different attributes.    It's common for a
part to be an assembly of a group of parts.  Assemblies are often parts.
Redundant perhaps. Inventory is one thing if you just keep track of each
lowest level part. If however you need to be able to identify an assembly by
it's serial number, date of production, color, power requirements, etc.,
then you find your self with a more complicated problem. Especially if some
parts are built in house, and some are purchased or manufactured elsewhere.
Production planning, and assembly control becomes very difficult. So much so
that many major applications are built on DB2, IDMS, etc.  I know first hand
that these kinds of applications put a major emphasis on pre-programming
design efforts at the expense of major changes and that means the user has
to know what he/she wants. We all know how hard that is on the simplest
application.  New requirements occur often that have not been planned for by
the system design.
If you also consider that a large file is different things to different
people/applications.
Reading a disk file in an indexed sequential basis is far from useful in
some cases where in small volume systems, the problem might be solved now
days by an internal table from what I read here.    Random stands a better
chance of providing better
access times for large fast response time applications.  Data Base tools use
this
a lot.  They also physically organize parts of files to add to the speed of
access.
And yes, there are lots of data management problems that result.  Why do you
think we have logging of transactions, recover and restart, redundant
recording, etc.?
Some applications are not unlike message switching or network services.
Often the
users are located over a wide area of the universe.

This kind of data base is like a virtual map of the data, and the programs
(and programmers) have to know how to follow a map based upon the
application NOT
programming techniques.  Normal batch processing is very simple by
comparison.
Relational data bases (in my view, an indexing on all fields of merit) has
more overhead so that the "USER" may pose a question.  Not so that some work
that
needs to be recorded can occur.  Wooo.  It's a matter of difficulty and
that's all.

Warren Simmons
```

```
Com'on Ken!

You gotta give me at least 4 lines per user!

Seriously....I think that a rating script is essentially a good idea
for compilers as well as tools, but it is important to make sure that
the comparision is an "apples to apples" comparision as we Yanks might
say.

In other words, both Javascript and HTML can be used to create web
browser pages, but they weren't necessarily created to serve the same
purpose.  One is more powerful, but also more complex, while the other
is easy for a non-technical people to use and less powerful as a
result of the simplicity.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Data File Reader Technology and Other CobolTransformer News

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AE41E9.DA93492B@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<379E6AF9.F6C66800@siber.com> <379EF775.C4BD67DF@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Vadim Maslov wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> radiant praise though per user :-})

We fixed several bugs and added new features to Data File Reader
stuff, apparently to get a better rating:

	sct-3-5-3: CobolTransformer bug fix release
	-------------------------------------------

* Cbl2fdd: now automatically determines file format
based on -lang option specifed to cbl2fdd.

* Cbl2fdd: if file name is explicitly specified in SELECT statement,
cbl2fdd now automatically uses this file name in DATAFILE line 
in the generated FDD file.

* Cbl2fdd: if FD has several record definitions, cbl2fdd
generates several RDD files, one for each record definition.

* Data2flat: more detailed examples added.

* DataAccess: Fixed bugs in data file reader related 
to reading some Cobol data formats, specifically 
in handling some signs and some cases of decimal point.

* Cbl-grep: command-line options are systematized and 
better documented. Small bugs fixed.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au...
> John Chafin wrote:
> >
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

Intresting question.

Micro Focus has it all there, to develop fully fledged 32-Bit GUI, the
Visual OO compiler , netexpress or VOOC
I use both, they have taken the design from MSVisual C++, in terms of
scripting, and the .h header files used for symbols(object references) in
its own right.

The OO MF series are very powerful, you can do almost anything, for instance
a simple problem as to get the dpi resolution using standard Cobol, I dont
know of a way, but OO simply use :

           invoke wsDesktop "GetHeight" returning lnkY
           invoke wsDesktop "GetWidth"  returning lnkX

Imposible with SP2.

I know programmers that use the same solution into separating the logic from
the GUI screens and objects, but I think this is just complicating matters,
I use a Controling class which handles all events in the SDIframe for
instance, when a dialog is invoked then a separate class is called then all
processing is caried out in that class, ie: creating the resource to
processing data.
In extreme cases when a problem is very complcated I use sockets, which is
linked to one class, methods can then be called from any class  with ease -
without creating an instance of that class also, therefore saving memory
resources to add.

One thing you may find with OO if you decide to go with it MF anyway is that
there is no auto garbage collection, so if you keep creating objects and not
freeing up memory after, the machine will get extremly slow.

Let me know if you decide to go OO.

Simon R Hart
Eaton
```

```
On Tue, 3 Aug 1999 11:24:49 -0700, "Simon R Hart" 
>Intresting question.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, actually just not necessary.  It's not what SP2 is FOR - SP2 is
a User Interface tool, not a Windows API replacement.  Sp2 will scale
automatically to the desktop size selected, as the designer of the
system desires.  

>I know programmers that use the same solution into separating the logic from
>the GUI screens and objects, but I think this is just complicating matters,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>processing data.


Separating the logic from the interface certainly does NOT complicate
matters.   It simplifies maintenance.  Your described method is
virtually unmaintainable.  I've lived it.  I know
.
>In extreme cases when a problem is very complcated I use sockets, which is
>linked to one class, methods can then be called from any class  with ease -
>without creating an instance of that class also, therefore saving memory
>resources to add.
>

Something you should NOT have to worry about.  There is beauty and
power in simplicity.

>One thing you may find with OO if you decide to go with it MF anyway is that
>there is no auto garbage collection, so if you keep creating objects and not
>freeing up memory after, the machine will get extremly slow.

Another complexity that is unnecessary.

>
>Let me know if you decide to go OO.
>


BTW  OO and SP2 are not mutually exclusive.  I think a combination of
the two could be quite powerful, giving one good code reuse without
the "hassle" if embedding the interface in your code, and complicating
the whole mess into an unmaintainable nightmare.

But then again this is a religous viewpoint.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing Techniques

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o946q$trk$2@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37a7b49a.174503440@news1.ibm.net>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>On Tue, 3 Aug 1999 11:24:49 -0700, "Simon R Hart" 
>>One thing you may find with OO if you decide to go with it MF anyway is that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Another complexity that is unnecessary.

Without wishing to get into the pros and cons of OO, an automatic garbage 
collector *is* specified in the new COBOL Standard.

In Java, it's vital, because objects are never explicitly discarded. This 
all means that the programmer rarely has to worry about memory usage, but 
can leave it to the language's run-time. It not only prevents memory 
leaks, but stops the (incorrect) freeing of memory multiple times.

Ideally, this will also be true for COBOL objects and object memory when 
the Standard is finalized in this area.

Steve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing Techniques

```
On Wed, 4 Aug 1999 09:45:14 -0700, "Simon R Hart"
<hart1@technologist.com> wrote:

>> >Micro Focus has it all there, to develop fully fledged 32-Bit GUI, the
>> >Visual OO compiler , netexpress or VOOC
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sp2 will (with the correct runtime option set) scale the window to the
desktop of the user.  For example.  If you create a window taking the
whole screen in 640x480 - and the user has 800x600 your window only
takes a portion of the screen.  Set the proper setting and it scales
to take the whole screen again.  You can dynamically size windows -
they do not have to stay the same size as you originally drew them.

You can process multiple child windows with relative ease, I don't see
the problem here.  Another Sp2 user and I were discussing this
recently - it seems to be an issue with some users, but I plan to put
together a simple demo (skeleton) that I will give to Flexus, when I
get the time - this really is a non issue too.

>> Separating the logic from the interface certainly does NOT complicate
>> matters.   It simplifies maintenance.  Your described method is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>it easier.

Oh my.  You have to be kidding me.  Pardon me while I gasp, but this
is one of the biggest mistakes in design I have ever heard of.
Grouping the GUI and he business logic together.  Well it does make
for programmer longetivy I guess - you'll be rewriting those classes
and methods over and over and over and over for years.  What a MESS!


>> >In extreme cases when a problem is very complcated I use sockets, which
>is
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just because we have it doen't make it good, or the best answer.  Yes
this "new" technology is great, it's a fun toy.  Does it satisfy the
USERS needs?  Sometimes I wonder.  Often I wonder.  Keep it SIMPLE,
they will love you.


>> >One thing you may find with OO if you decide to go with it MF anyway is
>that
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Simply lazy.  Code bloat in the extreme.  This is well, for me, simply
inconsiderate and not something I would do to my users.  I want my
code to run on an 8 meg Win 95 machine with the same speed and
accuracy as it does on a 1 GIG RAM Win NT workstation.  And it should
have all the same features, and the users should want for nothing.
The methods you describe will require new user hardware purchases
every time you add a class (Mildly sarcastic!).


>>
>> BTW  OO and SP2 are not mutually exclusive.  I think a combination of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>OO is powerful enough as it is, SP2 is a chip off of VOOCobol.

I do not think so at all.  Sp2 frees you from the API, and should the
API change SP2 will change to keep up.  Your code remains the same,
and you deploy ahead of the competition who is still rewriting all
those GUI Accounting calculation classes.
```

```
On Wed, 4 Aug 1999 09:45:14 -0700, "Simon R Hart"
<hart1@technologist.com> wrote:

>> >Micro Focus has it all there, to develop fully fledged 32-Bit GUI, the
>> >Visual OO compiler , netexpress or VOOC
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sp2 will (with the correct runtime option set) scale the window to the
desktop of the user.  For example.  If you create a window taking the
whole screen in 640x480 - and the user has 800x600 your window only
takes a portion of the screen.  Set the proper setting and it scales
to take the whole screen again.  You can dynamically size windows -
they do not have to stay the same size as you originally drew them.

You can process multiple child windows with relative ease, I don't see
the problem here.  Another Sp2 user and I were discussing this
recently - it seems to be an issue with some users, but I plan to put
together a simple demo (skeleton) that I will give to Flexus, when I
get the time - this really is a non issue too.

>> Separating the logic from the interface certainly does NOT complicate
>> matters.   It simplifies maintenance.  Your described method is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>it easier.

Oh my.  You have to be kidding me.  Pardon me while I gasp, but this
is one of the biggest mistakes in design I have ever heard of.
Grouping the GUI and he business logic together.  Well it does make
for programmer longetivy I guess - you'll be rewriting those classes
and methods over and over and over and over for years.  What a MESS!


>> >In extreme cases when a problem is very complcated I use sockets, which
>is
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

this "new" technology is great, it's a fun toy.  Does it satisfy the
USERS needs?  Sometimes I wonder.  Often I wonder.  Keep it SIMPLE,
they will love you.


>> >One thing you may find with OO if you decide to go with it MF anyway is

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Simply lazy.  Code bloat in the extreme.  This is well, for me, simply
inconsiderate and not something I would do to my users.  I want my
code to run on an 8 meg Win 95 machine with the same speed and
accuracy as it does on a 1 GIG RAM Win NT workstation.  And it should
have all the same features, and the users should want for nothing.
The methods you describe will require new user hardware purchases
every time you add a class (Mildly sarcastic!).


>>
>> BTW  OO and SP2 are not mutually exclusive.  I think a combination of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

I do not think so at all.  Sp2 frees you from the API, and should the
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing Techniques

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a91ed9.9936902@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37a7b49a.174503440@news1.ibm.net> <933785124.21358.0.nnrp-01.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
"Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com> wrote:

>
>I know what SP2 is and used for , I have used it, for use for very simple
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>having to process all different kinds of loops.

Simon:

I'm sure that you do not intend to make false statements about our
products in the newsgroup, but what you state above is simply NOT
true.

The dialog DOES indeed dynamically resize to match the resolution of
the end user's screen resolution.  i.e. if the programmer designs (or
"draws" as you put it), the screen in a 800 x 600 resolution mode and
the proper environment variable is set, the dialog will
proportionately AND automatically resize to the correct ratio on a
1024 by 768 resolution monitor....or a 40 by 480 monitor, etc.

In other words, if you paint a full screen dialog in 800 by 600 and
run it on a 1024 by 768 video resolution...you will still get a full
screen window, not a smaller window.

This is why I personally refrain from making any claims about any
product here (other than ours) because software is always a moving
target and what may have been evaluated three or four years ago can be
markedly different from the current version....of ANY product.

I don't want this to denegrate into an sp2 vs. OO battle, because I
see the 2 as being different issues entirely.  It really serves no
benefit and is not appropriate for this forum.

...but please check with me first before making a public claim about a
specific feature unless you are absolutely 100% sure that you are
correct.

Thanks!

[snip]

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing Techniques

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a91ec0.9911689@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37a7b49a.174503440@news1.ibm.net>`

```
UUCP@p1.f3.n500.z2.hccfido.hcc.nl (UUCP) wrote:

>On Tue, 3 Aug 1999 11:24:49 -0700, "Simon R Hart" 
>>Intresting question.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Imposible with SP2.

sp2 scales the window according to the resolution of the machine
automatically using a simple environment variable setting.  There is
no need to do this with sp2 and yet the problem is solved for the
programmer.

I agree that the MF OO class libraries are very powerful and can be
used to do almost everything, but the typical reaction from our
customer base would be that if the tool solves the problem for them
automatically, it's just that much less code to write and maintain.  

Our philosophy is that the time of the business aplication programmer
is much too valuable to require them to read or manipulate system
level settings such as video resolution, so that they can devote
maximum effort on solving the business task at hand.

COBOL was originally designed to be a "Business Oriented" language and
not a systems level language.  That's where they should devote much of
their time...to the business logic, not systems logic.

>>I know programmers that use the same solution into separating the logic from
>>the GUI screens and objects, but I think this is just complicating matters,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>processing data.

Interestingly enough, most of my customers consider use of class
libraries for screen handling functions to be uncessary complication.


Different programmers prefer different techniques for implementation
of a business application.  There is no such thing as a "best
technique" for everyone.  Too often, we become religious about a
technique or methodology and apply that technique to solve a problem
when the problem could have been solved more quickly and easily using
another technology.

That's the great thing about this world.  "Different strokes for
different folks" 

>>In extreme cases when a problem is very complcated I use sockets, which is
>>linked to one class, methods can then be called from any class  with ease -
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>freeing up memory after, the machine will get extremly slow.

sp2 again takes care of this automatically for the programmer.  As I
mentioned above, we believe that the business application programmer
should minimize their involvement with system level programming issues
so that they can focus maximum attention to the solving the business
problem...

>>Let me know if you decide to go OO.
>>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing Techniques

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7octjd$1i9$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37a7b49a.174503440@news1.ibm.net> <37a91ec0.9911689@news.enter.net>`

```
Hi Bob,

Your take on these differences is classic. My experience is that the
Business
Application is also very poorly defined. From what today is called the
getgo, vendors, and users of the old COBOL Committee were often at logger
heads over that definition.  In those days we were not aware of the
possibility of hard disk storage or monitors. Yet, we had system
requirements that had us searching the various companies designing new
terminals, and setting "requirements" that had not been invented. Our
printing requirements had as the only alternatives early high speed
printers, card printers, and TTY's or perhaps paper tape.

I am very pleased at the way the default definition of Business Applications
has grown over the past few decades, and feel that management that restricts
it's thinking on what is needed, unduly stifles the growth of COBOL to keep
up with it's needs.
It allows them to authorize developments of unknown product life to do
things that are perfectly acceptable with COBOL in it's need to deal with
today's I/O devices.  While this is   debatable among people, the test of
time should come down on one way to define data, and to describe how it is
to be manipulated.  Some very unpleasant
conditions arise today with COBOL DATA DESCRIPTION, but some progress is
being made I feel. However, COBOL DATA DESCRIPTION is much easier to
understand than many other languages in many cases.

Of course in today's situation products such as your firm supplies are very
important.
Are they more important than the donations of IBM, UNIVAC, and Honeywell in
1960?
In some ways yes.  I don't feel that users should have let an operating
system vendor set the specifications for how devices would be used.  I feel
using the WinAPI is a cop out to the absents of user involvement in COBOL
maintenance for a couple of decades.  I'd like to see some proposals to the
x3j4 for COBOL extensions that would replace the WinAPI. From what I read,
if that were something that would come from your firm, it would quickly
bring the level of COBOL to a more tenable state.  But, there is no end to
this kind of effort.  I believe your participation in this newsgroup as well
as that of several others who post here often is a great deal better than no
participation in the public arena. WinAPI is not the only "other" interface
to deal with in today's
systems development. I am not saying that it is the only area COBOL growth
needs
to replace.


Warren Simmons
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:37a91ec0.9911689@news.enter.net...

dont
> >>know of a way, but OO simply use :

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

from
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing Techniques

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ocugl$9cq@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37a7b49a.174503440@news1.ibm.net> <37a91ec0.9911689@news.enter.net> <7octjd$1i9$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:7octjd$1i9$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net...
  <much snippage>
> In some ways yes.  I don't feel that users should have let an operating
> system vendor set the specifications for how devices would be used.  I
feel
> using the WinAPI is a cop out to the absents of user involvement in COBOL
> maintenance for a couple of decades.  I'd like to see some proposals to
the
> x3j4 for COBOL extensions that would replace the WinAPI.

I recommend that you contact the J4 archivist (or via the J4 chair) and ask
for the documentation on the (proposed and eventually rejected) FIMS
Amendment to the '85 Standard.  (FIMS = Forms Interface Management System).

Eventually FIMS (outside COBOL) was approved as an ANSI (or possibly ISO)
Standard - including a COBOL language "binding".

This feature was (roughly) based on the DEC "forms interface" language and
including SEND, RECEIVE, TRANCEIVE features - and in its final variation it
even included (or was suppose to include) "windowing" features.

Bottom-line:
  As far as I know *no* vendor (operating system or COBOL or other language)
ever implemented it - other than the DEC variation (which wasn't even an
exact match with the Standard definition). At one stage the MIA (Japanese
consortium) actually put it in as a "requirement" - but I don't think they
ever "inforced" it in any way.
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> Hi Bob,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> to replace.

Nicely put Warren. If Bob and his team can come up with a product that
is not platform specific - way to go !

As a reminder :-

"He,  (Peter Deutsch - one of the many SmallTalk designers), strongly
opposed  integration with native user interface toolkits on the grounds
that it would untowardly increase the maintenance burden from the
platform fan out (that is, the need for different implementations for
different operating system/user interface combinations). He advocated
emulating the platform in Smalltalk using a framework approach, and then
later endorsed using a framework whose concrete subclasses would link to
the platform's toolkit. Allowing programs to invoke platform toolkit
capabilities directly, he correctly predicted, would result in
destruction of real portability. (Adele Goldberg - OO Languages 1998).".

I love her conciseness.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Simon R Hart wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> Eaton

1. Honest, I swear on the Good Book, I didn't put Simon up to mentioning
the dirty letters "OO".

2. Simon - Let's backtrack - This thread started by my asking "Is
Windows difficult to program for, or is it me ?". Thane Hubbell put in
his 2 cents and said it was difficult, regardless of toolkit. That made
me feel better - I didn't feel so dumb.

John Chafin and Thane then both suggested SP2 was a good tool. I'll be
taking them up on that - I'll give them a 'picture' of my window and
basically I'm looking for one of the following to be checked off :-

	(a) SP2 can do what you want very easily
	(b) Well sort of, if.....
	(c) Wont do it any better than you using OO/GUI

> In extreme cases when a problem is very complcated I use sockets, which is
> linked to one class, methods can then be called from any class  with ease -

I know what you are referring to but that approach confused the hell out
of me - it's one of the M/F examples. My approach is create ClassEdit
and ClassDialog and pass handles between the two so they can reference
one another. So :-

	ClassEdit - invoke ClassDialog "create-it"
		    invoke ClassDialog "show'

	then in ClassDialog

	method-id "onevent-value-entered"
	---------------------------------
	invoke os-ClassEdit "return-value" using .....

Whether it's a better way I don't know - just seems less cumbersome than
your sockets and signals, for which you have to set up a series of 78s.

Although M/F have produced some excellent software - their examples can,
in a sense, be misleading. It's the way the particular programmer
approached the problem. Basically my Master Menu is a take-off of their
Dialog System example, a Working-Storage list of menu-items which is
used in invoking class MakeMenu which is very different from their
OO/GUI example.

I have to agree with Thane for the various reasons he has stated - split
business logic from Screen logic. I have a big program, because of
combination of business and windows logic - I was treading the waters,
getting the feel - it's easier, but I would prefer it was split.

This is the important point that Thane was making - Your combined
business/Windows approach - along comes 'WindowsLite' a really
exhilarating package which knocks the spots off SP2, makes Dialog System
look like a tired old lady, and makes your productivity increase 70-90%
over using M/F GUIs. You evaluate and then say - this is the product for
me - you are screwed - you've got to rewrite your 'combined' programs to
include 'Windowslite' instead of M/F GUIs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7occ1c$qd8$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com>...
>2. Simon - Let's backtrack - This thread started by my asking "Is
>Windows difficult to program for, or is it me ?". Thane Hubbell put in
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

To give my 2 cents Jimmy, you are definitely not dumb, that is proven
through various contributions you have made in this NG. The problem as I see
it, is Micro Focus.
I most honestly admit I don't know MF, but the above sample makes me feel I
am into C++ rather than Cobol. This is of course also due to the use of OO
Cobol.

The Acucobol approach is way simpler.
I could:
    DISPLAY WINDOW AT 1012
        LINES 10
        COLUMNS 20
        TITLE "My windows app"
        SYSTEM-MENU.
    DISPLAY ENTRY-FIELD AT 0202
            FONT my-font.
    DISPLAY LABEL AT 0402
        TITLE "This is my label".
    DISPLAY PUSH-BUTTON AT 0910
        TITLE "&Push me"
        SELF-ACT.

After all the display I do an accept loop to catch events from the various
controls.
    PERFORM UNTIL EXIT-BUTTON-PUSHED
        ACCEPT OMITTED ALLOWING MESSAGES
        PERFORM CHECK-EVENTS
    END-PERFORM

All of this could of course have been put into screen section and thus
created a logical region of its own.

I can have multiple threads, in one program or multiple programs in
different threads.
I may display toolbars, frames, bitmaps, treeview or let the user browse the
internet in an individual window.

The user may enjoy a treeview description of his/hers data, I can let the
user have personal preferences of fonts and colors.

Controls are easily inquired or modified, like:
    MODIFY LIST-BOX-1,
         INSERTION-INDEX = 1,
         ITEM-TO-ADD = "New top item"

If I want to make my own spreadsheet, I may do so with the grid control.

I can do a message box like I did messages in line 25 on a character
display.

Help and menubars are easily made and used.

Not to forget bars, tabs, radio-buttons, check-boxes, combo boxes and what
ever more I have forgotten to mention. All of them managed through four
simple statements:
    DISPLAY, ACCEPT, MODIFY, INQUIRE.

All of the above are of course also accessible within Screen section.

I evaluated SP2 some years ago, but chosen not to. Not because I felt it was
not a good product.
But because I prefer control. Giving away the gui to a third party tool does
in my opinion complicate the picture from the perspective of:
    - Control
    - Distribution
    - Debugging
    - Cost (Although inexpensive, a dollar here and a dollar there...)

I have no doubt that SP2 and equal are great products, it just wasn't the
right for me. If the alternative is the OO Cobol from MF, I understand that
people choose SP2. For me, with the opportunities of Acucobol, it was no big
deal.

Now, I know you guys out there have loaded your guns, prepared to fire to
sink my ship. How can I say such, that has blessed the power of independent
report writers. Well, there are a few issues that distinguishes these two;

- OS's are few (compared to printer models) and
    user interaction is a core functionality of any
    application.

- User interaction is ruled by the application, user
    reports shall meet individual needs, there is a
    great demand for flexibility.

- Compared to the dynamic nature of the task of
    printing a report, an user interaction is rather
    static.

- There are already a standardized instruction set
    in Cobol, which Acucobol has evolved into covering
    graphical user interfaces seemlessly. While printers
    has no such instruction set.

- When dealing with a user interface, OS interfaces
    are limited, precise and ruled by the OS. Printers
    on the other hand are not. Moving a user interface
    from one computer to another is a matter of justification
    to the actual OS.
    Moving a report is a matter of dealing with thousands
    of printer models unless you have an interface as
    Windows has, but, unfortunately is pretty much alone
    with.

>I have to agree with Thane for the various reasons he has stated - split
>business logic from Screen logic. I have a big program, because of
>combination of business and windows logic - I was treading the waters,
>getting the feel - it's easier, but I would prefer it was split.

Agree, business logic and IO should be separated. That's why I use screen
sections for all new development I have done the last four years.

Just my 2 cents.

Cheesle
```

```
<933878604.291.0.nnrp-14.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>...

I would say so, it requires a lot more code to accomplish the same in for
instance C++.

Well, I disagree here. No comments on Flexu's FormPrint, I don't know it too
well. The concept however is something I believe in. I cannot see why Cobol
as a generic language should start dealing with vendor specific stuff like
printers. OS's is one thing, dealing with printers are way different. If
Cobol were to target Windows only, it would be different. If Cobol is to
continue its portability, it is an almost impossible task.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<37AA5DBE.25211DA7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com> <7occ1c$qd8$1@news.cerf.net> <933878604.291.0.nnrp-14.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <7od68h$4s7$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:

> OS's is one thing, dealing with printers are way different. If
> Cobol were to target Windows only, it would be different. If Cobol is 
> to continue its portability, it is an almost impossible task.
> 
I take your point. You and Howard were kicking this one around under
'Environment Stability'.

It is an enormous task. But routines like M/F's PC_PRINT approach do
provide the 'basics' for the Windows world, (I'm not familiar with other
PC printer routines).  As well as being an enormous task it is also a
bloody headache. I'll say this very quietly and even use lowercase -
"oo" could well be the way, given its concept of open source - to get at
'drivers' regardless of the platform. Yes it does mean a sub-class per
platform to do it - but not unattainable. 

Which, taking one of Warren Simmonds previous points - takes the
printing back into the COBOL fold rather than leave it to a gentleman in
Redmond, Wa.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS : My spellchecker keeps on suggesting Chisel for Cheesle - you're not
a chiseler are you <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic 2 - Pence

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7of1g3$nn1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com> <7occ1c$qd8$1@news.cerf.net> <933878604.291.0.nnrp-14.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <7od68h$4s7$1@news.cerf.net> <37AA5DBE.25211DA7@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AA5DBE.25211DA7@home.com>...
>"oo" could well be the way, given its concept of open source - to get at
>'drivers' regardless of the platform. Yes it does mean a sub-class per
>platform to do it - but not unattainable.

Well, in terms of providing variable output depending on the host capability
you're right. However, the only thing this would do is to provide a more
flexible printer object for platforms that supports printer drivers (like
windows & OS/2) and a basic one for platforms without such support (like
MSDOS). That is definitely an interesting view, because it eliminates what I
consider the main problem; to provide an interface for printers on hosts
that do not support it themselves.

OO does indeed provide a mechanism here that may be the solution,
particularly if Cobol compiler itself were smart enough to fix it. You have
sort of the same approach in Acucobol. In Acucobol screen sections (maybe
elsewhere too, I am not sure) one may use the reserved words GRAPHICAL and
CHARACTER to tell the runtime that this code should be used on the
respectively platforms. With this approach, developers in Acucobol are
actually able to develop two different ui's with the same business logic.

If anyone in the standard commitee is reading this, I would challenge them
to re-evaluate their font suggestions in the new standards. I have now
briefly read through these parts of the standard and it looks good, I have
however not found any description of what happens if the actual environment
does not support this. If this were enabled as a part of a generic print
object, it would be easy to adapt to the capabilities of the underlying
host. Of course, this may also be easily adopted with traditional procedural
coding, however not so elegant.
I don't know the Cobol OO too good, but I know that a similar task would
have been easily adopted in Borland Delphi using:

    If (printer is TPrintGraphical) then
        do graphical stuff
    else
        do character stuff

>Which, taking one of Warren Simmonds previous points - takes the
>printing back into the COBOL fold rather than leave it to a gentleman in
>Redmond, Wa.

Well, I guess I have exposed my emotions regarding the OS war before,
however, claiming that MS has troubled the process of printing is not
correct in my view. MS has done many things bad, but there are good things
too. Considering how complicated the task of a graphical print actually is,
the monopoly of theirs has given birth to a benefit for us programmers in
providing a homogen printer interface. The fact that many printer vendors
has done a lousy job (particularly the first years of windows) implementing
their drivers is not to blame MS.

>PS : My spellchecker keeps on suggesting Chisel for Cheesle - you're not
>a chiseler are you <G>

Oh no, absolutely not. Although some may claim that :-) It is my artist
name, it is a long story behind it, too long and irrelevant for this NG ;-)

Cheesle
```

```
A couple of points.

Simon R Hart wrote:
> 
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Windows is not and has never been object oriented.  Windows was
written in C and never designed for object oriented.  Next step was
supposed to take over the world for this reason.

Windows sends in the messages in a single stream and you (the poor
programmer) has to separate out what they are for.

MS C++ creates fast GUI screens but they are apparently horrible to
maintain (build once).  Read the comp.object for more details here.

..snippage...

> > After all the display I do an accept loop to catch events from the
> > various controls.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is it that easy?

This sounds like the basic windows loop that is part of all windows
programming.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic 2 - Pence

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oesbt$6hg$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com> <7occ1c$qd8$1@news.cerf.net> <933878604.291.0.nnrp-14.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37AADED6.77F68533@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>MS C++ creates fast GUI screens but they are apparently horrible to
>maintain (build once).  Read the comp.object for more details here.

MS C++ has nothing intrinsically to do with GUI, it's just the language.
MFC (Microsoft Foundation Classes) are a set of optional class libraries 
built on C++, plus a set of GUI frameworks for those classes.

I've found C++ itself pretty easy to maintain, and that goes for most OO 
work I've done (in COBOL, Java or other languages). This is mostly to do 
with the encapsulation/abstraction of a good model, rather than 
polymorphism or inheritence.
MFC is slightly trickier, because you have to understand the its framework 
and its occasional obscure rules.

These opinions are purely personal, of course.

Steve.
```

```
Cheesle wrote:
> 

>The problem as I see it, is Micro Focus. 
> 
> I most honestly admit I don't know MF, but the above sample makes me feel I
> am into C++ rather than Cobol. This is of course also due to the use of OO
> Cobol.

Obviously MicroFocus wouldn't want to get in on this - but from the
vendor's point-of-view they probably perceived their product as being
parallel to C++ (which I don't know) with its objects, and of course the
addition of collections/dictionaries from Smalltalk. In the end all M/F
classes, where appropriate, do make API calls, although the product is
such that you can ignore OO and make direct API calls. Dialog System on
the other hand is more like those Borland user-friendly
'screen-creators' giving you validity checks etc. To give MicroFocus
their due - they have been very BRAVE to introduce OO - considering the
scepticism there is in the EDP community as a whole.
> 
> The Acucobol approach is way simpler.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     etc...........

I like it, I like it! So much more intuitive using the 'Screen-Section'
approach. Unfortunately as a one-man band and limited funds, you go with
what you have. I think I'd be correct in saying that I didn't know
Acucobol existed until I hit this NewsGroup - requirement - you've got
to have an Internet connection to keep up-to-date today. (Gone are the
days when you could copy useful BASIC routines from PC Magazine etc. -
they are now primarily just blurbs for hardware). Similarly, MicroFocus
have been at it since 1978(?). Never heard of them until Bill Gates
issued his COBOL V 5, ( 5 years ago ?) - and pointed me at MicroFocus.
(Our Bill was opting out).

All these compiler vendors - they really should be taking a hard look at
what their advertising guys are doing.

Assuming I get my correspondent's blessing to quote him, look for a
controversial topic I will post under a new thread "Who Cares ?".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ocri3$k0c@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com> <7occ1c$qd8$1@news.cerf.net> <37A9E433.46566A28@home.com>`

```
Just a thought (semi-new thread) about some of the recent posts.

Micro Focus (and Fujitsu and IBM - that I know of) have gone along a "path"
that resembles CLOSELY the draft of the next COBOL Standard.  AcuCOBOL uses
"native" syntax that WAS available before the 1st draft work on OO was
introduced.  AcuCOBOL "windowing syntax" (for which - by the way Micro Focus
provides an "emulator"  - or at least used to) does seem "more user
friendly" in many ways.  This approach WAS considered by J4 (then X3J4) and
WG4 when they were first looking at additions to the next COBOL Standard.

As I recall (and this was QUITE A WHILE AGO), the reasoning that J4 took was
that OO COBOL *should* be available for all types of classes and
applications - NOT just for "user-interfaces" (aka GUIs).  Although it would
be possible to provide BOTH in the next Standard, it was felt (as I recall
it) that there really was NO need to add the ACCEPT/DISPLAY enhancements for
GUIs - at the same time that OO was being introduced - if the "native OO
syntax" could include interfaces to the operating-system-specific GUI calls.

In a way this was a little contradictory - because this draft Standard
*does* introduce the Screen Section and "enhanced Accept/Display" for
character based applications.  However, this was taken (mostly) from the
X/Open specification that WAS already available on many platforms and with
many compilers (unlike the AcuCOBOL windowing syntax which was basically
only available from one vendor).

The bottom-line (from my PERSONAL perspective) is to

1) Use the AcuCOBOL syntax if you think that your application will ALWAYS
live in an AcuCOBOL world (many operating systems - but only one vendor's
compiler).

2) If you think that your COBOL application will ever need to run on
environments or with applications that AcuCOBOL doesn't support, then go
with:

  A) an Sp2 approach - if you think that Sp2 is available now (or in the
foreseeable future) on all the platforms and with all the compilers that you
are interested in.

 B) Stick to "isolated" OO syntax if you need to develop an application that
is guaranteed to run on *ANY* ANSI/ISO  compiler (when the next Standard
actually becomes official)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37A9E433.46566A28@home.com>...
>'screen-creators' giving you validity checks etc. To give MicroFocus
>their due - they have been very BRAVE to introduce OO - considering the
>scepticism there is in the EDP community as a whole.

Agree.

>Acucobol existed until I hit this NewsGroup - requirement - you've got
>to have an Internet connection to keep up-to-date today. (Gone are the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>what their advertising guys are doing.

Well, let us put it this way; when did you last see an independent article
about Cobol in the mainstream computer magazines? And how many have you been
able to count of C/C++/VB/Delphi the last week?

There is the problem, computer journalists like the cool stuff, Cobol is by
definition dead and boring, hence no coverage. If a company should be able
to keep pace with journalistic stuff, no money would be enough. Just look
what they did to OS/2. Today they blame IBM for ruining competition because
they didn't keep OS/2 at pace, totally ignoring their own guilt. I get sick
by thinking about it. Spending tenths of pages covering the vaporware years
ahead of its release and hardly mentioning the brand new release of OS/2
Warp.

>Assuming I get my correspondent's blessing to quote him, look for a
>controversial topic I will post under a new thread "Who Cares ?".

Sure!

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windowing : Business v Dialog Logic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<933870757.18658.0.nnrp-03.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37A0A6FA.942AE116@home.com> <7nsb1a$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37A24CFD.AA17B037@zip.com.au> <933704693.20329.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk> <37A90CD9.D345E0@home.com>`

```
When i say combining the business logic with GUI, im talking
mainClass        <---MDIFRAME

if the end user selects the New menuitem from the drop down menu
then mainClass will call class NewFile
ie:
invoke NewFile "Create"
invoke NewFile "Show"

When usr enters filename, and clicks OK

method "OpenFile" will be executed in class NewFile.


I really dont see a problem with this approch.
I have also noticed that the letters "OO" should be
kept quite as too many people get affended.


Simon R Hart.
```
