#### ↳ Re: interesting bug

- **From:** allenmc@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ocn4e$oso$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net>,
  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
> Found the following under Fujitsu 4.0 today.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Craps out the *second* time it runs with a "divide by zero!" error.
Not the
> open statement itself, but the perform. I was able to fix it by
changing the
> UNTIL to a flag check, and setting the flag in the IF statement of the
> OPEN-THE-FILE paragraph, but a divide by zero error on a PERFORM/UNTIL
> through me off for about an hour.
>
>


I'm gonna skip all the digs about the different COBOL's and try to stay
with your point.  I agree with Walter, you're best bet would be to use
a "WITH TEST AFTER" clause in this type of logic.  I typically
incorporate the use of a 88's and declaratives with it.   Here is an
example:


       01  OK-SW    PIC X(01).
           88  OK       VALUE "Y".
           88  FAILURE  VALUE "SPACE".

                  :

       PROCEDURE DIVISION
       DECLARATIVES
       OUTPUT-FILE-ERROR SECTION.
            USE AFTER STANDARD ERROR PROCEDURE ON OUTPUT
       OUTPUT-FILE-ERROR-ROUTINE.
            SET FAILURE TO TRUE.
  ***  GET YOUR EXTENDED FILE STATUS IN THIS AREA.
       END DECLARATIVES.

       PERFORM OPEN-THE-FILE THRU
               OPEN-THE-FILE-EXIT
               WITH TEST AFTER
               UNTIL OK.


   OPEN-THE-FILE.
       SET OK TO TRUE.
       MOVE "00" TO FILE-STATUS.
       OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE.
       IF FAILURE
  ***     DO WHATEVER YOU HAVE TO HERE TO FIX THE ERROR
       END-IF.
   OPEN-THE-FILE-EXIT.
       EXIT.

In this example you can get any extended error code such as RM/COBOL's
errors via C$RERR or the 9/xxx errors in Micro Focus.  I've got
Fujitsu's version 3.0 but havn't had time to see how they get their
extended codes but I hope you have the idea here.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-b05413d848"></a>
## Thread: Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o5s6u$p4j@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The following is a direct quote from the *draft* minutes of the J4 meeting
that occurred in July,

"19.2  99-0217 - Dates, Times and Durations (Jones)

Mr. Murray will prepare a proposal addressing Miscellaneous Date and Time
Changes to expand the allowable range of seconds on the CURRENT-DATE and
WHEN-COMPILED functions from 00 through 59 to 00 through 60 so as to
accommodate leap seconds that are inserted from time to time.  This change
will permit COBOL compatablity with the ever slowing rate of rotation of the
earth."
```

#### ↳ Re: Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7o5s6u$p4j@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Will the function that computes time differences also take
these leap seconds into account? This is actually a serious
matter.

```

```
> Will the function that computes time differences also take
> these leap seconds into account? This is actually a serious

>

(FYI, IBM's mainframe "LE callable services" has such a routine - and I
think it pays attention to leap-seconds - but I won't swear to it.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a795e9@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7o5s6u$p4j@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net> <7o7ace$b93@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill,
I've lost track of what the standard does, what the proposed standard does,
what the various
vendor do, but IF such a function were available, it SHOULD take into
account leap seconds.
Other languages, e.g. C, do that.

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7o7ace$b93@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com...
> Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in message
> news:37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o6ts5$k5k$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7o5s6u$p4j@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
There are no intrinsic functions for calculating the difference
between two times. Any such calculation is the responsibility
of the programmer. This means that the program, to be
accurate, must maintain a table of dates for whch leap
seconds have been added and will be added.

I agree that the proposed change is serious as it has the
potential to break every program that relies on
CURRENT-DATE having values of only 0 through 59 seconds.
For example, a program which verifies the time stamp on
a transaction will incorrectly reject a transaction with a valid
time of 23:59:60. It may also incorrectly accept it!

I think the change to WHEN-COMPILED is humorous; but
ultimately it is better to make everything correct as soon
as possible.

It is my understanding that leap seconds may be added
at the end of June 30th or Dec 31st; but not more than one
leap second will be added to either of those dates. There
is also a possibility that leap seconds may be added
to both dates in the same year.

The 1985 ANSI C standard allowed for two leap seconds.
The current draft of C contains changes to reduce that to
one leap second! Those COBOL language implementations
that use C or C++ for their runtimes or those running on
Unix already have some ability to accomodate this change.

COBOL is late in correcting for this problem; but better late
than never.

For the US, the correct current time, from the US Naval
Observatory (?), is broadcast at several frequencys in
both an oral and encoded format. There are receivers
that translate the encoded format and, with appropriate
software, update computer clocks automatically. Those
computers must be able to accommodate leap seconds
and, by extension, those programs (and languages) used
on those computers must accommodate leap seconds,
as well.

Many VCRs are already capable of receiving a translated
time signal though their connection to the local cable
operator. (Actually, I don't know if it is the cable operator or
TV stations; but my VCR is always accurate and shows the
correct time within seconds after a power failure.) Soon, we
are likely to have the ability to inexpensively synchronize all
computers to the correct time. This means that all programs,
sooner or later, must be able to accommodate leap seconds.
------------------
Rick Smith

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net>...
>Will the function that computes time differences also take
>these leap seconds into account? This is actually a serious
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

meeting

>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> accommodate leap seconds that are inserted from time to time.  This
change
>> will permit COBOL compatablity with the ever slowing rate of rotation of
>the
>> earth."
```

```
calendars from ours?  Do they have their computers and languages use
those calendars, or do they hard code any needed conversions?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o7j16$kv4@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7o5s6u$p4j@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net> <7o6ts5$k5k$1@news.hitter.net> <37A740CD.602048EC@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:37A740CD.602048EC@NOSPAMhome.com...
> What is typically (programmatically) done in places which use different
> calendars from ours?  Do they have their computers and languages use
> those calendars, or do they hard code any needed conversions?

There are currently (current Standard *and* draft of next Standard) NO ways
to handle other calendars in the ANSI/ISO COBOL definition.

IBM (mainframe at least) does have some LE callable services to convert
dates to the Japanese and Chinese calendars.  I assume (but could be wrong)
that other "cultures" have standard routines for doing this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Maybe J4 has a sense of humor after all

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o78q7$qq7$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7o5s6u$p4j@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37a6e0e0@news3.us.ibm.net> <7o6ts5$k5k$1@news.hitter.net> <37A740CD.602048EC@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37A740CD.602048EC@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>What is typically (programmatically) done in places which use different
>calendars from ours?  Do they have their computers and languages use
>those calendars, or do they hard code any needed conversions?

I haven't done that much international work. I know that every culture,
that uses computers, recognizes the international calendar rules.
Where there are differences, between local and international, none
have ever requested changes to my employer's software products.

There was an interesting newsgroup discussion I followed some
time ago. It involved internationally recognizable date formats.
As usual, someone pointed out that the ISO already has a
standard covering date formats that may be used internationally.

This site discusses the format and also has links to other
interesting pages.
< http://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/~mgk25/iso-time.html >.

There is also a page for Calendar FAQs
< http://www.pip.dknet.dk/~c-t/calendar.html >.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
>Monopoly money, and I'm borrowing money to finance my drilling
>operations, and I'm paying, say, $1 mill a day interest on the loan - I
>can see where that leap-second would make me sit up and pay attention!

Since banks charge interest, either daily or monthly, that one
second is free of interest. You could jump for joy; but you might
land at the feet of Bertha and there is no telling what she might
do!

Just think about how much money workers don't receive
because they work that leap second for free. Let's see! The
leap second only occurs at midnight. Taking some rough
guesses about how many people might be working at midnight:
include police, fire, hospital, nursing homes, other security,
hotels, restaraunts, bars, 24-hour convenience, grocery, and other
stores, airlines and other transportation, manufacturing plants,
computer data centers, etc.; don't include programmers called
out to fix problems with transactions containing leap seconds;
because that is after the fact. Crunch! Crunch! Maybe US $4000
on weekdays; US $800 weekends. Now if I can only find a way
to get that money to my checking account!

Note: The next leap second will likely occur at the end of
30 June 2000. Previous was at the end of 31 December 1998.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7n79f1$8ah$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Check out  the Communications document on the Net Express CD.  The Client
Server Bindings example is a program example of a server to client file
transfer.  We tried it and it works fine (after correcting a linkage area
typo in the sample program).

FS_COPY_FILE will copy client to client or server to server files only.
This limitation let me to try Client Server Bindings.



Ken Mullins wrote in message <7n79f1$8ah$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
>anyone use NE to copy a file from a fileshare server to a client...I'd like
>to be able to have an .ini file on the FS server machine, and copy it to
the
>client machines each time my client app starts...NE doc says FS_COPY_FILE
>works with files on the server or local files, but it does not...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress FS_COPY_FILE

- **From:** brian.hawco@hrdc-drhc.gc.ca
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oa6s0$uh3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7n79f1$8ah$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken
you're right....as the command is executed on the Fileshare server, it
simply copies the file to the drive(s) that it can see. It can't see
any client drives. I ended up doing a series of Open/Read/Writes to do
the same thing.

In response to a diff msg that you had posted about getting a list of
the install files/libraries required for Fileshare and FSVIEW, let me
know if you still need this. We had created an install CD for Fileshare
running on NT and the same for FSVIEW.

Brian

Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-50ddc578d9"></a>
## Thread: OT: (???) Obscure (!!!) Standard note — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: (???) Obscure (!!!) Standard note

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o5per$gg0@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Given some of the recent threads about "open stuff",  I thought I would pass
along the following *OBSCURE* information that was just sent out to the J4
distribution list.  Before you try and fight your way thru it, I think it is
talking about combining a couple of "ANSI" (or ISO?) committees.  It also
talks about the status of some of the existing projects and Standards.  I
think this *MIGHT* be of interest to some of the readers of C.L.C. because
it deals with (among other things) some of the

 - Language Independent Datatypes
 - Language Independent Arithmetic
 - Language Independent calling conventions
 - Abstract Schemas and language notations

  ***  <obscure quote follows> ***
From: Daniel Gillman, gillman <at> census.gov

Dear L8 Members, T2 Members, and Interested Parties,

As many of you know, there is an effort underway to try to merge L8 and
T2.  The merged group will be known as L8.  L8 is willing to accept any
current T2 project for which there is active interest and support.  This
means there needs to be 2 or 3 people who have the expertise and time to
devote to developing,  maintaining, reviewing, or revising each project.

Please review the list of T2 projects attached below (Murray Freeman
supplied this information).  The projects are broken into 3 categories:
schema, syntax, and SC22/WG11.  If you can support and actively
participate in the work of one or more of these projects, please let me
know NO LATER THAN 17 SEPTEMBER 1999 via email.  Please make sure you
list each project you can support.

For your information, L8 meets informally monthly in Washington, usually
at the National Institute for Standards and Technology.  We always have
a conference telephone link to the meeting and often have video
conferencing set up.  Both have proven to be effective ways to allow
participation for people who can't travel to the meetings.  The L8
plenary sessions (3 or 4 times per year) also use these links, although
members should try to physically attend at least one plenary each year.

At the NCITS meeting last week, the status of T2 and the possible merger
with L8 came up.  NCITS is very concerned about the vialbility of T2.
So, we need to decide which projects can be supported in L8 as soon as
possible.  T3 expressed interest in taking the ASN.1 projects.  They may
be interested in others as well.

Let me know if you have any quesitons.  My phone number is
+1-301-457-3690.

Yours,
Dan Gillman
Chair, L8

--------------------------------------------------
-----------<1a: T2 AHG-Schema projects>-----------

Generic Conceptual Schema Specification for Data Interchange
NCITS project 689-D, JTC1 project 21.63: FCD 14481
>T2's first effort on conceptual schemas; forther work on it has been
suspended in order to concentrate on the international CSMF project.
Expert: Tony Sarris <tony.sarris@unisys.com> or <asarris@home.com>

IRDS Normative Schema
NCITS project 988-D, JTC1 project 21.63: FCD 14481
>Inherited from X3T4 on its demise. Presently, only a maintenance
responsibility, I believe.
Expert: Tony Sarris <tony.sarris@unisys.com> or <asarris@home.com>

Conceptual Schema Modelling Facility (CSMF)
NCITS project 1043-L, JTC1 project 21.63: FCD 14481
>This is the "active" Conceptual Schema project.
Editor/expert: Tony Sarris <tony.sarris@unisys.com> or
<asarris@home.com>

Knowledge Information Format (KIF)
NCITS project 1058-D
>T2 unanimously voted this out recently as a dpANS, but the editor, as
of 28
June (see 2a below), had not yet sent Sarris the version for
distribution to
NCITS (T2 had voted on the basis of NCITS.T2/98-004, an HTML document).
Editor/expert: Michael Genesereth <genesereth@cs.stanford.edu>

Conceptual Graphs (CG)
NCITS project 1059-D
>T2 unanimously voted this out recently as a dpANS, but the editor, as
of 28
June (see 2a below), had not yet sent Sarris the version for
distribution to
NCITS (T2 had voted on the basis of NCITS.T2/98-003, an HTML document
with 5
GIF inserts).
Editor/expert: John Sowa <sowa@west.poly.edu>

Concepts and Terminology for the Conceptual Schema and the Information
Base
NCITS project 1354-L, JTC1 project 01.00: ISO TR9007:1987
>Probably inherited by T2 because of the initial dependence of
Conceptual
Schema efforts on this famous "orange book" TR with its "three-schema
architecture."
Editor/expert: Tony Sarris <tony.sarris@unisys.com> or
<asarris@home.com>

-----------<1b: T2 AHG-Syntax projects>-----------

>>Abstract Syntax Notation One (ASN.1) & its encodings

NCITS project 520-M;
JTC1 projects 33.17; 33.17.03.01, IS 8824-1:1995; 33.17.03.01.01, IS
8824-1/AM1:1996; 33.17.03.01.02, IS 8824-1:1995/PDAM2;
33.17.03.01.02.01,
NP 8824-1; 33.17.03.01.03, IS 8824-1:199x/FPDAM1; 33.17.03.02, IS
8824-2:1995;
33.17.03.02.01, IS 8824-2/AM1:1996; 33.17.03.02.01.01, NP 8824-2;
33.17.03.03, IS 8824-3:1995; 33.17.03.04, IS 8824-4:1995;
33.17.03.04.02, IS 8824-3:199x/FPDAM1; 33.17.03.04.02.01, NP 8824-3;
33.17.03.04.02.02, NP 8824-4; 33.17.05, IS 8824:1990; 33.21.01.03.01,
IS 8824-1:1995/PDAM3.

NCITS project 521-M;
JTC1 project 33.17.04.01, IS 8825-1:1995; 33.17.04.01.01, NP 8825-1;
33.17.04.02, IS 8825-2:1996; 33.17.04.02.00.01, NP 8825-2;
33.17.04.02.01, IS 8825-2:199x/FPDAM1; 33.17.04.02.02, IS
8825-1:199x/FPDAM1;
33.17.06, IS 8825:1990.

>Expert/Editor: ISO editor Bancroft Scott <baos@oss.com>

All these projects are presently in maintenance mode. ASN.1 standards
are
divided between the 4-part ISO 8824 (NCITS project 520-M) which
specifies
the abstract syntax, and the 2-part ISO 8825, which specifies its
concrete
encodings. With the demise of SC21, Bancroft has indicated that he may
use
the ITU-T channel to handle any maintenance responsibilities on these
collaborative standards (ISO 8824-1, for example, refers to itself as
"this
ISO 8824-1 standard|ITU-T Recommendation X.680").

ASN.1 was used initially to specify a "meaningful" data syntax for use
at
the OSI presentation & application layers, as opposed to the treatment
of
message data at lower levels only as strings of octets. I consider T2's
efforts, to avoid its overspecialization to OSI usage, a success story;
ASN.1 is now used, for example, by the VISA/MasterCard system.

Briefly, ISO 8824, the abstract syntax, specifies how the data is
organized,
and ISO 8825 how it is encoded. ISO 8825-1 specifies its full blown
Basic
Encoding Rules (BER), in which each data item carries a tag and length
indicator in addition to the data itself, whereas ISO 8825-2's Packed
Encoding Rules (PER) carries abbreviated tags and length indicators only

where items are either optional or may vary in length. In spite of T2's
advice to the contrary, an IEEE standards group, as might be expected,
insisted on referring to BER instead of the abstract syntax, leaving
them
out in the cold as far as the more efficient PER was concerned.


Specification for a Data Descriptive File (DDF) for Information
Interchange
NCITS project 40-M, JTC1 project 21.08.01; IS 8211:1994
>This is in maintenance mode, a revision of an older version which
happened
to be the first "A/I" standard. Generally, an ISO 8211 file carries a
description of its structure in its first records, plus further item
descriptions when needed therafter.
Expert/Editor: Al Brooks <brooks@icx.net>


Representation of Numerical Values in Character Strings (NRICS)
NCITS project 104-M, JTC1 project 21.32; ANS X3.42:1990, ISO 6093:1985
>This NCITS project, whose object was to bring X3.42 into alignment with
ISO
6093 except for the latter's "decimal comma," became "stalled" when its
editor (myself) was unable to keep up with the necessary work in
addition to
his duties as T2 Secretary and Vice Chair. In addition, following the
project instructions involved "destandardizing" X3.42's treatment of
exponent signs.
Generally, both ISO 6093 and ANS X3.42 specify three numeric formats:
NR1
(Fortran Iw), "nnnnn"; NR2 (Fortran Fw.d), "nn.nnnn"; and NR3 (Fortran
Ew.dEe), "nn.nnnnEsnn". where "s" is the exponent + or - sign (only
X3.42
allows it to be blank if the exponent is zero or positive).
Expert/Editor: Murray Freeman <x3t2mf@ptd.net>


Transfer Syntax Description Notation (TSDN)
NCITS Project 735-M; ANS X3.208:1996
> In maintenance mode. Although this was initially developed as a "first

step" in fast-tracking it as an international standard, NASA's efforts
to
get support outside the U.S. ran out of steam. X3.208 was intended to
provide a standard method for describing the organization of archival
data
from space
(and for bringing the Consultative Committee For Space Data Systems -
CCSDS -
into the ISO world).
Expert/Editor: Fred Billingsley <FBILLINGSL@aol.com>

-----------<1c: T2 AHG-SC22/WG11 projects>-----------

Language-Independent Datatypes (LID)
NCITS project 593-MI, JTC1 project 22.17; ANSI/ISO 11404:1996
>in maintenance mode. Primarily intended to provide a way of describing
data
types such that the descriptions do not rely on assumptions implicit in
the
various programming language.
>Expert & former editor: Ed Barkmeyer <edbark@cme.nist.gov>

Language-Independent Procedure Calling (LIPC)
NCITS project 602-MI, JTC1 project 22.16; ANSI/ISO 13886:1996
>in maintenance mode. Intended to provide a method of defining the
different
methods various languages (and sometimes implementations of them) use to

invoke other procedures. Although project originated in T2, the people
associated with it are long gone.
>Expert/editor: none; check with SC22/WG11 Convenor Willem Wakker
<willemw@ace.nl> if necessary.

Language Compatible Arithmetic (LIA):
(LIA-1) Part 1: Integer and Floating Point Arithmetic
 NCITS project 686-MI, JTC1 project 22.28; IS 10967-1:1994
(LIA-2) Part 2: Elementary Numeric Functions
 NCITS project 786-I, JTC1 project 22.33; CD 10967-2
(LIA-3) Part 3: Complex Floating Point Arithmetic and Complex Elementary

Numeric Functions
 NCITS project 785-I, JTC1 project 22.34; WD 10967-3??
>LIA-3 is currently under development, but both LIA-1 & LIA-2 are
presently
in maintenance mode. Although Mary Payne of Digital initially served as
ISO
editor for these projects, her recent death, and other resignations from
T2
by Digital representatives, have forced T2 to rely on others interested
in
LIA in order to respond to CD ballots and the like.
Experts: Kevin Harris <harris@cairn.enet.dec.com>; plus, from outside
T2,
others in the US active in SC22/WG11 with LIA interests:
 Tom Turba <turba@rsvl.unisys.com> liaison with SC22/WG2 - Pascal;
 Donald Nelson <nelson_don@comm.tandem.com> liaison with SC22/WG4 -
COBOL; and
 Bill Carlson <carlson@inmet.inmet.com> liaison with SC22/WG9 - ADA

Guidelines for Preparation of Language Independent Service Specification

NCITS project 1220-L, JTC1 project 22.46; DTR 14369
>essentially now in maintenance mode. T2 inherited TAG responsibility on

this project because of its other SC22/WG11 TAG responsibilities, and
this
TR's references to LID and LIPC above. These guidelines are intended to
serve to help in the specification of application-OS interfaces without
depending on the "implicit" relations expected in the use of certain
programming languages.
Experts: see LIA experts above.

  >> end of quote <<
```

## Thread: Micro Focus file errors — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus file errors

- **From:** craigdiaz@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o5ojd$o4k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,
I'm familiar with COBOL, but not on a PC. I'm getting errors trying to
read input files with Micro Focus version 3.0, the status is usually 39
which appears to be an operating system incompatiblity error,
diagnostics say recreate the file. Okay, but with what? I tried using
REBUILD.EXE, all it did eventually was change the error status for the
file to 22, an illegal OPEN error. The files are part of a 'legacy'
system that was running under Novell netware, Win95. I've copied the
files to my stand-alone running Win98. Some of the files I can
read/write no problem, others won't cooperate at all. The error
messages in the manual just don't seem to help. Anyone met this problem
before?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus file errors

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o5pti$gol@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7o5ojd$o4k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
File status 39 means that your file is NOT defined in your FD the same way
it exits on the hard-drive.  Find out the actual "attributes" of the file,
and then change your source code to match.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus file errors

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A6735B.F73CC030@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7o5ojd$o4k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
craigdiaz@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> before?

I'm using NetExpress. The only time I've hit an Error 39 -  
incompatibility - is where I have changed a record size, recompiled the
program and then used a data file which was in the old record format.
As regards the Error 22 - are you positive you haven't got any dup keys
?

Beyond that I can't help.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
 - example - ascii delimited - length 178
```

<a id="t-bb189960f5"></a>
## Thread: Problem with Fujitsu ListView Control — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Problem with Fujitsu ListView Control

- **From:** "JM_EURO" <euromercante@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o4jij$hj4$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
hi,

I have a program in powercobol that has a listview control, i'm trying to
move a PIC 9(6)V99 (PEDPV1) ,from a cobol file to that listview,but the
field displayed in the listview has two more zeros, for example:
                file------------->150----------> listview----->15000

i have already try every thing (i think), i think that the problem was with
the decimal positions, but the PowerCobol gives the following warning:

 Pedidos CmCommand6-Click(184) : JMN3022I-W   NON-INTEGER ITEM 'PEDPV1'
CANNOT BE MOVED TO ALPHANUMERIC ITEM 'POW-0015'. AFTER MOVING SENDING
OPERAND TO UNSIGNED EXTERNAL DECIMAL ITEM WITH THE SAME DIGITS AS SENDING
OPERAND,ALPHANUMERIC ITEM REDEFINING THAT EXTERNAL DECIMAL ITEM IS MOVED TO
RECEIVING ALPHANUMERIC ITEM.

so i would to know what can i do to solve this problem????

thank you

        Cristov�o
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with Fujitsu ListView Control

- **From:** murka <marko@oring.si>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A682D4.208E32EF@oring.si>`
- **References:** `<7o4jij$hj4$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Move PEDPV1 to a numeric-edited PEDPV1-E (pic zzzzz9.99), which is redefined
as PEDPV1-A (pic x(9).) Then move PEDPV1-A to POW-0015.

JM_EURO wrote:

> hi,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Cristovï¿½o
```

<a id="t-c4a6131efd"></a>
## Thread: Migrating to COBOL for OS390 — 2 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1999-07](1999-07.md#t-c4a6131efd)._

#### ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o3k2a$74i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com>`

```
In article <379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com>,
  Peter Marshall <marshall.peter@canadatrust.com> wrote:
> Hello all!
>
> We are in the process of evaluating a move from our current COBOL, VS
> COBOL II (version 1.3.0), to the new COBOL for OS390 (version 2.1).

<snip>

> We are curious to know if anyone else has done this type of migration
> and if so, have run across any problems/pitfalls.

<snip the rest>

Peter,

In addition to what the other persons wrote about your question (and to
whom I fully agree), I can give you some hints about problems you
can get when you recompile existing VS COBOL II R3 code to a newer
compiler (thanks to Sabine Lauterbach from DATEV, Germany!):

1. The period in area A is not tolerated anymore
   (ISPF: change '.' 8 11 '     .' all)

2. A field defined as 'PIC X' may not been compared with 'NOT ZERO',
   only with 'NOT = ZERO'. NOT ZERO is interpreted as Sign-Condition,
   NOT = ZERO as a Figurative Constant.

3. You may not SET and INDEX to 0 (and then add 1 in a loop). This
   was never allowed, but COBOL II accepted this. If you don't want
   to change your existing logic, you can SET the index to 1 and then
   SET it DOWN BY 1.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A599F3.68A7D471@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <7o3k2a$74i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Daniel Jacot wrote:

> 3. You may not SET and INDEX to 0 (and then add 1 in a loop). This
>    was never allowed, but COBOL II accepted this. If you don't want
>    to change your existing logic, you can SET the index to 1 and then
>    SET it DOWN BY 1.

I never knew that COBOL II had this improvement over this silly
standard.
```

<a id="t-5313fa36db"></a>
## Thread: MFC signals — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [1999-07](1999-07.md#t-5313fa36db)._

#### ↳ Re: MFC signals

- **From:** "Ian Marshall" <Ian.Marshall@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o57o1$m33$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<W6qo3.2761$L96.1789991@typhoon1.austin.rr.com>`

```
The Micro Focus COBOL runtime attempts to trap all of the standard UNIX
signals. The default operation is to clean up (close files, etc) and to
exit.  This includes SIGHUP (1) and SIGTERM (15)

You can specify your own signal handlers to operate in conjunction with (or
override) the RTS by using the cobpostsighandler API call. The docs for this
are in the "programmers guide to writing programs" in the "mixed language
programming" chapter.

NB: It is not always possible for the RTS to terminate and close everything
cleanly - with a SIGSEGV or SIGILL for instance, all that's really possible
is to terminate as the process is in an illegal state.  With SIGKILL (9),
the signal is totally uncatchable by any UNIX process - so, don't use it
unless your process won't respond to anything else.
```

- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o6lp4$bfi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I copied a batch of Micro Focus I-O files and executables from my D:
(CD) to my C: drive. Exactly the same files, same path - as far as I
can see, same everything. The programs execute okay from D: but not
from C: - the file-status error rc I get is 9A 65. Has anyone any ideas
why? Thanks.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: micro focus file errors

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B18A92.9428BB0@gfc-net.de>`
- **References:** `<7o6lp4$bfi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It seems like the file is locked (that is what 9A means, 65 is simply the
decimal ascii code of the letter A).
This might happen if you simply copy the files from the CD where all files have
the readonly attribute. Go into that directory and type "attrib -r *.*" and your
problem might be fixed.

Regards
Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

<a id="t-18ab6024d9"></a>
## Thread: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data — 15 msgs

### Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I have a two-part question about word versus byte storage mode for USAGE IS
COMP[UTATIONAL] COBOL data.

With byte storage,  data use as many bytes are required to hold the value;
with word storage,  COMP data is always rounded up to use either 2,4 or 8
bytes of storage. For example, given:

 05  FOO    PICTURE IS S(06)V9(04) USAGE COMP.


With byte storage, 5 fives are used; with word storage, 8 bytes are used.

Question, Part I:

Does ANSI or any other organization, or your experience, tell you that the
excess bytes of storage are initialized to x'00' if positive or 'xFF' is
negative?

That is, if I need to translate the data, do I interrogate the sign of the
value in the 8th byte, or in the 5th byte (if bit 7, the high bit, is on and
the data are signed, it's negative)?

I ran some tests on an IBM mainframe (COBOL V2 r4) and IBM does initialize
these bytes as above; but I don't know if that is a COBOL standard or "just
what IBM does" or even "just what IBM does today in this release."

Question, Part II:

On the IBM mainframe I could find no way to force byte storage for COMP. On
my Microfocus Personal COBOL (PC) (the cheap version without a real
compiler) I could find no way to force word storage. (Not that I necessarily
wanted to do either).

What, in your experience, is the "default" or "my way or no way" method of
COMP storage by (what, hardware?  Operating system?  Compiler? )

Thanks,

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Easy (but unfortunate) part of the answer:
  USAGE COMPUTATIONAL is TOTALLY non-Standard. It can (and does) mean
different things for different compilers on different platforms. (On some it
means binary, on some it is display, and on some it is packed-decimal.)

If you switch to USAGE BINARY is part of the Standard - but what it means is
ALMOST as implementor-specific.   (It is always binary - but storage amounts
and byte-order ARE specifically up to the vendor).

IBM mainframe explicitly does NOT support what Micro Focus calls
"byte-storage".  Neither does it support "little-endian" storage.

The ANSI Standard is QUITE explicit that when you don't put a VALUE clause
on a numeric (or any other field) that "RESULTS ARE UNPREDICTABLE".  With
IBM Mainframe COBOL (IBM COBOL for this-and-that) the run-time option
STORAGE(00) tells the compiler to set it up as binary zeroes - but that
isn't part of the COBOL language. When you  say COBOL 2.4 - I think you may
be using OS/VS COBOL and that is an unsupported IBM product - and one of the
MAJOR migration inhibitors for sites getting to a supported version is this
very "bug" in initialization.  Micro Focus has its own compiler option to do
this too.  (I can't remember which it is right now - but someone should be
able to provide it for you.)  HOWEVER, this is a dangerous thing to rely on.
Use the INITIALIZE verb, a VALUE clause, or a MOVE statement to get initial
values into fields that you want to "guarantee" a value for.

I hope this answers most of your questions - unfortunately not with "happy"
replies for what you want.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NMKp3.533$n66.24552@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>The ANSI Standard is QUITE explicit that when you don't put a VALUE clause
>on a numeric (or any other field) that "RESULTS ARE UNPREDICTABLE".

.....>Use the INITIALIZE verb, a VALUE clause, or a MOVE statement to get
initial
>values into fields that you want to "guarantee" a value for.

I guess I wasn't too clear in what I was asking.VALUE IS, MOVE and
INITIALIZE are not part of my question.

 In the example I gave (PIC S9(06)V9(04) COMP, IBM mainframe), 5 bytes are
required to hold the data. With WORD storage, 8 bytes are allocated.

When I interrogate the bytes (which is after I MOVE) , the IBM compiler is
actually using  8 bytes of "data" even though 5 bytes are sufficient to hold
the value.

In hex, for example, the value 257 (decimal) as COMP would look like this in
5 bytes:
00 00 00 01 01

But I get 8 bytes:
00 00 00 00 00 00 01 01

But what if those eight bytes looked like:

12 34 56 00 00 00 01 01

This is still 257 (decimal) if I only read the five LSB, but a big number if
I read all 8.

AFAIK, the three extra bytes prepended by the compiler when using WORD
alignment are inaccessible to COBOL programs and by definition SIZE ERROR
occurs when there would be a radix 10 overflow; so what may be said of the
three prepended bytes?

The '00'  run option does not fit: all my negative numbers came back with
x'FF' in these three bytes, which is the value you'd expect if (and I think
I just figured out the correct question to ask) THE DATA ARE STORED AS A
64-BIT INTEGER.

So, when word alignment is in use are COMP data types stored as 16, 32 and
64 bit integers?

Does this vary by compiler? hardware? Phase of the moon?

FWIW, if I can count on 16,32,64-bit integers, the conversion DLL is done
and tested as well as I can and the documetation, test data files and sample
conversion program all work.  I'm waiting on some BASIC/C testers to run it.
But you just gave me a good remoinder: I better state in the documentation
that COMP and COMP-3 etc are "ANSI".

MCM


>    wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o8rug$v6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <NMKp3.533$n66.24552@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <NMKp3.533$n66.24552@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
  "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

<snip>

>  In the example I gave (PIC S9(06)V9(04) COMP, IBM mainframe), 5 bytes
> are required to hold the data. With WORD storage, 8 bytes are
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does this vary by compiler? hardware? Phase of the moon?

<snip the rest>

It does vary by the TRUNC compiler option. If you compile with
TRUNC(STD), then you get an overflow if your data does not fit into the
PIC clause (in your sample if the number is greater than or equal to
1,000,000.000).
If you compile TRUNC(BIN), your program can compute with the full binary
values of 2, 4 or 8 bytes. In the IBM mainframe world, you need this
option if your COBOL program calls or is called by a PL/I or C/C++
program since these languages always use the full binary value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HFYp3.119$OO4.8148@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <NMKp3.533$n66.24552@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o8rug$v6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Daniel Jacot wrote in message <7o8rug$v6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
>It does vary by the TRUNC compiler option. If you compile with
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks for the tip on the TRUNC option. I did not think it applied, because
TRUNC mostly deals with the way *decimal* truncation occurs vis-a-vis the
PICTURE
clause and the generation of SIZE ERROR conditions. However, I dug out that
section of my manual and found this:

" The TRUNC(BIN) option applies to all COBOL language that processes USAGE
BINARY data. When TRUNC(BIN) is in effect:...
[other stuff]
* The full binary content of the field is significant
[more other stuff]
"..TRUNC(BIN) is the recommended option when interfacing with other products
that may place values in COBOL binary data items that are larger than that
defined by the data item's PICTURE clause (such as DB2,FORTRAN and PL/I)..."

According to IBM, " TRUNC(STD) conforms to the COBOL 85 Standard, while
TRUNC(OPT) and TRUNC(BIN) are IBM extensions.... TRUNC(STD) applies only to
USAGE BINARY receiving fields in MOVE statements and arithmetic expressions.
When TRUNC(STD) is in effect. the final result of an arithmetic expression,
or the sending field of a MOVE statement, is truncated to the number of
digits in the PICTURE clause of the BINARY receiving field."

Reviewing the examples of "what the bytes look like" in this section, I can
tell the
IBM compiler uses the full binary value (halfword, fullword or doubleword)
of the field at all times for
storage, even if the binary mantissa has been adjusted by decimal truncation
in a
previous MOVE or arithmetic operation. Thus, the only numeric value errors I
could expect using the full binary field are those which ALREADY OCCURRED
because the data were stored with a decimal truncation; and of
course, my conversion program cannot be responsible for "garbage in."

So now, I have the answer "for IBM mainframes".

(Ten bucks it's the same on other "word storage" compilers, too.)

Thanks again for the tip.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o9uj9$5iq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <NMKp3.533$n66.24552@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o8rug$v6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <HFYp3.119$OO4.8148@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Almost every COBOL compiler that I know of has a "TRUNC" (or equivalent)
compiler option.  Certainly Micro Focus does and I am fairly certain that
Fujitsu and Reallia do too.

Start your search in the documentation under "TRUNC" or "truncation" and I
think you will find what you are looking for.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PSLq3.3130$Le1.31505@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
First off, Michael, you probably DON'T need to be worrying about how many
bytes an item takes.
2nd, William's answer is somewhat misleading and incorrect.

COMPUTATIONAL on EVERY Mainframe and all IBM and other Big Iron
Midframes/minis
is ALWAYS the SAME PROVIDED you SPECIFY not using the vendor's 'more
efficient'
format for COMP.  Otherwise, COBOL wouldn't be able to send data between
different
platforms w/o making everything DISPLAY, and then how would you diferentiate
between
a 'DISPLAY' COMP?? and DISPLAY?

On Wang, HoneyWell, IBM, AMDAHL, etc, COMP,COMP-1/2/3/5 (yes HoneyWell had
COMP-5
(Comp-3 with Sign on left side!)  If you've specified that you DON'T want
the mroe efficient
COMP format, and specify TRUNC(BIN) then the data is interchangeable.

William M. Klein wrote in message <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
>Easy (but unfortunate) part of the answer:
>  USAGE COMPUTATIONAL is TOTALLY non-Standard. It can (and does) mean
>different things for different compilers on different platforms. (On some
it
>means binary, on some it is display, and on some it is packed-decimal.)
>
>If you switch to USAGE BINARY is part of the Standard - but what it means
is
>ALMOST as implementor-specific.   (It is always binary - but storage
amounts
>and byte-order ARE specifically up to the vendor).
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>be using OS/VS COBOL and that is an unsupported IBM product - and one of
the
>MAJOR migration inhibitors for sites getting to a supported version is this
>very "bug" in initialization.  Micro Focus has its own compiler option to
do
>this too.  (I can't remember which it is right now - but someone should be
>able to provide it for you.)  HOWEVER, this is a dangerous thing to rely
on.
>Use the INITIALIZE verb, a VALUE clause, or a MOVE statement to get initial
>values into fields that you want to "guarantee" a value for.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> With byte storage,  data use as many bytes are required to hold the
value;
>> with word storage,  COMP data is always rounded up to use either 2,4 or 8
>> bytes of storage. For example, given:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Does ANSI or any other organization, or your experience, tell you that
the
>> excess bytes of storage are initialized to x'00' if positive or 'xFF' is
>> negative?
>>

the

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7og5s9$2m7@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <PSLq3.3130$Le1.31505@news3.mia>`

```
Check out Deja.com for many threads that report on what USAGE COMPUTATIONAL
means on various platforms.  Just to mention one - you say "on IBM" - and
I'll bet you are talking about OS/390 (previously MVS) or VSE.  Because on
OS/400 COMPUTATIONAL is very DEFINITELY the same as USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL and
*not* the same as USAGE BINARY.  Similarly, there have been a number of
reports of different compilers/platforms where USAGE COMPUTATIONAL is
actually the same as USAGE DISPLAY.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VoXq3.90$9X.5769@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <PSLq3.3130$Le1.31505@news3.mia>`

```
James King wrote in message ...
>First off, Michael, you probably DON'T need to be worrying about how many
>bytes an item takes.

Well, yes, I do. You see, I have written something which parses the PICTURE,
determines the size of the data item, and allows the non-COBOL programmer to
read COBOL data simply by reading and passing the correct number of data
bytes to a procedure.  The product also converts EBCDIC to ASCII (on
request, of course), except when the fields should not be converted (binary
and BCD fields), so I have to know where the qualifying fields within the
record begin and end. I kinda need field "sizes" to do that.

In truth, I already have this problem solved thanks to previous answers
received here but thank you for your efforts.

>2nd, William's answer is somewhat misleading and incorrect.

Not so; while it did not directly answer my question, Mr. Klein's response
was close enough that it got me to look in the right place and has been a
great help.  Although, most responders just assumed I was using COBOL as my
data access method and slanted their responses that way. In fact, I did all
this stuff a bit, nibble or byte at a time in - and don't laugh now - BASIC.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oi01r$56b@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <PSLq3.3130$Le1.31505@news3.mia> <VoXq3.90$9X.5769@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael,
  I don't know if this showed up in any of the earlier posts - but you also
have to worry about "packed-decimal" data.  This comes up every once in a
while in comp.lang.cobol.  The problem is with "even number of digits" in a
packed-decimal UNsigned field.  Given

   05 Whatever  Pic 9(06) Packed-Decimal (or commonly Comp-3 too)

The question is whether this takes 3 or 4 bytes of storage.  In some (I
think the majority) of implementations, there is a nibble reserved for the
NON-sign, so it takes 4-bytes of storage.  While a few implementations
"believe" the PIC and only allocate 3 bytes of storage.)

In fact, this also relates to a general conversion issue.  It some
implementations, the sign-nibble value in both USAGE DISPLAY and
PACKED-DECIMAL is the same for positive and un-signed data - while in others
there is one nibble value that indicates "unsigned" and another that
indicates "positive".  Whether or not there is a separate storage nibble for
the sign in unsigned items, is independent - I think - of which vendors use
the same value in DISPLAY items for positive and unsigned.

Do you have a copy of the '85 COBOL Standard?  You might want to look thru
the hardware dependent and implementor-defined lists for which USAGEs are
there.  Pretty much, what it boils down to is that you need to use USAGE
DISPLAY *and* SIGN IS SEPARATE LEADING - if you want to "guarantee" that you
know what a COBOL source really means.  Otherwise, you MUST know the
"vendor" to interpret the meaning (storage and permissible values) in a
numeric data item.
```

```
>
>   05 Whatever  Pic 9(06) Packed-Decimal (or commonly Comp-3 too)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>"believe" the PIC and only allocate 3 bytes of storage.)

9(06) COMP-3 takes 4 bytes, only 3-1/2 of which are significant . At least
according to Microfocus citing ANSI.

The software I've written will work "as is" for IBM Mainframe and all
Microfocus (and all "true" compatibles thereof). At the price (free) that's
a hell of good deal. Considering how I earn a living, maybe I will do
"vendor implementation variations" for a price.

MCM
```

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:XZ3r3.500$9X.28144@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
  <snip>
> 9(06) COMP-3 takes 4 bytes, only 3-1/2 of which are significant . At least
> according to Microfocus citing ANSI.

I'll bet you almost anything that the section on "COMP-3" is in an
"extension" box in the MF LRM (speaking as the MF LRM "owner" in a
past-life).

HOWEVER, even if you go to the ANSI "Usage Packed-Decimal" - it is ANSI
conforming to store it in either 3 bytes OR 4 bytes - because the ANSI
Standard says that this is completely up to the implementor.  (If an
implementor wanted to store it in 10 bytes, they could - but I doubt they
would sell many.)

P.S. Also from my "past life" - the name is "Micro Focus" - not "Microfocus"
or "Micro Focus" - and should (theoretically) be followed by a "registered
trade mark" character in any of your (you get what you pay for)
documentation.  This always used to be a pet peeve of mine - but believe
me - where they don't pay me a salary any more, I won't tell - if you don't.
```

```
In this life, the "extension box" for storage requirements in the LRM is
marked "OSVS" "VSC2" , "XOPEN" AND "MF".

"PACKED-DECIMAL" is listed *only* as an  'alternate description' of COMP-3
for "ANSI85"

Besides, as as I said before, this is the way it is. You want "special"
you'll have to order it.
(Although if you read the documentation, you'll see you can handle it
without custom programming. If you can't figure it out from that, I'll
accept checks).


>P.S. Also from my "past life" - the name is "Micro Focus" - not
"Microfocus"
>or "Micro Focus" - and should (theoretically) be followed by a "registered
>trade mark" character in any of your (you get what you pay for)

Where I use the name "Microfocus Ltd" it is so identified. I also provide
(registered) trademark credits for PowerBASIC, Microsoft, Windows, IBM and
MS-DOS. I am religious about this kind of thing.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word v byte Storage in COMPUTATIONAL data

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul Keirnan" <paul.keirnan@det.nsw.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ae9374@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<F4Cp3.50$n66.3986@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7o7b54$bjv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <PSLq3.3130$Le1.31505@news3.mia> <VoXq3.90$9X.5769@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7oi01r$56b@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7oi01r$56b@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>...
>Michael,
>  I don't know if this showed up in any of the earlier posts - but you also
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>


In fact, the Honeywell implementation on their L66/L68/DPS8 class machines
provided two definitions of Packed Decimal.

From memory they were COMP-3 and either COMP-5 or maybe COMP-6.

I think that COMP-3 allowed for either signed or unsigned numbers. Therefore
'pic 9(7) COMP-3'  or 'pic s9(6) COMP-3' would take up 4 bytes.
(Also, given that this was running on a 36-bit word architecture, I can't
remember
the idiosyncrasies of the actual mapping).

COMP-5 (or 6, whatever) differed in that it allowed for successive Packed
Decimal number to be tightly packed. That is to say the above definitions
would take up 3 1/2 bytes each if declared next to each other.  In fact the
Network DBMS offering (IDS2) stored any Packed Decimal item on the
database in this format.

Regards,
Paul Keirnan
```

```
portability to another system was rarely an issue.
```

## Thread: Standard dialogs in NetExpress — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Standard dialogs in NetExpress

- **From:** "Martin" <hnetkong@post.cz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o63oq$27td$1@ns.felk.cvut.cz>`

```

Hi everybody,
 can anybody help me with calling standard dialogs (save and print dialogs),
please? I'm beginner in Cobol, so I need very detailed description (with
example).
                                                                Martin
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard dialogs in NetExpress

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<933705454.20720.0.nnrp-10.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7o63oq$27td$1@ns.felk.cvut.cz>`

```

Martin <hnetkong@post.cz> wrote in message
news:7o63oq$27td$1@ns.felk.cvut.cz...
>
> Hi everybody,
>  can anybody help me with calling standard dialogs (save and print
dialogs),
> please? I'm beginner in Cobol, so I need very detailed description (with
> example).
>                                                                 Martin
>
>

Of course:
OpenDialog is class opendlg

           invoke OpenDialog "new" using self
                                                   returning lsOpenDialog

           *> set the file filter so only *.DEF files are shown.
           invoke self "setFileFilter" using lsOpenDialog
           invoke lsOpenDialog "show"


To receive the text entered in the open dialog use

           invoke lsOpenDialog "getFile" returning oPath

oPath is an object, convert it to text if not equal to null.

To print is a little different:

           move length of PrintFileN to FileNameLgth
           move FileName to DocumentTitle
           call 'PC_PRINT_FILE'
               using by reference FileNameGP,
                     by reference DocumentTitleGP,
                     by value     1 size 1,  <-------- play with this value
to execute diff dialogs, ie: font etc.
                     by value     0 size 4,
               returning          statusCode;
           move statusCode to lnk-PrintErrCode

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

```
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Are you certain that the DLL was NOT compiled with the option MAIN, making it 
a MAIN Program and not a SUB program?  If you Linker is saying the DLL cannot
be called from any program, I would assume that it has been compiled WITH MAIN.
 Try recompiling/Linking  the DLL without the MAIN option and then
try linking your CALLing program to the LIB/OBJ of the DLL.
```

```
Hm, there is a misunderstanding, maybe I described the prob not clear
enough:

There is no(!) call at that time, just a LOADLIBRARY.
And the LOADLIBRARY is done by a C (ms-c++6.0) program, the DLL is a
cobol (MF-COBOL) program.
The call to a fnc inside that DLL is done at a later time.
```

## Thread: migration workbench 2 netexpress — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### migration workbench 2 netexpress

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7M9zKIL0OtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

We have some trouble to migrate our project from
the microfocus cobol workbench to microfocus netexpress.

We have a complex c++, cobol, db2 environment.

We use cobol as backend. Our cobol services do all the
database access.

But now, with netexpress our compiler directives do not
work. All SQL directives were rejected.

Our next problem is the linker.
SQLINI2.OBJ is not found.

our products:
-------------
ibm db2 database udb version 5.2
netexpress version 1.0

But there is also something i don't understand.

If i use $SET SQL(DB2) in the source and i build the
object code with the netepxress ide it works fine.

If i set some more directives like the following list
it does not work.

Our goal is a massmake with batchfiles like we did it with
the cobol workbench.

some compiler directives:
-------------------------
CONFIRM
EARLY-RELEASE
MAKESYN "COMP-5" = "COMP"
LITLINK(1)
CASE
ASSIGN "EXTERNAL"
OUTDD "sysout.txt"
AREACHECK
IBMCOMP
DEFAULTBYTE "0"
ARITHMETIC "VSC2"
NOQUERY
SQL(DB=Y, NOPRE, NOINIT, NOACCESS, BIND, COMMIT=1, PASS=X.Z, FORMAT=ISO)

There is no difference if we only set the SQL directives or if we set them
all in single lines instead of one line.

The parameters db and pass are hidden in this example, sorry about.
```

#### ↳ Re: migration workbench 2 netexpress

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o8vv1$sn0$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7M9zKIL0OtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```
Hi Jurgen,

If I remember there was no doucmentation with V1 of Net Express of using DB2
UDB.

This support for DB2 was introduced in version 2 of Net Express with the
documentation downloadable from the Net Express WebSync site. In version 1
the only SQL solution was to use the OpenESQL directives to access DB2 via
ODBC.

My advice here would be to get V3 (the latest version).

The format of the DB2 directives have changed from Workbench to Net Express
they now use the form DB2(...) eg

      DB2(INIT=PROT BIND COLLECTION=MYSCHEMA)

Hope this helps
David.


Juergen Vetter <Juergen@bop99.ping.de> wrote in message
news:7M9zKIL0OtB@bop99.ping.de...
>
> We have some trouble to migrate our project from
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-d16bddc596"></a>
## Thread: MF cobol 3.2 relative file 9/065-File Locked. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF cobol 3.2 relative file 9/065-File Locked.

- **From:** "Michael ValeCruz" <mvalecruz1@mcyber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o7jiv$r8e$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The program seems to be getting a lot of these errors....I think it is
related to a timing problem with the server communication... NT Servers and
Novell Servers.
There are points in the program were it closes the file then turns around
and  re-opens the  file for Output but my guess is that the file has not
fully closed on the server but  the server is going ahead and processing the
open statement and returning a 065-File Locked after seeing the file not
closed....These are temporary single user files for external sorting so
there is no multi-user accessing of the file.

Any experience in this file-status is appreciated....
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol 3.2 relative file 9/065-File Locked.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tcLp3.554$n66.26183@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7o7jiv$r8e$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
You know, a couple of months ago Jill McLeester started a thread on the
PowerBASIC web BBS about network problems in peer-peer networks. There was a
sort of similar problem which resulted from a change by a certain major
software developer in located in Redmond WA.

It seems this developer changed the network file access from a synchronous
to an asynchronous operation, and any file functions which assumed that a
previous operation invoked from a program would already be completed (as
they would have been when the operation was synchronous) were now failing,
because the operating system was now performing them asynchronously and
just had not got around to it yet.

I am not a network/synchronicity guy by any stretch of the imagination, but
if you go to the PowerBASIC web bbs at
www.powerbasic.com/cgi-bin/ultimate.cgi and use the "search" for topic
"Windows 95/98 Networking Havoc " in the "programming" section you might
find something of interest. The first entry in the thread was posted 2-2-99.
(You may have to register to have access to the BBS. But they don't spam you
or anything).

Or, I have the entire thread in html format (size 59K) , and would be happy
to e-mail it or post it somewhere.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol 3.2 relative file 9/065-File Locked.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a7b3f6.174338680@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7o7jiv$r8e$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I used to see the 065 errors when an Windows 95 Pentium workstation
was connected to a novell server and the files resided onthe novell
server.

1. Get the Client 32 drivers from Novell, don't use the Microsoft
drivers.
2.  In the Performance tab of System in control panel, go to
Diagnostics and "Disable new record locking semantics".

That seemed to cure the problem.



On Tue, 3 Aug 1999 16:28:52 -0400, "Michael ValeCruz"
<mvalecruz1@mcyber.com> wrote:

>The program seems to be getting a lot of these errors....I think it is
>related to a timing problem with the server communication... NT Servers and
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Change to COBOL II Compiler — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** "Dirk Kalle" <rewe.dortmund.kalle@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o6d75$es6$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```
We have problems with COMP-3 Fields.

The field-definition is:

02 field-x   pic s9(5)V99 COMP-3 VALUE ZERO.

At run-time the field have following structure:

"40404040404040".

Have anybody an idea, what we make wrong?

Thanks

Dirk Kalle
REWE Dortmund
Dortmund (Germany)
```

#### ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990803082424.00349.00001988@ng-fo1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7o6d75$es6$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Dirk Kalle writes...

>We have problems with COMP-3 Fields.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Have anybody an idea, what we make wrong?

First the field you show us is a four byte field; the data you show us is seven
bytes; are you looking in the correct place?

Second, before you display your data item do you move something (a record, a
literal, a figurative constant like SPACES, another structure) into the 01
level that contains your field-x? Group moves left justify data and pad on the
right with spaces (what you are showing us is spaces).

Also, check your compiler setting for the ZWB (zero when blank) option,
although I'd be surprised if that had any effect like you describe.

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A6EA0A.C607A324@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7o6d75$es6$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Check to see what you're doing to the 01 level for the record holding
these tables.  I suspect your problem is there.  Also check to see if
this redefined anywhere.  The third possibility I can see is a overflow
on a table ahead of this.

But it sure looks like an initialization problem to me.

Dirk Kalle wrote:
> 
> We have problems with COMP-3 Fields.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dortmund (Germany)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990803152441.03573.00003336@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37A6EA0A.C607A324@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <37A6EA0A.C607A324@NOSPAMhome.com>, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> writes:

>Check to see what you're doing to the 01 level for the record holding
>these tables.  I suspect your problem is there.  Also check to see if
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would tend to think that the problem is related to the use of INITIALIZE and
the 
level at which the field is defined.  I have found that using INITIALIZE at the
01 level does nicely for all Elementary items to the 01, but any Group level
items
will have its subordinate fields initialized as SPACES due to Group level
default
is ALPHANUMERIC.
If your  01 (or Group) entry has subordinate Group level elements, these Group 
level items will need an additional INITIALIZE statement for their sub-ordinate
fields to be properly initialized.
I've been burned by this on several ocassions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o7ior$ks8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37A6EA0A.C607A324@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990803152441.03573.00003336@ngol01.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
   <snip>
>
> I would tend to think that the problem is related to the use of INITIALIZE
and
> the
> level at which the field is defined.  I have found that using INITIALIZE
at the
> 01 level does nicely for all Elementary items to the 01, but any Group
level
> items
> will have its subordinate fields initialized as SPACES due to Group level
> default
> is ALPHANUMERIC.
> If your  01 (or Group) entry has subordinate Group level elements, these
Group
> level items will need an additional INITIALIZE statement for their
sub-ordinate
> fields to be properly initialized.
> I've been burned by this on several ocassions.
>

This is simply wrong. (Either your testing was wrong - or you compiler has a
bug).  No matter how many levels of group-items exist under an outermost
group (assuming we aren't talking about REDEFINES) - an ANSI/ISO conforming
compiler MUST initialize at the very "bottom" elementary level.  (Obviously,
besides redefines - FILLER items also impact how INITIALIZE works - as do
some other factors like USAGE INDEX - but in general your report just should
NOT happen that way.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o836h$6t6$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<37A6EA0A.C607A324@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990803152441.03573.00003336@ngol01.aol.com> <7o7ior$ks8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
What happens when you initialize a record that has
a redefined field in it?

William M. Klein wrote in message <7o7ior$ks8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>...
>Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
>   <snip>
>>
>> I would tend to think that the problem is related to the use of
INITIALIZE
>and
>> the
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is simply wrong. (Either your testing was wrong - or you compiler has
a
>bug).  No matter how many levels of group-items exist under an outermost
>group (assuming we aren't talking about REDEFINES) - an ANSI/ISO conforming
>compiler MUST initialize at the very "bottom" elementary level.
(Obviously,
>besides redefines - FILLER items also impact how INITIALIZE works - as do
>some other factors like USAGE INDEX - but in general your report just
should
>NOT happen that way.)
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ INITIALIZE (was Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o8cfs$4o1@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37A6EA0A.C607A324@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990803152441.03573.00003336@ngol01.aol.com> <7o7ior$ks8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7o836h$6t6$2@news.igs.net>`

```
I have the draft of the next Standard online, so I'll quote from it (but I
think the current Standard works the same way),

"First, the following data items are excluded as receiving-operands:

  1. Any identifiers that are not valid receiving operands of a MOVE
statement.
  2. If the FILLER phrase is not specified, elementary data items with an
explicit or implicit FILLER clause.
  3. Any elementary data item subordinate to identifier-1 whose data
description entry contains a REDEFINES or RENAMES clause or is subordinate
to a data item whose data description entry contains a REDEFINES clause.
However, identifier-1 may itself have a REDEFINES clause or be subordinate
to a data item with a REDEFINES clause."

In other words the REDEFINED item (or group) gets initialized, but not the
redefining items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> What happens when you initialize a record that has
> a redefined field in it?

Not sure what answer you're going to get from Bill but if you look at
that Client Record (oofiles.zip), to play it real safe I say :-

	initialize client-record
	if client-record type 1 initialize client-data
	if client-record type 2 initialize facility-data ( which is 			
client-data redefined)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Bill, I usually take out INITIALIZE insurance:

INITIALIZE  01-LEVEL-DATANAME
  REPLACING  ALPHANUMERIC DATA BY SPACE
                      NUMERIC DATA BY ZEROES.


This seems to do the trick regardless of compiler, hardware, defaults or
installation options.
```

#### ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<933689835.16889.0.nnrp-09.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7o6d75$es6$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Check your 01 level.  I've seen cases on other compilers where if it implies
character, it forces every lower (higher?) level to character.

e.g. 01  X VALUE SPACES.

Regards

Rick


Dirk Kalle <rewe.dortmund.kalle@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:7o6d75$es6$1@news08.btx.dtag.de...
> We have problems with COMP-3 Fields.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Change to COBOL II Compiler

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A76D5F.2D7A4A8@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7o6d75$es6$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```
You might have an existing program bug (feature) exposed by a
change in compiler options.

COMP-3 is packed decimal data.  Assuming your 14 characters are
actually 7 hex bytes (i.e. 7 blanks), the old compiler generated
code would pack them into x'00000004'.  Note that the x'4' at the
right hand side is not a valid packed decimal sign nibble.  The
generated code would then fix the sign digit giving x'0000000C'
which then can be added, subtracted etc.

Check the NUMPROC option to see if the old code got this "fix any
bad packed decimal sign" support, while the NUMPROC on the new
compiler left bad signs alone.  Symptom would be an S07C (data
exception).

Dirk Kalle wrote:
> 
> We have problems with COMP-3 Fields.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dortmund (Germany)
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

```
I know that J4 has answered the question with a NO!

This is a footnote on page 1 of the draft C standard:

"1. This International Standard is designed to promote
      the portability of C programs among a variety of
      data-processing systems. It is intended for use by
      implementors and programmers."

So the C standard is intended for programmers; but,
according to J4, the COBOL standard is not. Why?
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-348a060103"></a>
## Thread: MF cobol relative file i/o on Nt Server is slow. — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF cobol relative file i/o on Nt Server is slow.

- **From:** "Michael ValeCruz" <mvalecruz1@mcyber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o6nfv$95j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have a database made up of releative files and index files using MF cobol
3.2. For years I processed the files on Netware sever.... then comes along
Nt server...
now workstations process the files are slow...Nt Server is obeying the rules
of record locking but the speed it processing the read/write request are
slower then  of Netware. I have found that forcing the workstation to IPX
protocol (defualt) has helped some but still not the speed of Netware....

Comment?
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol relative file i/o on Nt Server is slow.

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o6vro$8i0$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7o6nfv$95j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Michael,

I have had discussions with Microsoft on this very subject and the basic
problem is that as soon as you apply a lock on a network file the operating
system disables file caching which makes the file accesses a large amount
slower. Unfortunately there is nothing you can do, short of removing the
locks, to eliminate the problem.

Michael ValeCruz wrote in message <7o6nfv$95j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>...
>I have a database made up of releative files and index files using MF cobol
>3.2. For years I processed the files on Netware sever.... then comes along
>Nt server...
>now workstations process the files are slow...Nt Server is obeying the
rules
>of record locking but the speed it processing the read/write request are
>slower then  of Netware. I have found that forcing the workstation to IPX
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol relative file i/o on Nt Server is slow.

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a71a06.1525308@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<7o6nfv$95j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"Michael ValeCruz" <mvalecruz1@mcyber.com> wrote:

>I have a database made up of releative files and index files using MF cobol
>3.2. For years I processed the files on Netware sever.... then comes along
>Nt server...

MF changed the way, relative and line sequential files are handled in
3.2. That increased the access time extremely on DOS with smartdrive <
4.01, some NetWare requesters and some NT installations.

Try compiling your program with compiler directive CALLFH"EXTFH". If
that does not work try using coblib.dle from rev. 3.4.

Best regards

Sven
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol relative file i/o on Nt Server is slow.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a7b49f.174508315@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7o6nfv$95j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

>I have a database made up of releative files and index files using MF cobol
>3.2. For years I processed the files on Netware sever.... then comes along
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think your message says it all, but I have hear rumors that if you
add RAM to your NT server, then performance improves.  Once it can
cache the entire hard drive, performance is almost equal to Novell.
```

```
for stepping thru the program code and showing the content of variables as the
program processes.
The main things to remember are to set the TEST option at Compile time and 
select the Options|Debug buttons before doing a LINK operation.
Without this , you probably won't get the SVD file needed for the Debugger to
work.
```

<a id="t-a5f1e1ce4d"></a>
## Thread: Umstellung COBOL 74 -> COBOL 85 hier COMP-3 Felder — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: Umstellung COBOL 74 -> COBOL 85 hier COMP-3 Felder

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a6e05c@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7o6bro$eld$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```

Joachim Nacke <rewe.dortmund.nacke@t-online.de> wrote in message > Diese
sind wie folgt definiert:
>     02 FELD-X    PIC S9(5) COMP-3 VALUE ZERO.
> Zur Laufzeit hat dieses Feld den Inhalt "4040404040" und bekommt einen
DATA
> EXCEPTION.
> Kann mir jemand sagen, wie dieser Wert in ein gepacktes Feld kommt und wie
> man dies verhindern kann?

FELD-X is part of a larger structure (because of its level 02). If you move
spaces
to that structure you have the result you describe, e.g.:
01  BIG-FIELD.
    02  FIELD-1      PIC X(25).
    02  FELD-X      PIC S9(5) COMP-3 VALUE ZERO.
    02  FIELD-3      PIC X(10).

MOVE SPACES TO BIG-FIELD
```
