#### ↳ Re: Windows Printing in COBOL

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ogn4n$d36$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7occ8m$fr2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<allenmc@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7occ8m$fr2$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I'm in the middle of a slow migration of my COBOL
> text based application to Windows.

> The problem I have now is that when I go into
> compressed mode (Pitch 6), my 6 lines per inch
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> great help.

    Most printers (Some of the bubble jet printers from Canon and others are
exeptions) use more or less standard HP escape codes.  PCL-4 is basic and
widely accepted.  Although some of the PCL codes are printer specific, stuff
like lines per inch are reasonably standard.

    Also note that many printers have a printer resident font called "line
printer" that works well at 17 pitch. (132 characters on a std 8.5 inch
peice of paper).  If you use line drawing characters you will need to adjust
the character set as well (PC-8 or some such).

    Problem:  HP does not give these books away.  But you might get summarys
from the HP web site.
```

```
highlighted with _highlighted_.

Bill Lynch
```

```
Windows3.11 but I had remembered it and had been wanting to try it.

I set the line spacing to 6lpi, the font does not *stretch* to fill the
area.  The print itself is still hard to read.

know.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows Printing in COBOL

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B2D5B3.E46045B6@gfc-net.de>`
- **References:** `<7occ8m$fr2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ogn4n$d36$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7omll9$dis$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

If you use the windows printer driver, you possibly have to use Cobol's windows
printer functions for printing which possibly does not allow escape sequences
because it uses the windows fonts. 

Not the state of the art but the following method is simple, fast and
easy-to-use:

1. print into a sequential file
2. then copy the file to the good old lpt1

BY this you use the printer-built-in fonts that can be changed (aside with page
orientation etc) by escape sequences.

You may also do the two steps in one program, but performing two steps allows
you to write a copy program that translates your own control words into PCL5 or
other control codes. With PC_print, you are delimited to use one font for the
whole document.

If you use a network printer, simply assign it to a lpt connection with the net
use command.

Hoe this helps

Juergen Ibelgaufts

-----------------------------------------------------------------

> Thanks Russell,
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows Printing in COBOL

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p0i56$ilr$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7occ8m$fr2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ogn4n$d36$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7omll9$dis$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> I've been looking for the line-printer font.  I've not seen it since
> Windows3.11 but I had remembered it and had been wanting to try it.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> know.

    Note that lines per inch refers to verticle, ie top to bottom. Lines per
inch has NOTHING to do with pitch or font size.  Pitch and font size are
both different ways of saying the same thing, ie how WIDE (not tall) is my
stuff.


;*******************************
; Portrait, 6 LINES PER INCH
132-COL-XDATA=1B
132-COL-DATA=&l0o6D
; PC-8 SYMBOL SET
132-COL-XDATA=1B
132-COL-DATA=(10U
; Fixed, 16.67 pitch, height=8.5 points, upright,
; Medium (not light or bold), Line printer
132-COL-XDATA=1B
132-COL-DATA=(s0p16.67h8.5v0s0b0T

    Above - XDATA means hexadecimal (normally escape)

    Note that Courier also works well for print outs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows Printing in COBOL

- **From:** allenmc@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pjlvm$oc6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7occ8m$fr2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It's not perfect but I finally came up with a solution.  It took me
quite a bit of time but, I developed a compressed print font that I
treat as a 12pt font.  This font keeps my line spacing and gives me
16cpi character spacing.  It actually look better than the native font
in the printers.

The real drawback is the each user has to install the font but you have
to do this if you buy any windows word processor so I guess it's not
too bad.

I had already tried many of the suggestions that everyone has shared.
My real problem is that I've been told that I could not limit our
customers on the hardware they purchase.  Many of our customers with
all their money have opted to buy these cheap !@$#^%& windows printers
which do not appear to have a native font.  They rely on the True Type
fonts in windows.

Anyhow thanks everyone for your suggestions.  If you have any further
questions as to what I did, drop me a line.

Keep the bits flippen!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-ec650ffb93"></a>
## Thread: Internationalisation — 4 msgs

### Internationalisation

- **From:** mikefry@iafrica.nospam.com (Mike Fry)
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EdHxIHxd9CDV-pn2-Qvr48sDqg7lQ@minitower>`

```
I wonder if there's anyone out there who can assist me in my efforts to 
do something that I've never done before.

I've got a COBOL program (MicroFocus, OS/2 Warp 4) that runs perfectly 
well in most english locales. I now want to change it so that it will 
work in Moscow.

My main problem is with the input of descriptive fields: some must be in
Cyrillic and some must remain in English. How can I achieve this using 
the facilities available within my COBOL runtime system?

Also, (slightly out of context) what changes would I need to make to the
CONFIG.SYS file to accommodate my Cyrillic-cum-English program? I 
presume that I'd need to alter the COUNTRY, CODEPAGE and DEVINFO(KBD) 
statements. Is there anything else to be considered?

Any help would be much appreciated.

Regards,
MIKE FRY
mailto:mikefry@iafrica.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Internationalisation

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AAE380.9D113C53@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<EdHxIHxd9CDV-pn2-Qvr48sDqg7lQ@minitower>`

```
Mike Fry wrote:
> 
> I wonder if there's anyone out there who can assist me in my efforts to
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> mailto:mikefry@iafrica.com

The GNU trick is reasonably easy.   Have a look at how GNU do it for
there C programs (www.gnu.org).  Basically they pass all their text
through a subroutine first that (might or might not) remap it to a
different language.

This does not help when the language structure changes the order of
the values in the output (e.g. language 1 will have count 10, at
$10.00 language 2 will say $10 for 10.)

Don't do cute tricks for plurals.  If you do this.
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Internationalisation

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aef8ea@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<EdHxIHxd9CDV-pn2-Qvr48sDqg7lQ@minitower>`

```

Mike Fry wrote in message ...
>I wonder if there's anyone out there who can assist me in my efforts to
>do something that I've never done before.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Mike,

As you are only using the labels on screens in Cyrillic, I wouldn't even try
to change the environment to support it. Try using the double byte character
set of COBOL (DBCS) to provide Cyrillic text for field labels on screens.

If you need to input text fields in Cyrillic you have 2 options:

1. Configure the system as you suggested above to support the Russian
Alphabet.

2. Take the input as a DBCS string and write your own routines to decode it.

I would strongly advise that you avoid option 1 like the plague. Only as a
last resort...


Pete.
```

```
>
>Mike Fry wrote in message ...
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Having faced the same thing in Thailand it is not that difficult to
support English characters as well as the local characters in this
case.  I don't think you need DBCS for Cryllic.  I've also done work
in Cyprus where Greek is spoken and we did not have to use DBCS for
Greek/English character input/output.  DBCS is needed for Chinese,
Japenese and possibly Korean languages.

What we found in Thailand and Cyprus was the IBM code page was
modified to handle the alpahbets.  I don't remember the specifics of
Greek, but the Thai alphabet,  which consists of 44 characters, were
assigned to normally unused EBCIDIC positions.  For example B0, B1, B2
etc.  

Now all of this was done on a mainframe, but I'd have to believe that
the same facility would exist for the PC as well.  If you're having to
do the programming here in America with hardware built to only be used
in America, then you are really up against it.  

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Some days your the dog.  Some days your the hydrant.


 Steve
```

---

<a id="t-bdaf6c1f36"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and Windows NT — 7 msgs

### COBOL and Windows NT

- **From:** "Ralf Ciemilewski" <schimmi@soluter.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>`

```
I want to use an old Application with Windows NT.

When I start then Application in a DOS-BOX then following Error occurs:

'Cobol run time library not installed'

My question:
Is it possible to run COBOL-Applictions with Windows NT?
Where can I get the 'run time library'?
Is there anyting else I need to do?

Thanks
Ralf
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Windows NT

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oc1p9$ku5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>`

```
Ralf,

The problem you have hit, assuming you are referring to an application
created using a Micro Focus product, is likely to be as a result of the fact
that many applications are dual 16-bit DOS and OS/2 applications. Therefore
you need to add the directory containing COBLIB.DLL to the Os2LibPath
environment variable. Once you have done that it should work okay.

Ralf Ciemilewski wrote in message <01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>...
>I want to use an old Application with Windows NT.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ralf
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Windows NT

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a9c8cb.177365157@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002> <7oc1p9$ku5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
"Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com> wrote:

>The problem you have hit, assuming you are referring to an application
>created using a Micro Focus product, is likely to be as a result of the fact
>that many applications are dual 16-bit DOS and OS/2 applications. Therefore
>you need to add the directory containing COBLIB.DLL to the Os2LibPath
>environment variable. Once you have done that it should work okay.

As it is an old DOS Application, he might not have the OS/2 libraries.
But I bet he has XM which is a 16-Bit DOS-EXE. With the startup line
"XM.EXE APPLICATION.EXE" it should work fine (at least until Jan.
1st...).

Best regards
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Windows NT

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oc19n$ghe$3@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>`

```
It sounds like a MF or MS Cobol compiler for DOS.  If that is so, then you
need the file COBLIB.DLE.  That should get it to run in a DOS window for
you.

Ralf Ciemilewski wrote in message <01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>...
>I want to use an old Application with Windows NT.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ralf
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Windows NT

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990805093008.23716.00003284@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>`

```
In article <01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>, "Ralf Ciemilewski"
<schimmi@soluter.de> writes:

>My question:
>Is it possible to run COBOL-Applictions with Windows NT?
>Where can I get the 'run time library'?
>Is there anyting else I need to do?
>

sounds like the application had some support files that you did not copy over
to the NT system.   There is nothing intrinsic to any environment that
precludes it 
from running an application created in any language.
It depends on the platform the application was targeted for and whether the
application was self contained (only needs the EXE) or whether there are 
support files required (.OVL, RTL, .DLL).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Windows NT

- **From:** "IceBerg" <IceBerg@titanic.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oc5b7$c29$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>`

```
What you need is the 32-bit version of MF-Cobol. We switched to it for the
NT platform and it has been much faster and more stable. However, you need
to compile your code on an NT workstation (true 32-bit OS) in order to reap
all of the benefits! Good luck!



Ralf Ciemilewski <schimmi@soluter.de> wrote in message
news:01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002...
> I want to use an old Application with Windows NT.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ralf
```

- **References:** `<01bedf28$2adf5d80$0181500a@ws066002>`

```

wrote:

>I want to use an old Application with Windows NT.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Try preceeding your command with FORCEDOS

Such as:  FORCEDOS MYPROGRAM.EXE

Also, DOS support is supposed to be gone from Windows 2000 so get
ready for that too.
```

## Thread: Anyone w/ CICS 4.1 Upgrade Problems? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### RE: Anyone w/ CICS 4.1 Upgrade Problems?

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B014E2BF6@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
Been there, done that, changed the CICS runtime options....

Our users were using the fact that "maponly" ACTED as the default before 4.1
and now "from mapname" IS as the default when neither is specified...Another
case of the user using UNDOCUMENTED features.....

-----Original Message-----
From: X [mailto:x@yz.com]

snip

Anyone have problems with their COBOL programs when upgrading to CICS 4.1?  
Specifically with 4.1, is storage really not initialized to low values?  We 
have problems with SEND MAPs when the symbolic map is loaded with junk and 
there is no explicit code to move low values to the symbolic map before the 
SEND.  Is this just a set up issue of the CICS environment or do we have to 
modify our programs?  I'm not a CICS administrator, but I can hardly believe

that IBM would make such a change of questionable benefit, but  with serious

negative consequences.

snip


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

## Thread: [Fwd: Cobol compiler sources] — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: [Fwd: Cobol compiler sources]

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aa151c.4846589@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au> <memo.19990705194351.224A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk>`

```
On Mon, 5 Jul 1999 19:43 +0100 (BST), John Watkins wrote:

>In article <37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au>, tej@melbpc.org.au (Tim 
>Josling) wrote:

>> The main problem I have is in devising names that are guaranteed
>> not to be reserved words. When you trespass onto the reserved
>> words, you get a strange error message. I tried ws-* but they
>> broke that one too. It's just an annoyance that adds to the

>Which ws- prefixed words are reserved?


As far as I know, there are no reserved words that contain two
consecutive hyphens.  Therefore, your scheme should work if you put
two hyphens behind your data name prefix, such as WS--ITEM-COUNT, and
the like.  (Obviously, more meaningful and structured names
could/should be used, but this is just to illustrate an example.)

I also am curious which compiler has WS- reserved words and what those
reserved words are.  I have not run into that.
```

- **Date:** 1999-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7od647$rjh@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au> <memo.19990705194351.224A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk> <37aa151c.4846589@news1.frb.gov>`

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Cobol compiler sources]

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BmBq3.25$tJ2.936@client>`
- **References:** `<37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au> <memo.19990705194351.224A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk> <37aa151c.4846589@news1.frb.gov> <7od647$rjh@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7od647$rjh@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>...
>And the BAD news is - check out the Micro Focus reserved words:
>
>  FH--FCD
>  FH-- KEYDEF


Then Micro Focus is violating the 1985 ANSI (i.e. current) standard,
section 4.2.2.1.3, viz:

"Reserved words satisfy the following conditions:
(1)  ....[does not apply]
(2)  ....[does not apply]
(3)  Reserved words do not start with 1 or 2 characters followed by '-'
except for the reserved words I-O, I-O-CONTROL, and reserved words which
begin with 'B-' or 'DB-'.
(4)  Reserved words do not contain two or more contiguous hyphens."

Are these rules being 'discontinued' in the new standard?  The 4.2.2.1.3
rule exists so that COBOL programmers may design sets of user-words that are
guaranteed not to be reserved words.

Tom Morrison
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Cobol compiler sources]

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oeq7l$set@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au> <memo.19990705194351.224A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk> <37aa151c.4846589@news1.frb.gov> <7od647$rjh@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <BmBq3.25$tJ2.936@client>`

```
No, Micro Focus is NOT violating the letter or spirit of the ANSI Standard.
The "whole point" of that paragraph is to show which reserved words ANSI
will not use - so that vendors and users CAN use them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Cobol compiler sources]

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jQEq3.100$tJ2.2783@client>`
- **References:** `<37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au> <memo.19990705194351.224A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk> <37aa151c.4846589@news1.frb.gov> <7od647$rjh@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <BmBq3.25$tJ2.936@client> <7oeq7l$set@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Apology alert!  Apology appears later in this posting!

William M. Klein wrote in message <7oeq7l$set@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>...
>No, Micro Focus is NOT violating the letter or spirit of the ANSI Standard.
>The "whole point" of that paragraph is to show which reserved words ANSI
>will not use - so that vendors and users CAN use them.


Yes, this is true when the compiler implementor is implementing a
system-name, a COBOL word used to communicate with the operating
environment.  The standard is silent about the implementor inventing new
reserved words (which all COBOL vendors have and will continue to do).  My
personal opinion is that a compiler implementor, when inventing a reserved
word (which by definition is not a system-name), should observe the cited
rules in the ANSI standard.

My post wasn't meant as an attack on Micro Focus' implementation, but rather
was a too quickly typed post to inform the community that there is a name
space in which user-words can be formulated with an eye toward portability.
Micro Focus provides a means of 'dereserving' reserved words that get in the
way, as do other vendors.

Tom Morrison
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Cobol compiler sources]

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7og597$25m@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37735182.E684A339@melbpc.org.au> <memo.19990705194351.224A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk> <37aa151c.4846589@news1.frb.gov> <7od647$rjh@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <BmBq3.25$tJ2.936@client> <7oeq7l$set@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <jQEq3.100$tJ2.2783@client>`

```
Tom Morrison <t.morrison@liant.com> wrote in message news:jQEq3.100
>  <snip>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> My post wasn't meant as an attack on Micro Focus' implementation, but
rather
> was a too quickly typed post to inform the community that there is a name
> space in which user-words can be formulated with an eye toward
portability.
> Micro Focus provides a means of 'dereserving' reserved words that get in
the
> way, as do other vendors.
>
> Tom Morrison
>
>

My impression is (and always has been) that the words,

"That implementation meets the requirements of standard COBOL even though
additional reserved words may be introduced by the implementor, thereby
preventing translation of some compilation groups that meet the requirements
of standard COBOL."

basically gives each implementor the opportunity to USE anything as a
reserved word - and that there is no "safe" name space for user-defined
words.  Clearly this "problem" is one of the oft-stated uses of the REPLACE
statement - to avoid reserved-word/user-defined word "clashes".
```

## Thread: Who cares ? — 80 msgs

### Who cares ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>`

```
My correspondent would prefer not to get involved in NewsGroups - as he
says "I already spend 1.5 hours a day answering correspondence".

Any thoughts, comments ?

Firstly my reply to his message :
..........................................

Well there's a 'downer' on what I perceived to be an original idea. But
in your role as futurist I find it diffcult to flaw your argument, and
as somebody more systems-analyst than programmer, I find your ideas
exciting.

Mind you, we, (the COBOL community - not yours truly, who will have
checked-out), have about 15 more years of agony using COBOL according to
your timescale.

Jimmy
..............................................
Now his message to me : Written from the heart
..............................................

Message text written by "James J. Gavan"
>>>
...never spoken to a COBOLer working on the mainframe dinosaurs
downtown.
(I believe they think they are a special breed).
<<<
And so they are...just waiting for the KT comet to impact the Earth like
it did 65,000,000 years ago...

>>>
As regards NewsGroups, I take your point, but I know some there would
love to see what you are saying about Fujitsu........
<<<
Please feel free to quote any of my correspondence to you in a Newsgroup
(unless I specifically say not to). I've never heard of Flexus SP2.
Fujitsu will do me now until I quit (at least as far as PCs go).

>>>

Different folks, different strokes. If the
following doesn't turn you on - just say so.

Open COBOL - my loosey-goosey definition :-

A COBOL compiler, written using object-orientation,  which is "Open",
that is, programmers and add-on tool developers,  have complete access
to the compiler's source to see, in COBOL syntax, how it handles COBOL
verbs/syntax, generates executable code and how it interacts with 
specific operating systems. 
<<<
Hmmm...who cares? Most of today's COBOL programmers don't even
understand the beginnings of what is generated. The days when we used to
patch object code are long gone. The skills of yesteryear (whether we
like it or not) are now irrelevant.

As for how it interacts with specific operating systems, again, who
cares? As long as it does. I have written COBOL on every major mainframe
(IBM and the BUNCH), at least 5 intermediate range machines, and PCs
using everything from DOS and OS/2 to NT. I have never worried about the
operating system. My code runs. I don't use vendor-specific constructs;
the same code runs across the range. Re-compile and away you go.  As far
as COBOL compilers go...I have worked on 2: 1 for Burroughs (remember
them? they were the half of Unisys that WASN'T Sperry...), the other for
a very obscure CDC machine. In the first case I was actually patching
machine code and disassembling object code to determine how the compiler
(which had been written for Burroughs on contract and for which they had
no source and no-one capable of maintaining it) worked. I then modified
it and improved it. Eventually, I was getting letters from Detroit
Michigan asking me (in Auckland NZ) what pass 7 of their compiler did,
and could I please fix it to take magnetic input, etc. All very
interesting and exciting at the time, but in the long run a total waste
of time. Today you will find the B500 in museums or, very occasionally,
as a comms server somewhere. I wrote stuff like syntax scanners which
parsed using transition diagramming and JIT keyword extraction, and all
sorts of interesting stuff...for what? It had a useful life of around 5
years and it took a year to do.

I believe the whole question is academic as "computer programming" as we
know it has a limited remaining life span. History will show that
"computer programming" was a phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th
Century. By 2020 there was no longer a requirement for it. (Just look at
all the rubbish surrounding standard laguages.  1st, 2nd, and 3rd
generation languages (4th generation is H.S., and 5th generation
exsts in the alcohol befuddled minds of some marketing men who never
programmed a computer in their lives and wouldn't know a compiler if it
bit them in the arse) like COBOL, C, BASIC, and Assembler, are already
in their twilight years. OO, CORBA, COM compliance (ActiveX, OCX) will
not push back the frontiers (although they are all steps in the right
direction and bring closer the scenario I shall describe shortly.))

It is too costly, labour intensive, and time-consuming.  OO is included
in this category. Once all the necessary objects and classes are
established the rest is just stamp collecting. The next step is to
develop intelligent classes which can spawn their own methods using
firmware building blocks.

No, it's NOT Science Fiction and I AM serious! The advent of artificial
intelligence, voice activated interfaces in standard English, and more
and more "user-friendliness" will enable non-technical people to
interact easily and effectively with a computer until it does exactly
what they want.

Imagine the following scenario:

Small businessman with no technical knowledge, goes to corner shop and
buys "small business" computer. Brings it home unpacks and connects it
using extensive colour handbook and video. Switches on and says: "Print
me an invoice."

computer: "What's an invoice"

user: "An invoice is a document which should be created whenever we SELL
PRODUCTS to a CUSTOMER. It shows what we sold and how many and how much
they cost. It also shows the taxes and other sundries which the CUSTOMER
has to PAY."

computer: "OK, I know what PRODUCTS and CUSTOMERS are and I recognise
the SALE and PAYMENT as standard business transactions, so I know when
to trigger this INVOICE. Shall I print a sample INVOICE using my sample
CUSTOMER and PRODUCT databases?"

user: "Yes, please"

The machine produces a very poor attempt at an invoice. The user
interacts with it and explains that the figures need to be in columns
and what should go on the document (like, description of the product,
quantity, unit cost, and the necessary calculations.) After about 20
minutes the machine is getting very close and within half an hour the
user has his invoice. 

There are 2 fundamental things which make the above scenario possible:
1. The CONCEPTS of CUSTOMER, PRODUCT, TRANSACTION etc. can be built into
firmware as standard intelligent agents. By today's standards this will
require some interesting programming using techniques derived from OO,
neural networking and heuristics, but there is no doubt that it is
achievable. (I used the INVOICE as an example. In fact, the "small
business" machine would almost certainly know how to produce an invoice.
It would start from a standard template and allow the user to modify it
by interaction.) Machines for all kinds of applications would be
produced simply by installing the appropriate firmware. LAN/WAN
connection and connectivity would be fundamental, built in, and almost
completly transparent to the user.

2. The use of ITERATION. Trial and error. The learning curve. Don't even
try and get it right first time (that is one of the major failures in
the way we currently develop systems...waste a ton of time getting it
"right" then find it all has to be changed anyway....(you may have
guessed I am a devotee of RAD for project management...<grin>)) Charles
Darwin showed that trial and error has been the way we have developed
over the last 3,000,000 years. It works. Compare what we are today with
what we were 3, 000,000 years ago and you start to see what I mean. Then
translate it into computer terms. 3,000,000 years against the 15 billion
year life of the Universe is the blink of an eye. For a computer, the
same blink of an eye can be 10 minutes. Hence I make no apology for
claiming the system can produce an invoice within half an hour. Not only
that, but the invoice will be EXACTLY what the user wants because he has
specified it interactively himself.

So, goodbye "computer programming" with its attendant mystique, jargon,
tedium,  cost, and intensive mental effort. I believe we will see
systems like the one above within the next 15 years. The technicians of
the future may study COBOL the same way we study Latin and Classical
Greek; purely as academic exercises which help to grasp the concepts of
what is going on. The man in the street will have no interest
in, or need to learn, computer programming as we know it.

Nowadays, I see COBOL as purely recreational to allow me to develop
useful and fun applications for today's primitive operating systems.
Why? I've done it all my working life (since 1965) and I enjoy it. It
also means I can generate a few quid on the side, but I am under no
illusions as to the future of it. My career (what's left of it) does not
lie in computer programming. It lies in managing the technology,
assisting users to come to grips with it, and showing Management how it
may be of benefit to the business. It lies in applying imagination to
technical projects and designing things carefully and iteratively, in
close co-operation with end-users. 

I therefore cannot share your enthusiasm for Open COBOL. It isn't worth
the effort and who would appreciate it anyway?

>>>
It just doesn't look good for COBOL. The mainframe compiler vendors are
not terribly interested and at the PC-end they can't make any money at
it - selling to single developers. We are competing against all these
neat cryptic languages, Java being the current darling, although
Simula/Smalltalk was doing the same stuff back in 1970 at Xerox PARC.
<<<
Yes, I agree. It's not just COBOL. The problem is endemic to all current
languages. I spent a couple of weeks learning JAVA (mainly so the
techies I have to manage can't B.S. me...) and was totally
underwhelmed. A breeding ground for hostile classes (viruses),
inefficient because of its virtual machine (no wonder MS are trying to
persuade everyone to their MS-specific version), and esoteric. 

>>>
Take my definition about Open COBOL and kick it around and modify it -
but I think you will see it represents at least a model of what we
should be doing. The way to achieve it - OO. Open source as with
MicroFocus for classes. As Adele Goldberg said of Smalltalk - in
hindsight they should have designed Smalltalk without being
platform-specific. Same applies to COBOL - write an indexed file on a
mainframe - why can't I read it on my PC etc......
<<<
I dunno...why can't you? I can. In fact I can transfer and manipulate
files on the mainframe or the PC running from either platform. You need
some layered software to do the ASCII to EBCDIC translation (if you are
transferring the files) but I have no trouble accessing data on a
mainframe indexed COBOL file from my PC and vice versa. I also don't
understand the fuss about transportability. COBOL was originally
designed to be, and always has been, transportable. I mentioned above
that I have always written transportable COBOL. (I've encouraged others
to do so too, with varying degrees of success...)

>>>
Let me know.
<<<
Well, I've certainly tried to do that...

I know you probably will disagree with what I've written but It is what
I honestly believe. I see little future for the existing methods of
interacting with computers, at least as far as current languages go. The
real answer has to be a direct interface with the end-user, in English
(or whatever....but not Technospeak). I believe it is not that far away.
There is no reason to believe that technology will stop its exponential
growth in the new Millennium. As hardware races ahead in leaps and
bounds, it would be tragic and pathetic if we continue to use the same
old dull, unimaginative, techniques for software development.

These days I no longer encourage young people to think about computer
programming as a career.

All the Best, Jimmy.  I look forward to your thoughts.

......... End of quote .............
```

```
does their train building?

Your small business?

Your schools?

Your government? And likewise around the world.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4zkr3.1711$9X.63736@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <kfNq3.607$d05.42948@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37ABC802.6707D5@home.com>`

```
The Macola system for DOS, a fairly successful accounting/mfg/distribution
package, was written in COBOL. Don't know about their Windows product.

Trading Partner/MVS, an EDI translator/mapper, is written mostly in COBOL.

STX/DOS (later Harbinger TrustedLink, now no longer marketed) was written in
COBOL. (another EDI translator/mapper).

Gentran/MVS and Gentran/400, yet more EDI translator/mappers, are written
mostly in COBOL.
(Less COBOL in Gentran/Server for AS/400, but still there).

These commercial packages are not necessarily used by "the EDP community"
but are used in industry every day.

But bottom line, to cite the thread title, Who Cares? what source language
is used as long as it gets the job done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rgfs3.78$sM4.4304@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <kfNq3.607$d05.42948@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37ABC802.6707D5@home.com> <4zkr3.1711$9X.63736@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AE5EAB.DEC19FBF@home.com> <sgzr3.24$I4.950@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37aedcee@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood wrote in message <37aedcee@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
>
>Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Unfortunately, it was not intended to be humorous. This little "platitude"
is something I have religiously spewed forth on BBs, Compuserve forums and
Internet newsgroups for ten years.

While academic discussions - OO-COBOL, the "correct" indentation of COBOL
code, how all periods in COBOL source code should be on their own line in
column twelve, which is the "best" compiler, etc.  - are valuable,
necessary, interesting and certainly one of the great utilizations of SIGs
and newsgroups, I often get the impression that programmer-wannabes think a
new tool or the latest compiler technology is somehow going to fundamentally
enhance their ability to develop useful, efficient code.

Hampered as I am by my twenty years industry experience as a non-IT person,
I just cannot bring myself to accept that postulate. In fact, I am
hopelessly enamoured of its antithesis: a good programmer with inferior
tools produces a better product than does a poor programmer with the best
tools.

Ok, so call me a dinosaur; but remember who gets the primo spots in the
museum!

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AEFC25.6147FA3A@home.com>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AE5EAB.DEC19FBF@home.com>...

> MCM

Now look here Michael(angelo),

'Been away from mainframes for a long time - but I'm guessing there are
installations where the annual bill hits $10 mill. Commerce can't be
expected to continue paying for expensive hired-help like yourself and
your buddies Raphael and Leonardo.

Not as elegant - but commerce will eventually opt for
painting-by-numbers (templates). Who will make this happen - not the
end-users but 'switched on' systems analysts who see the merits of
compartmentalizing into reusable modules.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_Iis3.265$sM4.12947@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <kfNq3.607$d05.42948@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37ABC802.6707D5@home.com> <4zkr3.1711$9X.63736@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AE5EAB.DEC19FBF@home.com> <sgzr3.24$I4.950@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AEFC25.6147FA3A@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AEFC25.6147FA3A@home.com>...
>Not as elegant - but commerce will eventually opt for
>painting-by-numbers (templates). Who will make this happen - not the
>end-users but 'switched on' systems analysts who see the merits of
>compartmentalizing into reusable modules.

Please see my reply to Mr. Dashwood for why I believe programming is
artistry as much as engineering. But you bring up a good point: where *do*
we draw the line between artists and engineers? What is the optimal mix of
these very different skill sets for a given project?

If there were more managers who have been programmer/analysts, or more
programmer/analysts who have been managers, we might see some movement
respecting artistry on behalf of management, and some acceptance of
engineering (and financial) reality on behalf of programmers.

"Painting-by-the-numbers" gets a lot done for the present while doing little
for the future; "painting-by-the-beauty" takes longer to show a return, but
one may savor (i.e., profit from ) the work product for a long time.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B39121.14A9DECF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <kfNq3.607$d05.42948@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37ABC802.6707D5@home.com> <4zkr3.1711$9X.63736@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AE5EAB.DEC19FBF@home.com> <sgzr3.24$I4.950@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AEFC25.6147FA3A@home.com> <_Iis3.265$sM4.12947@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AEFC25.6147FA3A@home.com>...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

To be truthful Michael, I agree. As a lover of Renaissance art I like
the works of Michaelangelo, Rapahel and Lenonardo. I abhor where
paint-by-numbers leads us to Andy Warhol, Picasso, Salvador Dali and
every other modern splurger. (To give Dali his due he could be a 'true'
artist and produced his magnificent "Crucifixion").

Touching briefly on Pete's futurist approach, (I really don't want to
get into futurism - it's not my bag), I can see the validity of some of
his arguments. However, I think it was Ray Leech who made reference to
the human brain as a model which computers with AI wont be able to
match. If they ever did then I see a dark spectre on the horizon - HAL.
A self taught computer could arrive at the conclusion that it doesn't
need human beings, let alone programmers - and then come up with its own
sinister solution to that problem. Other than that let's leave the very
proper Brit and the Kiwi to go at it hammer and tongs. 'Nuff said about
futurism.

I'm really more concerned about the present and say the next 20/30
years. From what I've written 'some' accept the concept of common
modules in some systems, possibly in common accounting systems - but
they don't go with me all the way.

I was told quite categorically there was no semblance of similarity
between Safeway, Sears and Walmart. Well having worked with a Department
Store group ('Sears-model') with Department Store foodhalls
('Safeway-model'), supermarkets ('Safeway-model') and in Canada with a
hardware retailer (take your pick 'Sears-model' or 'Walmart-model') - I
can assure you there are similarities between all three. It is these
similarities which represent the basis of determining objects in OO.

Quite rightly - you have picked up on artistry - and why not; why
shouldn't you be pleased(proud) of what you have achieved. We have to
differentiate between what Ray Leech aptly called "grunt coding" and
those who have the smarts and vision to bring it all together. No matter
how sophisticated computerization becomes there will always be a need
for the Information specialists. As many of the contributors have
pointed out - the end-user just doesn't know what it's about - which is
not arrogance, but facing reality. (As a corollary - I drive my van,
quite proficiently, but I never look under the hood, and could care less
about the theory of a combustion engine). If however we can put some of
the 'coding' into libraries - then there will be less need of 'grunt
programmers'.

I'll avoid the terms Open COBOL and also GNU-COBOL/Open Source - the
first didn't really turn people on and the second, with a reticence from
Laura Leeky to tell us about it, appears to be restricted to just Linux.
I'll coin a new term COBOL Open Libraries.

Open Libraries - Now to proposition on this one presents a problem
because, to my mind, the most practical tool is OO. However, with only a
couple of compilers that actually have OO for COBOL is does appear
academic at this stage. (How do you evaluate if it is a practicall
approach without being able to program using the tool ?).

You, Judson and others have created access to your sites where other
COBOLers can freely copy routines, adapt and use them as they wish.
It means cutting, pasting and compiling. Take what you are currently
doing with numerics, given an OO approach this could be part of an Open
OO Library. The same goes for that topic currently being discussed under
'Arrays' - the efficiency of the approach to in-line performs. (I've
stored the messages for later digestion). The ins and outs of these
in-line performs can only be truly proven when people sit down and
program, using a common base, and produce timings on their individual
approaches.

Thia is not a push for OO - but just to illustrate its flexibility to
achieve Open Libraries. If I copy/paste your/Judson's routines I then
have to compile. Given standard classes in an Open Library, which are
generic to all users, mainframe down to PC - and gven a consus from
compiler vendors - these 'generic' library classes could be provided by
the vendors already compiled to an operating platform. You the
programmer then merely call(invoke) the compiled routines as necessasry.

The number of classes is limitless, with the ability to cover all
platforms, and differences can be held as sub-classes to the main class.
This doesn't mean that every programmer has to store the source for all
classes - they selectively extract those they need for their
application.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NeWs3.159$1c2.6615@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <kfNq3.607$d05.42948@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37ABC802.6707D5@home.com> <4zkr3.1711$9X.63736@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AE5EAB.DEC19FBF@home.com> <sgzr3.24$I4.950@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37AEFC25.6147FA3A@home.com> <_Iis3.265$sM4.12947@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37B39121.14A9DECF@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37B39121.14A9DECF@home.com>...
>..  From what I've written 'some' accept the concept of common
>modules in some systems... but they don't go with me all the way.
> ...
>... If however we can put some of  the 'coding' into libraries - then there
will be less need of 'grunt
>programmers'...
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmer then merely call(invoke) the compiled routines as necessasry.

What is somewhat remarkable to me is how the concept of "objects" and
"precompiled libraries" is treated as news. From the time I started
programming, I have never been averse to using libraries. I have always
programmed in small, independent procedures, where data to be manipulated
was passed and returned. Now, teaching myself Windows programming, where the
canned API is like a whole warehouse full of useful precompiled  routines, I
find I like the idea more than ever.

The only real obstacle I've run into at client sites is The Big Mo: "We've
never done it that way," or,  "I can't use it if I can't control the source
code."  Some clients have common date routines; a handful, database access
routines. Save these, I just do not encounter nice collections of
precompiled, thoroughly-tested, debugged and documented code which can be
either linkedited in or called dynamically.

But this paucity of reusable routines seems more the result of short-term
management strategy than anything else.  Developing a good library does not
contribute to *this* quarter's bottom line, even though the development
costs will be recovered many times over in the future. Purchasing a library,
the same holds: the cost is now; the payback, later.

Inasmuch as the the industrial environment, where much of our software
development is done,  continues to be dominated by short-term thinking, "OO"
programming will languish in that same "when we get around to it" file,
right alongside 4GLs, end-user computing for end-users, real beta testing
and better documentation.

Not to throw cold water on anyone, but that's just the way I see it.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> on behalf of
Mr. X...
>So, goodbye "computer programming" with its attendant mystique, jargon,
>tedium,  cost, and intensive mental effort. I believe we will see
>systems like the one above within the next 15 years. The technicians of

I agree in this vision. That day will eventually arrive, when the programmer
profession is heavily limited. Limited in that sense there will still have
to be developers, but those will be few, working for companies that
specialise on software types, after a while we end up with general purpose
AI. I am not so sure of his time perspective, it may last another decade or
two before we have general purpose AI. There are two problems that needs to
be solved before this may happen. Computers has to been significantly
faster. I have no sample number, but it is high. The second is storage
capability and access speed. IBM's recent break through is promising good,
however, in order to obtain functional AI, we will need 3d biochemical
storage, that is the only thing that can realise AI. Once this is in place,
we will be amazed of computers learning ability. The university of my
hometown has done research on 3d storage and has had inexplicable results.
It seemed like the computer through the use of 3d storage somehow became
able learn by itself. No discussion of this subject however, I do not know
the details good enough for that.

AI sounds cool. But is important to distinguish between an expert system
which could be used for his suggestion and a real AI system. While the
expert system completely depend on a predefined set of rules, and so some
degree is able to learn of that. Is AI intended to obtain knowledge by
itself. To push some cold water into this subject; remember even an ant is
more intelligent than the smartest computer, not at least it is many times
smaller.

>These days I no longer encourage young people to think about computer
>programming as a career.

I wouldn't say that, but they will have to be prepared for a though fight.
Anyway we are talking decades ahead.

Cheesle
```

```
>Subject: Re: Who cares ?
>From: "Cheesle" cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no 
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I definitely agree that things can't go on the same way - JAVA, VBA, etc. are
definitely big steps in the wrong direction. Can't say that I much care for
SMALLTALK as well.

About the only programming environment that's "WOW'ed" me is SAS, and that's
been around for decades. Can often do the same thing with a few dozen lines of
SAS as a couple of thousand lines of COBOL. IBM Visual Age For Smalltalk
instructors were perplexed that the 30 page example that they used to "sell"
the product to students could be done with five lines of SAS. Runs on Windows,
MVS, Unix, etc. while simultaneously reading ORACLE, flat files, EXCEL, DB2 (no
kidding!). Too bad SAS Institute makes getting a personal license
(economically) impossible.

Don't write off computer programming too soon though. 

The Director of the computer science program I attended long ago pointed out
that while the job description may be called something else and be structured
somewhat differently, but a lot of people (including successful CEO's!) just
aren't able to figure out even fourth grade word problems and will always need
some type of problem solving assistance!
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>`

```
Some of the conclusions come from false assumptions.

The first, of which, is the assumption that a business owner,
or other user, wants to build a custom system from components.
It is far more likely a packaged system would be purchased
that does almost, but not quite, what the user wants.

Once the user determines what is needed, the user will ask
the package to connect them with a specialist. After the voice
and video link is establish through the computer, this package
specialist will then create what is needed by telling the package,
in techno-speak, the desires of the user. The user will see the
solution take shape on the screen. When the user is satisfied,
the result will be saved and the user billed for the time taken.

The reason that specialists ("programmers") will continue to
exist is that the business owner, or other user, is far more
interested in their chosen occupation than in spending time
trying to determine how to make the package do anything
more complex than adding a logo or changing a standard
message. We are living in an age of increasing specialization
and business owners and other users are not computer
specialists. (If they are not more interested in their current
occupation, they should choose another!)

The second of these false assumptions is that computer
programming will continue to be costly, labor intensive, and
time-consuming.

I think it possible that, within the next five years, we will see
a technological breakthrough that will greatly reduce the
cost of software development and maintenance. Since
most expect the advance to occur in OO or new languages,
these are the least likely sources!

My guess is that it will come from re-engineering tools.
No one is talking about them; the people developing these
tools have worked on the problems for years, if not decades,
in some cases; and software maintenance consumes more
money than new development. For the past several years,
companies, offering re-engineering tools, have been spending
their resources developing Y2K solutions. Since this is almost
history, these companies are re-directing their resources (and
Y2K experience) to the more general maintenance problems.
Furthermore, a breakthrough in re-engineering tools likely will
apply to new development.
------------------
Rick Smith

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>...
[...]

>know it has a limited remaining life span. History will show that

[...]
>It is too costly, labour intensive, and time-consuming. ...
[...]
>No, it's NOT Science Fiction and I AM serious! The advent of artificial
>intelligence, voice activated interfaces in standard English, and more
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Imagine the following scenario:
[...]
>There are 2 fundamental things which make the above scenario possible:
>1. The CONCEPTS of CUSTOMER, PRODUCT, TRANSACTION etc. can be built into
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>achievable. ...
[...]
>2. The use of ITERATION. Trial and error. The learning curve. Don't even
>try and get it right first time (that is one of the major failures in
>the way we currently develop systems...waste a ton of time getting it
>"right" then find it all has to be changed anyway....(you may have
>guessed I am a devotee of RAD for project management...<grin>))  ...
[...]
>So, goodbye "computer programming" with its attendant mystique, jargon,
>tedium,  cost, and intensive mental effort. I believe we will see
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```
Well I goofed. I had assumed my 'correspondent' wished to remain
anonymous. Well we are all 'artistes' and he wants to protect his 
'own thoughts', so here goes and his name appears above.

Michael Mattias - remember the name ? You two used to chat on M/F
Compuserve Forum, and as I remember, invariably were both on the same
side of an argument/discussion.

>>>
I proposed not to quote you by name on the basis that you didn't wish to
spend time on NewsGroups - you have enough correspondence already. If
you like I'll archive a summary and send it to you when the subject has
exhausted itself.

Jimmy
<<<
Absolutely NOT. If you submit anything said by me to the group, you MUST
attribute it. Apart from just being common courtesy, it is necessary to
protect my intellectual property rights. (As I mentioned before, some of
what I wrote to you may become part of a published article. I don't want
someone in the newsgroup to use my ideas and effort and pre-empt me.)
Please ensure that you mention the source of the correspondence by name,
and that all rights are reserved. That is the ONLY condition that I will
agree to you publishing on. Otherwise, keep my opinions to yourself
<grin>.

Regards,

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
: Some of the conclusions come from false assumptions.

: The first, of which, is the assumption that a business owner,
: or other user, wants to build a custom system from components.
: It is far more likely a packaged system would be purchased
: that does almost, but not quite, what the user wants.

I think that your assumption is just as false.  Some users (or
clients to me) do want custom systems either hand crafted or
from components.  They do not want to buy a packaged system
which would require to be purcheased for each user or each
site.  They sometimes want complete source code so they
have complete control.

Often there is not a cost effective packaged solution that
could fit their needs.

: Once the user determines what is needed, the user will ask
: the package to connect them with a specialist. After the voice
: and video link is establish through the computer, this package
: specialist will then create what is needed by telling the package,
: in techno-speak, the desires of the user. The user will see the
: solution take shape on the screen. When the user is satisfied,
: the result will be saved and the user billed for the time taken.

This has been claimed for decades and yet it still is as far off
as ever.  The earliest that I looked at was 'The Last One', but
system like 'LINK' were billed as this sort of system.
.
: I think it possible that, within the next five years, we will see
: a technological breakthrough that will greatly reduce the
: cost of software development and maintenance. Since
: most expect the advance to occur in OO or new languages,
: these are the least likely sources!


I'd have to agree that these will not produce a significant
cost decrease.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ad7f58.554142306@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37AD251A.CDDD0AF4@home.com>`

```
On Sun, 08 Aug 1999 06:27:44 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:


>Keep it going guys. I like what I'm reading - it's taking away some of
>the depression I felt when first reading Pete's original message - not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>etc....

I kept quiet because I thought the whole idea silly.

First - I don't consider HTML work "programming" until one gets to the
level of Javascript and/or "decision making" - it's word processing.
(Just to give you an idea of what programming is)

Second - To get to a programmerless world, you would have a static
technology.  The "interface" would have to re-write itself at the
users demand.  It only knows what it knows.  The computer only does
what we tell it.  Until the time that there is a truly intelligent
machine there will be programmers for the machine.  

Third - If there were such intelligent machines  - that could program
themselves, we would still have programmers.  These machines.

As long as we have computers, we'll have computer programmers.  

On the related subject - of products written in COBOL.  There are few
consumer purchaseable "off the shelf" systems written in COBOL. COBOL
has been the language of Business, not games and word processing.  

There isn't a common business application , business is diverse, so
there isn't a common program with COBOL as it's source language that
we all use. 

There off the shelf business products written in COBOL.  MOST hide the
fact that they are COBOL.  COBOL has an "antiquated" reputation.  Some
GASPED when they found one of my recent systems was COBOL - but they
love the application.

I'm not going to waste any more time on this silly thread. <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>: Some of the conclusions come from false assumptions.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>from components. ...
[...]
Perhaps I should have said "... user, will want to ..." to retain
the future-ness of the assumptions.

>: Once the user determines what is needed, the user will ask
>: the package to connect them with a specialist. ...
[...]
>This has been claimed for decades and yet it still is as far off
>as ever.  The earliest that I looked at was 'The Last One', but
>system like 'LINK' were billed as this sort of system.

It is not THAT far off!

Imagine, today, two phone lines: one to connect the user
and the specialist; the other to connect the computers, By
using an application, such as Carbon Copy, the specialist
can control the user's computer. If the application is based
on MS Access, the specialist could add tables, reports,
forms, modify code, anything! The user would see all of this
on their screen, as it happens.

Now imagine 15 years in the future with higher speed Internet 3
connections and interactive capabilities. The package on the
user's computer contacts the package vendor's server (It called
home!), the vendor's server connects the user's computer with
a specialist. The link is established and the specialist and user
can talk while their computers are connected digitally.

Using the latest in system development and maintenance
technology, the specialist ("programmer") retrieves a graphic
system diagram of the user's current configuration, including all
changes that were done by the user. After listening to what the
user wants, the programmer then proceeds to make the change,
interactively. Sample output is produced and the user agrees
that it satisfies their needs. The definitions and process scripts
are translated to COBOL and compiled. A final test is run,
confirmed, and the program is added to the package.

After the interactive link with the user is disconnected, the
programmer rejoins an interactive discussion on i.comp.lang.cobol
to protest an assertion that, in the future, programmers will not be
necessary.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

: It is not THAT far off!

It is as far off as it was 20 years ago when they thought they
had actually solved the problems with '4GL's and system
builders.

: Imagine, today, two phone lines: one to connect the user
: and the specialist; the other to connect the computers, By
: using an application, such as Carbon Copy, the specialist
: can control the user's computer. If the application is based

I've been doing that for over a decade.

: on MS Access, the specialist could add tables, reports,
: forms, modify code, anything! The user would see all of this
: on their screen, as it happens.

You don't do a lot of systems analysis and design do you. Your
first wrong assumption is that the 'user' has much idea about
what the system actually needs to do or how it should do it.
Most users have a vague idea about what they want to see in the
output but usually no idea about where the input needs to
come from.

The second false assumption is that MS Access would be useful
for doing a system.

: Using the latest in system development and maintenance
: technology, the specialist ("programmer") retrieves a graphic
: system diagram of the user's current configuration, including all
: changes that were done by the user. After listening to what the
: user wants, the programmer then proceeds to make the change,
: interactively. Sample output is produced and the user agrees
: that it satisfies their needs. The definitions and process scripts
: are translated to COBOL and compiled. A final test is run,
: confirmed, and the program is added to the package.

Nice recipie for disaster. When you get to look at how businesses
really work you will find that you need to ignore what the
_users_ ask for - this is usually irrelevant even if the user
is the managing director, and must find out what the *business*
needs.

Taking an overly simplistic view (as above) merely results in
each employee having their own isolated and proprietry toys
that they individually use to try to establish their own
domains.  What is _actually_ required is appropriate data
capture at point of production and distribution to those who
need it.  This is _not_ done with individual toy systems
specified by each user.

.
: After the interactive link with the user is disconnected, the
: programmer rejoins an interactive discussion on i.comp.lang.cobol
: to protest an assertion that, in the future, programmers will not be
: necessary.

A fundemental issue of computer systems is that they must map
onto the working of the business.  Every business is _different_.
If two businesses are actually, and accidentally, the same then
they will have no competitive advantage.  To overcome that each
will _change_ to try to establish a distinction over the other
in an effort to gain market share or change its market.

Thus every business is not only different from every other business
but it is also different from what it used to be.

As the computer system must map how the business operrates this
means that the computer system must also change.  This is not
just that the report layouts change, but the way the data is
captured, the way decisions are made, the way the data is
distributed also has to change.

As they haven't yet written code for every possible combination
that is used today, let alone how it may be done in the
future, I see no reduction in the numbers of programmers
required.

I myself am writing new code for application areas which have
not previously been done by computers by anyone.  This is
not available even as a bespoke system (yet), let alone as
a package or component system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net>`
