```
Although you made several worthwhile and interesting
comments, most are related to having multiple users
within one organization using software that was designed
specifically for that organization. My comments and
speculation were about single users, each in their own
organization, using packaged software that does almost,
but not quite, what that user wants. I have removed most of
the comments that appear related to multiple users.

Richard Plinston wrote in message <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
[...]
>: Imagine, today, two phone lines: one to connect the user
>: and the specialist; the other to connect the computers, By
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>come from.

Since the user owns (the license) and controls the (packaged)
system, it is their responsibility to determine the business need.
It is up to the specialist to determine if that need can be filled.


>The second false assumption is that MS Access would be useful
>for doing a system.

Useful systems can be built with any development tool,
even MS Access. ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AE718D.729FB785@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
First, it is a wrong assumption to say that Pete Dashwood is 'claiming'
there will be a programmerless society. He is indicating, as a futurist,
that there will be a considerably reduced role.

I can't discuss the topic in the abstract - I have to look at it from my
own experience :-

Firstly let's take Pete's concept and give it a name "Verbal Templates".
We know the verbal bit is already active or being developed.
The template bit also already exists (from my experience) :--

- QuickBooks - request a Balance Sheet/Profit Loss - I can select end
and start dates, comparison this year to last, condensed or expanded
detail etc. - I make the final mouse click - No! I don't get an hour
glass telling me something is happening - I immediately get a 'picture'
of what I asked for.

- WordPerfect - what "template" layout do I want for my document,
formal, informal, headings, trailers etc.

My own experience, so you know where I'm coming from :-

"Commercial"

(a)  12 years in RAF

- Accountant/Systems Analyst/Systems and Programming Manager

(b) Dairy Industry 		 
(c) Department Stores

'Programming" - the latter 90% being COBOL
- Point of Sale
- Production Accounting - Agricultural chemical company - now
  subsidiary of Union Carbide
- One-off General Ledger
- Small system for calculating re-bars (re-inforcing bars) required
  when using concrete
- NDT(Non-Destructive Testing) Ultrasonic Corrosion surveys at oil/gas
  plants.

There's one very common thread to (a) (b) (c) above - Payroll. How many
times have we seen programming teams re-inventing the wheel for payroll.
OK, much is now off-loaded by banks offering a payroll service, but
payroll systems are probably still being written. So what is so
different between paying me in the RAF and my salary cheques for the
Dairy/Department Store companies ? From a systems sense there are
basics - identify me, agree a salary rate and pay me. I know much is now
done by Direct Credit to banks - but if you do cut a cheque, what is
dramatically different between the cheque you cut for me and the one
used in Accounts Payable. The essential difference is that remittance
detailing what is being paid - so three templates (1) cheque (2) Payroll
slip or (3) Payables - list of documents being paid.

Dairy Group above - Milk Collection System. Provide truck drivers with a
list of farmers to visit, collect churns, bring them back to dairy
factory for weighing (gallons), segregate into low and high butterfat
content (latter used for butter, creams, yoghurt etc.), and pay farmer
for milk collected - same Cheque concept as above but with Template (4)
details of what we are paying the farmer for. In this instance the
system has an additional template - computerized scales for weighing the
churns. Other than the product differs - what is very different from
what I've described above and collecting fruits/vegetables from
California farms or collecting grapes from vineyards in the Napa valley.

Point-of-Sale. Agreed equipment, (cash registers) tends to be
specialized - but assuming you are designing - there are templates for
(a) recognizing product by store SKU or UPC (b) cash, charge or plastic
(c) City/Province-State/Federal taxes. UPCs have relieved much of the
necessity of identifying items and the NRMA(National Retail Merchants
Association) has produced a 'red book' using a 4-digit department number
to identify products, (and I kid you not - everything from a locomotive
to a condom). Do you think Point-of-Sale is dramatically different in
Safeway, Sears-Roebuck and Walmart.

Production and Accounting - Based on parts explosion you will probably
concede to me that their are similarities between ship-building, Ford,
Mercedes and Boeing. Templates of these systems apply to the Agriculture
system I produced and the Dairy Company. Oh yes, we thought we had about
125-150 products when we started. At my insistence we contacted every
factory and asked them what they made. Final total - 3,500 products and
labels. Two-thirds of  our catalogue was liquid milks and various milk
powders sold to confectionery and cookie manufacturers, over and above
domestic products sold to supermarkets. Again the dairy production can
use templates from the other industries.

Inventory Control - whether it is butter, cheese, agriculture chemicals,
autos or plane parts - raw materials are used, products produced,
stored, sold. Yes different templates are introduced (a) refrigeration,
(b) dangerous chemicals, liquids (c) heavy parts etc.......  Regarding
inventory same similarity applies to Safeway, Sears-Roebuck and Walmart.

Specialization - Yes it exists, NASA, NYSE, LSE and although sizeable
not a great daffy of them that don't fit general commercial templates.
What I have specifically designed - the Corrosion Testing System - while
not necessarily unique you can probably count them on one hand.

I think I've conveyed what I'm getting at.  Does anyone seriously
suggest that after 40 years of EDP we haven't discovered probably in
excess of 80-90% of the templates required for commerce.

As yet I haven't mentioned the word 'programming' other than as a
heading. Keep your heads in the sand if you wish. Do you want to wait
until some jerk markets Java-Lite, Java-Sprite as the best thing since
sliced bread to create these templates. (Remember it's not necessarily
the best that wins out, but the product/approach which has the best
marketing smarts). I wont be around, but wouldn't it be nice if COBOL
was the major template-builder, its clarity for communicating,
introduction of those new data-types, the ability to use Function
'programs' or OO to build the templates - and with a little thought it
could generate new function programs or OO classes. No, I not getting
into Alan Turing's territory of AI - probably that could be another 50
or 100 years away.

I don't see this seriously happening while we have the current status
quo with regard to COBOL-2000+. COBOL has to be updated EVERY year to
keep ahead. 

Back to the beginning - I trained as a stenographer in the RAF - there
aren't any now - they use dictaphones etc. - same as they do in the
Pentagon.  Back to Pete's picture of the future - it will happen, like
it or not. Wouldn't it be real cool if all the major development was
done using COBOL and the remaining specialists left were using COBOL to
produce new templates as required.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7onace$gpg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

: - QuickBooks - request a Balance Sheet/Profit Loss - I can select end
: and start dates, comparison this year to last, condensed or expanded
: detail etc. - I make the final mouse click - No! I don't get an hour
: glass telling me something is happening - I immediately get a 'picture'
: of what I asked for.

But it will only do what is currently programmed into it. making
simple selections of simple reports is not like having new
facilities.

: There's one very common thread to (a) (b) (c) above - Payroll. How many
: times have we seen programming teams re-inventing the wheel for payroll.
: OK, much is now off-loaded by banks offering a payroll service, but
: payroll systems are probably still being written. So what is so
: different between paying me in the RAF and my salary cheques for the
: Dairy/Department Store companies ? From a systems sense there are

There are likely to be _considerable_ differences.  This is
primarily because Payroll is not a stand alone system in larger
organizations.  In the RAF they are probably very centrailsed
with pay all being processed at one site (Devizes?).  This
system must interface with personnel and staffing systems so
that staff can have skills and promotions recorded and can
track where everyone is in the world.

With, say, a chain of supermarkets, each would generate the pay
locally as there is no need to be able to move staff to another
site.  However it should interface with a scheduling system to
produce (or recognise) the rota.  It may also need to record
arrival via a time clock.

The systems are as different as a motor car is from a cable car -
those both shift people, but the driver and engines are rather
different.

: basics - identify me, agree a salary rate and pay me. I know much is now
: done by Direct Credit to banks - but if you do cut a cheque, what is
: dramatically different between the cheque you cut for me and the one
: used in Accounts Payable. The essential difference is that remittance
: detailing what is being paid - so three templates (1) cheque (2) Payroll
: slip or (3) Payables - list of documents being paid.

You are not looking at the system requirements at all, merely at
your own subjective view of it - getting money from the company.
There is a _lot_ more to Payroll systems than producing cheques.

: churns. Other than the product differs - what is very different from
: what I've described above and collecting fruits/vegetables from
: California farms or collecting grapes from vineyards in the Napa valley.

'What is very different ?'.  The requirements are very different,
the input is different, the origin of the input is different,
the processing is different, the output is different. Not
only would they be different between collect milk and/or
collecting produce, but different collection companies would
be vastly different.  They work the way they work, not the
way some computer thinks they should.

: to a condom). Do you think Point-of-Sale is dramatically different in
: Safeway, Sears-Roebuck and Walmart.

Yes I do.

: Production and Accounting - Based on parts explosion you will probably
: concede to me that their are similarities between ship-building, Ford,
: Mercedes and Boeing. Templates of these systems apply to the Agriculture

No I certainly will not 'concede' any such thing.  

It is a serious mistake in systems analysis to pre-judge a system
as being 'like one in x-industry'.

: I think I've conveyed what I'm getting at.  Does anyone seriously
: suggest that after 40 years of EDP we haven't discovered probably in
: excess of 80-90% of the templates required for commerce.

Yes, I seriously suggest that.  Currently a new model that of
eCommerce is being implemented.  This is a global model that is
not just mail order stuck on the side of existing systems.

New models of business require new ways of data flow.  

I recently looked at the problems inherent in 'loyalty cards'
being added to an existing sales system.  The company wanted
to adopt a system written by another.  It was totally useless
to the new company because the originator had a distributed
system with maintenance being done at each site.  The new
company was going to maintain centrally and there wouldn't
be enough hours available each day to collect and process
all the data on one machine - a completely different approach
would be required.

The 'loyalty cards' also required new features in POS, EFTPOS
and Ledgers, things which did not exist in anyone's systems.
Another new mechanism could be invented which would again
invalidate all existing templetes.

: Back to the beginning - I trained as a stenographer in the RAF - there
: aren't any now - they use dictaphones etc. - same as they do in the

And one day they may change to voice input, or to some new
mechanism that hasn't been thought of yet, with changes
required in document storage and retrieval systems that aren't
in anyones templetes.
```

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> arrival via a time clock.

Perhaps - but when with Debenhams we did it centrally on an ICL
mainframe for both our stores and our supermarket subsidiary, Caters (in
SE London area)
> 
> The systems are as different as a motor car is from a cable car -
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A well reasoned argument Richard - but I don't buy into the concept of
uniqueness - in the same context that you view it. Let's narrow it down
and keep to Payroll and Personnel systems - we all have a vested
interest in the first <G>

The essence of the Payroll module is to pay people, and as a by-product,
postings to the General Ledger. Ken Foskey touched on the same topic
citing the differences in dealing with the 'pluses' available to
directors, bonuses, share issues, perks like cars etc. Of course there
are different ground rules - but it is still the common cycle of
collecting data and then producing payments. Directors' payments, union
dues, sales commissions, taxes are complexities that add to the system -
but you don't have to start from scratch because your organization is
considered unique.

The majority reject the concept of OO being a useful tool to do this -
but it does exactly that. Prime classes to do payroll, in its simplest
sense, then treat the add-ons or exceptions  as new classes or
sub-classes. This is nothing to do with the general structuring of your
data files - certain keys in payroll records trigger off OO classes to
handle these specific differences. If you don't like the ugly letters
"OO" substitute Function programs or calling sub-programs. Each will
take you the same route to payroll exceptions. But do them once and
treat them as general commercial 'canned routines'. Plagiarizing a claim
used with a certain new language, "Write COBOL once and use anywhere".

From a corporate or military point-of-view, of course it is not just
payroll. Depending upon the organization it has to be backed up by  a
Personnel Records system, (or the ugly 'Human Resources'). If military -
is this man a candidate for SAS/SEALS. What other skills do people have.
Pentagon wants to send a brigade or whatever to the Gulf, scan
your personnel records for men or groups that meet your criteria. Same
with Big Blue, look for a particular salesman who is a match, knows the
machine and has knowledge of the specific industry you want him to sell
to. The data record for 'employees' has a set of common fields - then
you add to this by specialization, new records, sub-records or
redefines. And again you get at the exceptions through canned routines.

Some months ago I smiled when I read that agriculturists were
recommending that land shouldn't be continuously used until exhausted. 
I recall vividly as a little boy those colourful pictures of the
Anglo/Norman farming style. Tract of land divided into three strips -
grow, graze and rest in a three-year cycle. My ! We require people to
study agriculture to tell us the same thing some 900 - 800 years later.

Let me give you personal experiences, (in UK), of dumb technology where
people just didn't look at what was available, adapt it or expand on it.
They obviously considered their own 'home-grown' environments to be
unique.

- 1961 Bought a Keymatic Washing machine, front loader with a square
plastic card that had 8 'programs' for washing settings. Excellent -
does a better job not swirling the clothes vertically. Manufacturer -
Hoover. Do any of our 'cousins' recall a similar machine being marketed
by the parent corporation in the US ? Had to wait until last year to get
a front-loader in Canada - but it wasn't Hoover.

1968(approx) -  Bought a vertical freezer - 18 cu ft. Manufacturer -
Woods of Canada. Still can't get the same model here in Canada.

1972(approx) - Lloyd's Bank introduced cash dispensing machines. They
liked what manufacturer was offering, but said they wanted to change
specification. Successfully implemented with no hassles. Who provided
the dispensers - IBM. It took 10 - 13 years before Canadian banks
introduced this 'new concept' to their banking.

That movie title - it has got to be a re-issue of an old movie - "Eyes
Wide Shut".

Jimmy, Calgary AB 

PS: Your (?) query re Devizes - way after my time. Although the Dairy
company (Unigate) was also in Wiltshire at Trowbridge, about 20/30 miles
between the two.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b068f5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com> <7onace$gpg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Hi Richard,

I'm not going to re-quote everything you replied to Jimmy, but I read it,
thought about it, and my comments are as follows:

1. You see the differences rather than the similarities. This is NOT a bad
thing and I'm not being critical of you. It is very necessary to identify
differences with the state of technology as it is at the moment. I liked
your point about the difficulty of trying to integrate the loyalty card
system with a system which was designed around a completely different
architecture. System design architecture is still an essential consideration
AT THIS POINT IN TIME. As a responsible Applications Analyst you pay
attention to detail and see every development as a new requirement. I don't
totally agree with this approach, but I respect your right to do it that
way.

I believe (and I think Jimmy was coming to this in his examples) that there
is much common processing at a CONCEPTual level in Industry and Commerce.
(Come to think of it, there probably is in every field of endeavour). We
need to look above the day-to-day grind of how we analyse and develop
systems at the moment to see a new direction. Let's postulate for a moment
how we might do things IF computers were capable of understanding
concepts...

Coming back to your loyalty card example, we would explain what loyalty
cards were and how they fitted into the overall business. We would describe
why we were implementing a loyalty card scheme and how it would work in
terms of card issue, updating at POS, and the kind of information we would
expect to get. This would be done by interaction with the computer which
would probably ask questions about whether these cards were connected to a
CUSTOMER or a PRODUCT and the new and existing Transactions required.
Eventually the computer would arrive at a new concept: LOYALTY CARDS. It
would then amend the existing architecture to incorporate this concept.

The architecture would be totally flexible because it would be comprised of
objects which would be connected and disconnected by the computer. (Humans
would not need to know or understand these connections; simply describe what
was wrong if something was wrong. There would probably be a facility similar
to the EXPLAIN function in SQL, which would allow us to see how the system
had put things together (and maybe even learn from it), and also describe
the heuristics being used by the system at any given moment.)

You may think this sounds far-fetched, but it is not really so far from what
we do now when we implement a new system. The difference is that at present
the work is done by a human (or humans), interacting with the end user, and
translating his requirements into something another set of humans can
program into a computer. This is necessary because the computer NEEDS to be
programmed. Why? Because it doesn't understand anything. What is
understanding and where does it come from? It starts with a CONCEPT (before
that, there is just data gathering and experience).

If we didn't like the first cut of the new Loyalty Cards system we would say
so, interact with the system, and eventually arrive at a suitable solution.

OK, so that looks all right IF the computer can understand concepts... Think
about what we would need to implement "conceptual software". I have an
ActiveX control that provides a timer interrupt to start sub-tasks for me. I
simply tell it how frequently I want the alarm raised and what task I want
started. Whether you visualise this as a COBOL CALL...USING or an object
with certain properties and methods is irrelevant. If I put the right
interface in place I could simply say to it, in English: "Start my Web
Browser at 7:00pm on Thursday and access the TV GUIDE."  This ActiveX
control can be simply dropped onto any Windows Window (or Web Page) and will
do its job. I have others that deal with calendars, accessing databases, all
sorts of stuff... They are fun to write and represent truly re-usable code.
It can be argued that each one represents a concept. However, they do NOT
represent UNDERSTANDING.
(Not yet, anyway...)

Now extrapolate the idea to objects that are smarter and can write their own
methods to handle new situations (given a set of inputs and output goals).
Add to this computer hardware which is maybe 100 times more powerful than we
have today (that's probably 6 months down the line, given the current rate
of hardware evolution...<grin>) and an Operating System that incorporates
Natural Language and some basic AI (at least with the capability to
learn...(neural networking?)). You can see where I'm going.

I predict that within 20 years we will have computer systems which will give
us solutions to our problems based on concepts, heuristics, and
trial-and-error, and WE WILL NOT KNOW HOW THEY ARRIVED AT THEIR SOLUTION!
(Neither should we worry about it). They will modify themselves so quickly
and so often that it will be impossible to "debug" (and pointless; if the
solution is "wrong" we simply tell them to fix it).

"History will show that "computer programming" was a phenomenon of the the
latter half of the 20th Century"
(OK so maybe it will persist for a few years yet, but it's days are
definitely numbered. As more and more people have access to computers they
will demand the right to user-friendly interfaces which will allow them to
configure their systems without the need for specialised knowledge and
TechnoSpeak. This will replace "programming" as we understand it.)
Application development will come down to the production of more re-usable
conceptual objects. These will be developed initially using the techniques
we have today (it's all we've got!), but later they will spawn and modify
themselves without Human intervention.

2. Responding to some of your specifics...

Richard Plinston wrote in message <7onace$gpg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are both right here. Jimmy is drawing comparisons and you have correctly
made the point that it only knows what is programmed into it. I would argue
that some concepts are (almost) in place, but there is no UNDERSTANDING. See
my dissertation above.



>
>: to a condom). Do you think Point-of-Sale is dramatically different in
>: Safeway, Sears-Roebuck and Walmart.
>
>Yes I do.


It's interesting that the Companies mentioned don't.  I wouldn't mind
betting that they all used the same consultancy (or one of the top 4) when
looking at POS systems. Accepting that it is not in their area of expertise,
they go out and find who is the tops in this field. How does somebody get to
be tops in the field? By implementing the technology successfully in
different organizations. Jimmy is right here, there is no dramatic
difference.

>
>: Production and Accounting - Based on parts explosion you will probably
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>as being 'like one in x-industry'.

Why? We have to start somewhere. What's the first question they will ask you
when you go for an interview? ("What experience have you had in our
industry?") If you are applying for a job in the car industry, "Mercedes" or
"Ford" cuts a lot more ice than "McDonalds" or "Marks and Spencer", yet they
are all household names.

>
>: I think I've conveyed what I'm getting at.  Does anyone seriously
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>not just mail order stuck on the side of existing systems.


I guess us old-timers really wasted our time...<grin>

Don't hold your breath on e-commerce; the US Stock Market dropped by 45% in
this sector last week as people became disillusioned with Internet Stocks in
general and e-commerce in particular. e-commerce will come but not in it's
current form. I see individual businesses running their own e-commerce,
rather than the "go-shopping" type Mall approach which tries
to put it all under one roof.


OK, that's about it from me. I won't be placing more lengthy responses in
this NG as time constraints forbid it. I have been pleased to see the
responses stimulated by my original mail and will happily discuss any points
or elucidate my thoughts if anyone is interested (Who cares? <grin>) by
personal mail or a very brief comment to the NG.


Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oq1ef$8ql$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com> <7onace$gpg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37b068f5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

: 1. You see the differences rather than the similarities. This is NOT a bad

I do see the similarities too.  But often the similarities are
only superficial.

: Coming back to your loyalty card example, we would explain what loyalty
: cards were and how they fitted into the overall business. We would describe
: why we were implementing a loyalty card scheme and how it would work in
: terms of card issue, updating at POS, and the kind of information we would
: expect to get. This would be done by interaction with the computer which
: would probably ask questions about whether these cards were connected to a
: CUSTOMER or a PRODUCT and the new and existing Transactions required.
: Eventually the computer would arrive at a new concept: LOYALTY CARDS. It
: would then amend the existing architecture to incorporate this concept.

The issue you raised was that the _user_ would be able to do this
description to the computer.  On this particular issue I discussed
with several different users - marketing, management, IT, sales,
accounting.  They all had different views, often contradictory, 
they failed to notice the other's needs.  

I think your ideas are rather naieve.

: The architecture would be totally flexible because it would be comprised of

It cannot be _totally_ flexible.  It can only be flexible within the
limitation programmed into it, just as programmers and users can
only operate within their limitations.


: You may think this sounds far-fetched, but it is not really so far from what
: we do now when we implement a new system. The difference is that at present
: the work is done by a human (or humans), interacting with the end user, and
: translating his requirements into something another set of humans can
: program into a computer. This is necessary because the computer NEEDS to be
: programmed. Why? Because it doesn't understand anything. What is
: understanding and where does it come from? It starts with a CONCEPT (before
: that, there is just data gathering and experience).

Yes, the system must be programmed in order to understand concepts
and interact with the user.  This programming costs money, there
will be a charge for the software.  It then comes down to cost:

     Buy a futuristic conceptual computer for x million 
  or Buy computers for a few thousand and spend a few
        thousand on having it programmed by people

Your idea can only become a viable proposition when it is cost
effective.  A simple computer with a simple accounting package
will always cost less than one large enough to be able to
waste most of its storage space and processing power on some
horrendously complex software that can build the system.


: If we didn't like the first cut of the new Loyalty Cards system we would say
: so, interact with the system, and eventually arrive at a suitable solution.

Sorry, trial and _error_ is not an acceptable process in business.
No business would risk their ability to operate on such a basis
deliberately.


: ActiveX control that provides a timer interrupt to start sub-tasks for me. I
: simply tell it how frequently I want the alarm raised and what task I want
: started. Whether you visualise this as a COBOL CALL...USING or an object


You talk rockets to the moon and then exemplify it with a skateboard.

: "History will show that "computer programming" was a phenomenon of the the
: latter half of the 20th Century"
: (OK so maybe it will persist for a few years yet, but it's days are
: definitely numbered. As more and more people have access to computers they

That has been a claim for at least 20 years, just as the death of
Cobol has.  

: will demand the right to user-friendly interfaces which will allow them to
: configure their systems without the need for specialised knowledge and
: TechnoSpeak. This will replace "programming" as we understand it.)
: Application development will come down to the production of more re-usable
: conceptual objects. These will be developed initially using the techniques
: we have today (it's all we've got!), but later they will spawn and modify
: themselves without Human intervention.

But computers will also be pushed into _new_ and unforseen
application areas.  What you say _may_ become true eventually
for at least a part of, say, accounting syatems and payroll.
The new systems areas will simply not have components available
and bespoke systems will be required, then packaged solutions,
eventually they may become available as components.

In spite of cars having been around for 100 years there is still
development done by building bespoke parts, the number of
reusable components is very small.  Imagine a car industry
where there were standard bonnets, boots (hoods and trunks), guards,
dashborads, roofs, doors and all cars were made by assembling
these standard parts used by all manufacturers.  This would
save all those expensive designers, all the extravegences of
the marketers.  Think of the saving.

Of course nonoe would want the cars, because they would be
compromises they would be too heavy or too cramped or ...

Manufacturers would build bespoke parts to make the
products more effective or more attractive or more salable.

Same with computer systems - or indeed business systems.

: It's interesting that the Companies mentioned don't.  I wouldn't mind
: betting that they all used the same consultancy (or one of the top 4) when
: looking at POS systems. Accepting that it is not in their area of expertise,
: they go out and find who is the tops in this field. How does somebody get to
: be tops in the field? By implementing the technology successfully in
: different organizations. Jimmy is right here, there is no dramatic
: difference.

A statement made with no understanding of what system they _actually_
use based on an entirely superficial view.

Your 'argument' is like claiming that engines in Austins and 
Ferraris are the same because they both move cars and the
companies would 'obviously' use the same engineers.

: Why? We have to start somewhere. What's the first question they will ask you
: when you go for an interview? ("What experience have you had in our
: industry?") If you are applying for a job in the car industry, "Mercedes" or
: "Ford" cuts a lot more ice than "McDonalds" or "Marks and Spencer", yet they
: are all household names.

But your original included Boeing.  Having a job in the car 
industry would be no more appropriate than McDonalds.  You
originally asserted that car and aircraft industry were the
same and now ignore my assertion that Boeing and Ford have
different requirements by saying that cars and cars are
the same.

: I guess us old-timers really wasted our time...<grin>

Well I'm an old timer too, over 30 years working with computers.
I didn't stay with mainframes (which have stayed much the same
over that time) but noticed that different forms of computing
were being used and even more differences will occur in the
future.  Your predictions may come about, but not at the 
expense of all other ways of getting computers to do what is
wanted.

In other words if your ideas are implemented it will be an
_additional_ mechanism, programming will still continue as
it has been done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AEC4A8.B6F8CD54@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> The template bit also already exists (from my experience) :--
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> - NDT(Non-Destructive Testing) Ultrasonic Corrosion surveys at oil/gas


I generally agree with the sentiments.  The above applications can and
should be boiler plated and delivered on mass with clearly defined
interfaces (the other major consumer of programmer time).

The reinforcing stuff would probably be done in a quick Access system
today rather than get major development going.  (Assuming it is what
it sounds like and I bet it was not).

The problem is that people like to give themselves market
differentiation, as stated earlier.

Take payroll.  I worked at a company that differentiated it's payroll
from others.  It offered a comprehensive packaging system including
cars, computer leases, investment property interest only packages.  So
much so that one person had a zero income from the company everything
went in investments.

Take bonuses.  How are they calculated, apportioned, statistics
captured over a year, etc.  These are systems that need to be built,
they tend not to be done by boiler plates.

The boiler plates for word perfect are not a good example.  Before
they existed the users were doing the same thing, just taking longer. 
A short cut so to speak.  What about automated templates, get a user
to create an invoice template or modify the one in Word.  You will
find that they quickly revert to using a calculator and entering the
number rather than figuring out how the calculation was done in the
stock template.  I deleted the tax and broke the calculations.

There is definitely scope for this sort of thing and there should be
standard packages for accounting, it has a very clear definition
generated by an external force.  Packages should be available for all
facets of business but they are to constrained in steps the
programmer.

SAP is a classic case.  Buy a package and spend literally millions on
tailoring it.  This is the wave of the future.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aedcef@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com> <37AEC4A8.B6F8CD54@zip.com.au>`

```
Hi Ken, I read your response to Jimmy and you make good points.

There will always be a need for differentiation in business and you rightly
pointed out some good examples.

What I am contending is that the differentiation can be done by the end user
if we provide the right technical environment. Unfortunately, in some areas
of this thread it is being interpreted that I am simply talking about
"templates" and "packages". I'm not.

I'm suggesting that computers in the next couple of decades will recognise
"concepts" (like, a CUSTOMER, a PRODUCT, a PAYROLL...and the transactional
concepts that go with them....BUY, SELL, PAY A BONUS...(unfortunately, this
last is a concept foreign to many businesses)) and will be able to interact
with an end user, using natural language, to tailor these concepts into a
solution. The corollary to this is that "computer programming" as we
understand it will no logger be required. It's a bit like the collapse of
the cottage lace-making industries during the industrial revolution. Once
machines could do it faster (and everyone said it would be impossible to
build such a machine) the home industry was relegated to a hobby or pastime.
Can you see yourself writing COBOL for fun in 20 years? (I can...I do it for
fun now...!)

There are 2 keys to the success (or otherwise) of this vision:

1. We are able to build computers and operating systems capable of grasping
concepts.

2. The power of iteration. Trial-and-error. The user will interact with the
system and by trial-and-error it will arrive at what he wants. Once it has
done so, it will then forever be able to reproduce that solution. As a
spin-off it will also have expanded its concepts, a kind of true learning.

I have covered in other parts of this thread how I see the current state of
the art developing into stored concepts. Major contributions will be made by
OO (re-usable classes, properties, and methods), COM CORBA and ActiveX (the
software equivalents of plug-and-play), heuristic programming, and neural
networks. I see the concepts being embodied in firmware to provide cheap
computers tailored for a certain basic job. Add to this a smart operating
system with a natural language interface and I believe we can eliminate the
need for programming as it is currently understood.


>
>SAP is a classic case.  Buy a package and spend literally millions on
>tailoring it.  This is the wave of the future.
>
Yes, sadly, that is the direction for the short-term future. However, I
believe these solutions will grow and develop along the lines I have
outlined above.

I sure hope so.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7onb4f$h16$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com> <37AEC4A8.B6F8CD54@zip.com.au> <37aedcef@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

: There are 2 keys to the success (or otherwise) of this vision:

: 1. We are able to build computers and operating systems capable of grasping
: concepts.

: 2. The power of iteration. Trial-and-error. The user will interact with the
: system and by trial-and-error it will arrive at what he wants. Once it has
: done so, it will then forever be able to reproduce that solution. As a
: spin-off it will also have expanded its concepts, a kind of true learning.

You are describing what was the basis of 'LINK' (latterly Burrough's
LINK and Unisys LINK).  However, the great failure of your vision is
the capabilities of the 'user'.  User's may be very good at what
they do - running a business, selling, knowing their client's
needs, producing accounts, but that does not make them any good
at specifying computer systems.

Different people have different skills.  The salesmen that I
know couldn't add up a column of numbers (unless it was their
commision) and could never do accounts.  Programmers couldn't
sell a heater to an Eskimo.  You are expecting 'users' to be
able to specifiy their computer needs - even selecting options
is way beyond the vast majority of them.

LINK is actually what you describe, with templetes, and it was
done 20 years ago.  It was designed to be be used by 'end users',
but again they didn't have the skills to make the choices or
know how the data linked together.  LINK is still in use -
by systems people.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oruoh$jg7$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7okj3u$p8n$1@news.hitter.net> <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com>`

```
James, while I can agree with a lot of what you are saying, I cannot agree
with your premise.

Most of what you are saying has nothing to do with making the programmer's
role less important.  In fact, the exact opposite is true!  You are
describing a new generation of programming tools ... tools require
programmers.

If you walk into any shop today, you will find "cheat sheets" sitting beside
the computer.  These are, in reality, "programs" for the users. They are
usually created by programmers.  They reflect reality ... as a task becomes
too complex, we have to rely on pre-constructed sets of instructions to do
it, we cannot rely on "intelligence".  All programs are really just examples
of that generalised methodology ... "memorise this method, and apply it to
this situation".

As computer get more sophisticated, the "canned routine" library gets
larger.  As a result, it gets easier to "tell it" what to do.  However, the
analyse skills, or at least the need for analytical skills, does not go
away.  All that changes is the sophistication required to communicate.  I
was not really talking tongue in cheek when I said earlier that I would just
change professions from programmer to computer psychiatrist.

I run a downtown crack cocaine clinic on a voluntary basis.  The skill set
that I use there (and I have taken ten hardcore addicts off the street in
the last five years) is the exact same skill set that I use to design
software.  It is difficult to explain in words, because thirty years of
programming have given me a private mental language ... but the
relationships between people and social systems are "algorithmic" and
"programmable" in much the same way that relationships between computer
systems are.  *People* are not able to program themselves ... it takes an
outsider to see it in most cases.  Then you can help them re-program
themselves. I do not see intelligent computers being much better.

Mind you, I have wished a million times that I could just use Cobol in the
clinic.  It would be 1000 times easier.

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37AE718D.729FB785@home.com>...
>First, it is a wrong assumption to say that Pete Dashwood is 'claiming'
>there will be a programmerless society. He is indicating, as a futurist,
…[123 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aedced@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard, you wrote this in reply to Rick...

>You don't do a lot of systems analysis and design do you. Your
>first wrong assumption is that the 'user' has much idea about
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hahahaha...<ROFL>!

You're right about the majority of users. That's why we need to remove the
tech layer and let them interact iteratively in a non-technical way, using
natural language. The challenge for us as techies is to be able to provide
such an environment.

I don't think you're being quite fair about MS-ACCESS. I use it all the time
to provide systems to corporations and small businesses, BUT...I access it
ONLY by ODBC using Standard SQL embedded in Fujitsu COBOL (MF would be just
as good in this area). In this way I see MS-ACCESS as purely a Relational
Database. If the user's requirement grows we move him onto ORACLE or INGRES
or (sometimes, if he has a mainframe...) DB2. In every case there is no need
to recompile the programs. The same DB structure is created in each
environment. Reports are produced using Crystal Reports (provided free with
Fujitsu COBOL) and Crystal will access any Relational DB. In my opinion,
anyone who uses MS-ACCESS macros (thus tying themselves to ACCESS) is simply
asking to have their systems re-developed unnecessarily....

I had to comment on this bit...

>A fundemental issue of computer systems is that they must map
>onto the working of the business.  Every business is _different_.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>in an effort to gain market share or change its market.

Yes they will change to try and attain competitive advantage, but the
fundamental processes will NOT change. When you say the systems must map
onto the working of the business, you are, of course, correct. But what
you're missing here is HOW they map. No matter how it strives for
competitive advantage, the business will ALWAYS require certain fundamental
processes (Buy/sell Transactions, CUSTOMERS, PRODUCTS and SERVICES, etc)
Without these, there IS no business. Furthermore, ALL present and future
businesses will require these processes (they are pre-requisite to the
definition of "business"). So, what I am arguing is this: "Businesses are
infinitely different in the way they attend to the detail of how they
operate, but at a CONCEPTUAL level, they are so similar that it is possible
to develop conceptual software which will provide a GENERAL SOLUTION. This
solution can then be refined by a user with no technical knowledge, through
iterative interaction with a smart operating system, in natural language."
It is (or certainly will be soon) therefore possible for a user to use a
computer without any need for a programmer as we understand the term.

Throughout my career I have always looked for general solutions to problems.
It gets very tedious developing the same old stuff every couple of years.
The logical extension of this is
a computer which has concepts built into it and an operating system smart
enough to select and refine the conceptual solutions.

>
>Thus every business is not only different from every other business
>but it is also different from what it used to be.
>
Nope. That's only true at the nitty gritty. (this is the view that most
technical people see (particularly programmers)). At the Board and Senior
Management levels where strategy is formulated, they see things at a
conceptual level and will tell you that there is very little difference
between them and their competitors. They will also tell you that "business
as usual" is the working maxim, so really very little actually changes. Of
course, many CEOs will try for new directions and diversity to ensure the
survival and growth of the company, but they are still doing the same
fundamental (conceptual) things...trading.

I have worked in hundreds of businesses (large and small) and every one of
them believes that they are unique and have special problems which no-one
else has. They're wrong (but of course I don't tell them so...<grin>).
Instead I pull a rabbit out of a hat and solve their problem. It is the same
rabbit I used a dozen times before for the same "unique"
problem...thankfully, when I first met the problem I went for a general
conceptual solution that could be used anywhere, rather than a one-off
solution specific to the particular instance. (I have a library of COBOL
techniques, methods, and solutions which I have built up over 30 years. It
is very rare to be confronted with a problem which cannot be alleviated or
solved by something in this bag of tricks. That's whay I'm persuaded that
businesses are basically the same.)


>As they haven't yet written code for every possible combination
>that is used today, let alone how it may be done in the
>future, I see no reduction in the numbers of programmers
>required.
>
So you advocate we should write one-off code for every variation?

What's the point of re-usable modules? Where do you stand on COM, CORBA, OO,
ACTIVE-X? If plug-and-play is OK for hardware, why not for software? (There
are infinite variations in the various hardware boards, but the concepts are
the same...Engineers have had the foresight to ensure that standards apply
at the conceptual level (a video board, sound card, etc.) NOT at the
component level.)

Can you never see the time when the system will have most of the knowledge,
will understand the concepts of the user's business, and will only need the
fine tuning from the user himself? I stand by what I've said all along:
"History will show "computer programming" was a phenomenon of the latter
half of the 20th Century"

Cheers,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ae4fad.607483725@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <7oj5kj$fun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ok0sl$dgg$1@news.hitter.net> <7okpai$si5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
On 8 Aug 1999 20:29:06 GMT, Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
wrote:

Tagging on your thread here, because we are on the same wavelength.  I
have been involved in several systems that had rudimentary - and I
mean simple - report generators.  Choose the fields you want, the sort
order you want the subtotals you want and run the report.  The end
users, with a few exceptions, would not do it.  They would call ME and
tell ME what they wanted, and I would go to the simple report
generator, fill in the blanks, save the report and let them run it as
they pleased.   This has changed little in the last 15 years.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37adac60@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
OK you finally did it...I had promised myself NOT to get involved in any
more COBOL forums after the collapse of the MF one, but I guess what I wrote
to Jimmy (originally as private correspondence) is controversial and the
lively responses show that I touched some nerves.

First off, I have been involved with COBOL for more than 30 years. I love
the language, run a
HELP section on my Web Site which attracts about 5 people a month (not a
lot, but enough to keep me interested). Some of the problems are fascinating
and I correspond with people from all over the world. There is quite a bit
more to my original letter than has been quoted here but Jimmy has certainly
passed the gist of it to you.

Now to specifics....


Rick Smith wrote in message <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net>...
>Some of the conclusions come from false assumptions.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>that does almost, but not quite, what the user wants.

No Rick, my assumption is NOT that the user wants to build a custom system
from components; my assumption (and I think it is axiomatic) is that the
user (you may remember I specified he was NON-TECHNICAL) wants a SOLUTION to
his problem. He will achieve this solution by interacting with the computer
in whatever his native language happens to be. He is not in the least
concerned whether the solution is component based or packaged or whatever.
All he sees is an iterative interaction which eventually arrives at the
required solution to his problem. Unlike a packaged solution (although,
beneath the covers a "package" starting point may well be involved, and I
mentioned this in connection with the invoice "template") he will get
EXACTLY what he requires.


>The reason that specialists ("programmers") will continue to
>exist is that the business owner, or other user, is far more
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>message.

That's exactly why "computer programming" as we understand it today will be
 shown to be a phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th century. The user
will tell the computer what he wants and the intelligent operating system
will use the "concepts" related to the solution and embodied in firmware, to
create exactly the right solution by "trial and error". I agree with you
that the user will have no interest  in or need to know anything about
computers; that's why there wont be "programmers" as we understand the
words.

Before 1980, when the PC was just a twinkle in the eye of some IBM
engineers, all of the data processing was done on mainframes (and a few old
calc machines which had not yet been replaced). A highly skilled specialist,
known as the "programmer", was needed in order to make the machine do
anything. We had a great time. It was a license to print money. IBM's profit
for one year exceeded the gross national product of New Zealand. I got rich,
so did many of my friends. Then they brought out the PC and suddenly this
exciting technology was within the reach of everybody and anybody.
Youngsters were buying books on a simple language for Beginners, called
"BASIC", and an obscure, spotty, computer nerd wrote a BASIC interpreter and
became the richest man in the world. IBM's profits declined drastically
as the mystique of programming slowly rolled away, in fact the company was
in severe danger within 6 years of the launch of the PC, which they
invented.

Since then the user interface has moved on and more and more people are
getting into making their desktop machines do what they want. As machines
become more "user friendly" more people will get friendly with them. I guess
if you look at what I originally wrote
it boils down to the ultimate "user-friendly" interface; natural language,
combined with "understanding" on the part of the machine. The nub of my
argument is that this is no longer just a dream of science fiction, it is
foreseeable by extrapolation from today's technology. I give it 15 years.
(That bit is certainly arguable, but my opinion is as good as anyone else's
<grin>).

I had to smile when I overheard a young friend telling her chum that she had
"programmed" her computer by changing the colours on the desktop. But is she
wrong? "Programming" is
becoming more and more about achieving a solution. I believe there is a
limited lifetime for ALL procedural languages, not just COBOL.

We desperately need to change the way we approach software development.
Hardware takes a quantum leap every 3 years, software is STILL being
developed the way it was 40 years ago, based on the Von Neumann sequential
model. (I'm NOT saying that procedural languages will not be part of the new
solution; many of the components which will be required can and will be
developed using existing software technology...it's all we've got<grin>)

>The second of these false assumptions is that computer
>programming will continue to be costly, labor intensive, and
>time-consuming.


If you think that this is a "false" assumption you haven't had budgetary
control of any software development projects recently...<G>  Perhaps the
assumption that it will continue to be so is arguable, but I reckon I'm on a
pretty safe bet. I've been in this industry long enough to watch
many Daniel's come along, blow their horns and lead us all off into the
Wilderness by promising "Faster, cheaper, easier, etc." software
development. After wandering around
in circles for a year or so, the Management decide to plug the hole and we
all go back to doing it in COBOL because it's relatively cheap and easy to
get people who can write it. "The LAST ONE" was followed by "The NEXT ONE",
JSP became synonomous with "Jolly Stupid Procedure" (ask anyone who "Michael
Jackson" is and they won't talk about structured programming....), and so
on... There is NO reason to believe that development using the existing
paradigm is going to get cheaper or faster.

Finally, as this is a COBOL forum, let me finish by quoting some of my
original correspondence to Jimmy which I have been unable to find in this
Newsgroup.
(Forgive me if you've seen it already...)

In response to Jimmy's idea for an "Open COBOL" compiler which would be...
"A COBOL compiler, written using object-orientation,  which is "Open",
that is, programmers and add-on tool developers,  have complete access
to the compiler's source to see, in COBOL syntax, how it handles COBOL
verbs/syntax, generates executable code and how it interacts with
specific operating systems. "

START QUOTE
Hmmm...who cares? Most of today's COBOL programmers don't even understand
the beginnings of what is generated. The days when we used to patch object
code are long gone. The skills of yesteryear (whether we like it or not) are
now irrelevant.

As for how it interacts with specific operating systems, again, who cares?
As long as it does. I have written COBOL on every major mainframe (IBM and
the BUNCH), at least 5 intermediate range machines, and PCs using everything
from DOS and OS/2 to NT. I have never worried about the operating system. My
code runs. I don't use vendor-specific constructs; the same code runs across
the range. Re-compile and away you go.  As far as COBOL compilers go...I
have worked on 2: 1 for Burroughs (remember them? they were the half of
Unisys that WASN'T Sperry...), the other for a very obscure CDC machine. In
the first case I was actually patching machine code and disassembling object
code to determine how the compiler (which had been written for Burroughs on
contract and for which they had no source and no-one capable of maintaining
it) worked. I then modified it and improved it. Eventually, I was getting
letters from Detroit Michigan asking me (in Auckland NZ) what pass 7 of
their compiler did, and could I please fix it to take magnetic input, etc.
All very interesting and exciting at the time, but in the long run a total
waste of time. Today you will find the B500 in museums or, very
occasionally, as a comms server somewhere. I wrote stuff like syntax
scanners which parsed using transition diagramming (see my article in Edmund
Arranga's "COBOL Report" last year), and JIT keyword extraction, and all
sorts of interesting stuff...for what? It had a useful life of around 5
years and it took a year to do.
END QUOTE

Jimmy said he was depressed by my response. That was not my intention. I
simply believe we have to be realistic and recognise when something has
served its term. COBOL programming is still significant in terms of today's
software development (and besides, it's fun, right?) but I would hope that
some of the bright young minds reading this Newsgroup may be stimulated to
look for other directions for the future. Try reading up on Heuristic
programming (I've written COBOL programs which find their way through
mazes...by trial and error), neural networking (not beyond the realms of
COBOL if you really want to do it), and COM compliant programming (I write
my own ACTIVE X (OCX) controls using Fujitsu COBOL and plug them into Visual
Basic and C++ applications...it's such a lovely feeling and it really upsets
the VB guys....<grin>).

The future belongs to smarter operating systems, component based objects
encapsulated in firmware (that's about as close as a computer can come to
having a "concept", at least at the moment), and, as always, the ingenuity
of humans to put it all together.

Regards,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7olbtb$193f$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7oikgs$2jbm$1@news.hitter.net> <37adac60@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```

peter dashwood wrote in message <37adac60@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
>OK you finally did it...I had promised myself NOT to get involved in any
>more COBOL forums after the collapse of the MF one, but I guess what I
wrote
>to Jimmy (originally as private correspondence) is controversial and the
>lively responses show that I touched some nerves.

Apparently, I touched one of your nerves, so let's blame
Jimmy for posting it! ;-)

[...]
>Now to specifics....
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>user (you may remember I specified he was NON-TECHNICAL) wants a SOLUTION
to
>his problem. He will achieve this solution by interacting with the computer
>in whatever his native language happens to be. He is not in the least
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>EXACTLY what he requires.

Perhaps the scenario, you gave, just didn't sit very well with me.
In futurist real terms, the scenario, as you described it, could not
happen. (Actually, the computer would recognize an invoice as
something associated with accounting systems, which would
cause the rest of the interaction to change. Mainly, the user
would not need to describe any but simple changes.)

Nonetheless, a user will still need a specialist to do some things
due to the user's lack of vocabulary. Using only common native
language, the user simply will not be able to find the correct word
that will cause the computer to recognize an instruction. In every
language there are words that do not translate well, or at all.
In every occupation there are words and phases for which there
are no substitutes. Whether the user wants to or not, they will have
to learn some jargon to get the computer to respond. They will
also have to teach the computer some jargon, as well.

Which leads me to those intelligent agents. What if a concept
does not exist? How will the user create an intelligent agent
without resorting to jargon? So, what we have is a non-technical
person attempting to describe how something can build itself?
And providing this agent with all the intelligence it needs to use
itself?

Obviously, I can't share your vision of the future, I don't
understand how some of the problems will be resolved.

>>The reason that specialists ("programmers") will continue to
>>exist is that the business owner, or other user, is far more
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>will use the "concepts" related to the solution and embodied in firmware,
to
>create exactly the right solution by "trial and error". I agree with you
>that the user will have no interest  in or need to know anything about
>computers; that's why there wont be "programmers" as we understand the
>words.

See my earlier questions about concepts.

You are agreeing with something I didn't say, so I will re-phase
my thoughts. The business user will have little interest in
learning how to use the computer to create solutions, that is
why there will be programmers in the future.

[...]
>if you look at what I originally wrote
>it boils down to the ultimate "user-friendly" interface; natural language,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
><grin>).

I believe that natural language, on computers, will continue
to be used in much the same manner as today, for those
who use it. Mainly for commands and dictation. At this point,
I cannot see that artificial intelligence will make any significant
advances. But that's my opinion.

>...  "Programming" is
>becoming more and more about achieving a solution. I believe there is a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>model. (I'm NOT saying that procedural languages will not be part of the
new
>solution; many of the components which will be required can and will be
>developed using existing software technology...it's all we've got<grin>)

Procedural languages will continue to exist for two simple
reasons. Procedures are the most natural, easily understood
form for giving instructions and "To the computer, everything
is an instruction." (There, I've quoted myself!)

Furthermore, the computer language closest to natural language
procedures, for the English language, is COBOL.

I wholeheartly agree about changing the approach to software
development. As currently done, programs contain too much
detail that must be entered by the programmer. As the amount
of detail is reduced, programmer productivity will increase.

As we use voice commands to build reports, data stores,
and screens, we can tell the system to link the various fields
as required. Whatever definition is used in the data store
can be used automatically wherever it is referenced. This
is very close to what program generators already do.

What I propose to add is the ability to enter process scripts
verbally. The process scripts are close enough to COBOL
that some fuzzy logic could make most, if not all connections
to data without programmer intervention. Furthermore, the
process scripts could be attached to various input and
output fields to provide data validation or calculations, for
example. Process scripts can also be attached to other
process scripts to simplify the scripts. The interfaces for
these scripts can be generated automatically. (Think of
process scripts as psuedocode modules.)

Since all data, reports, screens, and scripts are managed
by the development system, it can constantly check to
make sure that everything is defined and connected. When
complete, the system is built. Much of the detail and
obvious operations, such as MOVEs, would be done
automatically, and the programmer would not be concerned
about source files, classes, or code reuse.

Finally, the system would have an interactive graphic
capability to display all the interconnections between the
parts of the system; to highlight those parts that are
incomplete, during development; and to highlight those
parts that are affected by changes, during maintenance.

The technology to do these things is available today.

The biggest problem is getting existing code into the
system. Another is acceptance, some programmers need
to see everything and do not trust other computer programs
with their code.

Overall, I see productivity increases of from 2 to 10 times,
for new development, depending upon the previous level
of the programmer, and from 5 to 20 times for maintenance.

My guesses about productivity gains are just that; but I tried
to be reasonable. For those who doubt the concepts, think in
terms of PCB-CAD for the interactive graphics and linking and
storage of data about the parts of the system, CASE tools for
the automatic code generation, natural language translators
for process script translation to COBOL, and fuzzy logic from
search engines.

Of course, if I am correct, some companies will make a lot
of money, some companies will save a lot of money; but
I will likely see none of it.

>>The second of these false assumptions is that computer
>>programming will continue to be costly, labor intensive, and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>assumption that it will continue to be so is arguable, but I reckon I'm on
a
>pretty safe bet. ...

See above.

[...]
>Finally, as this is a COBOL forum, let me finish by quoting some of my
>original correspondence to Jimmy which I have been unable to find in this
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hmmm...who cares? ...

I did see it already; but did not respond since Jimmy has seen
some of my opinions already.

Most organizations will not accept "open" anything unless they
are assured of support. Therefore, most who would use such
a product would be students or experimenters. They would care.

Also, I am not particularly fond of OO; though if I were to write
a COBOL compiler, it would use COBOL source. My preferred
output would be code for a COBOL virtual machine (CVM) and
each target platform would have a CVM to machine code
compiler.

But I would more likely try building a COBOL compiler generator,
possibly, using something like Prolog for lexical and syntax analysis.
I just seems that it might be cleaner than lex and yacc.
[...]
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>`

```
I still haven't gotten around to responding to the "concepts" in the
original post, but I would like to point out that SOME of the readers of the
NG may have come across Micro Focus' facility called "vocabularies".  I
don't know if this is still getting enhanced - or even maintained, but when
I left MF, there were some who were trying to push this as the "natural
language scripting language" for future "end-users".  (My description - not
the official MF one).

In short what this facility did, was allow a programmer to come up with
TOTALLY NEW COBOL syntax to do whatever they wanted - and the compiler (or
run-time? or something?) used this to convert the "end-user vocabulary" into
OO methods - which in turn were run as "standard" COBOL.

For example, you could define (and I don't fully remember how), that you
wanted to be able to say,

  POST invoice-amount TO account-name YIELDING new-balance

in "any old" COBOL program.  Any the "conformance checker" would make
certain that you put the correct type of "fields/information" in
invoice-amount, account-name, and new-balance - and then invoke the correct
method/object.

When I presented this feature to one IBM mainframe group, they "shrank in
horror" at the maintenance ramifications of this "define-it-as-you-go"
syntax - but I think it is relevant to some of this discussion.  I would be
curious if any of the VisOC or NetExpress users have ever seen (much yet
used) this feature - or if any of the MERANT folk would comment on whether
this is still being distributed or enhanced.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

>I still haven't gotten around to responding to the "concepts" in the
>original post, but I would like to point out that SOME of the readers of
the
>NG may have come across Micro Focus' facility called "vocabularies".  I
>don't know if this is still getting enhanced - or even maintained, but when
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Bill, I've described more on what I mean by "concepts" in a response to Ken

into

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "M.Kobobel" <classicmek@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
The concept of "vocabularies" is interesting.

OO, and black-boxes are interesting.

CASE tools are interesting.

The problem I have with visualizing the operating system
with AI and "templates" built in is that in 25 years of
programming/analysis in a multitude of organizations I've
rarely met an end-user or manager that could articulate a
complete concept without direct human interaction.  They
don't know what questions to ask.  And even hardware has a
long way to go before it has the horse-power to have built
into it the capacity to anticipate all of the necessary
questions for any given environment.

Another problem is management.  They're victims of the NIH
syndrome, even at the executive level - they all know they
are absolutely unique.  And even if the
programmer(analyst/artist) they contract to provide their
solutions does use mostly off-the-shelf
components(paintbrushes) to put the solutions together, it
will be a unique solution.

Humans are tool users, but they almost always rely on the
services of expert tool users when they have a complex
problem to solve, or when they can't get a handle on the
complexity of the problem.  Systems in a medium to large
organization are every bit as complex as law and contracts
in a society, and I don't see many proposing that we could
do without lawyers, legislators, and judges - though the
idea of do-it-yourself legal systems is just as reasonable
and just as attainable as the idea of do-it-yourself
enterprise systems, maybe even more so.

To make do-it-yourself systems happen, we're going to have
to consolidate to ONE vendor and ONE platform for all
possible solutions.  Otherwise, there will always be a need
for the artist and craftsman to put all the pieces parts
together so that they satisfy the needs of every possible
organization.  That's the real job of the
programmer/analyst.  The pure coder may be expendable in the
long term, but the programmer/analyst is with us for the
long run - just like the lawyer and the accountant and the
physician.  The tools may change, but there will always be
the need for the expert tool user as long as the tools
exist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AF1D14.6706B916@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
M.Kobobel wrote:
> 
> The concept of "vocabularies" is interesting.
> OO, and black-boxes are interesting.
> CASE tools are interesting.
 
> To make do-it-yourself systems happen, we're going to have
> to consolidate to ONE vendor and ONE platform for all
> possible solutions. 

Agree with your general argument - but I don't see that the above is
necessary given an 'Open Source' approach to classes and recognizing
platforms. I want to retain competition - 'Apple' v Linux v Unix v
Windows v various mainframe approaches; and Bill Gates will love you -
most certainly NOT one vendor. (Consider the diversity in the English
language - COBOL is parallel).

> There will always be a need
> for the artist and craftsman to put all the pieces parts
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> exist.

Agreed - but the template approach helps achieve this. This is where I
diverge from Pete's "Verbal Templates". As anybody who has had to work
with boards of directors or the hordes of dumb bean-counters knows,
users are just not going to be able to put it together. (That's not
computer arrogance - the observation comes from real life frustration). 

There will always be a need, reduced in numbers, for specialists to
assemble the parts into a whole. And add templates, where the problem is
truly unique to a particular business.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

M.Kobobel wrote in message <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>...

>Humans are tool users, but they almost always rely on the
>services of expert tool users when they have a complex
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It's a lovely idea though, isn't it? Maybe we'll extend conceptual
processing to the legal profession, say, around 2030...

>To make do-it-yourself systems happen, we're going to have
>to consolidate to ONE vendor and ONE platform for all
>possible solutions.
No, not necessarily. We can have a Virtual Concept Machine which will run
conceptual software. It works for Java.

>That's the real job of the
>programmer/analyst.
Yes, agreed. And in order to do that job (s)he requires specialist training
and knowledge. If we remove the need for specialist knowledge ANYBODY could
instruct the computer. I take your earlier point about Managers not being
able to articulate what they want. That's why the interaction is ITERATIVE.
It works by trial-and error in a very short timescale. (See Jimmy's original
posting of my letter to him, where I describe such an interaction.)

>The pure coder may be expendable in the
>long term, but the programmer/analyst is with us for the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>exist.
Sorry, I don't agree. Your points about human tool use etc. are very well
made but there is still one little problem...the use of the word "expert".
60 years ago only an expert could work a printing press and publish
documents. Today everybody does it with their desktop publishing package.
The desire to publish documents overcame the barrier of skill
acquisition...it was intolerable to NOT be able to do it UNLESS you had the
requisite skill and facilities, so, another way to do it was found. The
people who today don't do it because they are not computer literate will
acquire the skill when their motivation is high enough. At the same time,
the level of skill required will be minimized with each successive release
of DTP software... See my arguments about the demand for a truly
"user-friendly" interface. In the same way the
task of computer programming will be de-skilled until anyone can do it. At
this point it will not be recognisable as the activity it is today.
BOTTOM LINE: People will not tolerate "expert" tool users too long. They
expect computers to have the expertise and they expect to interact with them
on the same terms with which they interact with other humans (within a
limited frame...I am not suggesting we will have philosopical discussions
with computers before the end of the next century.)


Thanks for your response. It was lucid, interesting, and thought-provoking.
I am sorry we do not agree but it really doesn't matter...the future is
inevitable and one of us will be proved right...(or not...) <grin>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

: Yes, agreed. And in order to do that job (s)he requires specialist training
: and knowledge. If we remove the need for specialist knowledge ANYBODY could
: instruct the computer. I take your earlier point about Managers not being
: able to articulate what they want. That's why the interaction is ITERATIVE.
