would know that you cannot get 'anybody' to know what is required.
In many cases the management actually have a completely false
idea about how the business actually works.  They have an idea
about how it should operate, but when that idea fails to acyually
work the staff just do what is required in spite of the management.


: It works by trial-and error in a very short timescale. (See Jimmy's original
: posting of my letter to him, where I describe such an interaction.)

Businesses can't afford error.  And how do you achieve 'short timescales'
Errors may not show up for some time.  For example if you forgot
to add tax then at the end of the year you will have to pay the IRD
but have no idea how much and have not collected it.

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <7oq20t$8ql$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...

>
>You haven't done much systems analysis have you.
>
Hahaha! Enough to know the difference between Applications Analysis (which
is what you're talking about) and Systems Analysis (which deals with
Operating Systems, Compilers, and general Computer analysis). In fact I've
done both. Over a period which is probably longer than you have lived on
this planet...<grin> Not only that, but I've been pretty successfull at it.
My phone doesn't stop ringing (sometimes I wish it would...) and these days
I work when I want to. How's your career going?

> If you had you
>would know that you cannot get 'anybody' to know what is required.
I see. So there isn't much point in attempting to analyse it then, is there?
I have no trouble sitting down with users and deciding what is required,
both within the management strategy and on the "shop floor". Not only that,
but if we get it wrong, we can change it. Quickly. It's called RAD (Rapid
Application Development) and involves iterating to a solution, based on the
assumption that nothing in this industry is ever right first time.


>In many cases the management actually have a completely false
>idea about how the business actually works.  They have an idea
>about how it should operate, but when that idea fails to acyually
>work the staff just do what is required in spite of the management.
>
Sounds heroic. I think you'll find that it is a combination of both staff
and management which is needed to get it right (or wrong). What's important
is having enough flexibility to correct inevitable mistakes whether made by
staff or management or both together.
>
>: It works by trial-and error in a very short timescale. (See Jimmy's
original
>: posting of my letter to him, where I describe such an interaction.)
>
>Businesses can't afford error.
Have you ever seen one that had none?

>And how do you achieve 'short timescales'
For that one you'll have to cross my palm with hard cash...

>Errors may not show up for some time.  For example if you forgot
>to add tax then at the end of the year you will have to pay the IRD
>but have no idea how much and have not collected it.
>
IF you "forgot" to add tax (and I accept you are only using this as an
example) and had "no idea" of how much, then your system had no audit trail
of payments and you should be fired. (In fact, in my company, you would
never have been allowed to implement it.) In my scenario, we would simply
visit the history of payments (audit trail) and calculate the tax payable,
in agreement with the IRD. We would then amend the system to include the
essential function we "forgot". No problem. Proper testing should reveal
errors, irrespective of the time cycle. (Cyclic testing is an essential part
of the testing program for applications which are cyclic by nature)
-

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

essential; business analysts are quite capable of running RAD workshops)
mainly because of the limitations of today's technology and the necessity to

peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

: Anyway it doesn't really matter what you call it, the fact is I've had a lot
: more experience at it than you have. 

Given that you accused me of being arrogant, on what actual evidence
do you base this - you have only been in computing a couple of
years more than I have.

: That's why they'll use smart computer systems to help them. The "skills and
: experience" you keep talking about are only pertinent in the computer field,

No, that is not true.  Systems were being designed for businesses
well before computers.  Immediately before computers they were
based around unit record equipment (been there, done that), before
that based around paper flow (my father designed systems based
on invoice 'registers' and pure paper flow when he worked for
Paragon).

: and then only so long as computers "need" programming. You are totally stuck
: in today's technology and don't even seem to have the imagination to see
: that it not only needs change, but it MUST change.

Computer systems and business systems are changing all the time -
this is one reason why I disagree with the idea of a 
'smart' computer system - it will only ever do what it has
already been programmed to do - this is a natural consequence
of computer systems.  When changes are required that are beyond
the current capabilities then programmers will be wheeled in to 
make it 'smarter' or to patch around it.


: Richard, it seems to me you come across as an elitist intellectual snob of
: the worst order. Convinced that without your special skills "ordinary"
: people (end users) would never be able to do anything connected with
: computers.

No, I am just experienced in knowing that most users have very
real skills in their own jobs - skills that I do not have -
and often do not have skills in areas of systems analysis and
design.  

I do not make the mistake of thinking that analysis and design
are equivalent to "anything connected with computers".  Nor
do I think that systems need have anything to do with computers
at all, I have done 'systems' work on designing warehousing
where the system was work flow and not a computer in sight.

As I have already stated, _some_ users have skills required to
specify all their requirements, but most have selling skills
or management skills or people skills, ones that I don't have.

: Thankfully, you have underestimated both the intelligence of "ordinary
: people", and the capability of hardware, software, and the next generation
: of programmers.

Not at all.  But I do recognise that there are different skills
and I do not expect everyone to have all skills.

:>Of course not, but trial and error as a deliberate policy is
:>foolish.i
: Hahaha! Tell that to Charles Darwin. Trial and error is the whole basis of
: natural selection and evolution.

Exactly.  Those who make the error get 'selected' away.
Those who don't make errors get to evolve.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "M.Kobobel" <classicmek@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oq454$dds$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
Peter,

Typesetting and publishing was always a specific skill set,
that could be fairly easily defined.  So are all of the
other general purpose applications that have replaced
hand-work labor.  What these tools have done is change the
requirements for the people that did the old work.

If you haven't already, check out a product called GURU from
a company called MDBS in Indiana.  It's been around for
about 12 years.  It's a natural language interface to
databases, though it doesn't yet respond the spoken word.
It has been used to build some pretty good but quite limited
AI applications too.

AI has been a dream for quite a long time now.  It turns out
that so far it doesn't look like we're going to be able to
get away from a need for the artist.  All of the research
over the last 20 years or so suggests that it is a lot more
complicated than we ever thought.

I can actually visualize a world where "black boxes" have
been built and made available on the market to handle
virtually any specific piece of a process in any given
hardware environment.  I can even visualize a system where a
lot of the "black boxes" have been linked  together to
produce a generalized application - even a system that would
collect black-boxes to put together a more specific system
based on questions that are asked of an end user using
key-word triggers like invoice, payroll, etc.  Some of this
is pretty generic, and like the QuickBooks example, might be
reasonably creatable by the end-user from generic black-box
architecture, given an adequate interface.  There's a lot of
pretty good packaged application software out there.  I can
see it being installed vanilla by an end-user given adequate
improvement to the interfaces for such tasks.  It is even
possible to imagine the end-users prettifying screens and
reports in this vanilla installation - DTP type activity -
customizing the appearance and presentation.

What I don't see is education and inclination changing so
that everybody is a generalist, with specific knowledge of
everything.  I don't see already highly-paid professionals
taking the time and effort necessary to do the iterations
that would produce a customized application that could be
used enterprise wide.  It is hard enough to get them to
commit the time to answer the questions that will let me as
the analyst work through the iterations that will produce
the customizations.

Here's a comparable question.  Does everybody at GM - even
every manager - know how to design, engineer, define the
production line,  and build a car, at the detail level?  Can
even every engineer know and execute all the details of each
of these processes?  Do you suppose they'd all get paid to
do it if they had an improved interface that would allow
them to specify each of the details iteratively?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
In article <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>, dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk 
says...
> 60 years ago only an expert could work a printing press and publish
> documents. Today everybody does it with their desktop publishing package.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "user-friendly" interface. In the same way the

Your comparison with DTP makes the point in my mind. Yes, I see that RAD 
tools will move much more toward your prediction of nirvana. Where I 
disagree is that specialists will just disappear.

Look at some of the mess people produce with their favorite 
wordprocessor. Many 'untrained' people compose documents with no sense 
of aesthetics or 'publication quality', let alone well-formed English 
sentences that roll into coherent paragraphs. Two of the four executives 
at a certain firm still type business correspondence in all upper case. 
They also hit return at the end of each line and line items up with the 
spacebar, even when using a proportional font. Sure they can write a 
letter, but I wouldn't put their work at the level of a professional 
writer/typesetter. Because they have the tool to do DTP, doesn't really 
make them publishers/typesetters/etc.

How many times has a manager _tried_ to explain a business need to a 
staff of developers and analysts. The staff look at the manager like he 
was a crack-head because either he (1) can't communicate his ideas 
clearly, (2) was incomplete in the specification or (3) was just 
incorrect in some fundamental point. The project goes on 
(unsuccessfully) for ever because of the original communication 
problems. If a person can't communicate to a group of the most powerful 
computers in the world to date (the human brain), how will they ever 
communicate with an application builder, no matter how 'user-friendly'? 

Look at all the bad Access applications that users have created. We gave 
people a tool who weren't really qualified to use it. So we have SSNs 
and phone numbers in char(255) fields with no validations and joins on 
name fields when SSN would have done fine (I just went through this last 
week). I agree an intelligent front end with a concept of real-world 
objects would keep SSN and phone number fields with the correct number 
of digits and groups. I'm not sure we can code a user-interface that can 
out-think someone with flawed logic.

I still believe the need for specialists will exist. Someone to take 
over when the user is: incapable of expressing themselves correctly; 
incapable of analyzing the problem; or unwilling to spend the resources 
to complete the task that would be taking away from their primary 
objective (job).

Instead of specialists disappearing, these new technologies will enhance 
the ability of the specialist to perform their job. Rather than grunt 
coders, they will focus on the more analytical portions and the code 
that the RAD can't generate or doesn't generate well (don't want to 
start a performance topic on 'alter vs go to vs perform'). Over time, 
the RADs will do a better job of turning some 'pseudo-English' into some 
form of code (hopefully COBOL :). If we reach this point, an analyst's 
productivity will increase to the level that maybe some coders at the 
bottom will not be needed.

Unless, of course, we eliminate all the people at the bottom of the 
'mental food chain' and replace the end-user with people who are already 
specialists in several areas. Maybe a manager who has a complete 
understanding of the business, has excellent analytical skills, can 
communicate the needs clearly, can use the DTP tools correctly including 
good writing skills (documentation you know) and has no other 
responsibilities so that they have the time to write systems. By the 
way, if anyone knows where to find this person let me know -- I could 
sure use the help!

ray
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b16d29@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news>`

```

Raymond Leech wrote in message ...
>In article <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>, dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk

>
>Your comparison with DTP makes the point in my mind. Yes, I see that RAD
>tools will move much more toward your prediction of nirvana. Where I
>disagree is that specialists will just disappear.
>
Hmmm...I never said there won't be specialists, just that computer
programming will/must change.

I read your response carefully, Ray, and the points are well made, but still
within the confines of today's technology. (You see COBOL code being
generated; I see conceptual objects being selected by an intelligent
operating system, in response to requirements specified by an untrained user
interacting iteratively with the system to achieve a solution. The technical
details or merits or demerits of this solution will be of no interest to
anybody (human).)

I believe that once conceptual computing is established the "expert"s will
become computer systems specifically intended for this task. As an
illustration...

Consider a heuristic program which finds its way through a maze. (I wrote
one of these in COBOL 15 years ago...they said it couldn't be done!).  Our
imaginary program modifies itself many times, based on trial and error.
Maybe 1,000,000 times a second. After 30 seconds it achieves a solution.
(Note that this may not be an OPTIMAL solution, but for many classes of
problem ANY solution is acceptable...). The audit trail of heuristic
modifications now contains around 30, 000, 000 entries. I contend that it is
impossible for a human being to "debug" this trail in order to understand
how the machine arrived at its conclusion. (If a man took 1 minute to check
and absorb each line, it would take around 57 years working day and night
non-stop to complete the entire trail. And what would you have learned at
the end of it? That computers are unimaginably fast ?... The only meaningful
way to analyse this data is with another computer. Maybe the other computer
actually monitors what the heuristic program is doing as it runs and spits
out events which show a significant or fundamental change in the current
heuristic, or startling departure from the expected parameteric constraints.

Extrapolate this onto my conceptual computing and you can see that it is
fairly likely that a specialised conceptual computer will be used to "keep
the machines honest". This same expert system would be used to diagnose and
report faults,  sub-optimal concept networking, and problems with the
heuristic and AI software. I guess it's kind of a "CyberPsychiatrist"..."Now
then, IBM1234, what difficulty are you having with the concept of a
CUSTOMER?" "Forgotten the best way to store his Name and Address? " "Here
let me refresh your memory..." (please forgive the dreadful pun...<G>)

So where does this leave our human "specialist"? In a category similar to
the computer hardware engineer of today. If it's broke, replace the board.
As you state, there will always be specialists...the question is what their
role will be.

A much more significant role than that of technologist will be consulting
and advising Business on how and what they should consider computerising.
This is true consultancy (giving people expert advice), as opposed to the
"consultant" tag that many contract programmers endow themselves with. (When
I was a contract programmer I insisted on being called an "insultant"
because I spent most of my time swearing at managers...)

I take your point about having expert tools doesn't make expert users. (You
cited the DTP analogy, and quite properly). However, one of the things we
find in RAD workshops is that even non-expert users can very quickly attain
a vision of what they want if they are properly stimulated and allowed to
interact with prototypes and each other. Most people can very quickly tell
you what they DON'T what (especially if they can see a prototype) and it is
a relatively short step from there to deduce what they DO want. (This is
never assumed; it is always checked by further interaction and prototyping).
 I agree wholeheartedly that many inept users will totally misuse DTP
packages (they'll probably do the same when conceptual computing becomes
available), but the important thing here is that they achieve a solution. It
really doesn't matter that it is technically inelegant, next time they'll do
it better. As programmers we are guilty of the same thing;  don't you always
try and make the next program you write better than the last one you wrote?
If it's not perfect so what? You debugged it, it runs, and the optimising
compiler ensures that the machine is not degraded too badly... Next time
you'll apply what you learned and do it better. I have been programming
computers since 1965 and I still operate on this principle...everything I
do, I try to make better than the last thing I did (don't always succeed,
but customer complaints are so rare (and always immediately actioned) that I
guess I must have achieved some level of competence...)

Regards,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B17D95.F160039E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news> <37b16d29@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood wrote:

> (Note that this may not be an OPTIMAL solution, but for many classes of
> problem ANY solution is acceptable...). 

That is an interesting statement.  I imagine we all know of cases where
the study for an OPTIMAL solution took so long that an earlier
acceptable solution would have been much more useful and cost
effective.  We certainly don't re-write our completed systems as soon as
we get it working, even though we probably could do them better then.

And OPTIMAL will change.  Bill Gates started his career by making a
small Basic which could work in a hobbyist machine.  He should have
waited until we were done developing computers and developed the perfect
language - provided he were immortal and willing to wait for eternity...

A major requirement of managers (and coaches, players, and techies) is
the ability to make a decision without waiting to be absolutely
positively sure about your choice.  It's too late then, your team is out
of the running.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B22F73.EDA7745E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news> <37b16d29@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
Thought Pete Dashwood and others might find the following of interest.
Quite amazing - when I copied the block from the original, I saw that
the author lives in Calgary ! :-

Quote :

I've been wanting to put together a document that addresses some of the 
issues relating to "The ability to solve problems using software 
engineering".  I've been in this industry for about ten years and have 
experienced many humbling lessons that are, I believe, quite unique to 
this field.  Building software is difficult.  Why?  I'll quickly try and 
describe some of the activities that we, as software engineers, 
designers, architects, developers, etc.., have been tagged to perform.  
We try to:

-understand the problem i.e. formulate (analysis)
-solve the problem (analysis/design)
-implement the solution (design/programming) 

I don't like using the popular *analysis & design* words because of the 
connotations and ambiguities that are attached by the software
industry.  
I'm also NOT advocating any specific methodology.  Back to the issues at 
hand.  The preceding enumerated steps appear very reasonable when we
look 
at them in abstract terms.  When we begin to factor and decompose each 
task, we only then begin to understand the amount of variability each 
step entails.  Let's look at each of the three tasks and look a little 
further into one major impact; translation.

-understand the problem i.e. formulate (analysis)
This is probably more difficult than most people think.  I believe we 
need to better understand the properties of human cognition to better 
understand the fallacies in our problem solving abilities.  Research in 
the area of concepts is key.  Not only the concepts themselves, but the 
structure of concepts.  Understanding problem structure from a meta 
perspective facilitates better solutions because it creates an ability
to 
formulate solutions using constraints.  We assume that because we speak 
the same natural language there is no translation in understanding.  Our 
internal representation of language is somewhat unique and emits a 
certain vagueness.  There are formal models of perception drawn from 
mathematics, but our industry doesn't study these models.  This problem 
is obviously not unique to software, but problem solving in general.  
Where we get into trouble is the number of translations that occur
before 
an end product is achieved.  In this stage, a translation occurs.

-solve the problem (analysis/design)
Now that we're convinced we understand the problem, we begin to
formulate 
a solution.  Our solution is comprised of a set of iterations involving 
problem decomposition, problem redefinition, modeling, documentation, 
etc..  Modeling the solution comprises the translation of a solution 
using a modeling tool.  The purpose of this task is again cognition. 
For 
the purposes of this discussion, let's assume a translation into the 
Unified Modeling Language.  It is obvious that again we lose a
percentage 
of the solution with a translation to UML because no formal language is 
as rich and dynamic as any natural language.  Our modeling (formal 
language) environments don't allow us to express what our language 
(natural language) environments allow.  At this stage, at least another 
translation occurs.

-implement the solution (design/programming) 
Now that we've understood and solved the problem, we now get to write 
code.  We could do many things at this stage to start our
implementation.  
We could take the design documents (UML) and begin to structure the
code.  
We could generate the code from the UML documents.  We could use the UML 
documents as references to our code.  No matter how we begin, many 
translations occur.  For the purpose of this example, it doesn't matter 
what programming language we choose.  Smalltalk, C++, Eiffel, Java,... 
the same types of translations occur.  No programming language supports 
the rich set of semantic constraints that are assumed in UML/OCL.  For 
example, a simple aggregate relationship.  None of these programming 
languages intrinsically support the strong semantics that describe an 
aggregate.  The only way this is achieved today is by protocol and 
census.  This has nothing to do with cognition.  Our programming 
environments don't allow us to express what our modeling environments 
allow.  At this stage, we typically lose a very large part of the 
solution.

Having briefly touched on the high-level software development process,
we 
aren't even beginning to delve into the more tangible impact problems.  
Even if we assume everything else is perfect, our solution structure has 
flaws.  Building software is difficult.

Let's list some variables that impact our ability to deliver.  Typical 
items are:

-communication
-resource quality
-change
-scope
-technology 
-domain 
-management
-budget
-etc., etc., etc..

I'm not going to waste your time and list more.  The point I am making
is 
simple.  There are too many variable that are exactly that; variables.  
Corporations set IT direction and say "...we're going to build this and 
that and this and...".  They fail to understand that building software
is 
not trivial and it needs more attention.  They fail to mitigate risk by 
using experiences of others.  I strongly believe in small, synergistic, 
development teams.  Hiring ten different contractors that have never 
worked together doesn't usually get it done.  You need to find teams
that 
have done it before.  Teams that can bring their experiences to you 
organization and minimize risk by eliminating some of the crucial 
variables.

I welcome and appreciate insights and comments.

End Quote :

And the NewsGroup is - Eiffel. Topic "Building Software is Incredibly
Diffcult" - there are at least 50 messages, which I haven't read.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Ok, let me try again (that iterative process). During my response, I

in our nirvana because of a failure to grasp the tasks, depth, breadth,

secretaries for dictation" and "...managers with coding knowledge"

writer, good analytical and logic skills and can code reasonably well. 
This person, with the 'concept engine' could probably eliminate most of 
our coding staff, given that there were enough time to dictate it all. 
I'd even bet his solutions would be fairly optimal to boot.

If this is the user of our future, maybe your prediction is achievable.

skills to the table, maybe I can go for that too. If we're talking users 
like today's 'end-users', I'd still have to say "No way".

> 
> I read your response carefully, Ray, and the points are well made, but still
> within the confines of today's technology. (You see COBOL code being
> generated; I see conceptual objects being selected by an intelligent

"...confines of today's technology" Well, to give you your due, that's

"You see COBOL..." -- What else would I see when posting in

COBOL, OO, P-code or Language-x. Anyone remember SoftCode? Draw a 
screen, provide some knowledge (validations, etc) and generate using

> "consultant" tag that many contract programmers endow themselves with. (When

Agreed! Temporary employee does not equal consultant.

>  available), but the important thing here is that they achieve a solution. It

I would change that to "achieve an acceptable solution". Again, speaking

system to sequentially search through one million records (rows) looking 
for a match they would have a "solution" but not necessarily an

Thanks for discussion
ray

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b3145c@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news> <37b16d29@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121bfeb779fa2821989691@news>`

```

Raymond Leech wrote in message ...
>In article <37b16d29@eeyore.callnetuk.com>, dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk
>says...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Excellent! Touche!

During my response, I
>visualized the 'user' in today's terms. I still see many people of the
>same skill-level in our future. I still believe typical users will fail
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I hear what you say. I think that MOTIVATION will be an important
factor. People who are apathetic, don't have time, or don't have interest,
certainly WON'T be the ones interacting with the concept engine. People who
are highly motivated to obtain a solution (like the small businessman who
needs an invoice, in my original scenario) will be the successful users.
Remember, both the user AND the system benefit from successfull interaction;
the user gets his solution; the system may get some of its concepts modifed
which will make it even more effective in future interactions.

>Take for example one of our salesman. He has more knowledge of the
>business than most of his clients. He is well articulated, a fluent
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah, 2 points here.
1. The "coding skills" won't help him (if anything they will hold him back
because he'll be forever wondering what the Hell this machine is
generating...[in fact it's not generating code as we understand it today;
all that will probably be in firmware]...it's actually selecting and
modifying conceptual objects to meet the needs it is eliciting from him.)
2. ..."enough time to dictate it all." Nope. Interaction is exactly that; 2
way,with questions from the machine then trials of what it thinks the user
wants, then corrections to the trials. Dictation would reduce the process to
a voice operated typing system.

Having said that, your description of the man above sounds like an ideal
user. (I bet he's an asset to your company...)

>If this is the user of our future, maybe your prediction is achievable.
>If we can include a collection of people who bring one or more of the
>skills to the table, maybe I can go for that too. If we're talking users
>like today's 'end-users', I'd still have to say "No way".
>
OK, that's very fair. Remember that today's end users do get involved in
system design with analysts and programmers. (Or, if they don't, they
certainly should...this comes down to "Waterfall" vs "RAD" development and
there is too much there to go into here. (I'm planning a book on this but
God knows when I'll get round to writing it. In the meantime we (my company
and myself) are spreading the word in corporations by means of
presentations, seminars, and pilot projects.) It is being very well received
and I personally believe the days of "Waterfall" development are numbered
(but then, I think the days of everything are numbered...<G>) Anyway, all I
would say is that end users are essential to the development process, even
using today's technology. (I know we get frustrated with them and they can
make our lives a misery, but at the end of the day, if it weren't for them,
we'd be in a different business.)


>I would change that to "achieve an acceptable solution". Again, speaking
>about misuse, if someone managed to get the concept engine to design a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, you're absolutely correct. What we would do is tell the engine the
search needed to be faster. We'd probably set limiting parameters as we do
with Service Level Agreements. The engine would then try various strategies
(trial and error) [adding, removing, changing, buffers and indexes] until an
acceptable (maybe even "optimal") solution was found. I would expect this
process to take not more than 5 or 10 minutes and it would be going on in
background while the machine was gathering further requirements. (I worked
as a DBA for a few years and I am quite positive there is nothing that we
did manually that couldn't be done by a smart machine, using an iterative
approach. The algorithms are well stablished. By the way, it would take a
human  (well, me, actually...) at least half a day to do the stuff described
above.

>Thanks for discussion
It's a pleasure. If people are stimulated by reading this thread I will
consider my time well spent. Even if they disagree. (As long as they do so
intelligently and courteously <G>)


Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B17B89.FFD57D29@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news>`

```
At one time a major part of lots of businesses was having someone write
down the spoken word of business people, either taken directly or from
recordings.  A good secretary could also fill in gaps.

But now, lower level managers have learned to type and have discovered
that there is an advantage of thinking at the speed of type instead of
at the speed of talk.  These lower level managers have been promoted and
many (most?) upper level managers do a fair amount of typing now. 
Dictaphones are virtually obsolete.

Who would have predicted that automation would have moved managers into
doing the more visible tasks of secretaries?

Managers also have learned to use spread sheets, and now are learning to
mine data warehouses.  I see programming changing to be more
behind-the-scenes preparing data for access by managers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b3145d@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37aedcef$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7on42t$gs0$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <37b068f7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <MPG.121acc8aefd1646c989690@news> <37B17B89.FFD57D29@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37B17B89.FFD57D29@NOSPAMhome.com>...

>Who would have predicted that automation would have moved managers into
>doing the more visible tasks of secretaries?
>

Er..well, actually, I did <grin> After seeing a demonstration of voice input
on a CYBER 70 computer in 1972. It took the entire mainframe resources of
what was then one of the World's most powerful computers (designed by the
legendary Seymour Cray). It was pretty obvious that this would remove the
need for secretaries in their capacity as stenographers. (We will never
remove the need for a pretty smile, a kind word, and the abilty to organise
our schedules and support our business activities - no, I didn't put this in
because my secretary might read it, and yes, I am old-fashioned enough to
not be entirely PC...). I guess it is a tribute to both CDC and IBM (who
both had similar software) that neither company released it until well into
the late 80s. I believe they realised the social consequences of doing it
earlier. (Cynics would say the hardware power wasn't available...you decide)

>Managers also have learned to use spread sheets, and now are learning to
>mine data warehouses.  I see programming changing to be more
>behind-the-scenes preparing data for access by managers.
Certainly in the short term. In the longer term managers will simply ask the
system for data and get examples of what is available, which they can then
refine by interacting with the concept engine.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7olrhd$8dc@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37AEF992.4006727B@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:37AEF992.4006727B@home.com...
> William M. Klein wrote:
> >
> > I still haven't gotten around to responding to the "concepts" in the
> > original post, but I would like to point out that SOME of the readers of
the
> > NG may have come across Micro Focus' facility called "vocabularies".  I
> > don't know if this is still getting enhanced - or even maintained, but
when
> > I left MF, there were some who were trying to push this as the "natural
> > language scripting language" for future "end-users".  (My description -
not
> > the official MF one).
> >
> > In short what this facility did, was allow a programmer to come up with
> > TOTALLY NEW COBOL syntax to do whatever they wanted - and the compiler
(or
> > run-time? or something?) used this to convert the "end-user vocabulary"
into
> > OO methods - which in turn were run as "standard" COBOL.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > invoice-amount, account-name, and new-balance - and then invoke the
correct
> > method/object.
> >
> > When I presented this feature to one IBM mainframe group, they "shrank
in
> > horror" at the maintenance ramifications of this "define-it-as-you-go"
> > syntax - but I think it is relevant to some of this discussion.  I would
be
> > curious if any of the VisOC or NetExpress users have ever seen (much yet
> > used) this feature - or if any of the MERANT folk would comment on
whether
> > this is still being distributed or enhanced.
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

I use it alot when using OLE having being very new to this in COBOL i can
create
verbs and source code that is easier to understand, hence:

       Method-id. "New".
       linkage section.
       01 window-id        object reference.
       01 eventMgr-id        object reference.
       procedure division using eventMgr-Id returning window-id
       invoked as == launch window <self> with <eventMgr-Id>
               giving [handle] [in] <window-id> ==.
           exit method.
       end method "New".

VisOC and Netexpress let one create methods like above, it is easier using:

           launch application excel giving handle in excelServer

than that in the procedure division detailed above.

Mainframe people have'nt got much to worry about its really this simple.

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** trblshtr@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7os3uo$bbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>`

```
In article <37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
> My correspondent would prefer not to get involved in NewsGroups - as
he
> says "I already spend 1.5 hours a day answering correspondence".
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>>>etc. etc. etc.
To all:
Decided to post a reply at this level, after having followed through
the thread(s), because given the numerous responses, thoughts, sub-
threads, etcetera it seemed like a logical place to begin.

The "dream" of a "thinking machine" that could "do what we needed done"
by simply "asking" has been "stored" in my "mind object" for such a
long period of time that I can no longer "retrieve" the initial
"source" of the concept.  Nevertheless, given the many thoughts,
arguments, laments, emotions, expressed throughout this thread (credits
to all on a general basis) I feel a need to respond:

I started chasing the dream over 30 years ago.  My quest has taken me
down many paths, many systems and platforms and languages and tools and
......  I still have the dream and I still believe that, one day, I
will find that thinking machine and all of lifes problems will be
solved and I will be able to live happily ever after.  I thought about
just "building" it myself - after all there are millions of objects out
there (including whole "applications") that I can use - but reality
tends to dictate....  Well, I imagine those of you who have contributed
to the thread ON ALL SIDES can recognise the futility in that (not to
mention the "insanity" that would result without SOME DEGREE of CONTROL
over that machine's "intellegence").

So....  Who cares?

I do.

As a software developer I am embarassed by the fact that system
failures and down-time and costs of computing and user-frustration and
the inability of the software industry to respond have become so
commonplace as to be the subject of derogatory "humor" in daily comic
strips, television and radio programs, tee-shirts, greeting cards, and
just about anything else.  Yet, the "dream merchants" with their
millions of marketing dollars selling the dream to the un(or is it
overly)informed would have us believe that that is not only OK but
desireable(at least for THEM).

I am ashamed when my friends, knowing what I do, and having just
experienced one more inexplicable system/program "hang" or "crash" and
the loss of another 'n' hours of work, look at me as though, somehow, I
should be held to blame.  Even though I didn't even know they HAD a
computer.  Of course the "dream merchants", if they can be found, are
ready, and even eager, to explain that "oh. no problem. that happens. i
can look at it for ...." and make everything all better.  Frequently,
with a sense of perverse humor, that is where I direct my friends to
seek their answers.

Now, frankly, I DON'T care what tool, language, object, component, or
WHATEVER is used to achieve the "thinking machine" if A) it works, B)
it is consistent in its working, C) the cost is justified given the
benefits derived, and D) it makes the end-user happy.  In the final
analysis it ALL still comes back to "Load, Store, Add, Subtract, Branch
on [optional condition]" and little "bits" (yes I do remember CORE
memory) that are either on or off at a given point in time.  You can
all argue or discuss all of the merits and limitations of all of the
various hardware approaches to providing that, and all of the various
software approaches to providing that, and all of the various
theoretical approaches to that, it hasn't really changed and it does
not appear as though it REALLY will be changing any time soon.

In the meanwhile, I think programmers will be necessary as the primary
pursuers of that final, real, ultimate, "thinking machine" that we all
so desperately need.

Now, if you will excuse me, I have to continue my quest and I believe I
may be seeing the tip of a windmill in the far distance.

:-)
J. Steele
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<934387236.13594.0.nnrp-03.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7os3uo$bbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Boy, what a long thread this is turning out to be.
"Thinking Machine" i'll go with that but not for many, years to come
People here are saying 10-15 years, no-way, look back over the last
30 years how much of software development has changed, not much!
it is still hard, and its getting harder, with "oo" I say under my breath,
well
harder I would'nt say but more complicated I would.
Microsoft own alot of the market place today Im sure everyone will agree
and i dont think they will let the market place turn into a non-technical
world
in the event that we will have "thinking machines" expert system yes, I've
developed
them my self, but this AI i dont.

I think in 50 years time we will be having this same conversation.

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b31460@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7os3uo$bbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <934387236.13594.0.nnrp-03.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

Simon R Hart wrote in message
<934387236.13594.0.nnrp-03.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>...
>
>Boy, what a long thread this is turning out to be.
Is that bad? Seems to me many people want to contribute... It started when
Jimmy Gavan asked me if he could publish some thoughts I wrote to him
privately. I guess Jimmy realised that it would spark some lively debate.

>"Thinking Machine" i'll go with that but not for many, years to come
>People here are saying 10-15 years, no-way, look back over the last
>30 years how much of software development has changed, not much!
>it is still hard, and its getting harder, with "oo" I say under my breath,
>well harder I would'nt say but more complicated I would.
It never was a "Thinking Machine". It was a concept engine. You need to
cover the whole thread, especially the early parts to see exactly what was
described. It was me who said 10 - 15 years, and I stand by it. As for
software development not changing in 30 years, that's exactly why it must. I
have covered this at length elsewhere. You are quite right there has been no
significant change, despite huge leaps in hardware development, it is
pathetic and sad that the software industry has not had the imagination or
will to do anything about it. As the use of computers moves to a wider base,
this situation will be considered intolerable, and, eventually something
will be done about it. The general public are not about to learn computer
programming as we understand it. Neither will they give up their computers.
The only answer is a truly user-friendly interface whch allows them to do
what they want with their machines. In industry the same applies. Networked
management means the empowerment of the workforce with various individuals
becoming responsible for all aspects of their department, INCLUDING the
computer systems. Again, these people are not going to learn computer
programming, neither will they be told to wait 6 months until a Programmer
can be assigned. The first vendor with a "smart" computer which can respond
interactively with an untrained (in computer tech) user, will clean up. Look
for it around 2018...



>Microsoft own alot of the market place today Im sure everyone will agree
>and i dont think they will let the market place turn into a non-technical
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>them my self, but this AI i dont.

No argument about MS being the market leader. So were IBM in the 60's. (See
elsewhere in this thread). IBM believed they could dictate to the Market
place...they nearly lost the company. There is no reason to believe that MS
won't do the same. It won't be "AI" in the Prolog sense...
>
>I think in 50 years time we will be having this same conversation.
>
If so, I'll be over 100. (Who knows..? There may be a medical forum
examining the future just as we are at this moment...replaceable organs,
bionic bits...frankly, I have no desire to live that long, but I bet if you
did a poll of 99 year olds you'd get a different answer <grin>.)


Pete
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b3145e@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7os3uo$bbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

trblshtr@my-deja.com wrote in message <7os3uo$bbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com>,
>  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>>

OK, I was that soldier. I got involved in the NG as I thought it was
important. Like you, I also care.
I read your entire posting several times. Have not quoted it as I don't
intend to respond to specifics in it
I liked what you wrote and found many sympathetic chords.
Two points:
1. Storage and instructions may be all there is to it, but quarks and
leptons are all there is to the Universe. The simplicity of the fundamentals
doesn't mean that infinite and almost unimaginable variety cannot be derived
from it.The fact that our brains have simple synapses, axons and neurons,
never prevented Shakespeare from completing his works or Beethoven from
writng his symphonies. Maybe if they knew how simple their brains were they
might have decided not to try... You are right that using today's technology
(especially the way we develop software) it is going to be very difficult to
achieve your dream (and mine, and, I suspect, many others...) of achieving a
"thinking" machine. What I have tried to do in this thread is suggest ways
in which we could do so. Data gathering, templates, objects, modules,
components, refined and reduced can lead to the idea of a software
"concept". Concepts are the basis of understanding. It isn't so difficult
(even with today's technology, even in good old COBOL) to program a very
simple concept (see my postings elsewhere in this thread for some examples).
OK, it doesn't have "understanding" but put some goal orientation and some
heuristic programming into it, allow it to modify it's own methods and add
new methods and properties to itself, and who knows?

2. Don't be ashamed or embarrassed about what you do, no matter what flak
you get from friends.  (By the same token, it is not good to be arrogant
about it either; "Well, speaking as a computer programmer...." gets straight
up my nose and I hate to think what it does to the general public. I
overhead this at a party one night and the person it was directed at
responded..." Computer Programmer! That's just a Space Age clerk!" I nearly
choked on my drink from laughing, but the lofty nerd concerned had it
coming. ) This industry is not perfect. That's not your fault, seems to me
you have perpetually done your best. There are many reasons things are not
right (including the ones you mentioned). I see a bright new generation of
people who will make things better. Hopefully, some of them are reading this
and thinking about it....

Finally, I promise when we eventually get the first concept engine, if it's
developed by my company, we'll call it "Dulcinea" in honour of all the Don
Quixotes like yourself who kept the faith and followed the dream...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares ?

- **From:** trblshtr@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p427k$pun$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37AB13D5.7AAECD6C@home.com> <7os3uo$bbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b3145e@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
In article <37b3145e@eeyore.callnetuk.com>,
  "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:
>>cutcutcut>>>>
still a nice dream.

> I see a bright new generation of
> people who will make things better. Hopefully, some of them are
reading this
> and thinking about it....
Ahh, to be able to see that (still).  Instead what I keep seeing is
more and more "loss" of the understanding of how the basic "underlying
mind" functions.  It will not be the fault of that "bright new
generation of people" if they don't eventually achieve the goal.
Rather, it will be the fault of those who failed to provide them with
the necessary foundation while dumping millions of "intellegences" into
their laps and saying "look, its easy...just put the ones you need in
this box....".  Can they achieve something? Certainly. Was it
worthwhile? Probably. Is it better than what came before? In some ways.
But, (and several have stated it in different ways) in the end its
somewhat like saying "anyone can be a xxxxx". I can create a decent
will, or simple contract --- doesn't make me a lawyer. I can diagnose
and treat various illnesses --- doesn't make me a doctor. I can keep a
reasonable set of books --- doesn't make me an accountant...... The
tools are all there to do those things too.  And there are more of them
every day.  All of those professions are "evolving" too.  But, is it in
a good (notice I didn't say 'right') direction?

I fear that when we lose sight of the foundation (or let go of it) in
the interest of speeding evolution we risk, also, the loss of some
necessary controls.  Those that prevent the dream from becoming a
chaotic nightmare. Given the natural speed of evolution within the
computer industry (as opposed for example to legal or medical
professions) we are far ahead in our ability to teach the young and
brightest that what came before has no meaning, that as long as they
can continue to put a bright face on things (regardless of the merits
or lack thereof of the particular "thing") the rest of the populace
will follow and that that, in itself, is justification for the
evolution.

By the time anyone realizes that something is inherently wrong and that
chaos is more than just approaching the knowledge which will be needed
to "fix" the "problem" will have been lost.

In short (sorry I got a bit long-winded)...
>
> Finally, I promise when we eventually get the first concept engine,
if it's
> developed by my company, we'll call it "Dulcinea" in honour of all
the Don
> Quixotes like yourself who kept the faith and followed the dream...

...where will your company find the people with the knowledge to
understand that there is much, much more to the creation of the concept
engine than painting pretty screens and printing a few things?

Oh!  And thanks Mr. Dashwood (and all those who have spoken) for the
thread.  Food for thought is always a helpful item to have around while
following the quest. Never know when I might need to banish another
misconception.

J.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

+> heart of Texas", with Darlene of course.
+
+Not saying he's not in the USA, just identifying my biases. Which
+holiday is 8/11?
+
Thane Hubbell's birthday, of course. Also my ex-wife's birthday. Also my 
parents' wedding anniversary. Take your pick.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: the 11th of August 2008 w9.9sq9.45wo

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AEFC6E.8F1D1F0C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<9.459.93.64> <19990805202000.21937.qmail@uni.nowhere.to> <37aa43d9.342271495@news1.ibm.net> <37AA71AA.F40DEF76@att.net> <37AB513A.3C3C23A6@home.com> <U6Wq3.39$9X.2542@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37aca066.497059412@news1.ibm.net> <7oicor$9m1@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37AEF205.FB2@platinum.brooks.af.mil>`

```
Charles Hall wrote:
> 
> I seem to recall that one or two of the original 13 colonies actually
> declared independence before what we now call the Declaration of
> Independence was approved.  Wasn't there also a short-lived Republic of
> California?

I don't think so.  There was, but few people believed California would
remain a separate country.  

e.g. Texas
> > > Yes.  But it's the only state with actual experience being a separate
> > > country.  Things could change.
> > >
> >
> > You better not say that very loud - on your next visit to Hawaii !!!

The only state with a royal palace on it.

It is confusing when we call our components "states".  The term relates
back to when they were hoping that our federation would be like the
League of Nations, with independent states as members.   The Unites
States is a state.  Our states really don't fit the original
definition.  This didn't stop other countries such as Mexico from using
the same term even after it was obvious it didn't really apply.  But
this was done for the same reason people use the word "president".  One
copies terms of the successful - it is easier than copying the important
stuff!
```

<a id="t-e0aced607e"></a>
## Thread: Using COMREG to get date — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Using COMREG to get date

- **From:** "Tim Mueller" <tmiller16@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aad36d@news2.one.net>`

```
I'm experimenting with the code that Bob Botsis posted on using COMREG to
get the system date and other info, but so far, I'm getting garbage.
Following is the code I'm using to just retrieve and display the info.  Can
anyone tell me what I'm doing wrong?


000210 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000220
000230 01  PAGE-ZERO.
000240     03                           PIC X(20).
000250     03  POINT-COMREG             USAGE POINTER.
000260
000270 01  WS-CURRENT-DATE              PIC X(11).
000280 01  WS-VSE-JOB-NAME              PIC X(8).
000290 01  WS-CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE       PIC X(8).
000300 01  WS-CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-CC    PIC XX.
000310 01  WS-PARTITION-ID              PIC XX.
000320 01  WS-PROGRAM-NAME              PIC X(8).
000330
000340 LINKAGE SECTION.
000350
000360 01  COMREG.
000370     03  CURRENT-DATE.
000380         05  CURRENT-DATE-MM PIC XX.
000390         05  CURRENT-DATE-S1 PIC X.
000400         05  CURRENT-DATE-DD PIC XX.
000410         05  CURRENT-DATE-S2 PIC X.
000420         05  CURRENT-DATE-YY PIC XX.
000430         05  CURRENT-DATE-S3 PIC X.
000440         05  CURRENT-DATE-CC PIC XX.
000450     03                      PIC X(13).
000460     03  VSE-JOB-NAME        PIC X(8).
000470     03                      PIC X(47).
000480     03  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE.
000490         05  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-MM PIC XX.
000500         05  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-DD PIC XX.
000510         05  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-YY PIC XX.
000520         05  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-DDD PIC XXX.
000530     03                      PIC X(12).
000540     03  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-CC PIC XX.
000550           03                      PIC X(110).
000560           03  PARTITION-ID        PIC XX.
000570           03                      PIC XX.
000580           03  PROGRAM-NAME        PIC X(8).
000590
000600 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000610 MAINLINE.
000620          SET ADDRESS OF COMREG TO POINT-COMREG.
000630          MOVE  CURRENT-DATE TO WS-CURRENT-DATE
000640          MOVE  VSE-JOB-NAME TO WS-VSE-JOB-NAME
000650          MOVE  CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE TO WS-CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE
000660          MOVE CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-CC TO WS-CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-CC
000670          MOVE  PARTITION-ID TO WS-PARTITION-ID
000680          MOVE  PROGRAM-NAME TO WS-PROGRAM-NAME
000690
000700          DISPLAY WS-CURRENT-DATE
000710          DISPLAY WS-VSE-JOB-NAME
000720          DISPLAY WS-CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE
000730          DISPLAY WS-CURRENT-SYSTEM-DATE-CC
000740          DISPLAY WS-PARTITION-ID
000750          DISPLAY WS-PROGRAM-NAME
000760        STOP RUN
```

#### ↳ Re: Using COMREG to get date

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aae07c$1$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<37aad36d@news2.one.net>`

```
In <37aad36d@news2.one.net>, on 06 Aug 1999 at 08:26, "Tim Mueller"
<tmiller16@yahoo.com> said:

>I'm experimenting with the code that Bob Botsis posted on using COMREG to
>get the system date and other info, but so far, I'm getting garbage.
>Following is the code I'm using to just retrieve and display the info. 
>Can anyone tell me what I'm doing wrong?

I have no VSE system to run a test, but I would try to move these lines
from the Working-Storage to the Linkage Section:

 000230 01  PAGE-ZERO.
 000240     03                           PIC X(20).
 000250     03  POINT-COMREG             USAGE POINTER.

and perhaps add

         SET Address of Page-Zero to NULL

to the top of the Proc Div.

Gilbert Saint-flour
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Using COMREG to get date

- **From:** clastname@nospam.com (Charles Haatvedt Jr.)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using COMREG to get date

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ad927d$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

In <MPG.1216bb001f7ee6a79896a3@news>, on 08 Aug 1999 at 03:52,
clastname@nospam.com (Charles Haatvedt Jr.) said:

>I am interested in retrieving the comreg area in a Cobol II ver 4 program
> in an OS/390 environment. I think that I can follow the examples 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>me to it.

There is no Communication Region (COMREG) in MVS.  What kind of
information are you trying to retrieve?  Date? JOB name? UPSI? 

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

the cited author.]

In article <37ad927d$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>, gsf@ibm.net says...
> In <MPG.1216bb001f7ee6a79896a3@news>, on 08 Aug 1999 at 03:52,
> clastname@nospam.com (Charles Haatvedt Jr.) said:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

	At my prior employer they had an assembler routine which would move 
information from "comreg" to a 224 byte variable passed to the routine. 
It used to get to the comreg area by loading a register from absolute 
address decimal 20. Then this register was used as the "from" location in 
an MVC to move 224 bytes to the variable passed to the routine. 

This module could be called from either Cobol 74 or Cobol II. At the time 
it was an MVS environment...

My current environment is actually OS/390   

===>>>  what I am specifically looking for is the layout for that 224 
byte area.  I am planning on using the jobname, jobstep, procstep, lpar, 
system date, system time fields. I would like to put the layout into a 
copybook member. Our shop still uses Panvalet, so I would use a ++INCLUDE 
member. We are moving to Endevor, but it is a slow process.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using COMREG to get date

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aed4a0$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<37aad36d@news2.one.net> <37aae07c$1$tfs$mr2ice@news> <MPG.1216bb001f7ee6a79896a3@news> <37ad927d$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <MPG.1217f9527a83ef3a9896bb@news.rdc1.wa.home.com>`

```
In <MPG.1217f9527a83ef3a9896bb@news.rdc1.wa.home.com>, on 09 Aug 1999 at
02:30, clastname@nospam.com (Charles Haatvedt Jr.) said:

> At my prior employer they had an assembler routine which would move 
>information from "comreg" to a 224 byte variable passed to the routine. 
>..... This module could be called from either Cobol 74 or Cobol II. 
>At the time it was an MVS environment...

Again, there is no such thing as a "communication region" in MVS or
OS/390.  Your prior employer may have converted from VSE some time before
and used a comreg simulation routine in MVS, but this would have been
managed at the application level, not by the operating system itself.  

What makes you think your _current_ employer has comreg simulation
routines on their OS/390 system?

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

```
Randall,

The suspense is killing me - let's get it over with..

You can come clean about JEOPARDY - we have no idea. And as to
your clue about the Jacksons - all I can think of is that Michael has
had more 'face jobs' than Phyllis Diller.

Now if you don't get it with additional clues, start unpacking. Don't
even contemplate that move to California and a 12th miserable attempt to
become a Jeopardy contestant.

Thought our English friend would have followed up, once he got one of
the actor's names.

Answer :

   (a)	Movie # 1 - Actor A produced/directed (?).
   (b)	Movie # 2 - non-event; Actor B - Stewart Granger(James Stewart)
      	She was married to him for short while.
   (c)	Movie # 3 - Actor B (Stewart Granger) played her lover and
	Actor C her father.
   (d)	Movie # 4 - Actor D produced/directed (?) and Actors A and C
	were in it.
   (e)	TV - she starred with Actor D in a TV version of
	a stage play which had been made into a movie.

Q. Who is she ?
 
Clues :

(a)	She is still alive; about my age.
(b)	Movie # 3 - popular theme in last Oscars
(c) 	Movie # 4 - another actor in the movie was a former US
	ambassador
(d)	One of our actors uttered immortal line "There will be no
	mutiny on my ship Mr. ......"
(e)	For you Randall - He said to our actress "Get thee to a Nunnery"
	A pox on you sir, if you don't get this one.
(f)	Come on you Americanos - The play, movie (Spencer Tracy was in 	
	it), and TV version - famous American play. Could say Charles
	Darwin was being put on trial
(g)	For Ken Foskey - re Richard Chamberlain - our actress was also 
	in this where you 'acquired' a gorgeous English Rose.
(h) 	One actor, with a speech disability recently said, "Thank you
	for this ......, and I'm proud of my sons".
(i)	Deborah Kerr was in Movie #3 - but for political reasons 
	Stewart Granger's film brother didn't like Stewart making passes 
	at Deborah.
(j)	Named his production company after his Mom.
(k)	One actor, (and a great one, I might add), has done it all -
 	two-piece suits, horse-operas and even put on a skirt for period 
	pieces.
(l)	With the exception of Stewart Granger, whom I suppose you could 
	call Second League, the other three were definitely First
	League.

With that number of clues - it's got to be a giveaway.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Does anyone have a list of modules needed to distribute FSVIEW.EXE from
MicroFocus...I have been unable to get a list of modules from their
answerlab support function...

Seems their response is to install the NE application server modules
supplied on the NE3 CD-ROM...What a waste...They supply no mechanism of
keeping these modules updated with fixpacks, so I would have to distribute
out of date modules...

I prefer to distribute only the required modules on my install diskette(s)
directly from the current development environment directories...Seems safer
and saves installation time, etc...

Any assistance would be appreciated...Someone out here must know what
modules are required...

use the NE app server installation from the CD-ROM? If so, how do you keep
from abending with bad modules that are not updated each time MF provides a
fixpack?

KenMullins
```

<a id="t-c882f5805b"></a>
## Thread: object properties in NetExpress — 2 msgs

### object properties in NetExpress

- **From:** "Martin" <hnetkong@post.cz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37aa8458.0@news.cvut.cz>`

```
Hi all,
I need help with accessing object properties (especially from MSWord through
OLE). I found in help, that I have to create new method returning property.
Is there any other simple posibility? I also need to access objects under
object word.application (for example word.application.selection).

Martin


P.S.: My friend made his webpage, where's just counter and picture "under
construction". I want to make him happy, so can you make some hits for him,
please? http://emil.altec.cz (counter counts only one hit from one ip)
```

#### ↳ Re: object properties in NetExpress

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oeca7$4hc$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37aa8458.0@news.cvut.cz>`

```
"Martin" <hnetkong@post.cz> wrote:
>Hi all,
>I need help with accessing object properties (especially from MSWord through
>OLE). I found in help, that I have to create new method returning property.
>Is there any other simple posibility? I also need to access objects under
>object word.application (for example word.application.selection).

Hi Martin,
OLE requires all properties to be accessed via methods.

To get any properties, you must invoke the method on the appropriate 
object, prefixing the method name with a "get" (and "set" to set the 
property value). 
If the property is another Word object, the COBOL type needs to be an 
object reference.
For example:
01 theSelectionObject object reference.
..
invoke wordApp "getSelection" returning theSelectionObject
*> Change the text in the selection
invoke theSelectionObject "setText" using z"New text"
*> We have finished with the selection object
invoke theSelectionObject "finalize" returning theSelectionObject

COBOL can't concatentate method calls as VB can (yet...), but the 
underlying number of method invocations to Word (including what the 
finalize does) is the same. You just have to be a little more explicit.

Other differences are that VB syntax "hides" the default method sometimes, 
so when you see syntax such as Selection.Paragraphs(1).Range.Select, this 
is read as:
01 theParagraphsCollection object reference.
01 theParagraphObject object reference.
01 theRangeObject object reference.
..
invoke theSelectionObject "getParagraphs" returning 
theParagraphsCollection
*> (1) means invoke the default "Item" method with the item required
invoke theParagraphsCollection "Item" using by value 1 returning 
theParagraphObject
*> Get the range object
invoke theParagraphObject "getRange" returning the theRangeObject
*> Select the range
invoke theRangeObject "Select"
*> Finished with the objects
invoke theRangeObject "finalize" returning theRangeObject
invoke theParagraphObject "finalize" returning theParagraphObject
invoke theParagraphsCollection "finalize" returning 
theParagraphsCollection.

Again, COBOL is doing the same as the VB code above, but more explicitly.

Steve.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9CDq3.67$tJ2.2088@client>`

```
Check out this free utility download for COBOL.  It's called COBOL Record
Sizer.   You copy any COBOL record layout to the clipboard and this
application will calculate "the size in bytes" of the (largest) record
found.

I would have thought that you would need to insure the "POINT-COMREG" was at
least initialized to low values.  Isn't the comreg decimal 20 bytes off of
low core?


snip

000210 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000220
000230 01  PAGE-ZERO.
000240     03                           PIC X(20).
000250     03  POINT-COMREG             USAGE POINTER.
000260

snip

000340 LINKAGE SECTION.
000350
000360 01  COMREG.
000370     03  CURRENT-DATE.
000380         05  CURRENT-DATE-MM PIC XX.

snip

000600 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000610 MAINLINE.
000620          SET ADDRESS OF COMREG TO POINT-COMREG.
000630          MOVE  CURRENT-DATE TO WS-CURRENT-DATE

snip

 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

## Thread: COMREG & COBOL 74 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COMREG & COBOL 74

- **From:** "Tim Mueller" <tmiller16@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ab2298@news2.one.net>`

```
I have been experimenting with using the COMREG to obtain the system date
and other system info and it is working very well using COBOL for VSE.
However, we have many COBOL 74 programs that need to access the same info,
but the following statements/instructions are fatal in '74:

    03    POINT-COMREG        USAGE POINTER.

    SET ADDRESS OF PAGE-ZERO TO NULL

    SET ADDRESS OF COMREG TO POINT-COMREG

Is it possible to access COMREG in COBOL 74?  How?

Thanks.


Tim Mueller
Hamilton County Department of Human Services
```
