#### ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`

```
John Romanowski wrote:
>Can anyone lend a hand? How do I set up an array ? How do I move input
>data to this array?

In 'COBOLspeak' array=table.  Here is a post I recently made on the
subject.  Perhaps you will find it helpful.

Think of a table (any item which contains an OCCURS clause) to be several
copies of that item, all with the same name.  Because they all have the
same name, you must have another way to differentiate between them.  This
is done by appending a 'subscript' (or 'index', see below) to references
to the item.  The term 'subscript' is taken from math, where variables of
the same name but different incarnations are indicated by subscripts.

   a    = (a  + (n / a )) / 2
    i+1     i         i

A subscript is always an integer that indicates which one of the items
of the same name are being referenced.  Since computer languages (with
the notable exception of APL) are usually written using plain text,
true subscript fonts are not available, so COBOL uses parenthesis (C
and Pascal use square brackets) to designate the subscript.  You can
think of the table as a row of boxes, each with the same name, and the
subscript designates which of the boxes is being referenced.  In this
example:

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...

   MOVE MY-ITEM(subscript) TO ...

The value of (subscript) determines which of the 20 MY-ITEM elements is
being referenced.  'subscript' can be a numeric literal, a numeric data
item, a numeric data item +/- a numeric literal, or an index, or an
index +/- a numeric literal.  It must always evaluate to an integer
between 1 and the limit in the OCCURS clause (20 in this example).
Indexes are a special type of subscript.  Indexes are associated with,
and must be used only with, a particular table, and are defined by the
INDEXED BY clause on that table:

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...

This creates an index named MY-INDEX associated with the table MY-ITEM.
The specific internal format of indexes is left to the implementor, but
the general idea is that an index can be in a form other than an integer
representing the 'occurrence number' of the entry in the table.  It is
essentially a construct for permitting a form which can be manipulated
more efficiently by the CPU.  The details vary by implementation, and
are not important here.  There are a few differences in the way indexes
are used, as opposed to subscripts.  One is that you must use the SET
verb to change the contents of an index.  Instead of

   MOVE 1 TO MY-INDEX
   ADD 1 TO MY-INDEX
   SUBTRACT 2 FROM MY-INDEX

you would use, respectively

   SET MY-INDEX TO 1
   SET MY-INDEX UP BY 1
   SET MY-INDEX DOWN BY 2

You can also set and change the value of an index by using the VARYING
clause of the PERFORM or SEARCH statements.

   PERFORM ... VARYING MY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MY-INDEX > 20

If you declare a table with an index you can also use the SEARCH verb
to search the table.

   SET MY-INDEX TO 1.
   SEARCH MY-ITEM
      AT END ...
      WHEN MY-ITEM(MY-INDEX) = ...

If your table is completely populated and the values are in ascending or
descending order, you can use the SEARCH ALL syntax (see ASCENDING and
DESCENDING clauses of OCCURS), which may do a non-linear search (such as
binary search) of the table, resulting in much faster execution.  You
also do not have to set the initial value for the index before executing
SEARCH ALL.

Note that when you reference an individual table element, you must use
a subscript or index to indicate which element you refer to.  But when
you reference the table as a whole, such as in the SEARCH statement,
you do not use a subscript or index.

The power and usefulness of tables and subscripting is best understood
in the context of loops.  It is the use of these two concepts together
which permits writing one set of code which can operate on a number of
different elements.  Consider a program that tested some sort of code
to be valid, and there were 500 different codes.  Without the use of
subscripts, you would probably need to individually code 500 IF tests,
or 500 EVALUATE ... WHEN clauses.  What if there were 5000 different
codes?  What about 50,000?  With a table, you can load all the values
into the table, either through VALUE clauses in Working Storage if the
table is static, or from a data file.  Then just use a short SEARCH
statement or loop to test the codes against the values in the table.

If your table has subordinate entries like this

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...
       05  MY-SUB-ITEM-1 ...
       05  MY-SUB-ITEM-2 ...

Then each of the subordinate items MY-SUB-ITEM-1 and MY-SUB-ITEM-2 are
referenced using subscripts or indexes in exactly the same way as the
item MY-ITEM.  The difference is that you may not reference any of the
subordinate items as the object of the SEARCH verb.  You can reference
subordinate items in the WHEN clauses of a SEARCH.

Two dimensional tables merely carry subscripting one step farther.

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...
       05  MY-ITEM-A
       05  MY-ITEM-B  OCCURS 10 TIMES ...

Each of the 20 copies of MY-ITEM contains one MY-ITEM-A and 10 copies
of MY-ITEM-B.  There are 200 total copies of MY-ITEM-B in the entire
structure.  A reference to MY-ITEM or MY-ITEM-A is resolved by the use
of one subscript.  But if you reference MY-ITEM-B, you must use two
subscripts, the first to designate which of the 20 MY-ITEMS, and a
second to designate which of the MY-ITEM-B entries is intended within
that MY-ITEM.  Both subscripts are enclosed in the same parenthesis,
optionally separated by a comma.

   MY-ITEM-B(14, 6)
or
   MY-ITEM-B(14 6)

This references the 6th MY-ITEM-B, inside the 14th MY-ITEM.  You can
think of this as a grid of boxes, with the first subscript indicating
the row and the second subscript indicating the column.

Additional levels of subscripting merely carry this concept farther.

   03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES ...
       05  MY-ITEM-A ...
       05  MY-ITEM-B  OCCURS 10 TIMES ...
           05  MY-ITEM-B1  OCCURS 5 TIMES ...
           05  MY-ITEM-B2 ...

   MY-ITEM-B1(14, 6, 3)

References 3rd MY-ITEM-B1, in 6th MY-ITEM-B, in 14th MY-ITEM.  You can
think of this as a 3D rectangle of boxes, with the first subscript
indicating the row, the second subscript indicating the column, and the
third subscript representing the level.  There are 20 * 10 * 5 = 1000
total copies of MY-ITEM-B1 in the structure, and 20 * 10 = 200 copies
of MY-ITEM-B2.

Two dimensional tables are used occasionally, three dimensional tables
are pretty rare, and you almost never see four or more dimensions.  I
won't even attempt to describe a method for visualizing a table with
more than three dimensions. ;-)  Thane Hubbell describes an interesting
way in chapter 12 of his book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", from
Sams.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> If you declare a table with an index you can also use the SEARCH verb
> to search the table.

That is the primary reason for using indices these days.  (at one time,
indices were much more efficient than subscripts - back when machine
efficiency was more valuable than programmer efficiency).  If you aren't
using the SEARCH verb, you will probably pick whichever seems more
natural to you - indices or subscripts.

I was a physics major and subscripts were always more natural for me.  I
also debug programs, and it's much easier to display the value of a
subscript than of a index.  Also the commands used to set and increment
subscripts are the natural English which Cobol is striving for.

My main point though is to realize that these are two different, but
similar ways to access elements of a table.  Beginners sometimes find
these confusing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > If you declare a table with an index you can also use the SEARCH verb
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I was a physics major

Hi, colleague!

> and subscripts were always more natural for me.  I
> also debug programs, and it's much easier to display the value of a
> subscript than of a index.  Also the commands used to set and increment
> subscripts are the natural English which Cobol is striving for.

Right. Subscripts are easier to manipulate, and SEARCH is no big deal. How
many lines of code does it take to program a linear search? Assuming that
there are 100 items to search and an extra, dummy item at the top of the
table (a must for efficiency to eliminate the extra comparison at the bottom
of the loop needed with VARYING),

   MOVE 0 TO SUB
   MOVE KEY TO TABLE(101)             
   PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)
      ADD 1 TO SUB   
   END-PERFORM
   IF SUB < 101 MOVE 'Y' TO FOUND                      

SEARCH ALL is no big deal, either. How many lines does it take to code
binary search? 

   MOVE   1 TO LO
   MOVE 100 TO HI
   PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL LO > HI OR FOUND 
      COMPUTE MID = (LO + HI) * .5
      IF KEY < TABLE(MID) COMPUTE HI = MID - 1
      ELSE
      IF KEY > TABLE(MID) COMPUTE LO = MID + 1    
      ELSE 
         MOVE MID TO SUB-FOUND
         MOVE 'Y' TO FOUND
      END-IF
   END-PERFORM 

Better still, subscripts offer other possibilities. If the keys are
uniformly distributed, this can be transformed into interpolation search by
computing an interpolation point instead of the midpoint (runs in O(loglogN)
time instead of O(logN) for binary). Or how about organizing a key-indexed
(if the keys fall in a limited integer range) or hash table (any keys)
instead of a sorted array? Then neither sorting nor deduping is needed, plus
any search is done in at most 2 comparisons, hit or miss, regardless of the
number of keys. O(1) time, that is.   
 
> My main point though is to realize that these are two different, but
> similar ways to access elements of a table.  Beginners sometimes find
> these confusing.

Agreed. At the current COBOL level, indices are there who cares? The
language is flexible and optimized enough, so that subscripts can be used
for programmatic implementations of sortless search schemes like hashing
next to which the revered SEARCH ALL is nothing but a resource hog.      

Kind regards,
====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
====================
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>

- **Message-ID:** `<7oqvp9$gru$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia> <7oqbkm$hgh$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Pete Withey <peterw@acucorp.com> wrote:
: Judson:

: Your example below will run forever or until you get a runtime error if the
: condition cannot be satisfied.  Correctly I think you want:

:      PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)  OR SUB > 100
: END-PERFORM

Actually no because element 101 has been preset to be a match (as long
as field sizes are correct) so the PERFORM will always terminate.

However if you are going to check the boundry you should always
do it first:

       PERFORM
           VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL SUB > 100
                OR Table(SUB) = Key

           CONTINUE
       END-PERFORM

This way the boundry is checked _before_ the subscript is used.
This is safe even if there are only 100 elements due to Cobol
short-cutting the condition.



:> >   MOVE 0 TO SUB
:> >   MOVE KEY TO TABLE(101)
:> >   PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)
:> >      ADD 1 TO SUB
:> >   END-PERFORM
:> >   IF SUB < 101 MOVE 'Y' TO FOUND
:>
:>     PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)
:>     END-PERFORM

A
A
A
A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B0DFAC.F6654CD8@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

Judson,

By asking "How many lines of code...?" I did not mean the challenge of
coding linear search using as few lines as possible, just wanted to say "not
many". Your code excerpt, as Pete Withey has noted, will loop in the case of
unsuccessful search. That is why Pete added OR SUB > 100 condition:

PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB) OR SUB > 100
END-PERFORM

But this is exactly what most programmers think is the right way to do a
serial search, whilst, in fact, it is inefficient. Note that this code makes
2 comparisons at every iteration of the loop. The code I gave (see above)
makes just 1 comparison. That's the entire reason for allocating a dummy
101th bucket and moving KEY there beforehand: It serves as a sentinel
allowing to eliminate the check SUB > 100 (or SUB = 100 if you code WITH
TEST AFTER). The subsequent logic is natural: If the loop stops at 101, the
search is unsuccessful, else it is a hit with SUB pointing to the node where
the right key resides.

Because of one less comparison, it will run, on the average, about 30
percent faster than the code you offered (with the change Pete has made), if
the number of items exceeds 5 in the case of a hit and 7 in the case of a
miss (tested; also see D. E. Knuth, The Art of Computer Programming, v.3.).

The above is a widely known general principle of speeding up inner loops of
high-performance algorithms: Instead of checking for a subscript still being
within the array bounds at each iteration (i.e. SUB > 100), place sentinels
next to the bounds and save the comparison. In sequential search, it is not
a very big deal since the algorithm is slow no matter whether it runs, 100
seconds or 70 seconds, say, - binary search will run 2 seconds, hashing will
do it in 0.5. But if you do something like SUB > 100 in the inner loop of
quicksort, expect its performance to plummet 2 or more
times.                  

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8_gs3.4902$lC5.45446@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia> <37B0DFAC.F6654CD8@earthlink.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>the case of unsuccessful search.

Neither does it test for found condition.  What I assumed would be clear
(sorry it wasn't), was that the move of the key to entry 101 of the array,
and the test for found condition, would be retained, like this:

    MOVE KEY TO TABLE(101)
    PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)
    END-PERFORM
    IF SUB < 101 MOVE 'Y' TO FOUND

I didn't redundantly list those lines, because their need should be
obvious to anyone able to comprehend the discussion.  Sorry I wasn't
clear. :-)

But there is are other issues here.  Some hardware platforms support
a hardware search instruction.  Those are usually only generated
in COBOL when the SEARCH verb is used.  Even for short searches, a
hard-wired search would trounce any PERFORM loop.  Generally, if the
table is large enough that using your method (which I first used in
the late 60's) makes a real difference, it is also large enough that
keeping the table in sequence and using a SEARCH ALL to generate a
binary (or hardware) search is preferable.  Unless the table is
volatile, maintaining a table in sequence is usually not that big a
deal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B10723.C19C2380@att.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>     END-PERFORM

Thanks, Judson. I would recommend that people, newbies especially, not
use the former construct. I think your example is better coded with the
"with test after" clause, so the PERFORM ends with the subscript
positioned at the table entry which was equal, e.g.:

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING SUB FROM +1 BY +1
	UNTIL KEY  =  TABLE(SUB)
END-PERFORM

The following could be added between the PERFORM & END-PERFORM:

	IF KEY  =  TABLE(SUB)
		SET FOUND-VALUE TO TRUE
	END-IF

Assuming that the "FOUND" field has an 88-level called "FOUND-VALUE" for
"Y", etc.

Bill Lynch
```

> > >   MOVE 0 TO SUB
> > >   MOVE KEY TO TABLE(101)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >     END-PERFORM

This is a piece of code which is dieing to have a comment in it,
preferably with the string 101 in it (or the variable containing the
value 101).  e.g.

*** PRIME THE END OF TABLE ELEMENT 101 WITH A HIT TO AVOID OVERFLOW
     MOVE KEY TO TABLE (101)
     PERFORM...

and again
**** IF SUB = 101 SEARCH WAS UNSUCCESSFUL
     IF SUB < 101 PERFORM XXXXX

(This has to be part of your search routine)

Think about some entry level maintenance programmer who changes the
table size.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zuks3.386$sM4.16206@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia> <37B10723.C19C2380@att.net> <37B175C3.D6CFEED9@NOSPAMhome.com> <7os4qe$mrl$2@news.igs.net>`

```
Instead of "priming" the end of the table with a "trigger" value, mabe you
could just let the system handle the element count using the LENGTH OF
operator, like...

01 Foo-Table.
    05  Foo    PIC whatever  OCCURS asmanyasneeded.

MOVE +1 TO SUB
MOVE +0 TO CURRENT-SIZE
PERFORM  UNTIL FOUND OR CURRENT-SIZE >= LENGTH OF FOO

    (check for the condition, exit if needed)

  ADD +1 TO SUB END-ADD
  ADD LENGTH OF FOO(1) TO CURRENT-SIZE END-ADD
END-PERFORM

I'm sure with a little work someone can clean this up and make it more
efficient, but this kind of approach would eliminate the need to worry about
someone not changing a "MAX-ELEMENTS" number or changing the "primer" value
whenever a table size is changed.

Just a thought...

MCM

>>*** PRIME THE END OF TABLE ELEMENT 101 WITH A HIT TO AVOID OVERFLOW
>>     MOVE KEY TO TABLE (101)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>table size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oslsj$h65@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia> <37B10723.C19C2380@att.net> <37B175C3.D6CFEED9@NOSPAMhome.com> <7os4qe$mrl$2@news.igs.net> <zuks3.386$sM4.16206@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
EXTENSION ALERT!!!

 "length of"

is an extension to the COBOL Standard and may OR MAY NOT be available on
your compiler.  (I think that IBM introduced it first - Micro Focus and
Realia followed - and I don't know who else has it.)

Use
  "FUNCTION LENGTH"

if you want portable code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<r0ls3.50$0K.4265@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In article <zuks3.386$sM4.16206@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>Instead of "priming" the end of the table with a "trigger" value, mabe you
>could just let the system handle the element count using the LENGTH OF
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

[snippage]

>Just a thought...
>

... and one which, in my opinion, should *stay* just a thought.  Leaving
aside the overhead of the adds and calculations it reeks, to my uneducated
nostrils, of 'Gee, Mom, Look!  I'm a Programmer!' code, of an unnecessary
using of functions and features because... They're Really Neat!

Yes, they *are* Really Neat.. and they're not the kind of Neatness that is
best to expose a two-year maintenance coder to at 3:AM.  *My* way of
dealing with this kind of situation (which, of course, is the Best Way,
bare *none*) is to initialise the table to HIGH-VALUES *and* keep track of
the number of table entries so that the code reads:

PERFORM EXIT-PARAGRAPH VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
 UNTIL THIS-TBL-ENTRY (SUB1) = WS-ENTRY
       OR SUB1 > NO-OF-TBL-ENTRIES
       OR THIS-TBL-ENTRY (SUB1) = HIGH-VALUES.

IF THIS-TBL-ENTRY (SUB1) = WS-ENTRY
    PERFORMED ITEM-MATCHED THRU IM-EX
ELSE
    PERFORM ITEM-NOT-MATCHED THRU IMN-EX.


If this can be improved upon and made to demonstrate a better balance
between elegance of execution and ease of maintainability I'd be
interested in seeing same.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia> <37B10723.C19C2380@att.net> <37B175C3.D6CFEED9@NOSPAMhome.com> <7os4qe$mrl$2@news.igs.net> <zuks3.386$sM4.16206@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37B2554B.F573CC75@att.net>`

I'm starting to think I should have never made this "contribution."

What prompted this in the first place was a post in which it was suggested
that the end of the table (that is, the physical end) be filled with a
"trigger" value to be tested, specifically to avoid searching beyond the end
of the table when the table size had been modified in the working-storage
section and the modifying entity (no doubt a junior programmer) had
forgotten to also modify references to the number of table elements in other
portions of the program.

Now you - and I - would never be so careless as to do this (or we would use
a MAX-TABLE-ELEMENTS constant in WS), but  for the specific circumstances
posited, I offered an idea.

BTW, while responses have noted the incredible complexity of using an
accumulated size counter and comparing it to the length of the table, and/or
the non-pure-ANSI-ness of "LENGTH OF" versus "FUNCTION LENGTH,"  NO ONE
REPORTED THE INCREDIBLY STUPID ERROR I MADE IN THE SUGGESTED CODE.

(I leave that discovery as an exercise).

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
William Lynch wrote in message <37B2554B.F573CC75@att.net>...
>Why, oh, why are you incrementing SUB instead of PERFORMing VARYING ...?
>And I don't see the advantage to accumulating the length of entries
>instead of the no. of entries.
```

Agreed, but my point is the state of SUB when the PERFORM is terminated.
Without "with test after" SUB will have been incremented and AFAIK we

clause I can count on the contents of SUB (this may be a mainframe/other
issue - don't know, all my experience is IBM mainframe).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fozs3.6443$Co3.66167@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <qU1s3.3405$bB2.34509@news2.mia> <37B10723.C19C2380@att.net> <Kihs3.4973$lC5.45608@news3.mia> <37B25627.503E00D7@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>issue - don't know, all my experience is IBM mainframe).


There should be *no* difference between the two, at all, in this
example.  The COBOL 85 standard says on page VI-113:

  "If the TEST AFTER phrase is specified, the PERFORM statement
  functions as if the TEST BEFORE phrase were specified except that
  the condition is tested after the specified set of statements has
  been executed."

In other words, the PERFORM statement logic looks like this:
Before logic (see COBOL 85 standard page VI-114, Figure 1):

      <Set initial condition>
    Loop:
      <Execute TEST BEFORE Logic>
      <Execute specified statements>
      <Execute VARYING Logic>
      <Go to Loop>
    Exit:

After logic (COBOL 85 standard page VI-116, Figure 3):

      <Set initial condition>
    Loop:
      <Execute specified statements>
      <Execute TEST AFTER Logic>
      <Execute VARYING Logic>
      <Go to Loop>
    Exit:

For each of the above PERFORM statements this will result in:

  PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)
  END-PERFORM

      <MOVE 1 TO SUB>
    Loop:
      <IF KEY = TABLE(SUB) GO TO Exit>
      <Nothing>
      <ADD 1 TO SUB>
      <Go to Loop>
    Exit:

  PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING SUB FROM +1 BY +1
    UNTIL KEY = TABLE(SUB)
  END-PERFORM

      <MOVE 1 TO SUB>
    Loop:
      <Nothing>
      <IF KEY = TABLE(SUB) GO TO Exit>
      <ADD 1 TO SUB>
      <Go to Loop>
    Exit:

Since nither of these PERFORM constructs has any object statements,
it makes no difference if the end test logic occurs before or after
'Nothing'. :-)  There will be absolutely no difference in execution,
or result, of either syntax.  If your compiler gives you different
results, it is broken. ;-)

Of course, if you ADD 1 TO SUB inside the PERFORM, instead of using
VARYING, then there would be a difference.  Perhaps that is what
you were thinking of?
```

William M. Klein wrote:
>William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Other than that, I thought that the results were identical.

The point to remember is that BEFORE and AFTER refer to before and after
the user code.  In other words, both are tested within the same loop,
with the same VARYING value, as the performed user code.
```

Paul's made?  

"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
> binary search?
>    MOVE   1 TO LO
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

am I missing something? If you could search in loglogN time a table with 1
million entries would be searched as fast as one with 1 billion since
loglogN is about 4.5 for both, right? Anyone ever heard of this
"interpolation search"?   

> time instead of O(logN) for binary). Or how about organizing a key-indexed
> (if the keys fall in a limited integer range) or hash table (any keys)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now, anyone heard of these? What do you mean by "neither sorting nor
deduping" and "regardless of the number of keys"? The statement that an

keys sounds pretty far fetched.    

> 
> Agreed. At the current COBOL level, indices are there who cares? The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Again, I've never thought of SEARCH ALL being a "resource hog". Nor have I

tabel or hashing) beat it. Anyone (but Paul) has other thoughts on the
subject?  
  
Best, Jacob

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B485EF.9BB99299@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <37AED2FE.45B8AC9F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B087D6.4FBACC0C@earthlink.net> <37B3134C.A8F1BD38@hotmail.com>`

```
Jacob Elgart wrote:
> 
> To branch the thread away from serial search, how about the other points
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> "interpolation search"?

Well, you've been taught wrong. No _comparison-based_ method can do better
than binary search in the general case. In the case when keys are
distributed evenly throughout their range, interpolation search does much
better (you are correct in your estimates of loglogN), can be and is used to
improve performance. When you are looking a word up in a dictionary you do
not start in the middle searching for "COBOL". Instead, you intuitively
estimate how far the word is from the beginning and start searching there.
"C" is about 12 percent from the beginning of the alphabet, so you open the
book about 12 percent from the beginning. Likewise, searching for "SAS", you
would probably start closer to the end. This heuristic process can be
simulated using a simple proportion. That is, you would replace computing a
midpoint by computing an interpolation point leaving the rest of the logic
intact:

    MOVE   1 TO LO
    MOVE 100 TO HI
    PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL LO > HI
                               OR FOUND
                               OR OUT-OF-RANGE
       COMPUTE INT-POINT = FUNCTION INT 
          (1+LO+(HI-LO)*(KEY-TABLE(LO)) /
          (TABLE(HI)-TABLE(LO)+1))
       IF INT-POINT > HI OR INT-POINT < LO 
          MOVE 'Y' TO OUT-OF-RANGE
       ELSE                                  
       IF KEY < TABLE(INT-POINT) COMPUTE HI = INT-POINT - 1
       ELSE
       IF KEY > TABLE(INT-POINT) COMPUTE LO = INT-POINT + 1
       ELSE
          MOVE INT-POINT TO SUB-FOUND
          MOVE 'Y' TO FOUND
       END-IF
    END-PERFORM       

+1 in the denominator is only to prevent zero-division without moving the
interpolation point far off target. If the keys are distributed uniformly in
a table with N=1,000,000 this code makes at most 5 iterations to either find
or reject the search key. Compare it with binary search (20 iterations).
Because a proportion is used to estimate the interpolation point, the
process can be badly fooled by grossly unevenly spread keys, so one should
exercise caution.  

> 
> > time instead of O(logN) for binary). Or how about organizing a key-indexed
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> keys sounds pretty far fetched.

Not at all. Any search based on address calculation (as opposed to
comparisons between keys) has this property. If a company has 50,000
employees each identified by a 5-digit non-negative integer ID, the entire
universe of possible keys is covered by the 0:999999 range. Suppose you have
10,000 IDs as keys to look up. You can sort them, load a table, and do
binary or interpolation search. However, you can also create a table with
100,000  entries and load it moving the satellite information accompanying
each key to the bucket whose number is equal to the value of the key:

IF TABLE (KEY) > SPACE MOVE SAT-INFO TO TABLE (KEY)

After that, searching is trivial: 

IF TABLE (SEARCH-KEY) > SPACE 
   MOVE 'Y' TO FOUND
   MOVE TABLE (SEARCH-KEY) TO SAT-INFO-OUT
END-IF
  
As the entire search amounts to a single array reference, the search time is
the same, no matter how many keys have actually been loaded. Note also that
the incoming lookup keys do not require any sorting, and unduplication
occurs automatically with SAT-INFO corresponding to the first instance of a
repeating key retained. If you want the last instance then load the
key-indexed table simply as

MOVE SAT-INFO TO TABLE (KEY)

I hope this illustrates the idea. Note that no comparisons between the
search key and the lookup keys are made. Of course, it works great only if
the keys are integers falling into a limited range, as in the situation with
IDs above. Hashing expands the idea onto keys of any nature and any range by
allowing short serial searches in the final stage of the algorithm. But the
fundamental properties remain intact: no sorting and/or deduping is needed,
and the operations INSERT, SEARCH, DELETE, RETRIEVE, UPDATE all occur in
O(1) time.
          
> >
> > Agreed. At the current COBOL level, indices are there who cares? The
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> subject?

Sorry for getting in the conversation uninvited. Compared to key-indexing
(when applicable) and hashing it _is_ a hog. COBOL is a general programming
language, and there is no reason for not using hashing methods in it. In
fact, certain COBOL aspects (chiefly, its fast access to all bytes of an
alpha field) make it ideally suited for hash implementations. Lastly, if
you've never heard of something, it does not mean it exists not. I bet it is
a hash table your COBOL compiler uses to look up words in the symbol table -
one reason it is fast.   

Kindest regards,
====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
====================
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia...
>
> In 'COBOLspeak' array=table.  Here is a post I recently made on the
> subject.  Perhaps you will find it helpful.

> >    03  MY-ITEM   OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY MY-INDEX ...
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> verb to change the contents of an index.  Instead of

    Very nice.

    My biggest complaint with indexes is the fact that they do not allow my
usual tactic for looping, ie set the subscipt to zero before starting the
loop, add 1 at top of loop, check for at end, blah blah blah, go to top of
loop.

    Putting the check for at end and add at end of loop works okay, but that
method is not as well broken in (ie like a shoe).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B2C8B7.B6927E04@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <7otd74$53u$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:

>     My biggest complaint with indexes is the fact that they do not allow my
> usual tactic for looping, ie set the subscipt to zero before starting the
> loop, add 1 at top of loop, check for at end, blah blah blah, go to top of
> loop.

I've seen this implemented different ways.  I have seen at least one
compiler allow me to set an index to zero, and more than one allow me to
set it to one then down by 1 before starting my loop.

But that's ugly.  

And it's uglier to have to create a working-storage element to set the
index value to in order to display an index in debugging.
> 
>     Putting the check for at end and add at end of loop works okay, but that
> method is not as well broken in (ie like a shoe).

I agree that subscripts are natural and fit comfortably.
```

allowed in the next Standard.  (Actually, it is a little more complex than

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: JCL question (PROCLIB)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b2df79$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <7otd74$53u$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37B2C8B7.B6927E04@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B2D7AB.73A0E8D4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In <37B2D7AB.73A0E8D4@NOSPAMhome.com>, on 12 Aug 1999 at 08:18, Howard
Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> said:

>I know how to use a PROCLIB statement to point to defined PROCLIBs.  What
>is the easiest way (other than making the proc in-line), of testing a
>proc in one of my own libraries?

The PROCLIB statement is NOT a standard MVS JCL statement, but a feature
of some optional vendor program products which is not available in most
MVS installations.  The JCLLIB statement was introduced around 1991 in
MVS/ESA 4.1 and is what anyone can use to test JCL procedures.  Here is an
example:

  //JOHNDOE1 JOB (acct#),'John DOE', CLASS=A,MSGCLASS=X
  // JCLLIB ORDER=(JOHNDOE.PROCLIB,JANEDOE.PROCLIB)
  //  EXEC PROC=MYPROC2,DATE=19990808

 Gilbert Saint-flour
 http://members.home.com/gsf/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: JCL question (PROCLIB)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B2E704.4DE23275@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <rOkr3.6850$on6.110031@news2.mia> <7otd74$53u$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37B2C8B7.B6927E04@NOSPAMhome.com> <37B2D7AB.73A0E8D4@NOSPAMhome.com> <37b2df79$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
I never knew it was an extension.   I learned JCL before 1991 (my
documentation is also older than that) - but the JCLLIB seems to work
fine, thanks!

…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>  http://members.home.com/gsf/
```

#### ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YZ3r3.501$9X.28144@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`

```
See the chapter in your manual on "Tables."

"Array" is a BASIC-language term; "Table" describes the same thing in COBOL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oimpr$5tr@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <YZ3r3.501$9X.28144@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:YZ3r3.501$9X.28144@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> See the chapter in your manual on "Tables."
>
> "Array" is a BASIC-language term; "Table" describes the same thing in
COBOL.
>

Just before others get into this discussion, "array" is a common term in
MANY programming languages (not just BASIC) - but the point about seeing
your documentation on "TABLES" is certainly correct (for COBOL).  If your
documentation doesn't include anything about "tables" (and that would
surprise me), see your documentation for the OCCURS clause - with or without
"INDEXED BY".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Array use...

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AD2AEE.EA0A82FC@att.net>`
- **References:** `<qH%q3.2495$rL2.1905@news.rdc1.mi.home.com> <YZ3r3.501$9X.28144@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> See the chapter in your manual on "Tables."
> 
> "Array" is a BASIC-language term; "Table" describes the same thing in COBOL.

"Array" was used in Fortran many years before BASIC (at least AFAIK). I
don't think it's tied to a specific language.

Bill Lynch
```

<a id="t-e6d34937a3"></a>
## Thread: Arrays use part2... — 6 msgs

### Arrays use part2...

- **From:** "John Romanowski" <jromanow@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Co6r3.2818$rL2.1907@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`

```
Hi again...
I know...I know...We are not here to check anybody's code but I'm finding
the concept of arrays a bit difficult. But I just need someone to toss me a
bone or throw me into the right direction. I'm trying to read 10 temperature
into an array and print them on seperate lines. Please, can someone help?



       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. PROGRAM4.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

          SELECT TEMP-FILE ASSIGN TO DISK "tempB.txt".
          SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "tempoutE.txt".
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD TEMP-FILE
          LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
       01 TEMP-REC.
           05  TEMPERATURE OCCURS 10 TIMES  PIC 9(2).

       FD PRINT-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED.
       01 PRINT-REC                   PIC X(80).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  STORED-AREAS.
           05  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS   PIC X(3)    VALUE 'YES'.
           05 SUB                  PIC 99 VALUE 01.

       01  TEMP-OUT-RECORD.
           05 TEMPERATURE-OUT        PIC 99.
           05                  PIC X(78).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-MAIN.
           OPEN INPUT TEMP-FILE
                 OUTPUT PRINT-FILE
           PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS = 'N0 '
           READ TEMP-FILE

               AT END MOVE 'N0 ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS

           END-READ
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM 200-ONE-TEMP-PER-LINE
           CLOSE TEMP-FILE
               PRINT-FILE
           STOP RUN.

       200-ONE-TEMP-PER-LINE.
           PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL SUB > 10
           MOVE TEMPERATURE (SUB) TO TEMPERATURE-OUT
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM TEMP-OUT-RECORD
           END-PERFORM.

Input files looks like this
10203040506070809899

Please can someone help?
thanks...
john...
```

#### ↳ Re: Arrays use part2...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oitoc$bn8@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Co6r3.2818$rL2.1907@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`

```
From a quick look - I don't see any major problems with your program (and
certainly not with your table-handling).

What I *think* might be happening is that you are processing your "input
record" - not when you really want to.  You try and read the values in your
table - but only AFTER you have hit an "end-of-file" condition.

Normally, I don't "give the answer" away - but try to "hint" at it - but in
this case, I think you are so close, that I am going to make two suggestions
(I think either would work):

Option 1:
   Change
>            READ TEMP-FILE
>                AT END MOVE 'N0 ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS
>            END-READ
  to
>            READ TEMP-FILE into Working-storage-copy-of-record
>                AT END MOVE 'N0 ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS
>            END-READ

  and then process the table from working-storage - instead from the FD.

Option 2:
  Again Change

>            READ TEMP-FILE
>                AT END MOVE 'N0 ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS
>            END-READ
  to
>            READ TEMP-FILE
                   Not At end
            PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
                    UNTIL SUB > 10
                        MOVE TEMPERATURE (SUB) TO TEMPERATURE-OUT
                        WRITE PRINT-REC FROM TEMP-OUT-RECORD
            END-PERFORM
>                AT END MOVE 'N0 ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS
>            END-READ

(In other words, move your PERFORM statement from AFTER the "at end" to
while you still have a "good record" in the FD.)

NOTE:
  I haven't tried to compile and run your program (with or without my
possible changes) so there may be some other problem (missing/extra period
or whatever).

P.S.  You don't tell us what compiler you are using - or how you are
"looking" at your output but you may want to add an
   "organization is record sequential"
statement to BOTH your select/assign statements.

P.P.S.  I am assuming that your input really "looks" like you show.  If your
input actually includes 10 separate records - each with one temperature in
it, then you would have to do a bunch of logic changes.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Arrays use part2...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Sun, 08 Aug 1999 02:59:46 GMT "John Romanowski" <jromanow@home.com> wrote:

:>I know...I know...We are not here to check anybody's code but I'm finding
:>the concept of arrays a bit difficult. But I just need someone to toss me a
:>bone or throw me into the right direction. I'm trying to read 10 temperature
:>into an array and print them on seperate lines. Please, can someone help?

   [ program snipped ]

The problem you are having is because you are trying to use the input buffer
after end of file.

You should copy the input buffer into working storage, or, for your example,
simply do a read and process the record. Why are you going for end of file?
There is no obligation to read until end of file.
```

platform. Has anyone tried it on the mainframe with COBOL?  If so could you let

#### ↳ Re: slickedit

- **From:** allenmc@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7omkmi$cpu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990807195625.09547.00002781@ng-bj1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19990807195625.09547.00002781@ng-bj1.aol.com>,
  sms4@aol.com (Sms4) wrote:
> Does anyone use SLICKEDIT by MicroEdge? It's now available on an
OS/390
> platform. Has anyone tried it on the mainframe with COBOL?  If so
could you let
> us know what you think of it?
>
> Thanks...
>

I've been using slickedit for about 6 years on AIX (IBM'S UNIX)and on
the PC.  I like it alot.  You can define keyboard macros and on-the-fly
macros to do nearly anything you want.  It's very configurable, has
online help, and you can edit many files at the same time.  I've never
used it on a mainframe but if it works anything like the version's I'm
using, it's heads and tails better than ISPF.

#### ↳ Re: OLE Automation, DCOM

- **From:** david.sands@tesco.net (David Sands)
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ad4bca.5002080@news.tesco.net>`
- **References:** `<934020499.25234.0.nnrp-13.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

Net Express has some samples on this in OLEdemos directory. And has
shows how to use Excel and Word together from COBOL.

The documentions on the OLE Automation Interface however is not
installed by default with WORD. To install this you need to select the
Visual Basic programmer reference.

The interface is complex and can be tricky to work out how to acheive
what you want. What I tend to do is RECORD a script in WORD of what I
want to do. This records in VB and tells me how to acheive the desired
functionality using the OLE Automation interface. Its then a simple
matter of doing the same from your COBOL module.

Regards
David.
```

and
styles.

The reason I wish to do process a report this way is because the user
can/needs to edit the doc after processing if he/she wishes, also the
program will not need to be updated in future years all you have to do is
edit the template for desired results.

One thing I cannot see is how to change the font from OLE after selecting a
peice of text in the word doc, but this wont really be an issue.


Simon R Hart
Eaton.

<a id="t-a46a2c23a8"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 and offline printer — 1 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-07](1999-07.md#t-a46a2c23a8)._

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 and offline printer

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ogsq5$l5$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<19990726152900.17079.00002232@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990726152900.17079.00002232@ng-fs1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
>
> If I compile my program with the CALLFH"EXTFH" and then run my program and
try
> to print to an off-line printer then my program is able to trap the fact
that
> the printer is offline by the status that is returned from the write verb.
If
> i remove the CALLFH"EXTFH" recompile and then run my program trying to
print to
> an offline printer I get the "Printer not available - Cancel, Retry or
Ignore".
>  I get this message even with the COBSW=L which i thought would by pass
all
> critical errors.  I would prefer not having the CALLFH"EXTFH" compile
directive
> as it impacts the size of my program.  Any suggestions ?
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey
>

    If the "P" (P0) switch is set, the built in function to check printer
status does not work.  If you do NOT use the +P0 switch (same as +P), than
network printers often do not work.

    Also note that if the +P0 switch is set, you MUST use the +L0 switch.
If not, you get the DOS abort, retry message.  Abort aborts by god, leaving
files open, etc.  The safe message is blinking and worded differently.

    The best bet is to use -P0 (default) when running DOS no network, else
trust the OS, etc to make a printer available.

    The connection with EXTFH is very odd indeed.

    If the users are running the program in a dos prompt under win95 etc,
you could try XM.  It works almost the same as without, with some (very)
small exceptions (ie you must use MODE to set com port specs, not ASM/ INT
calls).
```

## Thread: Microfocus MAKE with VERSION — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus MAKE with VERSION

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7MU7qGFlOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```


Is there any feature with micro focus netexpress 3.0 to put a
version number in a DLL.

If you know msinfo32.exe you can get with this tool a version number of
any dll.

I think it's a good idea to use a version number.
But i can't find a parameter in the tool cbllink or cobol to make a dll
with a version number.

Does anyone know how i can make a dll with a version number?
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus MAKE with VERSION

- **From:** david.sands@tesco.net (David Sands)
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37af2653.3223847@news.tesco.net>`
- **References:** `<7MU7qGFlOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

Use a RC file for include version info for your DLL. You should add
the RC to your Net Express project and add it to your DLL.

A sample RC is:-

1 VERSIONINFO
  FILEVERSION    1,0,0,0
  PRODUCTVERSION 1,0,0,0
  FILEFLAGS      0
BEGIN
  BLOCK "StringFileInfo"
  BEGIN
    BLOCK "04090000"            /* lang-charset : US. English, ACSII
*/
    BEGIN
      VALUE "CompanyName",      "MERANT International Limited\0"
      VALUE "FileDescription",  "MERANT Net Express Demo\0"
      VALUE "FileVersion",      "1.00,1.00\0"
      VALUE "InternalName",     "ADSAMP\0"
      VALUE "LegalCopyright",   "Copyright (C) 1999\0"
      VALUE "LegalTrademarks",  "MERANT\0"
      VALUE "OriginalFileName", "ADSAMP.dll\0"
      VALUE "ProductName",      "MERANT Net Express\0"
      VALUE "ProductVersion",   "1.0.00\0"
    END
  END
END

Regards
David.


On 08 Aug 1999 10:33:00 +0100, Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
wrote:

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

file, it should be in a .RC resource file. See David Sands' separate reply
for additional information.

-------------------
TEST.BAT

cobol testpgm, testpgm, testpgm, testpgm, @testpgm.rsp; > log.txt

------------
CONFIRM
NOEARLY-RELEASE

CASE
ASSIGN "EXTERNAL"
OUTDD "sysout.txt"

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus MAKE with VERSION

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ordnb$qta$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7MU7qGFlOtB@bop99.ping.de> <7MY8utLGOtB@bop99.ping.de> <37AEC709.7F2E0577@zip.com.au> <7MbAs$70OtB@bop99.ping.de> <7ooq6v$h50$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <7MbB-HdGOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```
Juergen,

The problem you have is that CBLLINK only knows about .CBL, .OBJ and .LIB.
Everything else gets passed straight to the linker. However, a .RC cannot be
passed to the linker directly, it has to be compiled using the Microsoft
Win32 Resource compiler first eg

rc -r testpgm.rc

That will generate testpgm.res which you can specify on the CBLLINK line as
the linker will accept it.

I hop that solves the problem for you.

Juergen Vetter wrote in message <7MbB-HdGOtB@bop99.ping.de>...
>gael.wilson@merant.com meinte am 10.08.99
>zum Thema "Re: Microfocus MAKE with VERSION":
…[135 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```

you are looking for a student version - check objectz.com where they

## Thread: Compiled Table Question. — 10 msgs

### Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com>`

```
In assembler I have tables that have been compiled (just a big list of
DC instructions).  The table can be LOADed into memory and used as is
for table lookups, data conversions, or whatever the situation calls
for.  The program keeps track of the entry point, etc.

Is there a way to accomplish the same thing in COBOL?
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bYTr3.3273$Cl2.18979@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com>`

```
On IBM MainFrame, IF you can get the Address use can access it using POINTER
data types.
Write an ASSEM prog that returns the address of the Table and the size, call
it from COBOL
and then set the POINTER-ADDR item to the address, the SIZE-OF-TBL to the
size and that's it.

>In assembler I have tables that have been compiled (just a big list of
>DC instructions).  The table can be LOADed into memory and used as is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>================================
```

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b39fbd@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com>`

```

Dave Polzin wrote in message <37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com>...
>In assembler I have tables that have been compiled (just a big list of
>DC instructions).  The table can be LOADed into memory and used as is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>================================
Are people still doing that?! We thought it was smart in 1966 when we wrote
programs in 16K and the Operating System ran in 8K...but nowadays...???

Anyway to answer your question: No. There is no direct equivalent in COBOL.
(Thank Heaven, I would say...<G>)

You could achieve a similar result by writing a COBOL program with tables
defined in the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, complete with their static values,
in the usual way. You could then have a LINKAGE SECTION which had an area
large enough to hold the table defined in it (this actually occupies no real
storage; it's equivalent to an assembler DUMMY
Section   (DSECT)). The PROCEDURE DIVISION of this pointless hypothetical
subprogram would contain a USING clause to establish addressability to the
LINKAGE, and the only statements it would execute would  be a MOVE (table
from working-storage to linkage), followed by an EXIT PROGRAM.

Anyone calling the program would receive the table into their own storage.
It seems a long way round but there are cases I guess where you might want
to share a table amongst a number of programs and the above has the
advantage that you only have to maintain it in ONE place. You might wonder
why you can't just define the table in the LINKAGE SECTION in the first
place. The reason is because this section doesn't really exist and COBOL
therefore forbids you to put VALUE clauses on the definitions in it. (It is
therefore impossible to define your table in it.)

The reason I say this is not EXACTLY equivalent to the Assembler solution is
because the above subprogram must be compiled and linked with the COBOL
runtime modules. Even if you use DYNAM there will still be COBOL "baggage"
attached, which would not be the case in assembler. (Having said that, the
overheads in this case are minimal, and the object module  (before linking)
should be approximately the same size as the assembler equivalent. (COBOL
places EXTRNs and weak EXTRNs to the run time, depending on the compile
options.))

Hope this helps,

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990813104950.05132.00000442@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37b39fbd@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood writes...

>Dave Polzin wrote in message <37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com>...
>>In assembler I have tables that have been compiled (just a big list of
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why put him down? It's actually an appropriate thing to do in certain
applications, even today.


>Anyway to answer your question: No. There is no direct equivalent in COBOL.
>(Thank Heaven, I would say...<G>)
>

Well, there is. And there [at least] two ways to look at the question.

1) Perhaps he wants to just access such an Assembled table from a COBOL
program. This can't be done without help from some other program.

2) If he wants to use COBOL to generate the table, it's possible, although as
you point out it's not particularly efficient.


>You could achieve a similar result by writing a COBOL program with tables
>defined in the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, complete with their static values,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>from working-storage to linkage), followed by an EXIT PROGRAM.

You made this too complicated. An alternate would be to use the COBOL EXTERNAL
attribute: define the table in both programs with the EXTERNAL attribute. No
need for linkage section, although you do still need one of the programs to
initialize the table.


>
>Anyone calling the program would receive the table into their own storage.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hope this helps,

Mmm.

Still, in most cases a simple flat file is probably the best way to go.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```

>>Are people still doing that?! We thought it was smart in 1966 when we
wrote

any offence was taken (and I hope it wasn't) I apologize wholeheartedly and
unreservedly. I know its an appropriate thing to do and I said so, it's just
that there are (better?) ways of doing it nowadays.
>
>>Anyway to answer your question: No. There is no direct equivalent in
COBOL.
>>(Thank Heaven, I would say...<G>)
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>2) If he wants to use COBOL to generate the table, it's possible, although
as
>you point out it's not particularly efficient.
>
And neither of these is directly equivalent to the Assembler solution. I
stand by what I said, and I gave the reasons why I think so.
>

>You made this too complicated. An alternate would be to use the COBOL
EXTERNAL
>attribute: define the table in both programs with the EXTERNAL attribute.
No
>need for linkage section, although you do still need one of the programs to
>initialize the table.
>
Yes, that's a very good point, and one I missed. I forgot that EXTERNAL is
now available in the mainframe environment. I have become used to only using
it on PCs.

As you point out, it still needs to be initialized in one of the programs,
and, as you can't put a VALUE on EXTERNAL data (any more than you can on a
LINKAGE SECTION), you are left with either, a table defined and initialized
in WORKING-STORAGE in one of the programs, or a series of MOVEs. Is this
really any less complicated that what I suggested?
>

>
>Mmm.
>
>Still, in most cases a simple flat file is probably the best way to go.
>

Ah! We can agree on at least one point and stay friends...<G>

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1koi$lss@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com> <37b39fbd@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
With the '85 COBOL Standard - there actually is a "funny" way of doing this.
(I know this in theory - but have never seen any program actually use it -
in production).

Believe it or not, the PROCEDURE DIVISION is *optional* in the '85 Standard.
Therefore, if you coded a COBOL program with an Identification Division
(name of the table) and a DATA DIVISION with a table defined with "IS
EXTERNAL" - and *nothing* else in the program, this would "load" the table.
(There is an "implied EXIT PROGRAM" in any sub-program without a Procedure
Division.)

Obviously, to access the "table" you would need to DYNAMICALLY (IBM
mainframe usage) call a subprogram that ALSO had the  table in it.  (The
main program wouldn't have the table "loaded" but the subprogram would.)  ON
THE OTHER HAND, I don't know what this separate - no procedure division -
program would gain you - as some subprogram WITH the table would still need
to initialize values for you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X5%s3.498$1c2.17215@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com> <37b39fbd@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7p1koi$lss@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill - and I'm sure you and others have run into this yourselves - there are
many times I've been called in to do some maintenance work and I wished the
PROCEDURE DIVISION had been omitted ....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiled Table Question.

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b6ce43@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37AF8420.772A@coredcs.com> <37b39fbd@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7p1koi$lss@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7p1koi$lss@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>...
>With the '85 COBOL Standard - there actually is a "funny" way of doing
this.
>(I know this in theory - but have never seen any program actually use it -
>in production).
>
>Believe it or not, the PROCEDURE DIVISION is *optional* in the '85
Standard.
>Therefore, if you coded a COBOL program with an Identification Division
>(name of the table) and a DATA DIVISION with a table defined with "IS
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>main program wouldn't have the table "loaded" but the subprogram would.)
ON
>THE OTHER HAND, I don't know what this separate - no procedure division -
>program would gain you - as some subprogram WITH the table would still need
>to initialize values for you.
>
Wow! I am blown away! Thanks for that, Bill. Never knew the standard made
the PROCEDURE DIVISION optional...
Maybe it was intended for exactly this scenario where people might want to
load compiled data only. As you point out, there are still problems in
gaining access to it.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-32eb3c44ba"></a>
## Thread: NEWBIE PROBLEM — 24 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** "Calatis" <nmourapires@mail.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
Hello,

i am a newbie using cobol under MVS system.
I have this problem: i have 4 input files that i want to work and 1 output
file.

The name of this files depend on the date. Here is an exemple of the name of
the file:  SLP.SIN.SPOUT.D990830.T2233.

My problem is how to open a file that i don't know the name before i execute
the program.

Can anyone help me? Some code would be useful.

Thank in advanced.

Nuno
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AEF9F6.C53567A0@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
Calatis wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the file:  SLP.SIN.SPOUT.D990830.T2233.

First, the recommended way of using the Web to ask for help is to think
of a Subject line which keys others into your problem.  "Newbie problem"
doesn't get scanners to stop and read your request.  You mentioned your
operating system - that was needed, but for COBOL problems, you should
also mention your compiler.

I don't have a Cobol solution for your problem.

The simplest solution is to have the operator or whomever submits the
job, pass a symbolic override for that portion of the file name.

A more complicated method is to have two jobs.  The first job creates
the either the JCL or a proc or a JCL copy member with the file name in
it (This can be a COBOL program).  The second job is the one with the
modified JCL or proc.  

I suspect there's a more direct solution using JES CA7.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM: File with date component

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rqLr3.1038$Co3.11032@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt> <37AEF9F6.C53567A0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
CA7 does NOT have a solution.

Either have REXX write the info as a proc (not likely you'll be allowed to
do this)
or you need an assembler subroutine that DYNAMICALLY ALLOCATES the files.

If your site doesn't have one, I have one I wrote and use (24 and 31 bit
versions: the
24 bit can be invoked directly via JCL to read SYSIN and DELETE / CREATE a
file)
also the COBOL COPYBOOKS to CALL it and verify results.

I'd be willing to license it to your company for a reasonable price ( I
wrote it for myself)
Let me know if your interested.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37AEF9F6.C53567A0@NOSPAMhome.com>...

of

>

#### ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** jdeez@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7on5po$q9d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
There really isn't a way to declare the actual file name in cobol.  The
JCL contains this information as you have seen from the other posts.

In the MVS environment there is a great file management system called
Generation Data Sets.  If you can get the system folks to build the
base and the rest of the system to agree this would eliminate the
dependancy on the date in the file name.

Otherwise the job will have to be modified each run to get the date in
the file name.  See other posts for this method.

> I have this problem: i have 4 input files that i want to work and 1
output

name of

>

execute

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7on8c5$oah@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt> <7on5po$q9d$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <A0FDF5951E2E1C89.10A9015D7905273F.49DBDEC8BA4C77F5@lp.airnews.net>`

```
The file name given as an example looks suspiciously like one that has been
assigned by the operating system in the first place.
SLP.SIN.SPOUT.D990830.T2233 is a file that was created in proc SLP, step
SIN, ddname SPOUT which was interpreted by the initiator on 990830 time
2233.  If step SIN is a previous step in the job containing the program you
are trying to run, why not just declare access to it as:
//FILEIN DD DSN=*.SIN.SPOUT,DISP=(OLD,DELETE,KEEP)
and then process the file FILEIN in your COBOL program?

Ralph Coleman wrote in message ...
>You will be able to do this in the next iteration of COBOL (probably due
out
>within the next decade).
><jdeez@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7on5po$q9d$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AEE29C.680C5F43@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
Calatis wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nuno

The simple answer is that you cannot.

Now the hard answer.

1.  You can tune up your assembler and write an exit to SVC99 to
allocate a DD name that the cobol program knows about (not recommended
too unsupportable I have done it and it confuses most operators).

2.  You can write a program that writes a JCL out and use
SYSOUT=(,INTRDR) on that DD.  This automatically submits the built
JCL. Consider using ISPF and file tailoring to do it.

3.  Get the scheduling package that you own to do it.  These are add
ons and have names like OPC, JCLTRAC, etc.  Check you Ops division.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37AFAF97.3043FD1@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
Nuno,

COBOL is not designed to address this kind of processing. You need a
language with a macro processor, e.g. PL/I or SAS. It is unlikely that you
have PL/I in your org, which is infortunate, since as a general programming
language, PL/I is unbeatable. However, you should definitely have SAS;
virtually any MVS shop has it, if not for production data processing at
large, then for processing SMF records. The latter, because of their
complexity, are never attempted to process in anything but SAS, whose
input/output flexibility far exceeds that of _any_ existing programming
language. What I am saying is, COBOL is a venerable and great (for what it
can do right) programming language, but your task is out of its league.

//<job card and stuff>
// EXEC SAS,OPTIONS='ERRORABEND'
//SYSIN DD *,DLM='$$'
/* Assign ddname to file with current date */
FILENAME FILE1 "SLP.SIN.SPOUT.D%SYSFUNC(DATE(),YYMMDD6.).T2233" DISP=SHR;

/*.....Similar for the rest of the files.....*/
/* Now you can use FILE1-FILE4 as you would ddnames in the program */
/* Say you want to copy FILE1 to the output file */
/* Next 2 statements do the same iefbr14 would */ 
/* The 3d allocates output. Do it all in JCL if you wish */ 
FILENAME OUTPUT "NUNO.OUTPUT" DISP=(MOD,DELETE);
FILENAME OUTPUT CLEAR;
FILENAME OUTPUT "NUNO.OUTPUT" DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE) RECFM=FB LRECL=100
BLKSIZE=0 SPACE=(CYL,(5,10),RLSE) UNIT=3390;
/* Copy FILE1 to OUTPUT */
DATA _NULL_;
   INFILE FILE1;
   INPUT;
   FILE OUTPUT;
   PUT _INFILE_;
RUN;

The example is simplistic but gives you an idea why, when it comes to MVS,
SAS programmers run circles around it. This is just the tip of a giant SAS
iceberg. Let's admit it, a well tuned COBOL program  would run faster than
SAS code doing the same job (usually, at the expense of zillion times more
lines of code), but a fairly dumb SAS guy would be done with the kind of
problem you are asking for long before the brightest COBOL guru would merely
sift through possibilities, and this thread corroborates it. 
A friend of mine, one of the best big iron guys I know, has a notion of a
"proficient mainframe programmer": one cannot be proficient without being
proficient in COBOL _and_ SAS. 

Kind regards,
=====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=====================           
  
            

Calatis wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nuno
```

those things for Cobol.  For example CICS Commands and SQL commands.

SkippyPB wrote:

…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> those things for Cobol.  For example CICS Commands and SQL commands.

Right. I never said this cannot be done by external means. But processing
files with data driven names is a quite common business DP task, and if the
principal business-oriented language does not provide a non-convoluted way
of accomplishing the task internally, it is an omission, to put it mildly.
     
> Also Paul neglected to include Assembler in his list which also has
> the ability to process macroes. 

Why should I compare apples to oranges? COBOL is a 3GL. 

> And it appears he is a PL/1 bigot anyway

No more than that of COBOL, SAS, COBOL, FORTRAN, C, or whatever else gets
the job done.   

> anyway since he stated that as a general programming language
> it is unbeatable.

To me, PL/I strikes the best balance between COBOL as too wordy and C as too
cryptic. Plethora of features and the macro processor (the reason it was
mentioned in the first place) give it an extra leverage. "Unbeatable" is
probably too strong a word but, frankly, no stronger than "bigot".  

>Cobol has been waged in this newsgroup more than once and is not worth rehashing here.

Couldn't agree more.

Kind regards,
+++++++++++++++++
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
+++++++++++++++++

SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
  <snip>
> >
> Right.  I should have said biased.  The problem with PL/1 is that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I still prefer Cobol.

Interestingly enough - like Paragraphs versus Sections - this is a
"continental" thing. (Gross oversimplification - but mostly true.)  PL/I
(IBM's - not ANSI/ISO conforming) is really almost as common as COBOL in
large European IBM mainframe shops. (In some areas, it is MORE common.)  On
the other hand, in the US, it really is less common (but hardly unusual).
In "yet bad olde days" - when IBM tried to "market" SAA, the fact that RPG
was an SAA language - but PL/I was NOT caused various fits of humor and
dismay.

Having said that, I will say "let's NOT get into another language war" in
this NG.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM (Enlighten me)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B1ED45.DB38223@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt> <37AFAF97.3043FD1@earthlink.net> <37AFBCAA.E7BC598C@home.com> <2F5E6CB0FB7EC20B.A32B7500D3CDF652.E966BBD9ED1450BA@lp.airnews.net> <37B0AD00.8C06B2B7@earthlink.net> <084CF0E874F71BCA.DB24EC40A6417818.B685FA5E8724D46D@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 10 Aug 1999 18:51:44 -0400, "Paul M. Dorfman"
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> this is not the same.

I mean that the name(s) and/or other characteristic(s) of output file(s)
(such as type, DCB, disposition, space, unit, layout) are determined at the
run-time. They may depend on data contained in the input, obtained as a
result of input processing, computations, etc. The simplest case, an output
file name comprising a date (system or computed) as its part, was the
subject of the original posting.

The most versatile package I know of that makes all such things elementary,
is SAS. In addition, one can issue any operating system commands, run any
utilities (e.g. IDCAMS, etc., SyncSort, you name it) straight from within
the program. However, it may have a dangerous effect on performance in the
hands of one approaching SAS as a user (as opposite to a programmer). In the
days of yore, it was possible to augment it by writing custom SAS procs in
PL/I (since SAS itself was written in PL/I; it is C now). That was a real
godsend.           

> >To me, PL/I strikes the best balance between COBOL as too wordy and C as too

native thing in Cobol is that the language would have to be tied to
closely to the operating system.  If you look at Cobol written for VSE
and Cobol written for MVS, there are very few differences.  In fact
off the top of my head the only differences I can think of are in the
Data Division and Configuration Section.

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

accomplished through native SAS verbs or through calls to other
services?  Regardless, while not native in Cobol, all those things can
be accomplished by calls from Cobol to other services or Assembler sub
routines.
