###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM (Enlighten me)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B77217.BEFC4941@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt> <37AFAF97.3043FD1@earthlink.net> <37AFBCAA.E7BC598C@home.com> <2F5E6CB0FB7EC20B.A32B7500D3CDF652.E966BBD9ED1450BA@lp.airnews.net> <37B0AD00.8C06B2B7@earthlink.net> <084CF0E874F71BCA.DB24EC40A6417818.B685FA5E8724D46D@lp.airnews.net> <37B1ED45.DB38223@earthlink.net> <4518A43649DC8AD1.7A20B440EFEF8C37.EC8B63BCC1A65E7B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 11 Aug 1999 17:38:13 -0400, "Paul M. Dorfman"
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

They are accomplished through native SAS constructs: Either FILENAME
statement in the open code, or FILENAME function in the DATA step and Macro
Language. There are also DSNEXIST statement and functions FILEEXIST and
FEXIST verifying the existence of a file based either on its physical name
or DD name (for all the above environments). There are functions that
interrogate the attributes of the file, and so on. However, if one prefers
to do all these things using TSO (or UNIX shell, or Windows) commands, they
can be issued from anywhere (but procs) as X-statement, SYSTEM statement,
SYSTEM call routine, TSO statement (if MVS) - the choice is yours. OS
Utilities can be run from a SAS program in the same manner SAS procs are.
SAS log issues messages informing what program external to SAS has executed.
Consequently, _everything_ that can be done in JCL can be done from within a
SAS program on the fly, of course, including writing to the internal reader.

Kind regards,
===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
===================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM (Enlighten me)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Lawrence Allen Free <free@mcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p7n6o$15b$1@Nntp1.mcs.net>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt> <37AFAF97.3043FD1@earthlink.net> <37AFBCAA.E7BC598C@home.com> <2F5E6CB0FB7EC20B.A32B7500D3CDF652.E966BBD9ED1450BA@lp.airnews.net> <37B0AD00.8C06B2B7@earthlink.net>`

```
In non-mainframe COBOL's such as MF/ACU/RM where you can
name the file in the program using a variable, one can simply search
a sequence of file names by attempting OPENs and checking status
codes.  It is not efficient, but it does work.  In the mainframe 
environment, I believe you must specify the name in JCL prior to
entering the program.  This is not really a COBOL issue as it is an
operating platform issue.

In article 37B0AD00.8C06B2B7@earthlink.net,
	"Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net> said:
>
>SkippyPB wrote:
…[99 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7opb6j$b2a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
In article <7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>,
  "Calatis" <nmourapires@mail.pt> wrote:
> My problem is how to open a file that i don't know the name before i
execute the program.

You may dynamically allocate a file with a dataset name of your choice
using the TSO/E programming services IKJTSOEV and IKJEFTSR.

The IKJTSOEV subroutine may be called once to set up the TSO/E
environment, and then  IKJEFTSR can be called as often as you like to
perform TSO commands.  The command you would use is ALLOCATE.

Let me know if you would like a sample program.

-Art


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7os6q5$dka$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt> <7opb6j$b2a$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B107EF.8184BA67@att.net>`

```
In article <37B107EF.8184BA67@att.net>,
  WBLynch@att.spam.net wrote:
> Art Perry wrote:
> > Let me know if you would like a sample program.
>
> Don't know about the original poster, but I sure would.

This is a post from the IBM-MAIN mailing list from several months ago:

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net> wrote:
You don�t need an assembler sub-routine to perform dynamic allocations
in COBOL.  You can issue the TSO ALLOCATE command directly from your
COBOL program; please see:
http://x14.dejanews.com/[ST_rn=ps]/getdoc.xp?AN=444695526
Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>

Here is a sample COBOL program that invokes the TSO ALLOCATE command to
dynamically allocate a SYSOUT data set.  You could use the same
technique
to dynamically allocate permanent data sets.Hope this helps.
Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net> //IBMUSERJ JOB (ACCT#),COB2TSO,
 // NOTIFY=&SYSUID, // CLASS=A,MSGCLASS=X,COND=(0,NE)
 //*--------------------------------------------------------------------
*
 //*
*
 //*   This sample program illustrates how to invoke TSO
*
 //*   commands from a COBOL program.  The TSO services used
*
 //*   are documented in the TSO/E Programming Services manual.
*
 //*
*
 //*--------------------------------------------------------------------
*
 //COB2 EXEC PGM=IGYCRCTL  CBL NOLIB,APOST,NODECK,OBJECT,NOSEQ,BUF
(32760),DYNAM
  CBL NOMAP,NOLIST,NOOFFSET,NOXREF         Identification Division.
          Program-ID. CB2TSOEV.
          Author. Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>.         Data
Division.
         Working-Storage Section.          01 Filler.
            05 ws-dummy        Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-return-code  Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-reason-code  Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-info-code    Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-cppl-address Pic s9(8) Comp.
            05 ws-flags        Pic X(4) Value X'00010001'.
            05 ws-buffer       Pic X(256).
            05 ws-length       Pic s9(8) Comp Value 256.
        Procedure Division.             CALL 'IKJTSOEV' Using ws-dummy
                                  ws-return-code
                                  ws-reason-code
                                  ws-info-code
                                  ws-cppl-address.
            IF ws-return-code > zero
              DISPLAY 'IKJTSOEV Failed, Return-code=' ws-return-code
                                      ' Reason-code=' ws-reason-code
                                      'Info-code='    ws-info-code
              MOVE ws-return-code to Return-code              STOP RUN.
            MOVE 'ALLOCATE DD(SYSPUNCH) SYSOUT HOLD' to ws-buffer.
            CALL 'IKJEFTSR' Using ws-flags
                                  ws-buffer
                                  ws-length
                                  ws-return-code
                                  ws-reason-code
                                  ws-dummy.            IF ws-return-
code > zero
              DISPLAY 'IKJEFTSR Failed, Return-code=' ws-return-code
                                      ' Reason-code=' ws-reason-code
              MOVE ws-return-code to Return-code              STOP RUN.
            DISPLAY 'ALLOCATE Worked ! ' Upon Syspunch.
STOP RUN. /*
 //SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=* //SYSUT1 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT2 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1) //SYSUT3 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT4 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1) //SYSUT5 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSUT6 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1) //SYSUT7 DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1)
 //SYSLIN DD UNIT=VIO,SPACE=(TRK,1),DISP=(,PASS),BLKSIZE=3200 //*
 //GO    EXEC PGM=LOADER,PARM=NOPRINT
 //SYSLIN DD DISP=(OLD,PASS),DSN=*.COB2.SYSLIN //SYSOUT DD SYSOUT=*
 //SYSLIB DD DISP=SHR,DSN=CEE.SCEELKED


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBIE PROBLEM

- **From:** Clifton Ivy <clif@purdue.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B1BF52.56D33336@purdue.edu>`
- **References:** `<7om98n$cjl$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
After looking at the responses to this question, I believe we are being
too literal in trying to answer the stated question, instead of trying
to solve the problem that causes the question.

I think we need to ask Calatis what he is trying to accomplish by
reading a file where the date is part of the Data Set Name (DSN).

Are you trying to process the file that was just created, and you are
using the date to differentiate between files created at different times
(so maybe you can keep all the files)? If so, other posts have suggested
DSN referbacks (DSN=*.STEP.DDNAME), and Generation Data Groups (GDGs).
Either of these could work, depending on what you are trying to
accomplish in total.

Do you need to process all the data files that have been created since
the last time you processed? GDGs can help here, also.

Are you trying to variably process data that was created on (?applies
to?) a particular date? Adding a date field to the record, and keeping
all the records in a single file would work until the bulk of the data
gets out of hand.

You know, sooner or later, your user is going to ask to run Process X
TWICE in a day, and probably have some very good reason why it is a good
idea -- and using the date may create problems in this case. 

Or, they may ask to skip Process X "for tonight", so that the work they
do today can be added to the work they do tomorrow and it be processed
as a single "day". They will have good business reasons for doing this,
also.

For these reasons, the best solutions may not be the first ones that
occur to you -- they may be the ones you are forced to after a few "You
want to do WHAT???" questions!

So, what are you trying to accomplish in total?

Thanks!

Calatis wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nuno
```

## Thread: Print on lpt1 with Cobol — 5 msgs

### Print on lpt1 with Cobol

- **From:** "Isabelle Lodewick" <i.lodewick@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7omtkm$fbs@news3.euro.net>`

```
Who can tell me how it's possible to print a file on lpt1 in Cobol 85 ?

Thanks.

                        Isabelle.
```

#### ↳ Re: Print on lpt1 with Cobol

- **From:** allenmc@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7onj8e$4qg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7omtkm$fbs@news3.euro.net>`

```
In article <7omtkm$fbs@news3.euro.net>,
  "Isabelle Lodewick" <i.lodewick@wanadoo.be> wrote:
> Who can tell me how it's possible to print a file on lpt1 in Cobol 85
?
>
> Thanks.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My experience has been yes.  There are a couple ways you can do it.

#1

       select print-file  assign to print "lpt1".


#2  This is what I'm doing with RM/COBOL


        select print-file assign to print "printer1"


     Note:  printer1 referes to a line in the RM/COBOL configurtion
            file that actually points to lpt1.

Good Luck!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

>

In simple terms :

select print-file assign to "lpt1"
   organization is line sequential.

I think thats how you do it, I aint coded like this for ages.

Simon R Hart.
Eaton.

Simply write to print-file as you would any normal file.

Pete

drive with NetExpress?

Bob Hennessey

```
With Visual Basic 6.0 and ADO it is possible to pass a complete
recordset to a module which runs in another process. The recordset is
then copied in the addressroom of the other program.
To do so makes the performance much better than working with a
referenz, because not every single property has to be invoked, instead
the whole recordset can be accessed at once.
I'm working with Netexpress 3.0. Does anybody know if it is possible to
use this technique with Netexpress, when the Cobol EXE is called from
Visual Basic ?

Thank's a lot !
  Oliver Klein

## Thread: COMREG & COBOL 74 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### RE: COMREG & COBOL 74

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B014E30F6@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
What is *fatal*?  I just can't remember which COBOL's allow which verbs, but
I didn't think that address setting was allowed in '74....

Which means that you would need corresponding full word registers in
linkage...

Which would mean that you should be able to move a zero to the appropriate
full word...

-----Original Message-----

snip

but the following statements/instructions are fatal in '74:

    03    POINT-COMREG        USAGE POINTER.

    SET ADDRESS OF PAGE-ZERO TO NULL

    SET ADDRESS OF COMREG TO POINT-COMREG

Is it possible to access COMREG in COBOL 74?  How?

snip


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMREG & COBOL 74

- **From:** "Tim Mueller" <tmiller16@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37af0bca@news2.one.net>`
- **References:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B014E30F6@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```

Thompson, William <wthompson@okc.disa.mil> wrote in message
news:90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B014E30F6@voyager.okc.disa.mil...
> What is *fatal*?  I just can't remember which COBOL's allow which verbs,
but
> I didn't think that address setting was allowed in '74....

Exactly.  The SET verb is supported in '74, but not the ADDRESS OF option,
which gives the addressability.  I know now (thanks, everyone) that the only
way to access the COMREG in '74 is with an Assembler subroutine.

By 'fatal', I just meant that it would not compile.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help, MFCOBOL & Btrieve database

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7otp94$aa0$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7oncgv$vlt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

kapoor <in_rkapoor@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:7oncgv$vlt$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I wnat to access and work on Btrieve database records (on Netware
> 4.11) using MFCOBOL 3.2 or NetExpress.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> kapoor

    I believe that MF has a built in betrieve interface that would eliminate
the need to know anything about Btrieve (except maybe on the server).

    Try, above the first line of the program,

000000 $SET CALLFH "XFH2BTR"

    I have not personally tried this.  I believe that this will redirect all
file operations in the program, so you may want to create a subprogram that
does nothing but file operations for this one file.
```

```
Computer Science - pure computer science - is an exercise in
manipulating type domains.  Its very mathematically
intensive and not for the faint hearted.

Its about abstract things like Finite State Automata, Lambda
transitions and language grammars.

If you told a Chemisty graduate that you supply the computer
to the program, he probably wouldn't understand it fully...


These large IT companies who accept graudates from virtually
any disapline and send them on C++ training courses are
harming the IT industry - I believe we're paying the price
for that now...

Paul.
BTW.
Why was this crossposted to an Ada group?  Looking for a
half decent language ;-) (Emphasis on "half")
```

<a id="t-45729eb9c5"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Conference? What do you think? — 25 msgs

### Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** earranga@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This question is for everyone who would care to respond. The question is
this: Would you attend a Cobol conference? Also, take a stab at how many
other Cobol programmers from your company might attend.

I believe, in many ways, Cobol is shooting itself in the foot. We have
one of the largest groups of programmers in the world, four times the
installed code base of every other language combined, yet we don't have
our own conference.

Merant has a conference, but it's not a Cobol conference, it's a Merant
conference. The same holds true for Acucorp and others.

Jave One, presented by Sun, has every major Java language supplier in
the world. As a result it draws tremendous crowds, good press coverage,
and converts. It is the philosophy behind car-marts, where you can buy a
Buick next to a Toyota, next to a Chrysler, next to a Volvo. Shopping
malls work the same. Sears doesn't say "count me out if J.C. Penny is
there."

A group of us have been discussing the possibility of putting on a Cobol
conference for a number of years. I envision the conference to be from
2-4 days with the world's leading experts providing talks, demos, birds
of a feather sessions, roundtables, etc. The conference would focus on
the Cobol programmer, rather than on the tools of one vendor. It would
also be Webcast so that those unable to physically attend the conference
would not miss out.

I am very interested in your thoughts. If you would like to discuss this
further you can reach me at earranga@objectz.com.

Edmund C. Arranga
Editor - The COBOL Report
earranga@objectz.com


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B0CC01.6A8D@coredcs.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
 > Would you attend a Cobol conference? 

Generally speaking, I am in favor of Technical Conferences regardless of
the focus.  However, I probably share that view with less than 1% of IT
management.

As an alternative suggestion, I will be attending the SHARE conference
in Chicago this month.  I have gone through the schedule and was pretty
much able to fill up the entire week with COBOL topics.  That conference
might be a better bang for the buck.

================================
Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
================================
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** earranga@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7or3ha$kkc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B0CC01.6A8D@coredcs.com>`

```
In article <37B0CC01.6A8D@coredcs.com>,
  dlpolzin@coredcs.com wrote:
> earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
>  > Would you attend a Cobol conference?
>
> Generally speaking, I am in favor of Technical Conferences regardless
of
> the focus.  However, I probably share that view with less than 1% of
IT
> management.
>
> As an alternative suggestion, I will be attending the SHARE conference
> in Chicago this month.  I have gone through the schedule and was
pretty
> much able to fill up the entire week with COBOL topics.  That
conference
> might be a better bang for the buck.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Dave -

Before addressing the conference issue let me ask you if you would like
to file an article about the SHARE conference for the COBOL Report? If
so you can attend the SHARE conference for free, plus we can work out
some amount to pay you for the article (not much, but something). You
can contact me at earranga@objectz.com.

The same offer goes for anyone else planning to attend a conference that
would be of interest to the COBOL Report. Every conference I am aware of
allows the press to attend free of charge. Drop me an email and we can
discuss it further.

On to the matter of SHARE vs. a COBOL conference. An analogy. In its
infancy computer science was a series of classes generally found in the
school of electrical engineering. As a result if you wanted to become a
tenured EE professor, but your interests lay in computers, you studied
and published topics such as "The Propagation of Electricity in
Metal-Dioxide Memory Banks." CS became a discipline unto itself in the
mid-'60s when Carnegie Mellon University made CS a fully funded
recognized department whereby students could graduate with a degree in
computer science. To become a tenured CS professor, even though your
interests lay in the filed of software languages, you studied and
published works such as "The Church/Turing Theorem Proof Re-Analyzed."
Computer science is really computing science - the study of what can be
computed. Again Carnegie-Mellon led the way and in the early '70s made
software engineering a discipline in its own right. Out from under the
yoke of EE computer science flourishes. Out from under the yoke of CS
software engineering flourishes.

My point is this. At the SHARE conference Cobol is "computer science"
stuck in the bowels of the electrical engineering department circa 1960.
A conference with Cobol at the core creates its own weight, gravity and
light. We (and by we I mean the Cobol professional) deserve and need our
own conference. Imagine a Cobol conference with 5,000 attendees; with
10,000; or 15,000 (by the way the Java One conference draws 16,000).
Imagine the amount of knowledge generated, of new articles, books,
ideas. Why has Gamma's books "Design Patterns" never been translated
into Cobol? We are a fragmented community. There are probably more
design patterns waiting to be exploited in Cobol than in any other
language. But because we don't present a unified force we are treated
like the redheaded stepchild. Gamma's book is but one small example.
There are thousands.

Best regards,
Ed


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8A83741BCFD7594B.3D2DC9769F3F5481.2B5B138EF6D039C3@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 10 Aug 1999 04:54:30 GMT, earranga@my-deja.com enlightened us:

>This question is for everyone who would care to respond. The question is
>this: Would you attend a Cobol conference? Also, take a stab at how many
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

This sounds like a good idea.  The only problem I see is one of
economics.  If you are anticipating companies would send one or more
of their programmers to such a conference, then there would have to be
a "return of investment" so to speak.  In other words, what would the
compamy that pays the expense of sending their programmer(s) to the
conference gain?  For consultants or 1099 programmers, the same could
be said, although the payoff for them might come in the form of
networking.  Also, the agenda would have to be such so that you get
actual programmers (if that is indeed your intented audience) and not
the manager of the programmers who may have never wrote a line of code
in his life and only knows about Cobol because his staff talks about
it.

Just a few thoughts.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Some days your the dog.  Some days your the hydrant.


 Steve
```

Steve -

The conference would not necessarily be just for the Cobol programmer,
it would be for the entire Cobol community and that includes managers,
analysts, designers, consultants, professors, students, trainers,
authors, research software engineers, and anyone else who has a
interest, vested or otherwise, in Cobol.

I received an email about this topic from a gentleman who mentioned
context. He stated: "�people could get an idea of other technologies in
context." I think he hit the nail on the head. Context is everything.
Most software vendors are tribal villagers and they want you to join.
Read the marketing spiel on any software vendor's Web page and then tell
me what it means. It means absolutely nothing. There is no context.
Nothing is a product; everything is a "solution." A solution to what?
Inprise is a perfect example. There is not one place on the Inprise web
site that mentions object Pascal - and that's what Delphi is. Inprise
now sells for about $2 a share - soon to be purchased by a larger
company. Inprise attempted to put a fence around themselves to protect
market share. They succeeded in becoming irrelevant. Does it remind you
of anyone?

Doesn't XML remind you of the Data Division? Isn't it something that
Cobol folks should be exposed to sooner, rather than later? XML and
Cobol go hand-in-hand. Where are the seminars at the Merant conference
that explain IBM's MQ Series or Microsoft's Transaction Server both
fundamentally important to Cobol folks? Where are the middleware vendors
explaining object request brokers? Where are the folks talking about UML
or OPEN? The Cobol community has absolutely no ties to any of the other
software engineering communities. Where is Linux at the Merant
conference? All these technologies require context to make sense. Folks
need to be able to contrast and compare. They need background so that
they can say, "Oh, I see how that works."

So to finally answer you question, folks would gain knowledge and
insight and a clear understanding of not what the future "holds in
store," but how to create the future.

Best regards,
Ed


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

I am attending AcuCorps 10th annual developers conference in October in
Manchester UK. They arrange their own conferences for Cobol-developers
focusing on AcuCobol, but I've always returned from the conference with many
new ideas regarding how to develop our Cobol-aplications in general. Many
interesting people to meet as well. They have several conferences around to
make it easier for the attendees to find a place not so far away. The had
three conferences in USA, and at least two in Europe as far as I know. I
guess they will have conferences in Asia as well, as I know they have many
users in Japan.

Erlend Moen
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7oruof$jg7$1@news.igs.net>`

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b340e9.34710778@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
earranga@my-deja.com wrote:

>This question is for everyone who would care to respond. The question is
>this: Would you attend a Cobol conference? Also, take a stab at how many
>other Cobol programmers from your company might attend.

Ed:

We would gladly attend as an exhibitor which always helps to
underwrite the cost of such a conference.  A central location is
always desireable....let's flip a coin...Heads, it's St. Louis,
MO...Tails, it's Rio!!

>I believe, in many ways, Cobol is shooting itself in the foot. We have
>one of the largest groups of programmers in the world, four times the
>installed code base of every other language combined, yet we don't have
>our own conference.

Agreed.

>Merant has a conference, but it's not a Cobol conference, it's a Merant
>conference. The same holds true for Acucorp and others.

Merant still allows 3rd party tool vendors to exhibit.

I'm not sure about Liant.

ICOBOL conducts an annual conference, but no 3rd party tool vendors.

Acucorp *used* to have a user conference with exhibitors and such, but
during the past few years, they have taken their show on the road and
had smaller regional user conferences.  They also don't allow 3rd
party vendors to exhibit at their regional conferences.

Computer Associates CA-World is great, but CA is so huge that COBOL is
just a small part of the conference.

I haven't heard anything about a Fujitsu conference.

I think that enough interest could be generated among the compiler
vendors and tool vendors that a significant portion of the cost of
such a conference could be shouldered by the exhibitor fees.

>Jave One, presented by Sun, has every major Java language supplier in
>the world. As a result it draws tremendous crowds, good press coverage,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>would not miss out.

I agree wholeheartedly!  When one vendor sponsors a conference, the
sessions generally only focus on the solutions offered by that single
vendor.  A "generic" conference allows for the presentation of all of
the potential solutions as well as discussion on COBOL issues in
general, such as the proposed ANSI standard.

It's good for the programmer, good for the vendors and good for the
language.

>I am very interested in your thoughts. If you would like to discuss this
>further you can reach me at earranga@objectz.com.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

Bob -

Thanks for the feedback and offer of support. Much appreciated.

Look at the effect the Open Source movement has had on the world of
software - a united group of folks who have re-written the rules. Why?
Strength in numbers and a passion for what they do. I don't believe most
software vendors understand the idea of passion. If you can't plug it
into a calculator, somehow it doesn't exist. Microsoft and Sun, on the
other hand, are two companies that do understand passion and how to
motivate their employees and markets accordingly. On the few occasions
I've met with Sun people it's like being in a room full of wovlerines.
As a Cobol supporter I didn't know if I'd have to mace a few of them
just to get out of the room with all my limbs. I do, however, admire
their results.

Best,
Edmund Arranga

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** earranga@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p86lk$ejb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b340e9.34710778@news.enter.net> <7p5gur$nq0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b7561d.1406955@news.enter.net>`

```
In article <37b7561d.1406955@news.enter.net>,
  rtwolfe@flexus.com wrote:
> earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >software - a united group of folks who have re-written the rules.
Why?
> >Strength in numbers and a passion for what they do. I don't believe
most
> >software vendors understand the idea of passion. If you can't plug it
> >into a calculator, somehow it doesn't exist. Microsoft and Sun, on
the
> >other hand, are two companies that do understand passion and how to
> >motivate their employees and markets accordingly. On the few
occasions
> >I've met with Sun people it's like being in a room full of
wovlerines.
> >As a Cobol supporter I didn't know if I'd have to mace a few of them
> >just to get out of the room with all my limbs. I do, however, admire
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bob -

Bingo! You make some telling points. The Open Source movement flourishes
because everyone comes together for the good of the overall community =
a "general" COBOL conference. When a single vendor controls the show it
reflects the single vendor's point of view. Not exactly the stimulating
environment we need to encourage COBOL folks to go out and "conquer the
world."

It's almost like the major COBOL language vendors are a bunch of
squabbling kids who have to be put in a room and told to play "nice"
with each other for their own good. Take TCP/IP. A simple protocol
designed far enough from the conflicted interests of the mainstream
where it couldn't be screwed up by tribal warfare. Same thing with HTTP
and HTML. The benefits? Unmatched wealth and prosperity for the very
companies who make the Balkans seem like one big happy family.

Many software vendors seem to see the market as finite,(here's your
slice)instead of almost infinite in size. In the past 40 odd years we
have only digitized the tip of the iceberg: 99.9 percent of the work
remains to be done.

Best,
Ed Arranga


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b80c13.2861011@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b340e9.34710778@news.enter.net> <7p5gur$nq0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b7561d.1406955@news.enter.net> <7p86lk$ejb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
earranga@my-deja.com wrote:

[snip]
>>
>Bob -
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed:

I know I am about to commit "vendor blashphemy" but I believe for the
language to continue to flourish, it is extremely important to have a
well established set of standards.

When an industry is "vendor controlled" the vendor is the primary
beneficiary and the programmers may or may not be beneficiaries.  Even
though we sell tools, I believe that the industry, programmers and
vendors would be best served by limiting the tangential directions
which seem to be taking place.

If all the vendors each establish their own unique rules, language
standards will erode.  The vendors who have a strong market position
will thrive and the smaller, fringe vendors will struggle.

Many complain about a single vendor's operating system dominance, the
lack of choices and the "forced choices" which these vendors place on
the industry...but language dialect dominance will result in exactly
the same scenario.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** earranga@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p9tko$n73$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b340e9.34710778@news.enter.net> <7p5gur$nq0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b7561d.1406955@news.enter.net> <7p86lk$ejb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b80c13.2861011@news.enter.net>`

```
In article <37b80c13.2861011@news.enter.net>,
  rtwolfe@flexus.com wrote:
> earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Bingo! You make some telling points. The Open Source movement
flourishes
> >because everyone comes together for the good of the overall community
=
> >a "general" COBOL conference. When a single vendor controls the show
it
> >reflects the single vendor's point of view. Not exactly the
stimulating
> >environment we need to encourage COBOL folks to go out and "conquer
the
> >world."
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >where it couldn't be screwed up by tribal warfare. Same thing with
HTTP
> >and HTML. The benefits? Unmatched wealth and prosperity for the very
> >companies who make the Balkans seem like one big happy family.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bob -

I don't believe you have committed, to use your words, "vendor
blasphemy." I couldn't agree more about the need for having a "well
established set of standards." Microsoft is a perfect illustration
regarding your comment, "When an industry is 'vendor controlled' the
vendor is the primary beneficiary and the programmers may or may not be
beneficiaries." In the beginning Microsoft's de facto standard operating
system resulted in an overall benefit for the industry. It is not that
Windows9x/NT are "better" than other operating systems, it is that more
work can be performed on their platforms (and by more work I mean more
independent software vendors (ISVs) create applications to run on
Windows than they do for other platforms). This results in the economies
of scale that allow vendors to offer their applications at a very
reasonable price. Then greed comes calling.

Microsoft will not support Java or Linux because Microsoft cannot
control them. The result is fragmentation and a net loss to the software
industry. Languages in general and Cobol in particular follow the same
trajectory. Although Cobol is a de jure rather than a de facto standard
the slow pace of ANSI and the ISO have resulted in fragmentation within
the Cobol language - a net loss to the industry. Some Cobol vendors see
the fragmentation as something to exploit something that may help
earnings for one quarter, or one year, but will in the long run end up
hurting Cobol. Cobol's economies of scale, including a large talented
pool of programmers and a well-defined syntax helps business.
Fragmentation will hurt every Cobol vendor. In the final analysis,
Microsoft will not be able to control the software industry through
their de facto standards. It gives me pause to think any Cobol vendor
could achieve de facto standard dominance pushing their version of the
language.

Perhaps the Italian poet Luciano de Crescenzo said it best: "We are all
angels with only one wing. We can only fly while embracing each other."

Best regards,
Edmund Arranga


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** "Colleen R. Diaz" <collen.r.diaz@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B88D2C.B6483B2E@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Sounds like a wonderful idea to me.  Just remember who will be footing
the bill...  In many case it will be companies who send their programmer
employees.  the only way I can get boeing to pay my way to the Merant
conference is to stress that it conssists mostly of training.

While a few companies are willing to support a general COBOL conference
for the 'good of the word as we know it', most will not.  Most will want
to see what they are geting for their travel dollars, and I doubt that
anything other than education will sell to them.  this will then be a
tough sell, casue who needs more training in COBOL?  If I don't know it
after 30 years of using it, I'm never gonna learn it...!

Clearly the conference will have to have a bunch of new stuff like
object orientation, web application design, and such.  whee are you
going to find trainers and speakers for those, except from the
vendors...?

Still I hope it works, and If can be any service in planning or
implementation, let me know.  It is possible that you can rely on the
Micro focus users groups as souce of help as well.

earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> This question is for everyone who would care to respond. The question is
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** earranga@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pb49l$hi8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B88D2C.B6483B2E@boeing.com>`

```
In article <37B88D2C.B6483B2E@boeing.com>,
  "Colleen R. Diaz" <collen.r.diaz@boeing.com> wrote:
> Sounds like a wonderful idea to me.  Just remember who will be footing
> the bill...  In many case it will be companies who send their
programmer
> employees.  the only way I can get boeing to pay my way to the Merant
> conference is to stress that it conssists mostly of training.
>
> While a few companies are willing to support a general COBOL
conference
> for the 'good of the word as we know it', most will not.  Most will
want
> to see what they are geting for their travel dollars, and I doubt that
> anything other than education will sell to them.  this will then be a
> tough sell, casue who needs more training in COBOL?  If I don't know
it
> after 30 years of using it, I'm never gonna learn it...!
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Colleen -

Thanks for the input. Let me address your questions by way of a likely
scenario.

You work for Boeing so I'll bet you dollars to donuts you may still have
OS Cobol programs executing on your mainframes and you probably have VS
Cobol II programs executing. You may still have the OS Cobol compiler
and you probably have the VS Cobol II compiler (and I am not talking
just about Seattle, I'm talking about the data centers from St. Louis to
Philadephia that Boeing acquired in the past few years). IBM stopped
supporting OS Cobol in the 93-94 timeframe. Support for VS Cobol II will
be discontinued in 2001.

OS Cobol is IBM's implementation of the Cobol-68 standard. VS Cobol II
(version 3.2, I believe) is IBM's implementation of the Cobol-74
standard. Come 2001 unless you have migrated to Cobol for MVS you will
no longer have IBM support. IBM will be glad to sell you a special
support deal on their older compilers. In the early '90s it was $50,000
a year per site. I'm sure it's more expensive now.

What does all this have to do with a Cobol conference? The conference
I'm suggesting would be all about training and education. As Bob Wolfe
correctly points out this type of conference would not be dictated from,
"One vendor's point of view," but from the point of view of the
programmer. Ask the programmers you work with which version of Cobol
they use. The answers will probably be in the IBM vernacular, that is,
folks will say they use VS Cobol II without realizing that is a subset
of the Cobol-74 standard. IBM likes it that way. The more you talk in
terms of their products the more "stuck" you are in buying solutions
from them.

At a Cobol conference where the vendor did not control the show there
would be multiple tracks, one covering how to migrate to Cobol for MVS
(the IBM implementation of Cobol-2001). The track would demonstrate how
to migrate to half a dozen other solutions (non-IBM), each one competing
against the other for your business. There's probably a company out
there who would support older IBM Cobol compilers at a fraction of the
cost IBM charges. And a company that would migrate your entire code base
in a matter of weeks. A great position to be in for you. Check each
Boeing mainframe for OS Cobol and VS Cobol II and come 2001 unless you
have dumped them Boeing probably will pay in the neighborhood of
$200,000 or more a year in support fees, which is simply money down the
drain.

Ask IBM about their latest Cobol compiler. Ask them what's good about it
and what's not so good. Do you think you will get a honest
straightforward answer? Wouldn't you rather have an expert who was not
beholden to IBM for a paycheck answering your questions.

You say, "who needs more training in COBOL?" And the short answer is
everybody, and not just for the object-oriented and Web stuff. The next
Cobol standard, Cobol-2001, has more cool stuff than all the previous
Cobol standards combined. In fact, read the opening paragraph of the new
standard. It talks about how Cobol is no longer simply a business
language, it is now a general purpose programming language capable of
effectivly handling all kinds of new problems. There are pointers and
Boolean data types and a new data validation routine that is so powerful
it will knock your socks off, plus a host of other best-in-breed
language features.

Who will teach at the conference? I am the guest editor for the March
2000 issue of IEEE Software magazine. The entire issue is devoted to
Cobol. When the issue comes out take a look and you will see just how
many great things are going on with Cobol - and not one will be written
by a vendor.

Best regards,
Edmund Arranga
Editor - The COBOL Report
www.cobolreport.com



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B963B3.A6848DAB@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B88D2C.B6483B2E@boeing.com> <7pb49l$hi8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The hardest thing about putting on a conference such as this is figuring
out how to sell it to employers.  They don't see a need to send their
best programmers to learn Cobol.  Nor do they see a need to have their
best programmers take time off to work on the future of Cobol.   If they
have immediate plans on exploring some new aspect (OO, Cobol on the PC,
client-server etc.), then those key words might catch their eyes.  Or if
they had a couple of Cobol gurus and nobody else understood anything
about Cobol, they would accept their gurus' suggestion that they needed
training.

But if they have a bunch of Cobol programmers in a typical, stable
environment - where's the selling point?

I would enjoy going, but I have other plans for my vacation time and my
money.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** earranga@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pdh3u$9j1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B88D2C.B6483B2E@boeing.com> <7pb49l$hi8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B963B3.A6848DAB@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <37B963B3.A6848DAB@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> The hardest thing about putting on a conference such as this is
figuring
> out how to sell it to employers.  They don't see a need to send their
> best programmers to learn Cobol.  Nor do they see a need to have their
> best programmers take time off to work on the future of Cobol.   If
they
> have immediate plans on exploring some new aspect (OO, Cobol on the
PC,
> client-server etc.), then those key words might catch their eyes.  Or
if
> they had a couple of Cobol gurus and nobody else understood anything
> about Cobol, they would accept their gurus' suggestion that they
needed
> training.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would enjoy going, but I have other plans for my vacation time and
my
> money.
>
Howard -

I appreciate the feedback. To be perfectly honest, however, I am, more
than a little, nonplused by it. You mention a "typical, stable
environment," to which I can only reply - Huh? The amount of change in
our industry over the past 5 years is staggering. Where is this typical
environment and let me know if they are a public company so that I can
sell any stock I might have. We all need to brush up on our skills and
that goes for the best pilots, doctors, engineers, writers, poets, and
Cobol programmers. In fact, the best are the ones who would probably
gain the most from exposure to new ideas.

Professional Cobol programmers should be reading between 8-15 software
engineering books a year, several journals from the ACM and the IEEE and
others, plus attending at least one conference (at a minimum). I could
give you a litany of 100 things that I know I would like to know more
about. Exposure and absorption to new ideas are the lifeblood of our
profession.

Best regards,
Edmund Arranga

> Howard -
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> gain the most from exposure to new ideas.

I don't disagree, but I do not control the purse strings.
 
> Professional Cobol programmers should be reading between 8-15 software
> engineering books a year, several journals from the ACM and the IEEE and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> profession.

It doesn't help to give me such a litany, I don't control the purse
strings.  Management needs to be aware of such conferences before they
create a budget, and they need to have some kind of idea of the
cost/benefit of budgeting people to attend such conferences.

I'm not sure how to grab their attention, nor how to persuade them after
their attention has been grabbed.  I felt a victory persuading them to
subscribe to The Cobol Report (duplicating my personal subscription). 
(I hope this subscription isn't wasted money - I don't know if any other
programmers read it).  Only a couple of my employers in the past have
been willing to pay for my DPMA/AITP membership.  General knowledge is a
hard sell when everything you do has a budget code on it.
 
> Best regards,
> Edmund Arranga

Best regards, and success in this endeavor,
Howard Brazee

earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Howard -
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> gain the most from exposure to new ideas.

The rate of change in the industry is not the same as the rate of change
in many shops.  Yes, we all know there are great new tools out there,
and we should be learning new skills all the time.  But many, many
shops  just can't re-invent themselves to take advantage of the latest
tool or technique.  (Look at all the shops still coding to COBOL-74
standards.)  "Typical and stable" doesn't necessarily mean "doing
nothing."  It often means "working feverishly" within realistic time and
budget constraints.  I don't think Howard is off the mark on this.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Conference? What do you think?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BC0CA1.FC67A9AD@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7ooba4$lbp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B88D2C.B6483B2E@boeing.com> <7pb49l$hi8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37B963B3.A6848DAB@NOSPAMhome.com> <7pdh3u$9j1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
earranga@my-deja.com wrote:
..snip.. 
> Professional Cobol programmers should be reading between 8-15 software
> engineering books a year, several journals from the ACM and the IEEE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> our profession.

Ed,

It is my experience that very few cobol programmers actually read
anything related to programming.  Unfortunately it is perceived as a
backwater and therefore those that are really interested in advancing
their career change to C or the latest silver bullet (e.g. power
builder).

After going to a major site of extremely well paid developers I would
estimate that the average for the programmers there would be less than
1 technical book per year and never reading the ACM or IEEE (I rarely
read them).

I have gone to a lower paid site and the programmers there are largely
care taking. Not really interested in advancing their skills just
grinding out solutions.

Two cents
Ken
```

ray

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ftp client

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u5Ns3.4605$TM2.86670@viper>`
- **References:** `<37b07473$0$19550@fountain.mindlink.net> <z%%r3.7$km4.468@iad-read.news.verio.net> <37b0b195$0$19550@fountain.mindlink.net> <yF2s3.101$km4.4039@iad-read.news.verio.net> <37b188e6$0$19543@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
>Yes I am looking for an ftp client that has drag and drop features.


All applications including DOS have drag and drop functionality through what
used to be called file associations in the windows explorer. These can be
created to have different actions associated with each file extension you
associate it with. If a file is simply dropped onto a DOS application the
file name is passed as if you typed the EXE name followed by the dropped
file name. In most instances in 8.3 format.

Drag and drop functionality works with nearly every application I have
tried. You probably need to figure out what information needs to be passed
to the application itself and what parameters are valid.

>The ftp also needs to have a GUI.
>I would not ask for an ftp client if ftp.exe was acceptable.
>I am looking for a product like smartftp, ftpexplorer or cuteftp.
>Something that allows me to call it from my application.
>I have already tried the above without success.


FTP.EXE may still be applicable. Changing the commands within the FTP
command file passed to FTP may be your only problem.

I think what you are specifically looking for is an FTP program which can be
connected to and watched to determine when file transfers are complete, and
reading transfer rate statistics. This may be able to be done through this
same app, but finding documentation on such a thing will definitely be
difficult.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ftp client

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.121eb0dd8dcbc983989692@news>`
- **References:** `<u5Ns3.4605$TM2.86670@viper> <19990813113019.11691.00000327@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
In article <19990813113019.11691.00000327@ngol04.aol.com>, 
> My interest in this is in the hopes of putting together an FTP Server/XFER
> Server
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> together.

The MS FTP client won't help you with the server portion but will work 
fine for the client. Do an "FTP -?" at the command prompt to get a list 
of options (see the -s:<filename> switch). Just put any FTP command 
inside the redirect file.

If you're using IIS for the server, the log is pretty easy to decode and 
flexible in what it can capture. I am using the log file to send email 
transfer confirmations and update an ISAM log file. I could dig up a 
sasmple if you need.

If you want to roll your own client, look at Ftp4w (I mentioned before 
but forgot to include the URL). It is a freeware library so no 
royalties. I use the companion library TCP4U with provides a good 
sockets library. The documentation and zip file can be found at:
http://home.rol3.com/~u0389227/ftp4w302.htm
http://home.rol3.com/~u0389227/ftp4w302.zip

Hope this helps...
ray
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ftp client

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nw0u3.6031$TM2.145350@viper>`
- **References:** `<u5Ns3.4605$TM2.86670@viper> <19990813113019.11691.00000327@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
The most significant problem I ever ran into was getting the USER ID and
password from a shell extension, or file association. Assuming you set up a
generic UID and PW the following is typically what I do:

-------------------------------
rem %1 = user id %2= password %3 = mainframe file name
rem 000.000.000.000=ftp address of ftp server

del "ftpcmds.ftp"
echo %1 > ftpcmds.ftp
echo %2 >> ftpcmds.ftp
echo get '%3' "%3.txt"  >> ftpcmds.ftp
echo quit  >> ftpcmds.ftp
ftp -i -s:ftpcmds.ftp 000.000.000.000
--------------------------------

Assuming above BAT file is named "ftpget.bat" the file association would
refer to the BAT file as:
"ftpget.bat" ANONYMOUS GUEST TEST.DATA.FILE

Where
ANONYMOUS = user id
GUEST = user password
TEST.DATA.FILE = mainframe file name

If you are looking to transfer from the mainframe to a PC server, you would
have to have the command file set up then run the mainframe FTP command,
typically FTP. Try scanning your current mainframe JCL and PROCS for
examples.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

pieces" of information in the MVS (OS/390) control blocks - in VS COBOL II
or later versions of IBM mainframe COBOL.

Gilbert Saint-flour has kindly provided sample code to do this - in the NG -
on several occasions.  However, he has now put it out on his web-page.  I
plan to add a reference to this in the FAQ (in the near future) - but also
figured it would be "nice" if this could be found in Deja.COM - sooner
rather than later.  Therefore,  please see

  http://members.home.net/gsf/tools/cob2job.txt

for the appropriate sample source code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Move the mouse pointer

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-08-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BD6AD6.3F694D90@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<19990810215002.27448.00000512@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
Hi Bob,

Use the SetCursorPos:

=== Start quote from SDK help files ==============
The SetCursorPos function moves the cursor to the specified screen
coordinates. If the new coordinates are not within the screen rectangle
set by the most recent ClipCursor function, Windows automatically
adjusts the coordinates so that the cursor stays within the rectangle. 
BOOL SetCursorPos(
    int X,	// horizontal position  
    int Y 	// vertical position
   );	
 
Parameters
X
Specifies the new x-coordinate, in screen coordinates, of the cursor. 
Y
Specifies the new y-coordinate, in screen coordinates, of the cursor. 
 
Return Values
If the function succeeds, the return value is nonzero.
If the function fails, the return value is zero. To get extended error
information, call GetLastError. 
Remarks
The cursor is a shared resource. A window should move the cursor only
when the cursor is in its client area. 
The calling process must have WINSTA_WRITEATTRIBUTES access to the
window station. 
=== End   quote from SDK help files ==================

I remember myself programming in client coordinates, not screen
coordinates. So before the call to SetCursorPos, first call
ClientToScreen. (The GetCursorPos also returns in screen coordinates and
needs the ScreenToClient afterwards).

=== Start quote from SDK help files ==================
The ClientToScreen function converts the client coordinates of a
specified point to screen coordinates. 
BOOL ClientToScreen(
    HWND hWnd,	// window handle for source coordinates 
    LPPOINT lpPoint 	// pointer to structure containing screen
coordinates  
   );	
 
Parameters
hWnd
Identifies the window whose client area is used for the conversion. 
lpPoint
Points to a POINT structure that contains the client coordinates to be
converted. The new screen coordinates are copied into this structure if
the function succeeds.
 
Return Values
If the function succeeds, the return value is nonzero.
If the function fails, the return value is zero.
Remarks
The ClientToScreen function replaces the client coordinates in the POINT
structure with the screen coordinates. The screen coordinates are
relative to the upper-left corner of the screen. 
See Also MapWindowPoints, POINT, ScreenToClient 
=== End   quote from SDK help files ==================

Jump Luck,

The Frog

Bob7536 wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bob Hennessey
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Move the mouse pointer

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move the mouse pointer

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7psidd$d45$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37BD6AD6.3F694D90@IMN.nl> <19990823204958.23592.00003575@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
These are the definitions used for Windows (from the
Windows Platform SDK).

"A cursor is a small picture whose location on the screen
is controlled by a pointing device, such as a mouse, pen,
or trackball. In the remainder of this overview, the term
mouse refers to any pointing device."

"A caret is a flashing line, block, or bitmap in the client
area of a window. The caret typically indicates the place
at which text or graphics will be inserted."

Use SetCursorPos to move the mouse pointer.
Use SetCaretPos to move the insertion point for text.
------------------
Rick Smith

Bob7536 wrote in message <19990823204958.23592.00003575@ng-fs1.aol.com>...

>

to
>move the mouse pointer not the cursor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move the mouse pointer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pu3me$26h3$1@news.hitter.net>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37C29861.82DD78DE@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>a SQL context.

No, but since you mentioned it, I have to look for it everwhere.
Interestingly, I pulled this from the Microsoft Bookshelf 98
dictionary.
----------
cursor (k�r�ser) noun
Computer Science.
A bright, usually blinking, movable indicator on a display,
marking the position at which a character can be entered,
corrected, or deleted.

[Middle English, runner, from Latin, from cursus, past participle
of currere, to run.]
----------
This is almost the same definition as given for "caret" in the
Windows Platform SDK. Furthermore, I found no reference
to SQL cursors.

The change in usage of "cursor" and the addition of "caret"
may have originated with the Apple Macintosh.

Nontheless, for text screens, the "cursor control keys" still
provide a useful means for "running" through text to place
the cursor, I mean caret, at a new position.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-a8bf5396f6"></a>
## Thread: Update on what happens to data to the right of the right margin — 23 msgs

### Update on what happens to data to the right of the right margin

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
A while ago, I put out a note about the draft Standard seeming to be a
"problem" for IBM mainframe programmers and others who put data between the
R-margin and the end of the physical source line (i.e., columns 72-80 on IBM
mainframes - and many other punched-card image implementations),  I just
thought I would pass along the PROPOSED change that is happening (that in my
opinion) is a GREAT improvement.

The '85 Standard says,
   "Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character position
of a line."

The (still in proposal state) draft says,
  "Margin R is a fixed number defined by the implementor that is immediately
to the right of the rightmost character position of the program-text area."

I think the "new wording" FINALLY officially allows implementors to define
the "real source portion of a line" (what the draft Standard calls
"program-text area") as ending BEFORE the physical end of a source line.

I should also note, that in other proposal(s), they are getting rid of
almost any "requirements" about what a listing needs to include - which have
always been pretty minimal anyway.  This means that implementors will be
able to do "whatever they think best" with the data from the "old" sequence
columns and from after the R-margin.  I think that this too will bring the
draft Standard into line with what implementors ACTUALLY DO (and what
programmers expect).
```

#### ↳ Re: Update on what happens to data to the right of the right margin

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7osieh$ct9$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```

immediately
>to the right of the rightmost character position of the program-text area."
[...]
>I should also note, that in other proposal(s), they are getting rid of
>almost any "requirements" about what a listing needs to include - which
have
>always been pretty minimal anyway. [...]

I like it! It is better than what I had in mind.

I had not yet sent my comments to J4 because I wanted to
review the prior documents; but, if your happy, I'm happy.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```

number as being space filled to the required length (and thus
mess up continuation of non-numeric literals)?

If so I don't see this as a good idea. You should at least have

that may be.

Tim Josling

> The (still in proposal state) draft says,
>   "Margin R is a fixed number defined by the implementor that is immediately
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Update on what happens to data to the right of the right margin

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au...
> William M. Klein wrote:
> So does this mean the implementer will have to chop the code off
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That is the way that the R-margin has *ALWAYS* worked in Standard COBOL.
Remember that "traditional" COBOL has ONLY supported "Fixed length" source
lines (none of this "fancy" line sequential or variable stuff).  On the
other hand, the draft Standard *does* introduce a NEW format that is
variable - and does mean that the compiler uses WHATEVER "source line" is
passed to it (with a few minor restriction).

Assuming today, you are using a PC or UNIX compiler today that defines the
R-margin as column 72 (or 80), and you pass it two source lines like,

      05    Long-Data-Item   Pic  X(100)    Value "123
 -            "456".
         ....
         Display Long-Data-Item

You will (or should) see that you get "lots" of spaces between the "3" and
the "4" - even if your "CRLF" is right after the "3".  This is becuae the
COBOL Standard *requires* that the alphanumeric literal be "filled with
spaces" out to the (virtual) R-margin - in fixed format (traditional) source
code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Variable length records & "fancy" code

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Remember that "traditional" COBOL has ONLY supported "Fixed length" source
> lines (none of this "fancy" line sequential or variable stuff).

Changing the topic.  I was thinking that variable length records are far
less common than they used to be.  They were more popular when storage
costs were high and databases were less common.  Lots of shops created
files with header and detail records which could be used like variable
length records anyway - but even these are being replaced by database
structures.

Several "fancy" techniques designed to save space and/or CPU time are
being replaced by simpler techniques designed for clarity and ease of
maintenance.  (hiding some complexity behind commercial database
internals).  This relates to the thread on subscripts vs indices, as
well as to some of our structured coding discussions.

I also see fewer real large programs.  A coworker had to migrate a
complex change to a program with 4 sorts and 5 output files - after he
analyzed it, he found that he could make several programs with simple
match merges and external sorts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records & "fancy" code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I don't disagree with your basic premise (too much) - but I am curious as to
WHY we keep getting so many IBM Mainframe programmers asking about "FS=39" -
if they aren't using variable length records?  I think that the problem (?)
is all those "legacy" systems with all those "legacy" files still being used
and promulgated.

P.S.  For those who missed it, my original note was talking about SOURCE
code - but this new thread is talking about "input/output" files.  I
understand the "shift" in topic - but if others think that "line sequential"
isn't being used for source code, they are mistaken.  Actually, it is still
being used for "printed output" files too - but not all that often for
storing data or passing it from program to program or application to
application.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records & "fancy" code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** kfeiler@cpiusa.com (Kent Feiler)
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b6e6a0.4066046@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Fri, 13 Aug 1999 13:58:33 -0500, "William M. Klein" 
>P.S.  For those who missed it, my original note was talking about SOURCE
>code - but this new thread is talking about "input/output" files.  I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>application.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

The real issue here is program editors. Unless you have a
cobol-specific editor they all use variable length records and when
you insert 10 characters on an 80 char line you end up with a 90 char
line that wraps around on the screen and listings.  That's the real
issue, saving disk space on source code files is an insignificant
consideration and heaven help the cobol community if variable length
source code records could be described as "fancy code".

Regards,



Kent Feiler
kfeiler@cpiusa.com
http://www.enteract.com/~kfeiler/mypage.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records & "fancy" code

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p70c7$r13$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37b6e6a0.4066046@news.enteract.com>`

```
This raises an issue that has crossed my  mind because one of the first
things we did in implementing a source program library was to construct a
file handler for the library that contained a compress and expand function
to considerably reduce the space requirements on the DASD.  With the
considerable change in systems from the 70's, I wondered if the big iron
does this a: as part of the OS, or as a vendor supplied utility, or b: do
the PC users even think about this.

We found that just replacing the reserved words with a code saved much
space, and the conversion could be in I/O time.

Any updates for this old man?

Warren Simmons

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records & "fancy" code

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p77p8$idk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37b6e6a0.4066046@news.enteract.com> <7p70c7$r13$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:7p70c7$r13$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net...
> This raises an issue that has crossed my  mind because one of the first
> things we did in implementing a source program library was to construct a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any updates for this old man?

I can't speak for other mainframes, but for IBM mainframe users (most
specifically OS/390 and MVS - not as true for VSE or VM but they have
similar situation too),

A) Most (well at least many) sites still use 3rd party "source control
products" (LIBRARIAN, PANVALET, ENDEVOR, etc) which ALL have "built-in"
compression routines - as well as "generation" storage and recovery, and
lots of other "goodies".  The last time I looked (many years ago) PANVALET
had a storage type of "COBOL" - but hadn't been updated for ANSI'85 (for
example, it still thought that all source code was all upper-case)

B) ISPF (formerly PDF and before that ISPF or SPF) editor has a "packed"
option - for files (source and otherwise).

C) Mainframe disk storage is really considered "cheap" - so even though all
these systems have "compression" methods, it really isn't a "big deal".
```

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I can't speak for other mainframes, but for IBM mainframe users (most
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> "big deal".

SMS  (storage management system i think) provides compressed datasets
directly.  No need for third party compression.

Most harddisk arrays are compressed, so it is not worth the CPU time
compressing the data anyway.

Ken
```

- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37b6e6a0.4066046@news.enteract.com> <7p70c7$r13$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7p77p8$idk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7p77p8$idk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com...
>
> I can't speak for other mainframes, but for IBM mainframe users (most
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    I currently use an old source code control system not for source code
control,
but for archival purposes.  To handle this sort of problem, I preprocess the
source
code, blanking cols 1-6 and 73-80, setting all source code to upper case,
removing
stray characters that disturb the SCCS (MF allows cntl/z embedded in the
source code).

    This minimizes the size of the archive, while allowing retrieval on the
rare ocassions
it is done.  The source code upper case utility was adapted from a MF demo
program.
The missing control characters are normally named in comments, and can be
found
in the current source code.

    Some of this might be helpful even with a "good" SCCS, such as
automatically uppercasing
all source code in the above example.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<37B80DA7.92DD8A0B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37b6e6a0.4066046@news.enteract.com> <7p70c7$r13$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7p77p8$idk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> B) ISPF (formerly PDF and before that ISPF or SPF) editor has a "packed"
> option - for files (source and otherwise).

I have noticed that I can type all of them from the TSO command line,
provided I am in an environment where I can get to the TSO command line
(but outside of PDF).  But every place I have worked for the last 20
years or so, employees just use the word TSO to describe ISPF.

The only reason I ever leave PDF without wanting to leave TSO is to
print my list log.  Most places that gets me out of TSO as well. 
Currently I stay in TSO, but I don't know how to get back to our
standard menu from there.  Entering PDF, ISPF, or SPF puts me into IBM's
standard ISPF menu, skipping our site menu (which contains Endeavor, JCL
check, ISPF, SDSF, and other stuff I don't use).

I don't think I have EVER heard anybody use the term PDF.
```

on the command line.

I'm rusty on the details but you can usually find out by looking at your
logon proc and then the CList or REXX exec that it points to and following
those until you get to the initial ISPF call. There are some dialog
variables that contain stuff like logon proc name, etc. use 7.3 I think to
see those, and also SDSF might show it on a display of your TSO session.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records & "fancy" code

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-08-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C1B09D.1EA4A747@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7osjt1$iji@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37B3FCE3.2D3315AF@melbpc.org.au> <7p1lo9$mie@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <37B4637F.7790422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7p1pqe$bt3@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37b6e6a0.4066046@news.enteract.com> <7p70c7$r13$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7p77p8$idk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <37B80DA7.92DD8A0B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
ISPF has two valuable global commands: LIST and LOG. They work from
everywhere after the
COMMAND ==>
or
OPTION ==>
and give you a menu screen what to do with the list/log.

After that, the ISPF session continues.
This is all STANDARD ISPF behaviour, use it!

(Stil hundreds of people out there using ISPF as if it has no recursive
EDIT, BROWSE, CUT PASTE etc, using it as if it were stil 1975 or so ...
shame!)

The Frog

Not insolvable, but another problem I see is switching between editors.
I use NetExpress V3 which has a good editor in so far as it highlights
RESERVED words, Level 78s', typos in red, and many other options.... -
but I find it 'clunky' - For preference I still use the Microsoft
Programmer's Workbench Editor which came with their COBOL V5. It's DOS
text, gives me full screen, highlight a copyfile and I can open it, or
highlight and use F3 or F4 for forwards/backwards search/search and
replace. 

NetExpress happily accepts my source from M/S. The M/S Editor is generic
(includes all languages they offer), but you can set it to COBOL format.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-d181f47a64"></a>
## Thread: Who can support Cobol II Compiler? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Who can support Cobol II Compiler?

- **From:** mwhold@aol.com (Mwhold)
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990811230539.05634.00000353@ng-fm1.aol.com>`

```
My company is currently running applications developed with the IBM Cobol and
Cobol II compilers.  We have no plans to upgrade to Cobol 390 before 2000. 
SInce IBM has dropped support, or is dropping support for all Cobol compilers
except Cobol 390, are there any companies out there that will provide support
if there are issues with these non-compliant compilers?  Thanks.  Anyone can
respond to mwholdren@fhsc.com.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who can support Cobol II Compiler?

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Oi5yESH5#GA.154@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<19990811230539.05634.00000353@ng-fm1.aol.com>`

```
I don't know. It depends on what you mean by support, this is probably not
what you'd want to hear: depending on your situation, we could help on a
case-by-case basis, but with no guarantees. Well, we might go so far as to
guarantee that we won't add any new bugs, as long as we're allowed enough
time to do as thorough a job as we're comfortable with. Of course we won't
guarantee full Y2K compliance of your whole system - it's far too late to
start a project of that magnitude.

In other words if a Cobol program starts failing we'd be happy to modify it
as needed to get it to work properly, assuming that that's possible despite
your using old software, which is usually possible if you approach each
case individually as I suggest. Are you running everything under LE? Is
every program compiled under Cobol II or later? I'm not saying those are
absolute requirements but they would be helpful. If not, we can help with
your migration process to that point while we look at any problems. We'd be
happy to look at your situation either way.

Eventually you'll likely reach the point where to keep everything running
OK you'll need/want to use Cobol/390 - unless by then you also happen to
reach a point where you'll be wanting to move everything to a different
platform anyway. Of course we could help with that also.

What city are you in?


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
