#### ↳ Re: Who can support Cobol II Compiler?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oth70$78n@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990811230539.05634.00000353@ng-fm1.aol.com>`

```
Mwhold <mwhold@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990811230539.05634.00000353@ng-fm1.aol.com...
> My company is currently running applications developed with the IBM Cobol
and
> Cobol II compilers.  We have no plans to upgrade to Cobol 390 before 2000.
> SInce IBM has dropped support, or is dropping support for all Cobol
compilers
> except Cobol 390, are there any companies out there that will provide
support
> if there are issues with these non-compliant compilers?  Thanks.  Anyone
can
> respond to mwholdren@fhsc.com.

Just because your note might cause some confusion for some readers (who
haven't read the 15 or 20 times I have posted this already)

1) IBM has stated that VS COBOL II, Release 4  *WILL* be supported thru
March 2001 (not 2000 - or 1/1/00 - or any other date). This applies to the
US.  There may be a different date in some other country/counties.

2) IBM has stated that object code CREATED with the VS COBOL II compiler -
and run with the LE run-time library (either RES or linked with it if NORES)
will be supported "indefinitely".  (Assuming they don't try and use any
software/hardware interfaces that are introduced AFTER March 2001 - or that
the program uses some technique which is already documented as "illegal".)

3) Although VS COBOL II R4 is NOT on the Y2K "ready" list of IBM, there are
no KNOWN problems with running it after 1/1/00.  HOWEVER, the dates that it
produces (ACCEPT FROM DATE, WHEN-COMPILED paragraph, and CURRENT-DATE
special register, etc) all use 2-digit years - not 4-digit years.

4) if by "IBM Cobol" compiler, you mean OS/VS COBOL compiler, then you are
already in "deep do-do".  That compiler hasn't been supported for years
(already) - but once again - OBJECT code created by it (which doesn't use
"new" hardware/software interfaces) *IS SUPPORTED* - if you are using the LE
run-time library - with a couple of obscure exceptions such as odd
Communications Sections.

5) Finally, what do you mean by "support" for the OLD compilers?  OS/VS
COBOL *was* provided with source code and a logic manual.  I suppose someone
COULD try and actually provide "fixes" to the compiler if you really paid
them enough. On the other hand, VS COBOL II is - and always has been - OCO
(object code only) so no one is ever going to be able to give you a "code
fix" if something in it "breaks" after 3/31/01.  If someone tells you that
they can, I sure wouldn't "buy a bridge" (or a used car) from them.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who can support Cobol II Compiler?

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bee8fd$6d45bf50$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<19990811230539.05634.00000353@ng-fm1.aol.com>`

```
Mwhold <mwhold@aol.com> wrote in article
<19990811230539.05634.00000353@ng-fm1.aol.com>...
> My company is currently running applications developed with the IBM Cobol
and
> Cobol II compilers.  We have no plans to upgrade to Cobol 390 before
2000. 
> SInce IBM has dropped support, or is dropping support for all Cobol
compilers
> except Cobol 390, are there any companies out there that will provide
support
> if there are issues with these non-compliant compilers?  Thanks.  Anyone
can
> respond to mwholdren@fhsc.com.  

The following views are my understanding:

IBM will support the Cobol II compiler for a while after 2000.  It will
probably work much longer than that.

If other companies were to support IBM's compiler, they must have access to
the source code.  It is not obvious that they will get it.

If you by compliant compiler mean a compiler that generates compliant
object code, then COBOL 390 is not much more compliant than Cobol II.  It
has some features that will help you write compliant programs.  Putting
your old cobol source through the Cobol 390 compiler will not in general
produce more compliant object code.

To make your program compliant you have to do some changes.  It may be
easier to do this with the Cobol 390 compiler than with the Cobol II
compiler.  But still you have to do it.

Gunnar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: can not load a DLL with LoadLibrary

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.vc.language,microsoft.public.win32.programmer.kernel
- **Message-ID:** `<7ov16s$988$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7om0rv$vq5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <O3$kXN$4#GA.288@cppssbbsa05> <7orvep$7to$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Roland,

I would strongly discourage you from specifying a COBOL program or entry
point in the /ENTRY statement when creating a COBOL DLL. The entry, as has
been mentioned in a separate note in this thread, is used for DLL
initialisation and terminationn and the MF product actually contains code to
do that. If you create a DLL using CBLLINK you should notice (if you use the
/V option) that it specifies /ENTRY:LibMain@12. The code that gets used will
ensure that a DLL initialises and terminates cleanly and that any programs
in the DLL are properly CANCELed, including freeing memory etc. By
specifying your own entry you prevent that code from being used and so
CANCELs may not work and you may have memory problems resulting in 114
errors.


dnalor@my-deja.com wrote in message <7orvep$7to$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <O3$kXN$4#GA.288@cppssbbsa05>,
>  "Lior" <lior@mail.usa.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: can not load a DLL with LoadLibrary

- **From:** "Ron Ruble" <raffles1@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.vc.language,microsoft.public.win32.programmer.kernel
- **Message-ID:** `<7oueo9$2ot$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7om0rv$vq5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <O3$kXN$4#GA.288@cppssbbsa05> <7orvep$7to$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

dnalor@my-deja.com wrote in message <7orvep$7to$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <O3$kXN$4#GA.288@cppssbbsa05>,
>  "Lior" <lior@mail.usa.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the cobol-progs name in the program-id. is roland .-)


I know nothing about MF-Cobol, but I wanted
to clarify something. Linker /entry:roland tells
the linker that the entry point for the DLL,
i.e. the function to invoke when the DLL is
loaded with LoadLibrary, is called roland, and
has a signature equivalent to this C declaration:

long _stdcall DllMain (void  *hInst,
        long ul_reason_for_call, void * lpReserved)

Does MF-Cobol generate such an entry-point
function for you with the name the same as the
program-id?

If not, run DUMPBIN /exports (a VC utility; you'll
see it in the docs) on the MF-Cobol DLL and see
if you can find a function that may be the entry-point.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: can not load a DLL with LoadLibrary

- **From:** dnalor@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.vc.language,microsoft.public.win32.programmer.kernel
- **Message-ID:** `<7oulhq$5oh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7om0rv$vq5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <O3$kXN$4#GA.288@cppssbbsa05> <7orvep$7to$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7oueo9$2ot$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```

lucky guy! :)

> to clarify something. Linker /entry:roland tells
> long _stdcall DllMain (void  *hInst,
>         long ul_reason_for_call, void * lpReserved)
Yes, i know this, these are the 12 bytes. But thank u 4 this hint!
And thanks for the DUMPBIN-hint!!!

> function for you with the name the same as the
> program-id?
yes. In the meantime, I CAN(!) load it with loadlibrary() :-))
```

<a id="t-974f800ca7"></a>
## Thread: Calling an Oracle Stored Procedure — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Calling an Oracle Stored Procedure

- **From:** Christina Candlena <candlena@acsu.buffalo.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7osjpe$g4m$1@prometheus.acsu.buffalo.edu>`

```
I am trying to call an Oracle Stored Procedure.  I am receiving an Ora900
Invalid sql statement with the following code and compiled with
sqlcheck=syntax
exec sql
   exec addrupd
     :variable
end-exec.

All of my other sql statements work fine.

If I change to compile with sqlcheck=semantics
  The compile flags all my end-execs as errors.  If I remove them and place
an ; at the end of sql statement.  My pre-compile works, but my compile
fails with a message "No SQL directives have been set"

Any help is greatly appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling an Oracle Stored Procedure

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b39fbb@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7osjpe$g4m$1@prometheus.acsu.buffalo.edu>`

```

Christina Candlena wrote in message
<7osjpe$g4m$1@prometheus.acsu.buffalo.edu>...
>I am trying to call an Oracle Stored Procedure.  I am receiving an Ora900
>Invalid sql statement with the following code and compiled with
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Christina,

This may be of no help as it's quite a while since I worked on Oracle and I
don't have a manual handy, but I think most dialects of SQL require a
"using" with EXECUTE.
DB2 has NO "using" if EXECUTE IMMEDIATE is used, but it does if EXECUTE is
used.

Long shot but try:

>exec sql
>   exec addrupd using
>     :variable
>end-exec.

EXECUTE is usually used with dynamic SQL to action a PREPARED statement.
ORACLE stretches the point to include not just a dynamic SQL statement but a
series of statements (stored procedure). It may therefore be necessary to
put the procedure name in quotes ...?

Do let us know what the solution is when you get there.

Pete.
```

## Thread: Cariage return line feed — 5 msgs

### Cariage return line feed

- **From:** yatno@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am using COBOL/400 standard (not ILE) in AS400
what i need is how to fill Cariage return line feed (break page) in
working-storage value.
i am trying using alt+012 and alt+014, it doesn`t work  :-)



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cariage return line feed

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G26s3.157$km4.8884@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <yatno@my-deja.com> wrote:
>I am using COBOL/400 standard (not ILE) in AS400
>what i need is how to fill Cariage return line feed (break page) in
>working-storage value.
>i am trying using alt+012 and alt+014, it doesn`t work  :-)

01  WS-CRLF.
    05  WS-CRLF-CR PIC X VALUE X'0D'.
    05  WS-CRLF-LF PIC X VALUE X'0A'.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Cariage return line feed

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b180eb@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

yatno@my-deja.com wrote in message <7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I am using COBOL/400 standard (not ILE) in AS400
>what i need is how to fill Cariage return line feed (break page) in
>working-storage value.
>i am trying using alt+012 and alt+014, it doesn`t work  :-)
>

Try this:
01   HexCR        pic xx value x'0D0A'.

If the AS400 compiler wont accept a hex literal try this:

01  NumericCR  pic s9(4) binary value 3338.
01 NumCR  redefines NumericCR pic xx.

You can then use NumCR or HexCR in STRING statements to cause a carriage
return.

like this:

move spaces to message-text
string
   'This is a message....'
           delimited by size
   HexCR
           delimited by size
  'This is line 2 of the same message...'
           delimited by size
                 into message-text
end-string

Hope this helps,

Pete.



>
>
>Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cariage return line feed

- **From:** "Jonathan Roberts" <zimboon @ hotmail . com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b1d947$0$19549@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I usually make a 78 level for these.

78  const-lf      value X'0A'.
78  const-cr    value X'0D'.

RBA.

> I am using COBOL/400 standard (not ILE) in AS400
> what i need is how to fill Cariage return line feed (break page) in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cariage return line feed

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7osm5b$hbe@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7oqhj0$8ok$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37b1d947$0$19549@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
EXTENSION ALERT (suggestion does not match the question's compiler)

The original question talked about COBOL/400.  The answer uses a level-78.
These two items do NOT match (unless COBOL/400 has had a change that I don't
know about).

You can use a 77-level in COBOL/400 (or any other "conforming" compiler) -
and you can "use it" as a constant - even if that isn't what it is required
to be.  (Then again, you can use an 01-level, or an 02 thru 49-level as a
constant.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Grammar/standard question

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```
    I have a better idea.  If you know where the original programmer lives,
write your question on a piece of paper, wrap it around a brick, and throw
it thru the window.  Then rewrite the flipping code, using performs or
whatever it takes to remove the ambiguity.  (See also following terror)

>
> Read File1
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    If the original programmer still works for you, a firing squad might be
in order.



William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7nskhk$42u@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com...
> There have been a variety of "interpretation requests" handled by J4 in
this
> area - but to summarize what the Standard says (according to J4 - but in
my
> words).
>
> 1) There are only 2 places in the Standard where a conditional statement
may
> be used: In an IF and in a SEARCH statement.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3) A sentence ends in a PERIOD (full-stop).  (This is why NEXT SENTENCE
and
> CONTINUE have such different meanings).
>
> 4) Within the conditional phrases (ON SIZE, NOT ON SIZE, ON EXCEPTION, NOT
> ON EXCEPTION, etc)  you *MUST* have an imperative statement.  Therefore,
if
> another verb appears withOUT its scope terminator (end-xxx) then you are
> starting another nesting level. If you have a conditional form WITH a
scope
> terminator then it is treated as part of the series of imperatives.
Looking
> at one of your examples,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> That NOT ON SIZE *must* go with the 2nd ADD if there is no END-ADD  by
your
> period but  must go with the 1st ADD if the END-ADD is there.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is "quite complex" but completely consistent part of the Standard
(and
> is consistent with the '74 and '68 Standards which didn't have END-xxx
> terminators).
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-48d487dbf9"></a>
## Thread: Indexed File Errors — 24 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Indexed File Errors

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>`

```
I am working my way through Thane Hubbell's TY COBOL book in
preparation for using it in the classroom in a couple of
weeks.  Chapter 14 raised a question (for me anyway) that I
do not know the answer to:  When working with indexed files
using both a primary and a secondary key, neither of which
allows duplicates, is there anyway to determine which key
generated a "duplicate key" error?  

It would seem useful to have separate error handling
procedures depending on which key generated the error (e.g.
one might print an error message to a log file, while the
other might ask the user to re-enter the data) but this
could only be done if one can detect the key causing the
error. 

Thanks for any feedback.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CAA0A44F85D318E1.DD05A8770C283A77.065E083A187163F0@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:
> 
> I am working my way through Thane Hubbell's TY COBOL book in
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Floyd,

AFAIK there is no way to detect which key caused the duplicate-key
error.  When file status "22" is returned you only know that the record
could not be stored because of a duplicate key on either the primary or
non-duplicate alternate key(s).  
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B4247B.F7B4BBF4@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <CAA0A44F85D318E1.DD05A8770C283A77.065E083A187163F0@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:

> It would seem useful to have separate error handling
> procedures depending on which key generated the error (e.g.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> error.

The only thing I know of is to have an error routine try to read the
record using various keys.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B44F50.F42201CC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <CAA0A44F85D318E1.DD05A8770C283A77.065E083A187163F0@lp.airnews.net>`

```
David W. Furin wrote:
> 
> Floyd Johnson wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> non-duplicate alternate key(s).

Suggestion - while you can't differentiate on which key caused the error
you can at least indicate which record and the Primekey as in the
following snippet :-

       *>  Note : display shows description for ws-filename-1
       *> (selected by using ws-filenum) and actual name,
       *> ws-filename-2 - in case there is a discrepacny between the
       *> two filename descriptions - this provides an indicator
       *> for ws-filestatus "39" errors

        string  "FILE NUMBER :         " delimited by size
                ws-filenum              delimited by size
                "  "                    delimited by size
                ws-FileName-1           delimited by "  "
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                ws-FileName-2a          delimited by "  "
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                ws-Filename-2b          delimited by "  "
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                ws-Filename-2c          delimited by "  "
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                "ERROR # : "            delimited by size
                ErrorNumber             delimited by size
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                " "                     delimited by size
                ErrorType1              delimited by ":"
                " - "                   delimited by size
                ErrorType2              delimited by "  "
                x'0d0a'                 delimited by size
                x"0d0a"                 delimited by size
                "RECORD : "             delimited by size
                ws-error-key            delimited by size
                " - "                   delimited by size
                ws-error-name           delimited by "  "
                x"00"                   delimited by size
                into ErrorDescription
              End-string

The above, Filename-2a, 2b, 2c - which is full path and long-filename,
is split into lines for a messasgebox display.

For the most part I find I'm picking up such errors in program testing.
However, should you have a "22" in an inter-active system, then having
displayed the error you can 'gracefully' return to the input screen and
allow the user to change data, for example duplication of a name being
used as an alternate key.

As regards PrimeKey duplicates - depends how you program - if user
enters a supposed new PrimeKey and you do a file read having set
RecordNotFound - if RecordFound then display record - this should
overcome PrimeKey duplicates because on update you say - if RecordFound
rewrite else write new.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B4DF2C.2902085@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <CAA0A44F85D318E1.DD05A8770C283A77.065E083A187163F0@lp.airnews.net> <37B44F50.F42201CC@home.com>`

```
File Status "22" . Thought about it a little more after supper, and
here's what I am actually doing. Principle applies to PCs/Mainframes :-

Inter-active Windowing system :

- select record from ListBox(get Primekey) or user enters
  Primekey
- set RecordNotFound and read Primekey
- RecordFound display/change; NotFound enter data/change
- OK update :
- Read using AlternateKey - if AltKeyFound display "Record already
  exists with this name......" - back to screen.
- AltKeyNotFound - RecordFound rewrite; RecordNotFound write new record

"Playing it blind" - input from cards/tape/OCR/OMR or an ascii-delimited
from a database etc. :-

- read next InRecord until FileFinished 
- read i-o DataFile using AltKey - AltKeyRecordFound writer error-line
  ignore InRecord
- read i-o DataFile using PrimeKey - RecordFound write error-line
  ignore InRecord else write record

This approach, (I think), gets rid of your "22" problem all together.	

Because I'm using sorted collections I don't need alternate keys with
the exception of one file containing calculations as part of the key -
and here I say alternate key with duplicates.

From a 'systems' as opposed to a 'programming' point of view, the
following name alternate keys don't make sense :

	CH1	Chevron Resources Canada Ltd
	CH2	Chevron Resources Canada Ltd
	CH3	Chevron Resources Canada Ltd

OK so the account numbers will be known by the lassie in A/P or A/R but
not necessarily by accountants or engineers. They need uniqueness to
identify them, which obviates duplicate alternate keys :-

	CH1	Chevron Resources Canada Ltd (AB)
	CH2	Chevron Resources Canada Ltd (BC)
	CH3     Chevron Resources Canada Ltd (SK)

PS: Unintentionally I do quite a bit of 'computing' when I'm walking my
dogs down by the Bow River !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y9Ns3.4606$TM2.86765@viper>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>`

```
Primary and secondary keys are not used at the same time. You are either
using one or the other per IO operation. Thus when one IO operation is
performed, checking the file status variable associated with that file
identifier will determine in which file the error exists. Duplicate key or
otherwise.
```

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>

- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <Y9Ns3.4606$TM2.86765@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
> 
> Primary and secondary keys are not used at the same time. You are either
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> otherwise.

Partially true when trying to read a file - but not when
writing a new record to the file.  Both indexes have to be
updated with the same write statement.  Or am I missing
something.  Thanks for the feedback.

Floyd Johnson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79B55F508DC5488D.6D3032CBA043B7E9.835A15134B54F59C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <Y9Ns3.4606$TM2.86765@viper> <37B411BD.8969DA6F@worf.netins.net>`

```
On Fri, 13 Aug 1999 08:38:22 -0400, Floyd Johnson
<floydj@worf.netins.net> enlightened us:

>Gumbo wrote:
>> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Floyd Johnson

I might be wrong about this, but I think you can only get a duplicate
key on the primary key.  This is because alternate keys can be
non-unique.  That is, an alternate can point to more than one primary
record.  You have to do a sequential read to get all of the records.
Also, generally, you do not update alternate keys from a program.  It
is normally done using IDCAMS after the file has been updated.  It is
possible to update alternate keys from the batch program, but it adds
a lot of overhead and run time, so generally it is not done this way.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I finally got my head together, now my body is falling apart.


 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B504C6.19B56042@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <Y9Ns3.4606$TM2.86765@viper> <37B411BD.8969DA6F@worf.netins.net> <79B55F508DC5488D.6D3032CBA043B7E9.835A15134B54F59C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
(snippage)
> 
> I might be wrong about this, but I think you can only get a duplicate
> key on the primary key.  This is because alternate keys can be
> non-unique.  That is, an alternate can point to more than one primary
> record.

You just defined the circumstances under which you can (and do) get
duplicate keys on an AIX, when the AIX is defined unique. My application
usually has the AIXes defined as upgrade. I've never heard of running
IDCAMS to update the AIXes after a file update. Any chance you're
thinking of the BLDINDEX step after loading the base cluster?

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C843DA1BCB8E6A68.00A8976FED603B43.71196F935DEA0D74@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <Y9Ns3.4606$TM2.86765@viper> <37B411BD.8969DA6F@worf.netins.net> <79B55F508DC5488D.6D3032CBA043B7E9.835A15134B54F59C@lp.airnews.net> <37B504C6.19B56042@att.net>`

```
On Sat, 14 Aug 1999 01:55:18 -0400, William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch

You are kind of right Bill.  What we do is rebuild our VSAM masters
every night from a sequential file or by reading an existing VSAM
master sequentially.  Then we run the IDCAMS BLDINDEX step.  We do not
define the AIX as upgradable because of CICS usage and the need to
keep run time down.  However, we do define them as non-unique.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I finally got my head together, now my body is falling apart.


 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dodt3.44890$bB2.194339@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>`

```
Floyd, I can't believe that either you're teaching COBOL, or that you can't
figure this out!

1) upon getting a File Status 22  move the record to a temp area
   2) move a unique value to the alt-key (time stamp, e.g)
     3) write the record
         (if sccessful, delete record and notify user)
         if not successful, notify user that prime key is already in file.

That's all it takes.

Floyd Johnson wrote in message <37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>...
>I am working my way through Thane Hubbell's TY COBOL book in
>preparation for using it in the classroom in a couple of
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>+-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>

- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <dodt3.44890$bB2.194339@news2.mia>`

```
WRITE the record, if no error, done.
REWRITE the record, If that fails, the alternate is duplicate.

James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote
> Floyd, I can't believe that either you're teaching COBOL, or that you
can't
> figure this out!
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >generated a "duplicate key" error?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b7f906.31014861@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <dodt3.44890$bB2.194339@news2.mia> <37b56b16@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 14 Aug 1999 08:12:07 -0500, "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
wrote:

>WRITE the record, if no error, done.
>REWRITE the record, If that fails, the alternate is duplicate.

Let's say there are 3 keys on the file.  One Prime key and two unique
alternate keys.  

The WRITE gets a 22 - something is duplicated.   Rewrite gets a "00"
you know it was the prime key because rewrite always operates on the
prime key - and as a byproduct you just lost the contents of the
original record.  But if you still get the 22 one of the alternate
key's was duplicated.  Which one?

I understand the problem, but I think it's something the programmer
will have to handle by determining if this is a record update or a
record add before doing any writing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hc0u3.6028$TM2.144944@viper>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <dodt3.44890$bB2.194339@news2.mia> <37b56b16@news3.us.ibm.net> <37b7f906.31014861@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Or how about having 4 file identifiers.
One for writing, and 3 for verifying each key.
Keep all records in WS and write FROm them.
Each write, on an error, would use the INPUT file identifiers to determine
which is in error, with a START command.

Each file identifier ASSIGNed to the same file, of course.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p4kk9$2q1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <dodt3.44890$bB2.194339@news2.mia>`

```
James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote:

: 1) upon getting a File Status 22  move the record to a temp area
:    2) move a unique value to the alt-key (time stamp, e.g)
:      3) write the record
:          (if sccessful, delete record and notify user)
:          if not successful, notify user that prime key is already in file.

While this may be quite efficient for the programmer and not too
inefficient for the computer, it is not particularly effective
for the _user_.

Just telling the user that the primary key or secondary key is
duplicated is not a lot of use.  It may be that the record
being inserted has already been created or it may be that
he has been given an incorrect or transposed key.

It probably be considerably more helpful if the records of
those keys were read and identifying data were displayed so
that the user can make a judgement as to why the error
occured in the first place.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p30f1$n1f@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>`

```
There have been lots of ideas and thoughts related to this thread - but I
thought I would had a couple of "inputs":

1) For those who commented on the fact that you can only have duplicates for
alternate keys - not for primary keys - that is true (according to the
Standard) *but*
  A) Some compilers have extensions that provide for duplicate primary keys
  B) It really is irrelevant to this question - as the problem is when
NEITHER the primary nor the alternate allow duplicates
  C) It is true that you DO get a FS=22 when EITHER the primary or the
alternate would cause a duplicate (and when duplicates are not allowed for
the alternate).

2) (I could be wrong on this) - but I think that whoever thought that you
"always know" which key you are dealing with is not right.  Lots of I/O
verbs do require this - but WRITE isn't one of them.

3)  OK - now for my suggestion to "solve" the problem:

If you open the INDEXED file
    a) as I-O and *not* as OUTPUT
       *and*
    b) you are in ACCESS RANDOM or DYNAMIC
then you can always do a READ on either key (or both keys) after the FS=22
and find out which one already exists.  (You probably would need to try
BOTH, because a FS=22 could happen if BOTH the primary and the alternate are
duplicates - and your logic should handle this too)

Having said that, I know of a number of compilers and file systems where
"loading" a file is MUCH more efficient when you OPEN OUTPUT and have ACCESS
SEQUENTIAL.   So taking this approach, might be a performance issue in a
"real" application.

4) Finally, I have passed this "question" on to J4, because I think that it
would be a "nice" enhancement in a future Standard (not the one that is
currently in draft) if you COULD figure out which key (or keys) get the
FS=22 when you do open a file OUTPUT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5%et3.542$7W6.26219@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <7p30f1$n1f@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <7p30f1$n1f@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>,
William M. Klein <wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com> wrote:

[snippolinio]

>If you open the INDEXED file
>    a) as I-O and *not* as OUTPUT
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>duplicates - and your logic should handle this too)

Point of Parsimony - since No Real Programmer needs more than 16K of core
and since every XCP costs money then it is more economical to use a START
EQUAL than a READ for verification of a pre-existing key.

>
>Having said that, I know of a number of compilers and file systems where
>"loading" a file is MUCH more efficient when you OPEN OUTPUT and have ACCESS
>SEQUENTIAL.   So taking this approach, might be a performance issue in a
>"real" application.

Point of Parsimony - using a system utility to 'load' a file is usually
*much* 'cheaper' than using custom-built code.  To use an IBM mainframe
example: a flat file is created by an application which removes/sums
duplicates and then passed to IDCAMS.  It might be interesting for someone
to take a Honking Big File and compare the efficiencies of some
cobbled-together code which sorts, de-dupes and WRITEs to an OUTPUT
indexed file versus passing said file through SyncSort to an IDCAMS.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b6d282.89135485@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net>`

```
On Thu, 12 Aug 1999 17:14:25 -0400, Floyd Johnson
<floydj@worf.netins.net> wrote:

>preparation for using it in the classroom in a couple of
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p9sun$ea9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <37b6d282.89135485@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote:

: I do not know of a way either.  Consider that the file could have "any
: number" of unique alternate key fields and you see the difficulty in
: implementation.  

I can't think of any system where several unique alternate keys
where they aren't unique deliberately (and thus will never
duplicate) would be useful.

Primary keys are unique because they are the customer number or
the product code or similar and their uniqueness is inherent.

If there is a genuine alterante unique way of referring to the
data item that the record represents then an alternate key without
duplicates is valid, however this would be unusual.  I would look
to the design if I saw this situation.

Where there is a need for an alternate key it is usually that
some specific ordering is needed below the 'obvious' sequencing.
In some cases I have repeated the primary key as part of the 
secondary and this does guarantee non-duplication with no
question as to the meaning of a '22'.

But to test which key(s) caused a '22' it is only necessary
to START on each key and check the result (and READ NEXT to
give the user a chance to know why it is a duplication).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b8ec71.11514217@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37B33930.7C3BC392@worf.netins.net> <37b6d282.89135485@news1.ibm.net> <7p9sun$ea9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
On 16 Aug 1999 20:39:51 GMT, Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
wrote:

>Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>duplicate) would be useful.

I've  a method of of file design (that I describe in COBOL Unleashed)
where the primary key of a file is a "handle" that is unique and never
changes.  What appears as a unique prime key to a user (Name for
instance), is really a unique alternate key.  If they want to change
the name, I let them, then rewrite (which use prime key) and I get a
22 if that person's name is already in the file with another handle.  

Now, suppose you added Drivers license for instance as a second
(really third key).  This is just one example of useful implementation
with multiple unique alternate keys.  It's easy to handle, and really
makes life simple down the road.  

For example, let's say you want to had a whole lot of detail
information about the individual in a different file.  Simple key by
the mail files "handle" - then "name changes" wihich are apparantly
primary key changes (but really aren't) won't lose the associated
detail information.

I've now used this design with several systems and I am very pleased
with the outcome.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed File Errors

```
ex:

perform ....
    read file1
       compute x= a + b
             on size error
                 display 'on size error'
                 peform abend-routine
                    display '*****'
                 end-perform
     end-read
end-perform.
```

```
Charles Dusheke <cwdusheke@dstsystems.com> wrote in message
news:7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com...
> Does anybody knows if there is a scope terminator for the on size error.
```

#### ↳ Re: On Size Error

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ov7bh$b1j@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com>`

```
Scope terminators are NEVER for phrases (such as ON SIZE, ON EXCEPTION, AT
END, etc).  They are for verbs (such as COMPUTE, READ, UNSTRING, CALL, etc)

Therefore, if you want to use a "scope terminator" for a STATEMENT with an
ON SIZE ERROR phrase it is always possible - but will depend on the verb
being used, e.g.

  Compute a = B * C
     On size Error
           Add D to E
                On Size Error
                    Divide F by G
                          On Size Error
                                 Call "ABC"
                                       On Exception
                                              Display "You have GOT to be
kidding"
                                 End-Call
                    End-Divide
            End-Add
   End-Compute
      .
```

#### ↳ Re: On Size Error

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b3a8a9.2132065@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com>`

```
On Thu, 12 Aug 1999 12:13:53 -0500, "Charles Dusheke" <cwdusheke@dstsystems.com> wrote:

>Does anybody knows if there is a scope terminator for the on size error.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, the END-COMPUTE is the scope terminator
 
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: On Size Error

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lsdt3.44897$bB2.194678@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com>`

```
NO, because ON SIZE ERROR is an IMPERATIVE
e.g.  COMPUTE X = Y **Z
    ON SIZE ERROR  DISPLAY "Too BIG"
   NOT ON SIZE ERROR  DISPLAY "JUST RIGHT".

ALL The Verbs Have END- Scope Delimiters
All ON xxx have matching NOTs as in
READ FILEX
  INVALID KEY
  NOT INVALID KEY
END-READ
Charles Dusheke wrote in message <7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com>...
>Does anybody knows if there is a scope terminator for the on size error.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers

- **From:** "Gary Warner" <gwarner@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner>`

```
I am trying to develop a routine to find all the possible unique
combinations of a set of numbers.
The end result is similar to the problem of balancing your check book. 
IE: you are out of balance by $34.22. and you have (maybe) a hundred
transactions. 
I need to find the set of transactions that adds up to $34.22. 
So far, I've been able to make things work if I limit say, up to 4
transactions at time out of the 100 transactions, 
but what I really need is to find the correct set whether it takes one
transaction or all 100.

Any Takers?
Gary Warner
Warnersoft, Inc.
...and whatsoever ye do, do it heartily as unto the Lord...
```

#### ↳ Re: Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QPWs3.405$UQ6.7107@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner>`

```
Gary Warner wrote in message <01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner>...
>I am trying to develop a routine to find all the possible unique
>combinations of a set of numbers.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>transaction or all 100.

I have written something like that in C, but it could be done in COBOL just
as well.  The algorithm works something like this:

If you have "n" checks outstanding, you have 2**n possibilities of including
or not including each check in a compare sum.  Of course the amount of each
check would be in an array, as would an array of multipliers (PIC 9 will do
nicely, they can only contain a zero or 1).

varying x from 1 to (2**n - 1)
    convert x to binary (divide x by 2 "n" times, save remainder in
multiplier array)
    zero a compare sum area
    varying y from 1 to n, add check-armount(y) * multiplier(y) to sum area
    if the sum area equals the compare amount (constant entered at
beginning) or
      (optional) the difference is less than any previous difference
         display all check amounts with a 1 as their multiplier

We leave the coding of this as homework.
```

- **References:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner> <QPWs3.405$UQ6.7107@axe.netdoor.com>`

```

>
>varying x from 1 to (2**n - 1)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B3CA1D.225F1AB4@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner>`

```
Gary Warner wrote:
> 
> I am trying to develop a routine to find all the possible unique
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any Takers?

Gary,

I hope you realize what you are running into. There is more than one issue.

1. The number of unique combinations among N elements if the order of
elements in a combination matters not (evidently, your case) is 2**N. As you
have N=100, it translates into approximately 1.27E30. You have got to have
an awfully fast parallel machine to be able to even test the sum of each
combination for equality to 34.22, to say nothing of creating the
combinations.

2. Down to the nitty-gritty level, let's see what kind of code one has to
concoct to do that. Let's start with very modest N=4:

PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 > 4-(1-1)
   COMPUTE SUM = Q1
   IF SUM = 34.22 DISPLAY Q1
END-PERFORM

PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 > 4-(2-1)
PERFORM VARYING Q2 FROM Q1+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q2 > 4-(2-2)
   COMPUTE SUM = Q1 + Q2
   IF SUM = 34.22 DISPLAY Q1 Q2
END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM

PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 > 4-(3-1)
PERFORM VARYING Q2 FROM Q1+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q2 > 4-(3-2)
PERFORM VARYING Q3 FROM Q2+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q3 > 4-(3-3)
   COMPUTE SUM = Q1 + Q2 + Q3
   IF SUM = 34.22 DISPLAY Q1 Q2 Q3
END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM

PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 > 4-(4-1)
PERFORM VARYING Q2 FROM Q1+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q2 > 4-(4-2)
PERFORM VARYING Q3 FROM Q2+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q3 > 4-(4-3)
PERFORM VARYING Q4 FROM Q3+1 BY 1 UNTIL Q4 > 4-(4-4)
   COMPUTE SUM = Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4
   IF SUM = 34.22 DISPLAY Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4
END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM  

I've adopted the Q-notation intentionally to make the induction pattern
clear. To go while nine yards to N=100, you will need 100 blocks whose
number of nested loops increases from 1 for the combinations containing 1
element to 100 for 100 elements. Of course, the latter will loop only once
(there is only 1 unique combination of 100 items out of 100), but you will
not be able to compile it. Heuristically, you can replace the last loop with
a straight test, but how about 50 out of 100? I doubt that COBOL permits 50
nested loops, anyway.

3. Even all the above points had nothing to do with reality, hand-writing
code like this for mere N=20 is, to put it mildly, tedious. You realize, of
course, that I did not do it - I let SAS Macro Language assemble it for me
with the absence of which, you will have to write a COBOL program writing a
code similar to the one above to a file, then compile and execute it. 

4. With that in mind, I would recommend doing it in SAS. There is no limit
as to the number of nested loops, and it does things like that rapidly
enough if you limit yourself to reasonable N=25. If you decide to test only
K combinations out of N, then the number of nested loops is determined by K
< N. For instance, with K=2, N=4, all such combinations will be given by the
2nd block of the code above. However, as you know, the number of the
possible unique combinations in this case is N!/[K!*(N-K)!], so you will
have to assess it first to decide if it makes sense to run the thing at all.
If you are interested in SAS code doing what you need (once again, provided
N is not large!) here it is (ready-to go; just change N accordingly and
populate the array T with your values):

%LET N  = 10;
%MACRO COMBINE;
   Q0 = 0;
   %DO I=1 %TO &N;
      %DO J=1 %TO &I;
         DO Q&J = Q%EVAL(&J-1)+1 TO &N-(&I-&J);
      %END;
      IF Q1
      %DO X=2 %TO &I; + Q&X %END; EQ 34.22
      THEN PUT
      %DO X=1 %TO &I; Q&X T(Q&X) %END;
      ;
      %DO J=1 %TO &I; END; %END;
   %END;
%MEND COMBINE;

DATA _NULL_;
   ARRAY T (&N) _TEMPORARY_
    (1.87 4.76 2.17 9.44 2.13 9.13 23.10 0.47 7.39 62.5)
   ;
   %COMBINE
RUN;

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
=================
```

```
would
have jumped in here. Paul's over the top discussion let me to ask if anyone
has heard
of transposition?  Expecting the error to add  34.22 seems to ignore the
possibility that the error could consist of several items over and under
which NET to this number. Yet I realize that I may be showing my ignorance.

Warren Simmons
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00

```
The real problem is that even if you managed all permutations, it would not
find the most common error.  A chequebook out by X is most likely the result
of a sign reversal on x/2, rather than two transactions that add up to x.

If I had minus a dollar for every time I have seen that error, I would be
rich.

;<$
```

```
Not ignorance from my perspective.   Usually, the algorithm to find an
out-of-balance
condition in accounting journals is to compare transactions in the
"register"/"journal"
to that incoming from the bank or invoices.   In this case, comparing bank
transactions
to the check register using a key of checknumber and maybe date to produce an
item to item comparison.


Warren Simmons wrote:

> I thought that someone with some basic experience in balancing a check book
> would
…[136 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B44E1A.734E214C@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner> <37B3CA1D.225F1AB4@earthlink.net>`

```
Humanum errare est... 

I've made one mistake (insignificant) and one blunder (quite significant).

The mistake first. The number of unique combinations among N elements, if
the order of elements in a combination  does not matter, is (2**N)-1, not
2**N. This is just for the sake of accuracy, as at N=100 it is immaterial.

Now, the blunder. In the innermost loops, we are supposed to sum the
elements of the table, not the indices pointing at them. That is, for
instance,

COMPUTE SUM = Q1 + Q2 + Q3

should read

COMPUTE SUM = T(Q1) + T(Q2) + T(Q3).

By the same token, in the SAS code, the portion

IF Q1
%DO X=2 %TO &I; + Q&X

must be replaced by 

IF T(Q1)
%DO X=2 %TO &I; + T(Q&X)

...et nihil humanum _me_ alienum essa puto... :-(

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL       
==================

"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
> 
> Gary Warner wrote:
…[114 more quoted lines elided]…
> =================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1n0c$91p@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner> <37B3CA1D.225F1AB4@earthlink.net> <37B44E1A.734E214C@earthlink.net>`

```
This is actually a problem to which our company has a solution.  The
situation is that we need to determine programatically which invoices a
customer meant to pay when he has several outstanding invoices and sends us
a check for amount X.  So the problem is to find all combinations of N
invoices, limited to M items in the set, which match amount X.  Currently N
is limited to 100 and M is limited to 10.  What makes this easier to solve
is to order to N items in ascending order and stop whenever the sum of the M
items is +AD4-X (i.e. a check for +ACQ-100 is obviously not meant to pay two
invoices of +ACQ-60 and +ACQ-70, nor will adding more invoices to that list (which
will increase the total) arrive at a match either.

The actual code is a little more complicated than that, since it takes into
account credits as well.  We did not code it in COBOL since we wanted very
tight code so it's written in assembler, but it could have been done that
way if we were not as concerned with efficiency.  As it is, the amount of
CPU time consumed is fairly minimal, despite the fact that we process
hundreds of payments every evening.

The code is proprietary so I can't share it here, but the basic outline I
have given should suffice.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah+AEA-apci.com
Home - AHRussell+AEA-computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

+AD4APg- concoct to do that. Let's start with very modest N+AD0-4:
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 +AD4- 4-(1-1)
+AD4APg-    COMPUTE SUM +AD0- Q1
+AD4APg-    IF SUM +AD0- 34.22 DISPLAY Q1
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 +AD4- 4-(2-1)
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q2 FROM Q1 BY 1 UNTIL Q2 +AD4- 4-(2-2)
+AD4APg-    COMPUTE SUM +AD0- Q1  Q2
+AD4APg-    IF SUM +AD0- 34.22 DISPLAY Q1 Q2
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 +AD4- 4-(3-1)
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q2 FROM Q1 BY 1 UNTIL Q2 +AD4- 4-(3-2)
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q3 FROM Q2 BY 1 UNTIL Q3 +AD4- 4-(3-3)
+AD4APg-    COMPUTE SUM +AD0- Q1  Q2  Q3
+AD4APg-    IF SUM +AD0- 34.22 DISPLAY Q1 Q2 Q3
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q1 FROM Q0 BY 1 UNTIL Q1 +AD4- 4-(4-1)
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q2 FROM Q1 BY 1 UNTIL Q2 +AD4- 4-(4-2)
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q3 FROM Q2 BY 1 UNTIL Q3 +AD4- 4-(4-3)
+AD4APg- PERFORM VARYING Q4 FROM Q3 BY 1 UNTIL Q4 +AD4- 4-(4-4)
+AD4APg-    COMPUTE SUM +AD0- Q1  Q2  Q3  Q4
+AD4APg-    IF SUM +AD0- 34.22 DISPLAY Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg- END-PERFORM
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- I've adopted the Q-notation intentionally to make the induction pattern
+AD4APg- clear. To go while nine yards to N+AD0-100, you will need 100 blocks whose
+AD4APg- number of nested loops increases from 1 for the combinations containing 1
+AD4APg- element to 100 for 100 elements. Of course, the latter will loop only
once
+AD4APg- (there is only 1 unique combination of 100 items out of 100), but you
will
+AD4APg- not be able to compile it. Heuristically, you can replace the last loop
with
+AD4APg- a straight test, but how about 50 out of 100? I doubt that COBOL permits
50
+AD4APg- nested loops, anyway.
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- 3. Even all the above points had nothing to do with reality, hand-writing
+AD4APg- code like this for mere N+AD0-20 is, to put it mildly, tedious. You realize,
of
+AD4APg- course, that I did not do it - I let SAS Macro Language assemble it for
me
+AD4APg- with the absence of which, you will have to write a COBOL program writing
a
+AD4APg- code similar to the one above to a file, then compile and execute it.
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- 4. With that in mind, I would recommend doing it in SAS. There is no
limit
+AD4APg- as to the number of nested loops, and it does things like that rapidly
+AD4APg- enough if you limit yourself to reasonable N+AD0-25. If you decide to test
only
+AD4APg- K combinations out of N, then the number of nested loops is determined by
K
+AD4APg- +ADw- N. For instance, with K+AD0-2, N+AD0-4, all such combinations will be given by
the
+AD4APg- 2nd block of the code above. However, as you know, the number of the
+AD4APg- possible unique combinations in this case is N+ACE-/+AFs-K+ACEAKg-(N-K)+ACEAXQ-, so you will
+AD4APg- have to assess it first to decide if it makes sense to run the thing at
all.
+AD4APg- If you are interested in SAS code doing what you need (once again,
provided
+AD4APg- N is not large+ACE-) here it is (ready-to go+ADs- just change N accordingly and
+AD4APg- populate the array T with your values):
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- +ACU-LET N  +AD0- 10+ADs-
+AD4APg- +ACU-MACRO COMBINE+ADs-
+AD4APg-    Q0 +AD0- 0+ADs-
+AD4APg-    +ACU-DO I+AD0-1 +ACU-TO +ACY-N+ADs-
+AD4APg-       +ACU-DO J+AD0-1 +ACU-TO +ACY-I+ADs-
+AD4APg-          DO Q+ACY-J +AD0- Q+ACU-EVAL(+ACY-J-1) TO +ACY-N-(+ACY-I-+ACY-J)+ADs-
+AD4APg-       +ACU-END+ADs-
+AD4APg-       IF Q1
+AD4APg-       +ACU-DO X+AD0-2 +ACU-TO +ACY-I+ADs-  Q+ACY-X +ACU-END+ADs- EQ 34.22
+AD4APg-       THEN PUT
+AD4APg-       +ACU-DO X+AD0-1 +ACU-TO +ACY-I+ADs- Q+ACY-X T(Q+ACY-X) +ACU-END+ADs-
+AD4APg-       +ADs-
+AD4APg-       +ACU-DO J+AD0-1 +ACU-TO +ACY-I+ADs- END+ADs- +ACU-END+ADs-
+AD4APg-    +ACU-END+ADs-
+AD4APg- +ACU-MEND COMBINE+ADs-
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- DATA +AF8-NULL+AF8AOw-
+AD4APg-    ARRAY T (+ACY-N) +AF8-TEMPORARY+AF8-
+AD4APg-     (1.87 4.76 2.17 9.44 2.13 9.13 23.10 0.47 7.39 62.5)
+AD4APg-    +ADs-
+AD4APg-    +ACU-COMBINE
+AD4APg- RUN+ADs-
+AD4APg-
+AD4APg- Kind regards,
+AD4APg- +AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQ-
+AD4APg- Paul M. Dorfman
+AD4APg- Jacksonville, Fl
+AD4APg- +AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQA9AD0APQ-
+AD4-
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Wanted: Algorithm for *Combinations* of a series of numbers

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Erot3.11174$C6.111992@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<01bee860$01db3440$b681d6d1@gwarner> <01bee9ea$dcf95760$e481d6d1@gwarner>`

```

Gary Warner <gwarner@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:01bee9ea$dcf95760$e481d6d1@gwarner...
> A big thanks to each of you that responded to my request.
> I had gotten as far the the nested loops which Mr. Dorfman proposed and
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

FYI, you can improve the 'nested loop' algorithm by pre-sorting the list by
amount. You can ignore any cheques which are larger than 34.22. For the
nested loops you can stop processing when the sums exceed 34.22.

Here;s a little rexx which handles a list of numbers. In this case the
number is also the index in order to simplify the example. It handles
combinations up to 3.  Recursion would simplify the algorithm...

    do i = 1 to nChecks
    if c.i = target then say c.i'='target
    if c.i > target then leave
    do j = i + 1 to nChecks
     if (c.i + c.j) = target then say c.i'+'c.j'='target
     if c.i + c.j > target then leave
     do k= j + 1 to nChecks
       if (c.i + c.j + c.k = target) then say c.i'+'c.j'+'c.k'='target
       if c.i + c.j + c.k > target then leave
     end
   end
 end
```

## Thread: help for migration — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help for migration

- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
hi!
if anybody know a utility to convert/port cobol's flat file (*.dat) to ASCII
or anyother Microsoft-compatible databse file, i want to convert file
(*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)
```

#### ↳ Re: help for migration

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kSVs3.418$7y4.1239@client>`
- **References:** `<7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>`

```
deil wrote in message <7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>...
>if anybody know a utility to convert/port cobol's flat file (*.dat) to
ASCII
>or anyother Microsoft-compatible databse file, i want to convert file
>(*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)

Actually, you might not need to 'convert' the data but rather use an ODBC
driver to gain access to the data.

Relativity is a product that might meet your needs.  See
http://www.liant.com/products/retooling/databridge/  for more information.

Each COBOL vendor has different file formats for its flat files, so it is
necessary to know which COBOL is used to make the application.  Relativity
is available for several different COBOLs.

Our Pakistan distributor is :

SBS Computers
7/10 Hina Palace, Civil Lines
Hoshang Road
Karachi-75530
Pakistan
Phone: +92 (0) 2 1 568 6075
Fax: +92 (0) 2 1 568 5079
Email: msyousuf@hotmail.com
Sales: Farooq Majeed


Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: help for migration

- **From:** "The Programmer" <thevaxer@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1b0e$atq$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>`

```
One sure-fire method: write a small converter program in COBOL to open the
existing data file for input, open a new text/sequential file for output, do
a read/write routine, close both files... voila, problem solved.
```

#### ↳ Re: help for migration

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1spq$4l3$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>`

```
deil wrote in message <7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>...
>hi!
>if anybody know a utility to convert/port cobol's flat file (*.dat) to
ASCII
>or anyother Microsoft-compatible databse file, i want to convert file
>(*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)

Just read in the file in the Windows tool of your choice and before you
write it to the windows database of your choice, make sure to run strings
through the OemToChar() function.

F.ex in pascal:

    While Not Eof(Infile) Do
     Begin
      ReadLn(Infile, mystring);
     strcpy(Buffer, mystring);
     OemToChar(Buffer, Buffer);
     WinDatabase.Post(Buffer);
     End;

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: help for migration

- **From:** allenmc@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p9ma3$h7l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>`

```
In article <7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>,
  "deil" <hameed@hancom-system.com.pk> wrote:
> hi!
> if anybody know a utility to convert/port cobol's flat file (*.dat)
to ASCII
> or anyother Microsoft-compatible databse file, i want to convert file
> (*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)
>
>

Since you are specifying *.DAT files, I get the impression your using
Micro Focus indexed files.  Unless your MF Configuration is setup to
compress data, you should be able to read the *.DAT file as a binary
sequential file.

As mentioned earlier you can use an ODBC to port the data to your
windows apps.

If you have a large number of files, you could do what I did and write
a simple batch program that has command line arguments, filename and
record length.  The program processes *filename* reading large blocks
(say 20480 bytes) of data into a buffer.  You move the data from the
input buffer in strings equal to the length of the command line, to an
output buffer.  After each string move, you append a x0D0A.  When the
output buffer gets full, you dump it to you output file and clean the
buffer.

Believe me when I say you want to use buffers rather than process the
files on a byte for byte basis.  The byte for byte processing is a
performance killer!  One of my On The Job Trainees did the byte for
byte business which took 10 minutes to process a 1/2 MB file where my
buffered program took 5 seconds.  Was a great learning exercise for the
OJT.

On other point you might want to make clear.  Are you converting file
formats or data type.  What I'm really trying to say is are you wanting
to convert EBCDIC to ASCII?  You can do this also in the program I
outlined above.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: help for migration

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BB273C.D2997883@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7p17dp$f6i$3@news.IAEhv.nl>`

```
deil wrote:
> 
> hi!
> if anybody know a utility to convert/port cobol's flat file (*.dat) to ASCII
> or anyother Microsoft-compatible databse file, i want to convert file
> (*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)

Siber Systems sells Data2Flat converter that does
just what you need: it takes Cobol data file and
translates it to text format. This text format
is values separated by commas (CSV).
Other separator characters can be specified.

Record layout for the Cobol data file is stored
in RDD (Record Descriptor Data) file that is
automatically produced from the Cobol program source
by Cbl2Fdd tool.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

## Thread: it doesn't know FD — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### it doesn't know FD

- **From:** "jack lam" <man_to@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1f4a$lm46@pegasus.hkstar.com>`

```
but if there are no FD and file sturcture , like index or sequential....
it has just *.dat and *.idx
then how solve it ?
```

#### ↳ Re: it doesn't know FD

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6nIt3.1968$wY.74857@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7p1f4a$lm46@pegasus.hkstar.com>`

```
jack lam wrote in message <7p1f4a$lm46@pegasus.hkstar.com>...
>but if there are no FD and file sturcture , like index or sequential....
>it has just *.dat and *.idx
>then how solve it ?
>


Well, then you get to engage in that time-honored practice of "looking
through the bytes and trying to figure out the FD."

Let me save you some research time: If you do not have the COBOL FD, the
only way you are going to figure out your file is as above. Period. There
are no tools, no compilers, no software, no silver bullet, so don't even
ask.

This is the same as me mailing you this..

"935@29nsdfns9u13mnkwe;sndvks"

... then giving you a quiz on it.

If you have a least a clue about what the file contains, someone with a lot
of experience may be able to "intuit" some of the data - by digging through
it byte-by-byte.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: it doesn't know FD

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BB29A3.A6FB5EAC@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7p1f4a$lm46@pegasus.hkstar.com>`

```
jack lam wrote:
> 
> but if there are no FD and file sturcture , like index or sequential....
> it has just *.dat and *.idx
> then how solve it ?

If there is no FD, then there is no way to tell what's in a file.
Cobol data files do not store record descriptions in them.

You can try to reengineer the FD by looking at the bytes
and imagining what they might mean. But the resulting FD
will be more a product of your imagination which
may be different from imagination of programmer
who wrote the program that wrote this data file.

One way to make FD guessing easier is to use our
data2flat http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/ converter. 
You can specify what the record layout is 
to this converter using RDD file.
RDD file is a text file that desribes format of Cobol record.
The RDD file is automatically generated from the Cobol source,
or you can create it manually in a text editor.

So in your case you can just try different record layouts,
put them into RDD file and use data2flat to view the results.

Evaluation version of data2flat is free and it can read first
200 records of your file (commercial version can read the rest
of the records). So it would be a cheap way to try various layouts.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

```
hi!
if anybody know a utility to convert/port cobol's flat file (*.dat) to ASCII
or anyother Microsoft-compatible databse file, i want to convert file
(*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)
```

#### ↳ Re: help for migration

- **From:** Lawrence Allen Free <free@mcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pag8t$64q$1@Nntp1.mcs.net>`
- **References:** `<7p17g1$f6r$1@news.IAEhv.nl>`

```
In article 7p17g1$f6r$1@news.IAEhv.nl,
	"deil" <hameed@hancom-system.com.pk> said:
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>(*.DAT) from cobol-made to window system...any idea/clue? :)

If you have the COBOL source and are willing to write a program,
output the data to a line sequential file using DISPLAY type fields.
You could write a C program to do the same converting a formated
bitstream.  Some companies have written utilities to manage this.
In almost all these cases, you need to know the format of the file.

By the way, outside of how some COBOLs name the index of a
file, the name of the datafile can be of any format in the various
dialects of the language.  The *.dat is often a programming
convention, not a requirement of the language.
```

- **From:** kontiki3136@my-deja.com

## Thread: Help! REPLACE not working for COBOL II — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! REPLACE not working for COBOL II

- **From:** Steve Way <stepway@box.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B439F9.56CE@box.net.au>`

```
I am having problems converting the following COPY statement from being
COBOL 2.3 to IBM M/F COBOL II (RELEASE 4.0 09/15/92) compliant....

01 WS-THIS-RECORD COPY RECORD-1.

01 WS-NEXT-RECORD COPY RECORD-1

I have tried replacing this with 

COPY RECORD-1.

COPY RECORD-1 REPLACING ==THIS== BY ==NEXT==

{Assume RECORD-1 contains

  01 WS-THIS-RECORD.
     03 WS-THIS-SUB-RECORD-1   PIC X(9). }

But the compiler will not do the substitution. Has anyone come across
this? Is there a compiler option which needs to be set? Or is it just
functionality that does not work according to the doco?

On the other hand,

COPY RECORD-1 REPLACING ==:THIS== BY ==THIS==.

COPY RECORD-1 REPLACING ==:THIS== BY ==NEXT==.

{Assume RECORD-1 contains

  01 WS-:THIS-RECORD.
     03 WS-:THIS-SUB-RECORD-1   PIC X(9). }

Does seem to work. But I am not at all keen on changing all our
copybooks to include ':' characters in them!!!

Thanks for any help,

Regards,

Steve Way
stepway@box.net.au
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! REPLACE not working for COBOL II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1l98$m75@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37B439F9.56CE@box.net.au>`

```
First of all, OS/VS COBOL Release 2.3 was NOT suppose to ever replace "part"
of a word (i.e. "THIS" and "NEXT" in the middle of a word).  When I worked
for Micro Focus, we heard lots of reports of the mainframe doing this, but
when we asked customers to show us actual compiler output where this worked
this way - they never could provide us with examples (except one company who
had actually HOME modified the IBM compiler).  Therefore, I suggest that you
try the code again with the OS/VS COBOL compiler and see if it really works
the way that you think it does.

Having said that, if (by any chance) you do have a version of OS/VS COBOL
that really does have that "bug" (or home-grown modification) to allow
partial word replacement, then NO, there is no way to do that in VS COBOL
II - or the newer (strategic IBM COBOL for this-and-that).  The correct way
*IS* to put the entire part that you want in colons (or parenthesis), e.g.
":THIS:" in your (every one) copylibs.

P.S.  The IBM documentation (just like the ANSI Standard - '68, '74, AND
'85) is quite clear that COPY/REPLACING occurs on an ENTIRE "text-word" - so
this is NOT a case of the compiler not working "as documented".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! REPLACE not working for COBOL II

- **From:** Steve Way <stepway@box.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B563CF.68FD@box.net.au>`
- **References:** `<37B439F9.56CE@box.net.au> <7p1l98$m75@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Thanks for your reply.
  
Just to clarify the problem - we have current COBOL 2.3 code structured
as follows -

01 WS-THIS-RECORD COPY RECORD-1.

01 WS-NEXT-RECORD COPY RECORD-1

{Assume RECORD-1 contains 

01 MA-THIS-RECORD.
   05 MA-SUB-RECORD-1    PIC 9(5).
   05 MA-SUB-RECORD-2    PIC 9(6). }

Followed by the code -

MOVE 9 TO MA-SUB-RECORD-1 OF CORRESPONDING WS-THIS-RECORD.
MOVE 10 TO MA-SUB-RECORD-1 OF CORRESPONDING WS-NEXT-RECORD.

We had hoped to use the REPLACE statement, so that when we converted to
COBOL 2.4, the above code would appear as - 

COPY RECORD-1.

REPLACE ==MA-== BY ==OA-==

COPY RECORD-1

REPLACE OFF 

Followed by the code -

MOVE 9 TO MA-SUB-RECORD-1.
MOVE 10 TO OA-SUB-RECORD-1.

But as we both agree that the REPLACE in this format will not work, the
only options that seem to be available are :

1. Insert ':' characters in all our copybooks, and use the REPLACE
command
or 
2. Create a new copybook which has all the variables coded as OA- rather
than MA-.

Have I missed any other options?

Thanks again for your help,

Regards,

Steve Way
  
PS.
The COBOL II documentation that we use is stored within QWIKREF, but the
COBOL II documentation stored here must be out of date, as it does state
an example of :

The source program segment which follows:         
                                           
      REPLACE ==RPL== BY ==EMP==.          
      01  RPL-RECORD.                      
          05  RPL-KEY    PIC X(5).         
          05  RPL-DATA   PIC X(95).        
      REPLACE OFF.                         
                                           
 would be modified as shown below:         
                                           
      01  EMP-RECORD.                      
          05  EMP-KEY    PIC X(5).         
          05  EMP-DATA   PIC X(95).                  

This documentation that we have must be out of date and in any case,
this is just a sideline issue.

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> First of all, OS/VS COBOL Release 2.3 was NOT suppose to ever replace "part"
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> > stepway@box.net.au
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I am REALLY curious,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 
Yes, Micro Focus (N/E V3) does include LEADING, (as Ken Mullins
indicated) : -

COPY Statement - General Format

COPY (text-name                  ) [(OF) (library-name         )]
     (external-file-name-literal ) [(IN) (library-name-literal )]
     [SUPPRESS]

[           [ (LEADING   ) ==partial-word-1== BY ==partial-word-2==) ) 
[           [ (TRAILING  )                                           
[           [ ==pseudo-text-1== )    ( ==pseudo-text-2== 
[REPLACING  [ identifier-1      ) BY ( identifier-2      
[		etc..........

Jimmy Calgary AB
```

## Thread: COBOL and ODBC — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COBOL and ODBC

- **From:** "Hartmut Wieczorek" <hwieczorek@meyermeyer.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>`

```
I'm not a cobol programmer, but my Q is :
is there a way to have access with a cobol app on
a Informix DB with the help of ODBC ?
I know the way to build the connect in other languages,
but what is the source code in cobol.
What exactly must I do to get the handle
if my ODBC datasource is "db1"
username "informix" and passwd "informix"

TIA
Hartmut
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ODBC

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lbXs3.10072$Co3.90972@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>`

```
Check out EXEC, EXECUTE and SQL.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ODBC

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1sfv$4gq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>`

```
Hartmut Wieczorek wrote in message <7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>...
>I'm not a cobol programmer, but my Q is :
>is there a way to have access with a cobol app on
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>username "informix" and passwd "informix"

Most Cobols support access to RDBM's. Many do also support the use of ODBC.

You do not specify which Cobol you talk of, in case you are looking for one
that does both SQL and ODBC native, check out: www.acucorp.com.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ODBC

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b6ce46@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>`

```

Hartmut Wieczorek wrote in message <7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>...
>I'm not a cobol programmer, but my Q is :
>is there a way to have access with a cobol app on
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You don't need a 'handle'. The COBOL syntax is:
CONNECT  [TO] datasource name [AS connection name] [USER] or [DEFAULT]
(User and password are optional; if you don't specify USER the system takes
the DEFAULT, which must be specified on the server.)

So, for your example...
EXEC SQL
     CONNECT TO "db1" AS "conn1"   USER "informix/informix"
END-EXEC

or, if you don't like writing...
EXEC SQL
    CONNECT "db1"   (You will be logged on as default).
END-EXEC

Note that you can set several connections (conn1, conn2, etc. in my example)
and use the SET CONNECTION  syntax in COBOL to switch between them.

SQLSTATE is set by the CONNECT command so you can check if your connection
was successful.

Hope this helps,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ODBC

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p94ht$2fd$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com>`

```
Hartmut,

Micro Focus' Net Express 3.0 product comes with a pre-processor called
OpenESQL.  This
allows SQL to be embedded in to your programs via EXEC SQL syntax.  This is
then converted
in to ODBC calls as part of the compilation process.  Your COBOL program can
then access
any ODBC datasource e.g. Informix, Oracle, Access etc.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT Micro Focus International.

Hartmut Wieczorek <hwieczorek@meyermeyer.de> wrote in message
news:7p0ss6$2e2r$1@mars.ms.tlk.com...
> I'm not a cobol programmer, but my Q is :
> is there a way to have access with a cobol app on
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1gp3$3q6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

