```
line parameters or auxiliary files.

If the following statement is valid, it must be possible to do what I need:

Select taberbloca assign to "$TAS_MAE/ERBLOCAEBT"
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
------

I�m using Micro Focus / Unix.

Thanks for any help.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing environment variables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p1ka8$lkn@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7p1gp3$3q6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
With Micro Focus (or any "X/Open conforming" compiler) check out

   ACCEPT from environment-name/environment-variable

syntax
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing environment variables

- **From:** "pmjones" <pmjones@netcomuk.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p8vq3$2so$1@taliesin.netcom.net.uk>`
- **References:** `<7p1gp3$3q6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Easy-peasy (when you know how !!).

To get environment variables in the procedure division, do this:

01    ws-env-val    pic x(80).
....
display "LOGNAME" upon environment-name
accept ws-env-val      from environment-value

That's all there is to it. This will get you the name of the logged-in user
on Unix, but you can use to retrieve any environment variable values you
like. You can also set values with "display .... upon environment-value",
but this can only be used to communicate with other programs within the same
process, since you cannot pass values to a parent process.

Of course, if you want to manipulate file-names, as in your example, you can
code like this:
select my-file assign to external ABCFILE.

This will be resolved by the Micro Focus rte as follows:
if the parent shell has an environment variable set with this id, it will
use that:
export ABCFILE=/adir/in/data
or the old, but still supported dd_ format:
export dd_ABCFILE=diff_name.txt
If no variable exists, the filename used will be ABCFILE in the current
directory.

Oh, finally, beware of underbars in variable NAMES when using MF Cobol.
select afile assign to external FILE_1
select bfile assign to external FILE_2
will both look for the variable FILE, as everything from an underbar onwards
is ignored (extremely irritating!).

Pete Jones


makolbe@my-deja.com wrote in message <7p1gp3$3q6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I need to retrieve the values of environment variables without using
command
>line parameters or auxiliary files.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Thread: On Size Error — 4 msgs

### Re: On Size Error

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B4F84E.BDF76E6A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com> <37b5aee8.540433@news.indigo.ie>`

```
> On Thu, 12 Aug 1999 12:13:53 -0500, "Charles Dusheke"
> <cwdusheke@dstsystems.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Dare I say it ? (Yes) - the above is HORRIBLE code. Why confuse the
file reading technique with a compute, wrapping READ and END-READ around
the COMPUTE - they are separate actions and the COMPUTE only comes into
play if you have a record that satisfies your processing conditions.
From your example 'read file1' you don't quote a key, so I assume you
are reading sequentially :-

	set FileNotFinished to true
	read infile next record at end set FileFinished to true
	End-read 				*> if you want 

	perform until FileFinished

	  If RecordKey = MyCondition		*> optiional
	     compute x = a + b
       	        on size error
	        set FileFinished to true
                perform error-routine
	      end-compute
	   End-if				*> optional

	   if FileNotFinished
	      read infile next record at end set FileFinished to true
	      End-read  			*> if you want
           end-if
	End-perform

Sure you can intermingle stuff - but KISS.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: On Size Error

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p3b19$dtl$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ouvo8$p41$1@mail.dstsystems.com> <37b5aee8.540433@news.indigo.ie> <37B4F84E.BDF76E6A@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37B4F84E.BDF76E6A@home.com>...
[...]
> set FileNotFinished to true
> read infile next record at end set FileFinished to true
> End-read *> if you want

Probably just a "thinko" but  the 'End-read' is not *really*
optional. The choice is either 'End-read' or a period. Failure
to have one of these will cause the following perform to be
executed only "at end".

> perform until FileFinished
[...]
> End-perform

And yes, I, too, have posted such "thinkos" ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: On Size Error

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B5CF76.99CE0423@home.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <37B4F84E.BDF76E6A@home.com>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Smith

You're right - I did a test after your comment and on first read - Boom
! - went straight to STOP RUN. Tricky, tricky this END and PERIOD thing.
This is where we need a on-line library of examples to overcome this
type of goof.

I don't program in this style, using OO to keep my file-handling as
separate classes, but if I'd checked my own STANDARD code first.......

          Evaluate TRUE

            When read-PrimeKey

             move lnk-PrimeKey to Data-PrimeKey
             Read Data-File
               Invalid key
                 Set record-not-found to True
               Not invalid key
                 Move Data-record to os-record
             End-Read

            When read-next

             Read Data-File next record
               At End
                 Set file-finis to True
               Not at End
                 Move Data-record to os-record
             End-Read

            When start-PrimeKey
		....................
	End-evaluate

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b6ce45@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s>`

```

Robert wrote in message <#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s>...
>What a discovery!
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>development and execution environment, allowing you to start building
robust
>client/server applications immediately. The PowerBSORT OCX is ready to
build
>into your applications. A full set of softcopy manuals is also available
for
>downloading.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There may be some better free COMPILERS, but Fujitsu provide a complete
environment. Like you, I was also persuaded to try version 3. I liked it. I
like version 4.2 (which I purchased, along with a years support, for around
US$400) even more.  In fact, 4.2 is light years ahead of 3 and provides full
32 bit support. I am building Windows applications easily and painlessly
using it. It interfaces easily to ODBC, has COM support so you can build
ActiveX controls which can be called easily from VB, C++, and any COM
compliant system, and allows easy integration of both COBOL and VB. It
supports full OO with class manufacture, inheritance, etc. but you don't
need to use the OO facilities unless you want to.

I am so impressed with Fujitsu PowerCOBOL (this is the product used for
development in GUI environments; COBOL97 is the standard batch COBOL
product. You get both if you purchase 4.2) that I have converted ALL of my
previous MicroFocus applications (they provide a software tool to do this
and it is over 90% effective) to the new environment.

Support is extremely responsive and on the very few occasions when I
actually needed support, I had a solution from them within 24 hours. (On one
occasion, one of their guys phoned me from Colorado (I am in England) and
was extremely supportive. We solved the problem on the phone.) I get the
impression that they really care about their customer base (even small guys
like me) and that they are committed to bringing COBOL into the next
Millennium.

After the problems experienced with MicroFocus, I have to say there is no
comparison in my book. The event processing (covered by DIALOG in MF) is all
written in COBOL with Fujitsu so there is no need for a separate language.

The only thing which MF do better than Fujitsu is the Animator. (Let's face
it, the Animator is a fantastic piece of work...) However, the Fujitsu
debugging environment is adequate and you can debug VB modules seamlessly,
so If COBOL calls VB or vice versa, you can step through the source of both
without having to change environments.

If any of you decide to change to Fujitsu, allow about 6 weeks to get used
to the new environment. If you have problems, write me. (I'm not on
commission, I just believe it's a really good product).

Pete.
```

```
limits what you are allowed to say about it unfortunately.

As a rule most COBOL compilers are slow compared to C/++
compilers, but if you are after relatively good performance in a
cobol compiler I would steer you in the direction of FJ cobol.

Tim Josling
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au...
 <snip>
>
> As a rule most COBOL compilers are slow compared to C/++
> compilers, but if you are after relatively good performance in a
> cobol compiler I would steer you in the direction of FJ cobol.
>

Out of curiousity, are you saying that the compiler itself runs slowly
(while compiling source code) or are you saying that the "object code"
produced by the compiler runs slowly?

My experience (generally more mainframe oriented than workstation oriented -
but some experience in both) is that "production programmers" can "stand" a
slow compile - if it produces well optimized object code.  Similarly
(especially with COBOL) the more optimization of run-time speeds that you
set the compiler to - the longer the compile takes. (This passes a
"reasonbility" test - at least IMHO - and almost all the COBOL compilers
that I know do offer various levels of "optimization".)

Having said this, I have also heard "rumors" that Object Oriented COBOL (for
those compilers that offer it) DO tend to produce fairly slow-running
programs - but that each release seems to get "faster".  On the other hand,
statistics that I saw (while still working for Micro Focus) indicated that
for straight "batch" type processing, many COBOL compilers produced MORE
efficient object code than many C compilers. (from the fact that you
mentioned C++, my guess is that you are talking about an OO type
application)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO Performance Issues and Testing (was: Re: FREE: COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p8r9l$304j$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37B74BBA.99EB5E2C@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37B74BBA.99EB5E2C@home.com>...
[...]
>This as I recall was one of the concerns that Rick Smith had about OO -
>a poor performance in C++ - but it depends what they were testing and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>course.

One of the big problems with performance testing, is making
sure of that which is being tested. We had a similar issues in
a thread titled "Right Justify Routine," during September 1998.
The results I posted were:

"The differential for Function Reverse in MF 3.2 comes to about 70:1
when following the requirement for the use of a "temporary elementary
data item." When treating it "as if" it used a "temporary elementary
data item," the ratio is about 106:1 when the argument and receiving
operands are different and 125:1 when the argument and receiving
operands are the same."

The question then was "Why is there such a big difference in
performance?" I was unable to explain all the difference, at that
time. Further analysis showed a nearly 3:1 difference between
static and dynamic linking. The balance appears to have been
related to dynamic allocation of the required temporary variable,
the overhead of the (compiler generated) call, checking for the
selection of character set (single-byte versus multi-byte), and
checking for crossing segment boundaries on a 16-bit
implementation.

The conclusion was that, if one is uses Function Reverse in
a loop, performance will be reduced. Furthermore, the tighter
the loop, the greater the loss of performance.

This is the same issue I raised concerning the use of OO.
If the general process model for an application is record
at a time processing, the use of OO to implement that model
will cause a loss of performance due to memory allocation
overhead. The amount of loss will depend upon the duration
of the object. (How tight the loop is.)

If one happens to use Function Reverse in a tight loop, one
could reverse the loss of performance by replacing the
function with tailored code. The same is not true of OO. This
is because the decision to use OO and the design of the class
is, somewhat, independent of the applications where it will be
used. Though this provides more flexibility for creating new
applications by "reusing components," it removes the ability
to optimize loops to reverse performance loss.

Just as the testing, I did, proved nothing about the
desireablility of using, or not using, Function Reverse; no
simple performance test will demonstrate whether or not
to use OO. This is because the tests do not reflect reality.

What would be useful for clarifying the performance issues
is for a testing organization to create a few identical
applications using modular and OO techniques. If these
applications can be shown to simulate "real" applications,
then the results should show "real" relative performance.
(No, I am not volunteering!)

At best, I have only seen *sketchy* evidence that the use
of OO *may* have been *partly* responsible for some
publicized failures of new C/S applications that were
intended to replace mainframe applications. Even this
"evidence" did not clearly blame performance as the
reason for the failure.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Performance Issues and Testing (was: Re: FREE: COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Performance Issues and Testing (was: Re: FREE: COBOL compiler)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pbnb8$22je$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37B74BBA.99EB5E2C@home.com> <7p8r9l$304j$1@news.hitter.net> <37B8DC53.72EC73CC@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37B8DC53.72EC73CC@home.com>...
[...]
>Trouble is Rick, this will go on ad nauseam until somebody does tests -
>there's got to be some grubby lab, somewhere in the academic world,
>where they do this sort of thing.

Some might argue that it already has gone on "ad nauseam." ;-)

Academia may not be a good starting point; "After all, what
do Computer Science professors know about COBOL.
They don't teach it!"

Assuming that a test is constructed and results published;
many will not accept the results. Some will question whether
any such test adequately reflects the needs of *their* business
(or business in general); whether computer performance is
more important than productivity; whether the sample
organization model and the OO and modular designs
compared are correct; etc.

Furthermore, it may not matter if (the proverbial) *you* like OO,
if *you* feel it increases *your* productivity, and if *you* are
willing to sacrifice a little performance. There will always be
a Shirley or Bob with a story about how *your* new program is
too slow and is making it impossible for them to do their job.
It is difficult to be proud of your work when your customers are
complaining.

I think the best that can be accomplished, in the near term, is
to identify where and why using OO might affect performance.
This allows individuals to determine whether to construct
their own tests using real problems for comparison. Perhaps
some will share their results.

In the long term, we will see the results in stories and job
postings.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

- **From:** Lawrence Allen Free <free@mcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p7og8$1ak$1@Nntp1.mcs.net>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On UNIX platforms, I have found well written programs in COBOL and
C run about the same, though COBOL will run circles around the C
program if COBOL stores data native while C uses a database.  The
two are roughly equal if both use a database.  Compiles only bug
the developers, not the users.  As compiling is such a small
fraction of the development process, since when is this an issue?

I've found writing programs appropriate to COBOL to take far less
time than writing the equivalent in C.  Then again, a program
appropriate to C can take longer to write in COBOL.  Given this,
why worry about compile time?  If you're trying to compare the
two languages, don't.  Each does what it does well far better
than the other and it would be foolish to try to use one to the
exclusion of the other for all programming.  Then again, comments
like the last sentence sometimes gets us multilingual programmers
in trouble in both camps.

In article 7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com,
	"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> said:
>
>Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>>


...<snip>...

>
>Don't see you should get into trouble in the COBOL camp. You've made a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

Some 'goodies' have migrated from C to COBOL (and vice-versa), while, for
others there simply wasn't any need. Like dynamic virtual memory
allocation... since when is there a need for that functionality in a
business oriented application? And the same can be said about the COBOL
'goodies' missing from C, such as language specific data file handling...
since when does anyone building a device driver need the ability to perform
indexed retrievals?

C/C++ (and BASIC) is a wonderful language to write compute intensive,
non-business related applications. For I/O based database applications,
nothing beats COBOL (or RPG).
```

```
For that matter, how would I go  about setting up PL/1 to call a module
dynamically,  I have yet to find the right reference manuals to explore for
continuing on the PL/1 development.

Thanks for any help/pointers you might be able to provide.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL... and other languages

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bee8f8$87517ab0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<7p999j$s98$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <19990816204524.15609.00000304@ngol06.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in article
<19990816204524.15609.00000304@ngol06.aol.com>...
> Would anyone have experience getting PL/1 calling a COBOL sub program
> mixing call by CONTENT with call by REFERENCE?
> For that matter, how would I go  about setting up PL/1 to call a module
> dynamically,

Those questions are better asked in the PL1 newsgroup.
But anyway:

PL/1 call in general by REFERENCE.  You may force a copy of the original by
enclosing it in parenthesis:   CALL SUB(A,(B));   A will be by reference to
the original A, while (B) will be by reference to a copy of B.

The CALL to SUB will be dynamic if you anywhere in the PL/1 program have a
RELEASE SUB statement (it do not have to be executed) (implied FETCH). 
This doesn't work if SUB is Cobol.

With LE and PL/I for MVS you can FETCH SUB and then CALL SUB when SUB is
Cobol.  SUB will be released when LE terminates.  This doesn't work with
PL/I 2.3.

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Report Writer Required in next Standard (was FREE: COBOL compiler

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pa3j2$3nd@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <7p7og8$1ak$1@Nntp1.mcs.net> <37B7896F.A2889ECB@home.com> <37B87A26.36B36701@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:37B87A26.36B36701@melbpc.org.au...
  <here a snip, there a snip>
>
> Why have they made it compulsary to support report writer? Do lots of
> people use it?

This can get into one of our "traditional C.L.C. religious wars".  A few
things that I think are medium-uncontroversial:

1) In shops where Report Writer is used (other than a single programmer's
"fad"), it is used heavily (both for maintenance AND new development). In
shops that don't use it heavily, you won't find it at all.

2) We have something of a "chicken-and-egg" issue here.  In past Standards
it was optional - so some vendors didn't support it - so programmers
interested in portability never learned it - so it wasn't used - so vendors
didn't implement it.  The hope/expectation of J4 (and WG4) is that once it
is required (and enhanced) in the new Standard, more programmers will learn
it - and learn how useful it CAN be.

3) Whether or not you see the need for this feature MAY depend (partially -
not completely) on whether you (or you compiler) is targeted PRIMARILY at
"on-line" (or real-time) applications - or more traditional "batch"
applications.  No matter how many of the former are being developed today,
J4 (and WG4) believe that many, MANY, applications of the latter type are
still being developmed and enhanced.  Besides enhanced (and required) RW,
the draft Standard also adds a VALIDATE facility that will be MOST useful in
"batch" type applications (but certainly CAN be used in real-time ones as
well).

4)  In general, J4 (and WG4) have removed ALL "optional" facitilites from
the next Standard - along with removing the concept of "subsets".  The
theory is (or so it goes) that a feature is either in the Standard or it
isn't.  Once it is in the Standard, any programmer can rely on it being
there in any conforming implementation.  (I say "in general" because of my
"pet peeve" of the Processor Dependent definition including the rather
amorphous concept of "market positioning".)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B8D79A.A8144282@home.com>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <7p7og8$1ak$1@Nntp1.mcs.net> <37B7896F.A2889ECB@home.com> <37B87A26.36B36701@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> Check out the new draft stadard. You could summarise it as "turning
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Why are you worried about increasing the size of the language. They
didn't have one back then, but do you think the size of a dictionary in
Chaucer's or Shakespeare's time would anywhere equal the size of the
current Oxford or Webster ? Has our language suffered - no it is even
richer with the additions made since Dr. Johnson introduced the 'first'.
Let's continuously have a richer COBOL.

And I don't dislike your definition of the new COBOL standard - just
what I would like to see. Freely purloin from other languages, (just
like English has), to make COBOL even more powerful and flexible.

If I'm gardening I use a set of different tools to do different jobs. I
don't consider spoken/computer languages to be tools in that sense; they
are a different category, communication tools. To communicate you don't
pick up a new tool, you add to the verbal/processing language. 
Dr. Johnson didn't coin it - "fair dinkum mate" - you Antipodeans did,
and we understand exactly what your 'new' syntax means, and the context
in which it is used, without having to rifle through a French or Greek
dictionary.

With regards to Report Writer - obviously somebody talked long and loud.
OO in the standards is a real "funny' - as most people consider it akin
to the Black Death, I wonder how it ever made it into the standards ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B95FC1.304F1F2B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <7p7og8$1ak$1@Nntp1.mcs.net> <37B7896F.A2889ECB@home.com> <37B87A26.36B36701@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> Why have they made it compulsary to support report writer? Do lots of
> people use it?

Didn't use it when it was available - because not enough programmers
were comfortable with it.  I needed to see a bigger advantage than
report writer provides to put it in programs which others would
maintain.

But that's a catch-22.  If it's not available in all platforms, my
reason listed above will be magnified, especially in environments with
multiple COBOL compilers.  Programmers are staying away from Windows
specific JAVA because they are not universal.  Cobol programmers want to
stay as portable as possible as well.  By making report writer
universal, it is more portable.

Still.  ALL Cobol programmers can code and debug non-Report Writer
reports.  Experienced Cobol programmers hardly need to think about
coding non-Report Writer reports, so the cost of giving up Report Writer
is minimal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler - speed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u90u3.352$MK5.23195@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37B87803.FC6B7AF7@melbpc.org.au>`

```

Tim Josling wrote in message <37B87803.FC6B7AF7@melbpc.org.au>...
>Again I am generalizing but both compilation and runtimes were generally
>slower for cobol compared to c/++, and not by a small margin.

Well, I can generalise, too.

1. Who cares about differences in compile time?  If you have something that
takes more than a minute or two to compile (yes, I know: two minutes
"waiting" can seem forever) , you probably have something else to work on in
the meantime anyway.

2. I just do not accept that the source language makes any difference in the
performance of the run-time. It may be be a function of the run-time
designer, a little; but more than anything else it is the way the programmer
(C/C++ or COBOL) has designed and coded the application program.

It's not the paintbrush, it's the artist.

MCM
```

```
<michael.mattias@gte.net> illuminated alt.cobol thusly:

> 1. Who cares about differences in compile time?  If you have something that
> takes more than a minute or two to compile (yes, I know: two minutes
> "waiting" can seem forever) , you probably have something else to work on in
> the meantime anyway.

People with large programs care about compile time.  I heard of a Unisys A
Series shop (I think they had an A-7) where their 50,000+ line online
program took about 20 minutes to compile with COBOL74.  They converted the
program to COBOL85 and compile time was about an hour and a half.  When
your online is down, and hour and a half is a very long time.  They
reverted to COBOL74.
```

```
compiled programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler - speed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pchbf$3qg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37B87803.FC6B7AF7@melbpc.org.au>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:
: Again I am generalizing but both compilation and runtimes were generally
: slower for cobol compared to c/++, and not by a small margin. COBOL
: Compilers varied by large margins (factor or 4-5 in compilation and
: runtime speed).


It would be extremely difficult to compare realistic programs
in either compile or run time as it is unlikely that similar
programs would be done in these languages - except as toy
benchmark programs.  Usually the two languages are used to
do different jobs.

It would be like comparing, say, the times to do an oil change
and the motorway speeds of a city bus and a sports car.

As a different way of looking at the situation: ACUCobol (eg)
produces intermediate compiled code which can be distributed
to any environment for which there is a run-time.  If several
different environments are being supported then the compile can
be done _once_ (and the testing done once) wheras with C/C++
the compile and testing must be done for each environment.

With compiles I find it is only the MS based developers that
complain about compile speed, they are often forced to sit
and stare while it happens as, even if the IDE allows multi-
tasking, it is not worth doing.

In any reasonable environment the compiles take place in
background while the developer writes up the documentation
of edits another program.

On run speed, this is usually so overwealmed by disk I/O on
systems for which Cobol is suitable, that it is usually
(not always) irrelevant.  Having had clients with 3 or 4
interactive users on an 8086 1 Mbyte machine with semi
interpreted (.INT) Cobol systems, I never have fears of
run time performance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler - speed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B9D396.AA9CA00D@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37B87803.FC6B7AF7@melbpc.org.au> <7pchbf$3qg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Compile times are important. If they are below a threshold (say 1
second), you can stay in flow on the one task. Otherwise you have
to keep switching contexts which impairs productivity. 

I know one second compiles are a dream to many people, but on my
PC with C it is standard. A lot of people are getting 30 minutes
or worse, so you have to multitask.

There are companies making a good living selling cobol tuning
tools, with CPU as a major component. CPU is a very expensive
resource especially on S/390. Especially if you want you programs
to be flexible, runtime speed is very important to some people.
Especially when processing millions of customer records.

Tim Josling

Richard Plinston wrote:
...
> In any reasonable environment the compiles take place in
> background while the developer writes up the documentation
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler - speed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pe7c0$mpg$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <7p7ckr$l9n@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37B87803.FC6B7AF7@melbpc.org.au>`

```

Tim Josling wrote in message <37B87803.FC6B7AF7@melbpc.org.au>...
>Again I am generalizing but both compilation and runtimes were generally
>slower for cobol compared to c/++, and not by a small margin. COBOL
>Compilers varied by large margins (factor or 4-5 in compilation and
>runtime speed).

The only time I ever did a head-to-head comparison
of runtime performance in COBOL versus C, C lost.
What I wanted to accomplish, at that time, was to
find the fastest language for implementing a particular
algorithm.

The C compiler was MS C 6.0. The COBOL was
MF COBOL/2. It was about 1990 or 1991.

I did a direct (naive) translation of the COBOL program
into C. The C program was about half as fast.

I then modified the C program to adjust for the standard
runtime features of MF COBOL. This included buffering
of I/O for sequential reads and writes, reducing the frequency
of displays to the console to compensate for the speed
of direct versus indirect screen output, etc.

Then I used available C language features to speed
other operations. This mostly involved using pointers
instead of subscripts for array (table) operations. At this
point, the C program was slightly faster than the COBOL.

I then looked at the assembler listing for both languages.
I found that I could optimize a critical COBOL in-line perform
by replacing it with loops using GO TO. This made the
COBOL program about 30 per cent faster. At that time,
I could find no additional changes, I could make, to the
C program, without resorting to assembler.

Much later I discovered a few changes that would have
increased the performance of the C porgram. I also
discovered additional changes I could make to the
COBOL program. I did not implement those changes
because I was more interested in modifying the
alogorithm.

I am at a loss to explain your experience with COBOL
performance being much worse than C. Obviously, my
experience is that compiled COBOL and compiled C
should have comparable performance for the same problem
using identical alogoritms.

As a practical matter, I think a 4:1 difference for COBOL
versus C would be tolerable with a version 1.0; if the
difference is primarily in internal performance that can be
optimized later. I think the target should be within 2:1 for
a version 2.0.

My rationale for these numbers is that, initially, it is better
to have something that works. Once it works, make it
run faster. Also, for business programming, a programmer
who knows both COBOL and C will not choose C based
solely upon performance. However, given multiple COBOL
implementations, performance will be a factor in the
selection process; that is why performance must improve
in a later release.

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** trblshtr@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pkf6j$css$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <37B7868B.FA805315@home.com> <zW4v3.3113$a4.286709@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
There are 2 issues John.
1) How fast (and accurately) you can develop the program
2) How fast will the resulting program run.

With regard to the first issue (and excluding ALL external influences
such as portability, compilers available, shop standards, etc) only you
know where your proficiency, and efficiency, lies.  Being bi/multi-
lingual can be advantageous but with programming, like any language, I
suspect you will find 1 language that you consider native or prime.

With regard to the second issue (and again excluding the external
influences) inside every computer is a "clock" that "beats" at a very
precise rate.  Each "beat" is a "cycle". The "speed" of the clock
determines the number of "cycles" available during a given time
segment.  For example, a 233Mhz system will provide 233 million cycles
during a 1 second time segment.  Each "instruction"/"action" which the
computer performs (at the lowest level) requires 'n' cycles to complete.
'n' is fixed for any given "instruction" performed by a given processor
(but NOT, necessarily, the same value for the same instruction
performed on different processors.  For example, 80286 vs Pentium would
yield different 'n' values for the same instruction).  That said; the
'speed' of the compile will be determined by the overall efficiency of
the compiler with regard to its usage of cycles.  Consider, it will
take more cycles to load a long value into a register (at the low
level) than to load an integer into that register.  Obviously, a
compiler which always deals with longs when integers would do the job
will utilize more cycles than one which has differentiated and
optimized the use of the cycles.  The same type of scenario will hold
true with the runtime (interpretive or directly executable) application
programs which have been generated.  That is; the more "efficient" the
use of cycles the "faster" the program will be.  Remember, the number
of cycles available during a given time segment is static. So too, are
the number of cycles each low level instruction will require.  So too,
given a given compiler (COBOL, C, C++, JAVA...), are the number of low
level instructions which will be generated from a given higher level
instruction (  if IsDate(txtMainNm.Text) then ... )when either compiled
or interpreted.  [It should be apparent that compiled would be faster
because interpretation will require the additional cycles to do the
interpretation].

That said: the bottom line is - the best language to use is the one
which provides the optimum results for the task at hand.  In some cases
the task at hand will be "I need report x in 2 hours" and in other
cases the task at hand will be "you have got to make that x run
faster".  Having some 'sense' of advantages/disadvantages of various
languages both in terms of ease of use and in terms of how a language
"works" is very important and will be acquired through your own
experience. From your comment/question I suspect you already knew that
but were just hoping it would be easier!  It isn't.  After all; only
experience will tell you that the same language, from the same vendor,
used to develop the same application with the same code, will yield
different results (with regard to "speed"/"efficiency") even on the
same hardware platform dependent upon such factors as configuration and
the sequence of activity which came before.  [And I don't even want to
get into various methodologies of writing specific applications].

Good luck!!  Have fun!! (because if you're not you're in the wrong
business)
<G>
trblshtr

In article <zW4v3.3113$a4.286709@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>,
  "john smith" <someone@microsoft.com> wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WCcv3.4746$rd6.44853@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<#uhYSZv5#GA.214@net003s> <37B72758.EA2E544A@melbpc.org.au> <37B7868B.FA805315@home.com> <zW4v3.3113$a4.286709@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
john smith wrote:
>Now that the original question whatever it was has been fragged to
>death, I want to know this:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tom

That's a healthy attitude, Tom.  On experience you will learn which
kinds of tasks each language is best suited for, and which tasks can
be reasonably addressed by either language.  For example, COBOL would
be a poor choice for writing most system software, and C is a poor
choice for writing financial applications.  But I use COBOL and C and
BASIC to write utility programs.  BASIC is superb for writing little
'quickie' programs.  But sometimes COBOL or C is better suited to the
particular task.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE: COBOL compiler

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** JD <beta1@bigfoot.com>

```
I don't know what its status is since Computer Associates bought it out, but
prior to the buyout the Realia COBOL compiler was WRITTEN in COBOL almost
entirely.  There were a few assembler subroutines where operating system
level functions were accessed.  And at the time, it was the fastest compiler
on the market, both in compile times and in execution times of the generated
modules.

In any event, COBOL is far more powerful than the average COBOL coder
realizes.
```

```
"Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" wrote:
> 
> Sounds interesting. Does anyone have an FTP program that can
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have not got the manual pages handy right now but this is possible
with standard FTP.

Here is the description I lifted from the man files.  I have seen this
work transferring xxx.cob to xxx on MVS.  I have not have the
opportunity (need) to really make this stuff work, most of mine are
simple one off transfers or totally program controlled (I write a
script that is input to FTP as a command file, has work reliably for
about 4 years through 4 changes of FTP client).

Ken

nmap [inpattern outpattern]

Set or unset the filename mapping mechanism.  If no arguments are
specified, the filename mapping mechanism is unset.  If arguments are
specified, remote filenames are mapped during mput commands and put
commands issued without a specified remote target filename.  If
arguments are specified, local filenames are mapped during mget
commands and get commands issued without a specified local target
filename.  This command is useful when connecting to a non-UNIX remote
computer with different file naming conventions or practices.  The
mapping follows the pattern set by inpattern and outpattern.
[Inpattern] is a template for incoming filenames (which may have
already been processed according to the ntrans and case settings). 
Variable templating is accomplished by including the sequences `$1',
`$2', ..., `$9' in inpattern. Use `\' to prevent this special
treatment of the `$' character.  All other characters are treated
literally, and are used to determine the nmap [inpattern] variable
values.  For example,  given inpattern $1.$2 and the remote file name
"mydata.data", $1 would have the value "mydata", and $2 would have the
value "data".  The outpattern determines the resulting mapped
file-name.  The sequences `$1', `$2', ...., `$9' are replaced by any
value resulting from the inpattern template.  The sequence `$0' is
replace by the original filename.  Additionally, the sequence `[seq1,
seq2]' is replaced by [seq1] if seq1 is not a null string; otherwise
it is replaced by seq2. For example, the command

    nmap $1.$2.$3 [$1,$2].[$2,file]

would yield the output filename "myfile.data" for input filenames
"myfile.data" and "myfile.data.old", "myfile.file" for the input
filename "myfile", and "myfile.myfile" for the input filename
".myfile".  Spaces may be included in outpattern, as in the example:
`nmap $1 sed "s/  *$//" > $1'.  Use the `\' character to prevent
special treatment of the `$','[','[', and `,' characters.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus /Sql Server performance issue — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### MicroFocus /Sql Server performance issue

- **From:** csm14@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7p9o80$itr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We have a job that reads a 6000 byte file, and for each record performs
various selects and then inserts into various tables.  The job was
compiled using MicroFocus COBOL 4.0. This process was running in about
10 hours.  We had to make a small change (an additional field on the
file was checked before processing) and after recompile the process is
now taking about 40 hours.  When we use the NT performance monitor we
are only seeing about 5% CPU usage and very limited I/O.  The source,
application and target are all on the same server which is used
exclusively for this one application.  What is even more confusing is
that running under animator (which is interpreted) is now faster than
running the compiled job.  After this problem surfaced we upgraded from
6.5 to version 7 hoping to improve performance, with no effect.  Any
suggestions?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6e%t3.15195$C6.153404@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
<csm14@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7p9o80$itr$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> We have a job that reads a 6000 byte file, and for each record performs
> various selects and then inserts into various tables.  The job was
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

All things being equal, it sounds like you used different compiler
directives than the last person who compiled the program. Try to find out
how the program used to be built. Failing that:

1) Check your precompiler directives for CONNECT and DISCONNECT. It may be
that the precompiler stuck them in all over the place.
2) Check if you're working in EXCLUSIVE or SHARED mode. Sharing adds
overhead.
3) Find and read the Micro Focus reference manual for working with SQL
Server.
```

## Thread: Hello. New Guy here. Just wondering if... — 17 msgs

### Hello. New Guy here. Just wondering if...

- **From:** "x" <rsb@la.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
...anyone in the MVS world has had any experience with Language Environment
(LE)?  In going through my OS/390 Collection (yawn!), two pubs, COBOL for
OS/390 & VM V2.1 Compiler and Run-Time Migration Guide and Language
Environment V1R4 Concepts Guide, I'm seeing a migration path of COBOL/VS and
COBOL/II apps to COBOL for OS/390 via LE.

This looked like a re-link job for existing code (mostly) and source
migration could occur at a more leisurely pace.  LE looks like an extension
of some of IBM's windowing routines for Y2K stuff and a lot more for people
on a Posix path.

While reading through the pubs, I just see a lot of potential gotchas (res
vs nores re-links, abend handling in CICS, sysout differences, etc.).  Just
wondering if there is anyone walking down the path yet and how many arrows
have been imbedded in the back?  Like so many other IBM tools, program
products, etc., I always wonder what is the value?  Anybody doing it?
Thanks in advance for comments!

BTW, looked through several several hundred headers and several postings and
was very amused by how you handled 8/11.  Nice job!
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello. New Guy here. Just wondering if...

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990817181340.23600.00000304@ng-fs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
rsb@la.com writes...

>...anyone in the MVS world has had any experience with Language Environment
>(LE)? 

Yes, lots. I seem to recall hearing that about 85% of MVS / OS/390 shops have
LE up (of course, you have to have it up at some level of OS/390). Got to have
it for new features.


[snip]

>LE looks like an extension
>of some of IBM's windowing routines for Y2K stuff and a lot more for people
>on a Posix path.

Well, it's that and a lot more. And it's not just for people on a Posix / Unix
path. For COBOL applications programmers, the most useful LE services aree:

* dynamic allocation of storage
   for tables

* powerful error handling capabilities,
    if you want to take advantage of
    them

* and, of course, the date / time services

Also, you must have LE available to support the new compilers. Lots of
improvement in InterLanguage Communication, and lots of language specific
improvements.

To name just a few capabilities in the LE COBOL compilers...

* ANSI intrinsic functions
   (includes use of ALL as a
    subscript when referencing
    tables for some functions)
* Local-storage section
* Recursive programs
* pass arguments by reference, 
     by content, or by value
* receive parameters by reference
    or by value
* null-terminated strings
* object oriented COBOL
* create DLLs for use by other COBOL
    programs or by C programs
* use DLLs written in C

[snip]

>Just
>wondering if there is anyone walking down the path yet and how many arrows
>have been imbedded in the back?  Like so many other IBM tools, program
>products, etc., I always wonder what is the value?  Anybody doing it?

Lots of folks walking down the path. And plenty of arrows, but it's getting
better. The value, of course, depends on your needs and how creative you can be
in using the new facilities.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hello. New Guy here. Just wondering if...

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B9FCCF.2434@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990817181340.23600.00000304@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock wrote:
> 
> rsb@la.com writes...
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> USA


I second everything that Steve says, and would like to share some more
stuff.

I have heard more than a few people say "but you have to upgrade your
COBOL to COBOL/LE to be Y2K compliant!".  THIS IS AN INCORRECT
STATEMENT.  A VS-COBOL application can be Y2K compliant.

My datacenter has upgraded 100% of its MVS COBOL source to the LE level
compiler.  This conversion project went something like this:

1) Implemented the COBOL-II compiler as soon as it became available
(several years ago).
2) Every time a developer checked out a COBOL module for maintenance, it
automatically went through a home-grown code converter to upgrade the
source to COBOL-II.  This converter only accomplished an 80% job.  The
other 20% came out during testing. (Yes, our developers had to be VERY
complete in their testing!)
3) When the LE compiler came along, we discovered that COBOL-II source
can compile just fine under LE without any changes.  LE simply added
more features that COBOL-II did not have.  So we replaced the COBOL-II
compiler with the LE compiler.
4) In the last 12 months, I identified only about 150 programs that were
not yet upgraded.  I upgraded and tested them, thus completing the
conversion!

A major benefit of upgrading, is your software support staff will have a
very difficult time supporting vendor products that are out-of-favor
with that vendor.  For a short while our shop was supporting three
different flavors of COBOL running on the machine, which impacts your
ability to de-bug and support your applications in a quick and effective
manner.

And repeating what Steve said, there are better error handling
routines.  If an LE-COBOL module fails while executing in the LE
run-time environment, you have a better chance that there will be a
report that points you to the real cause of the failure.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Converters

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BAB1D6.AE5359F8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990817181340.23600.00000304@ng-fs1.aol.com> <37B9FCCF.2434@saif.com>`

```
> 2) Every time a developer checked out a COBOL module for maintenance, it
> automatically went through a home-grown code converter to upgrade the
> source to COBOL-II.  This converter only accomplished an 80% job.  The
> other 20% came out during testing. (Yes, our developers had to be VERY
> complete in their testing!)
...
> Pete

We run all of our old code through IBM's Cobol for MVS converter.  I
think though that I am the only person here which questions its value. 
It seems to me that the choice is between:

A.
1.  Create the converter environment.
2.  Run a program through the converter.
3.  Examine the program to see what it does with dates and obsolete
commands.
4.  Fix the program 
5.  Test the program.

Or 
B.
1.  Examine the program to see what it does with dates and obsolete
commands.
2.  Fix the program 
3.  Test the program.

I prefer option B.  I like to see what I'm doing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bee9bc$12590560$0100007f@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990817181340.23600.00000304@ng-fs1.aol.com> <37B9FCCF.2434@saif.com> <37BAB1D6.AE5359F8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Now now, it is NOT acceptable to challenge management prejudices
with facts or logic.  Doing that can get you fired, or worse.
```

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:37BAB1D6.AE5359F8@NOSPAMhome.com...

>
> We run all of our old code through IBM's Cobol for MVS converter.  I
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

If you are talking about IBM's CCCA product, then I don't see any real
reason NOT to use it to do a LOT of the "grunt" work.  It can really convert
(automatically) a lot of that old OS/VS COBOL (both LANGLVL(1) and
LANGLVL(2) source code) - without you having to do anything.  It is true
that you still need to do some things manually - and there are other things
that it suggests that you look at.   (It is possible that you are talking
about something other than CCCA - because it creates the "environment" for
you - and it doesn't claim to deal with dates.  But it is certainly an easy
way to handle "old" syntax issues.)
```

```
We have some oldoldold COBOL which has been described to me as "A-N-S COBOL".
(Did you figure out that COBOL is not my first language? ;-)   I don't even
know _which_ compiler would have correctly compiled it, but I know we no
longer have that one installed.  

It's code that works... until it breaks, and then we're in deep yogurt
because, even if we (they, not me) _do_ fix it, it won't compile because of
language features no longer supported.   Backward compatibility?  WDNNS
backward compatibility!

So the question is... how do get here from there?  "Here" being a condition in
which the code can be maintained... if it ever needs to be.  



Frank Clarke
```

```
Frank Clarke <nisus$@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:37c6f808.92878177@NEWS.mindspring.com...
  <snip>
>
> We have some oldoldold COBOL which has been described to me as "A-N-S
COBOL".
> (Did you figure out that COBOL is not my first language? ;-)   I don't
even
> know _which_ compiler would have correctly compiled it, but I know we no
> longer have that one installed.
>
> It's code that works... until it breaks, and then we're in deep yogurt
> because, even if we (they, not me) _do_ fix it, it won't compile because
of
> language features no longer supported.   Backward compatibility?  WDNNS
> backward compatibility!
>
> So the question is... how do get here from there?  "Here" being a
condition in
> which the code can be maintained... if it ever needs to be.
>

OK,
  If we are talking about an IBM mainframe *and* we are talking about what
some programmers call "A-N-S COBOL"  then this is probably a product that
was officially called by IBM "ANS COBOL" and whose last release was "Version
4".  This product was DROPPED by IBM about 1980 !!!

It was relatively easy to migrate from that product to IBM's next product
which was called "OS/VS COBOL".  IBM support for that product was dropped
about 5 years ago.  I won't thru the 3 or 4 products since THEN, but I do
have some good news for you.

IBM has a product called "CCCA" (COBOL and CICS Conversion Aid) which will
AUTOMATICALLY convert over 90% (depending on your application even above
95%) of your source code to something that will compile with the "latest and
greatest" compiler.  Depending on your environment, there is a mainframe
version or a PC version of the conversion aid.  Cost is "minimal" - but the
PLANNING AND TESTING required for your applications during conversion is NOT
minimal.

Have you told us (the NG) yet what your operating system is?  If you are
already running on OS/390 (rather than the soon-to-be-obsolete MVS), then
your shop already has "LE" (Language Environment) which is the new run-time
product that you need to use the newer compilers.

Does this help at all?
```

```
I still think that the hardest is "NOT" in combined abreviated
conditionals - if your old code was compiled with "LANGLVL(1)".  On the
other hand, half the time (from my experience) the program wasn't doing what
the original/current programmer THOUGHT was happening with the old code
anyway - so the conversion really required figuring out the algorithm that
was INTENDED in the first place!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converters

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rsg3s5qcj7185@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990817181340.23600.00000304@ng-fs1.aol.com> <37B9FCCF.2434@saif.com> <37BAB1D6.AE5359F8@NOSPAMhome.com> <37c5c3ca.53658854@NEWS.mindspring.com> <37C68DBA.48925316@NOSPAMhome.com> <37c6f808.92878177@NEWS.mindspring.com>`

```
You could write some elaborate edit macro, but if you have a lot of source,
you'd never quite be sure that it made the changes correctly because macro
edits
work on text.

What you really want is syntax-directed transformations that say, in
essence,
if you see *this* language structure, then replace it by *that*.

The DMS Reengineering toolkit can do this.   See
http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMSToolkit.html.
It can handle "edits" written in the syntax of COBOL.  Here's a simple
sample:

     default base domain Cobol.

    pattern
conditional-if-then-else(c:conditional-expression,s1:non-if-then-statement,s
2:non-if-then-statement):conditional-if-then-else-statement = "
123456     IF
123456        \c
123456     THEN
123456       \s1
123456     ELSE
123456       \s2
".

    pattern
imperative-if-then-else(c:conditional-expression,s1:non-if-then-statement,s2
:non-if-then-statement):imperative-if-statement = "
123456     IF
123456        \c
123456     THEN
123456       \s1
123456     ELSE
123456       \s2
123456     END-IF
".

rule if-then-else-imperative-to-conditional(c,s1,s2) =
  conditional-if-then-else(c,s1,s2) -> imperative-if-then-else(c,s1,s2).

This example picks up non-terminated IF statements having arbitrary
conditions and statement blocks,
and isn't fooled by comments, spacing, continuations, sequence numbers, you
name it.

A set of these for ANS COBOL might make your life much easier.

Frank Clarke wrote in message <37c6f808.92878177@NEWS.mindspring.com>...
>On Fri, 27 Aug 1999 07:08:10 -0600, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
>wrote:
>
>>Frank Clarke wrote:

>We have some oldoldold COBOL which has been described to me as "A-N-S
COBOL".
>(Did you figure out that COBOL is not my first language? ;-)   I don't even
>know _which_ compiler would have correctly compiled it, but I know we no
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>So the question is... how do get here from there?  "Here" being a condition
in
>which the code can be maintained... if it ever needs to be.

>>  I was thinking I might have to write some elaborate ISPF edit macro to
>>do the upgrade.  It would have provided hundreds (maybe thousands) of
billable
>>hours, and I wouldn't have enjoyed anything but the checks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello. New Guy here. Just wondering if...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pd4ac$99b@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37b9c41d@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
First, I wouldn't try this at this particular moment - unless you are REAL
confident in your Y2K situation ... but

1) LE is a run-time product only.  It is entirely possible to stay with VS
COBOL II R4 (still supported thru March 2001) or even OS/VS COBOL (no longer
supported by IBM) as your compiler and switch to the LE run-time.  In fact,
this is the HIGHLY recommended way to go.

2) IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (with or without the Millennium Language
Extensions) is the most current and strategic IBM COBOL compiler (for the
OS/390 environment).

3) If your site is current (or even relatively current) on its operating
system, then you are using OS/390 - and already have LE in-house.  It is
probably even installed - if you are using such products as TCP/IP - which
require it.

4) LE has been available for about (over?) half a decade now, so there are
LOTS AND LOTS of sites that have migrated to it.  Any shop that wanted IBM's
native language support for the Intrinsic Functions or "enhanced ACCEPT FROM
DATE" syntax to get Y2K support is *guaranteed* to be on LE, because it is
the required run-time for the COBOL products with these features.

5) Besides the general migration information in BOTH the COBOL and LE
Migration Guides, there are some 3rd party publication that deal with some
of the issues.  It is *not* trivial upgrading to LE as a run-time.  For
sites that are already using VS COBOL II as their compiler, moving to COBOL
for OS/390 & VM (*AFTER* you have LE stabilized) is pretty easy.  Moving
there from OS/VS COBOL is a much more difficult (but also has some products
like CCCA to help with it).
```

```
was not enough demand, that is what we have to live with.

I agree that there sometimes are rather crowded with Micro Focus and
mainframe cobol discussions, on the other side, the message rate can be
pretty low sometimes, at that times, it is good that the cobol community is
united.

There is a great deal of people out there who believe Cobol should be dead,
we are stronger together.

Besides, although I am an Acucobol user, it is often interesting and
informative to read about other Cobols as well.

Do also remember that the frequency of Acucobol stuff here is up to
ourselves, we can't expect those in mainframe and Micro Focus world to
discuss Acucobol related stuff, that debate is something we that uses
Acucobol has to start.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: 173 CHECKFIL not found - NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BD3167.3DECF139@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7N2N7LQWOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

Hi Juergen,

Juergen Vetter wrote:
> 
> I can't fix this error
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

[snip]

> some more informations:
> -----------------------
> I can't find a file named opdynchk.obj on my system

It's in the RTS .LIB file.

> The DLL cblrtssi dosn't have an entry checkfil

There shouldn't be any such thing as CBLRTSSI.DLL - the
files should be CBLRTSS.DLL and CBLRTSSI.LIB

> So the runtime is right! it can't find checkfil.

There is a dynamically-loadable version of CHECKFIL in
%COBDIR%\UTILS.LBR
Therefore, if you make sure UTILS.LBR gets loaded at
startup time of your COBOL DLL, it should work.
eg:

cobol maindllentry.cbl INITCALL("UTILS.LBR");
cbllink -D maindllentry.int otherprogram.cbl otherprogram2.cbl
 
> If i link externl.obj i get the error
> 119 symbolic name is more then one time used.

Hmmm. I don't think this should happen (my initial thought was
that linking in EXTERNL.OBJ would fix the problem). Can you say
what your link command line was when this happened (I assume
you used CBLLINK to make the DLL) ?

> I think i have to get the file opdynchk.obj.

No, you already have this file (in the LIB).

Cheers, Kev.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 173 CHECKFIL not found - NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)

## Thread: Problem using ODBC connect to AcuCobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Problem using ODBC connect to AcuCobol

- **From:** David Au <macgyver_spider@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37B90863.FC2D3F8F@prudential.com.hk>`

```
I am trying the acucobol 4.2 in which acuserver is installed in aix
machine while the client in win95 connect using acuodbc. I find no place
to configurate the odbc driver to point to aix. Can anybody help?
Many Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem using ODBC connect to AcuCobol

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o7ErtFALZqu3IwKH@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37B90863.FC2D3F8F@prudential.com.hk>`

```
In article <37B90863.FC2D3F8F@prudential.com.hk>, David Au
<macgyver_spider@hotmail.com> writes
>I am trying the acucobol 4.2 in which acuserver is installed in aix
>machine while the client in win95 connect using acuodbc. I find no place
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

David,

You just need to configure the AcuODBC data source to point to
the AcuServer path to your files, so if your server if called "foo" and
the data files are in "/usr/data/files" you'd configure it to:

@foo:/usr/data/files

If you keep the XFD files on the server then you'll need to tell
AcuODBC which files are available, from the manual:

---------------
Using a remote file server, you can locate data dictionary files on
the remote server, but this is not necessary.  This is a feature you
can use at your convenience.

When you create a DSN and then decide to locate XFD files on the
remote server, you must complete the "Data Dictionary Directory"
entry field with the name of the remote directory containing the
XFD files.  When you type the name of the remote directory, keep
in mind that on UNIX, pathnames are case sensitive.

A sequential ASCII file called "xfdrmt.dir" (lowercase) must be
stored in the remote directory.  This file has to contain as many
lines as the number of XFD files in that directory.  Each line must
be filled with the name of each data dictionary file.  

ï¿½1998 Acucorp, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
---------------

Hope this helps, if not please do contact support in your local
office, they'll be able to get you going.

Best regards

Nigel
```

<a id="t-ed8e82ba80"></a>
## Thread: Printing error using MF COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Printing error using MF COBOL

- **From:** "Steven Jones" <Steven11@tesco.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pa7q9$h0p$1@barcode.tesco.net>`

```
I can not print direct from a  MFCOBOL compiler.  I am using Windows98 and
can print from my File menu

Thanks
Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing error using MF COBOL

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pb2t3$q2f$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7pa7q9$h0p$1@barcode.tesco.net>`

```

Steven Jones <Steven11@tesco.net> wrote in message
news:7pa7q9$h0p$1@barcode.tesco.net...
> I can not print direct from a  MFCOBOL compiler.  I am using Windows98 and
> can print from my File menu
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
    You did not mention which compiler, but try the +P0+L0 runtime switchs.

+P0 makes it use dos instead of bios (you lose the ability to detect printer
status), and the +L0 forces it to return a runtime error instead of abort
retry ignore.  sometimes, a printer error while using +P0 without +L0 will
produce the DOS abort retry ... message.  Abort aborts without closing
files.

    Also - an odd thing - try accessing the printer, from windows or command
line before trying from cobol.  I have had machines that need to be "jump
started" with an echo. or something before allowing normal access.  Mine
even accessed the floppys for some reason.
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice Programmer needs help doing something simple, MERGE

- **From:** Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pf75h$ijd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
In article <7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com>,
  "Ray" <rferruol@csc.com> wrote:
> I have 2 files coming into the program that need to be merged, there
is a
> common key field but the rest of the data does not match,
> 1st file 69 characters, 2nd file 33 characters, the only field that
matches
> up is the key.  Can you send some examples of what my code should look
like
> or offer suggestions, I know this is a simple one, but my college
books just
> graze over it.
> Thanks in advance
>
> rferruol@csc.com

You have your basic 2 file match here.  I'm going to call your 69
character file FILE-A and the 33 character file FILE-B.  Basic code is:

    PERFORM READ-FILE-A-PARA
    PERFORM READ-FILE-B-PARA
    PERFORM MATCH-PARA UNTIL END-OF-FILE-A AND END-OF-FILE-B

MATCH-PARA.
    IF KEY-A = KEY-B
        PERFORM CREATE-OUTPUT-PARA
    ELSE
    IF KEY-A > KEY-B
        what you want to do in this case
        PERFORM READ-FILE-B-PARA
    ELSE
    IF KEY-A < KEY-B
        what you want to do in this case
        PERFORM READ-FILE-A-PARA
    END-IF
    END-IF
    END-IF
    .

READ-FILE-A-PARA.
    READ FILE-A AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO KEY-A.

READ-FILE-B-PARA.
    READ FILE-B AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO KEY-B.

CREATE-OUTPUT-PARA.
     what you want to do here
     WRITE OUTPUT-REC

If you are putting the 2 different records on the same output file then
your output file will be variable length and in your JCL the LRECL would
be 73 (that's 69 the larger record + 4 for the variable stuff).  Be sure
that your FD specifies variable as well.

Once you've coded this up save it in your forever file, you will
definately be doing this again and why reinvent the wheel?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7pf058$28a$1@lore.csc.com>`
- **References:** `<7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
graze over it.

rferruol@csc.com
```

```
Ray wrote:

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1st file 69 characters, 2nd file 33 characters,

Ask yourself why the different length make a difference. Just match the
keys. Can you get duplicate keys? Does the output sequence matter when
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Novice Programmer needs help doing something simple, MERGE

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C36C2F.8378C4F1@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com> <7pf058$28a$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Ray wrote:
> I hope this one gets through....
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> rferruol@csc.com

A Generalized Merge Algorithm in pseudo code:

Initialize and Open files
Read next filea record
If EOF-on-filea Then{set filea_key to high_values}
Read next fileb record
If EOF-on-fileb Then {set fileb_key to high_values}
While ~EOF-on-fileb | ~EOF-on-fileb
  {Case
    {(filea_key < fileb_key):
         {Process unmatched filea record and get next filea record
          }
     (filea_key = fileb_key):
         {If filea_key = high_values Then Leave loop
          Else {Merge filea record and fileb record and get next
                record for both filea and fileb
                }
         }
     (filea_key > fileb_key):
         {Process unmatched fileb record and get next fileb record
         }
    }
  }
Close files and other termination actions 

As long as high-values is not an acceptable key value in the external
files, by forcing the fields used for the key comparisons to high-values
on an EOF, the loop logic doesn't need to handle EOF as a special case. 
Translating this into acceptable COBOL, which requires resolving the
details of the merge process and how to handle unmatched records,
depends on the details of your specific application and is left as an
exercise to the reader.
```

- **References:** `<7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com>`

```

>I have 2 files coming into the program that need to be merged, there is a
>common key field but the rest of the data does not match,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice Programmer needs help doing something simple, MERGE

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oGEu3.212$H23.3007@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Ray wrote:
>I have 2 files coming into the program that need to be merged, there is a
>common key field but the rest of the data does not match,

PROCESS.
    OPEN INPUT  FILE-1
                FILE-2
         OUTPUT FILE-3.
    PERFORM READ-FILE-1.
    PERFORM READ-FILE-2.
    PERFORM MERGE-FILES
        UNTIL (HIGH-VALUES = FILE-1-KEY AND FILE-2-KEY).
    CLOSE FILE-1 FILE-2 FILE-3.
    STOP RUN.

READ-FILE-1.
    READ FILE-1
       AT END
           MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO FILE-1-KEY.

READ-FILE-2.
    READ FILE-2
       AT END
           MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO FILE-2-KEY.

MERGE-FILES.
    IF (FILE-1-KEY NOT > FILE-2-KEY)
        <write FILE-1 to FILE-3>
        PERFORM READ-FILE-1
    else
        <write FILE-2 to FILE-3>
        PERFORM READ-FILE-2.
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice Programmer needs help doing something simple, MERGE

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37BC097C.5843D62D@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7peqp8$vst$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Ray wrote:
> 
> I have 2 files coming into the program that need to be merged, there
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> but my college books just graze over it.

Look up balanced line algorithm.

There was a link posted about six months ago that described this
method.

Ken
```

```
presume that it may have some clause which would include the word
```

## Thread: Missing Output — 6 msgs

### Missing Output

- **From:** Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pf1bb$e0n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have gone thru my entire list of 'dumb things programmers do to mess
up their programs' and cannot find the cause of this bug.  I have had
several people look at this and they can't find anything either.  What
makes this worse is this program worked just last week (I know, I know,
you've heard that before :)  ) Ok - here goes:

This is a COBOL/MVS program (we're running LE etc etc).  At one time is
was VS/Cobol then COBOL II, and now whatever version we're currently
running (compiler).  This program prints invoices.  The invoice is
output to 2 output files.  It consists of 17 header lines, 6 detail
lines per line item, a continuation line if more than one page, and a
total line on the last page.  All lines are defined in Working-Storage
as 01 levels.

What is happening is I'm getting no header lines, no continuation line,
and no total line.  I only get the 6 detail lines / line item.  Not
blank lines - NO lines on either file.

I have put displays into the program and yes there is data in the W/S
definitions and in the FD right after the WRITE statement was executed.

I have changed the program to do an explicit move of the W/S to the FD
and then perform a single WRITE statement (WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER 1) for
both the headers and the detail - I still get only the detail lines.

I can't see anything in the definition of the header lines or the ending
lines that would cause this but I'm leaning in that direction but don't
have a clue what to look for.  I will ask that if the name of the line
could have anything to do with the problem.  Headers are named HEADER-1
thru HEADER-17, the details are named DETAIL-1 thru DETAIL-6, and the
ending line used (depending on last page or not), are REFERENCE-LINE,
TOTAL-LINE, SHIPPER-LINE.

Anyone got any ideas?

Thanks all
Eileen


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<7pf1bb$e0n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Have you actually PRINTED the output - or are you looking at it "online"
(via SDSF, IOF, etc)?  Occasionally, there are issues with FBA or FBM items
viewed "online" not showing "lines created with carriage control
characters".
```

```
WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HEADER-1 AFTER PAGE for the paper file

Eileen (posting from home under her 'other' persona)
```

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL and SQL — 9 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Fujitsu COBOL and SQL

- **From:** "DB" <dantar@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yZFu3.326$iM5.2562@news4.atl>`

```
I just bought Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 and have discovered that it will not allow
access to fields in SQL Server 6.5 that are numeric 19,5, which would be
S9(14)v9(5) in COBOL.  Anybody have any workaround ideas? We use an
accounting system that has every dollar amount field formatted that way, and
I need access to them.  Thanks for any ideas you may have.



Dan  - dantar@bellsouth.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL and SQL

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37bb5077@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<yZFu3.326$iM5.2562@news4.atl>`

```

DB <dantar@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:yZFu3.326$iM5.2562@news4.atl...
> I just bought Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 and have discovered that it will not allow
> access to fields in SQL Server 6.5 that are numeric 19,5, which would be
> S9(14)v9(5) in COBOL.  Anybody have any workaround ideas? We use an
> accounting system that has every dollar amount field formatted that way,
and
> I need access to them.  Thanks for any ideas you may have.

In standard Cobol you cannot have more than 18 digits, so S9(14)V9(5)
with its 19 digits won't work. Do you really need dollar amounts in the
100 trillion range?
```

```
it could be an accrued interest field , although I would have expected 
more to the right of the decimal and less to the left.
```
