## Thread: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records — 34 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### RE: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records

- **From:** Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```

> Why on earth would one have line numbers at all?  :-)
> 
> I have never used it, I feel it is interfering. The editor should do the
> counting.
> 
	There should not be any counting at all.
	Line numbers are certainly interfering.

	Those who argument that they need to synchonize with a compiler
listing are wrong too : There should be no compiler listings. Those were
used in the good ol' days, when browsing a source from the faded-out
printing on the top of the punched cards was (relatively) hard.

	Line numbers are as obsolete as the program's red tape (author,
date-written, date-compiled, remarks, security)
	not even to mention the slash in column 7 or the EJECT n directives.

	Line numbers are for archaic BASIC programs. GOTO 10, GOSUB 20.

	And while I'm at it: incrementing data item level numbers by 2 or
even 5 is archaic nonsense as well.


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C29F66.72613202@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
Peter van Zeeland wrote:

>         Those who argument that they need to synchonize with a compiler
> listing are wrong too : There should be no compiler listings. Those were
> used in the good ol' days, when browsing a source from the faded-out
> printing on the top of the punched cards was (relatively) hard.

My compiler produces listings.  It has error numbers on the bottom of
the output which referer to the compiler's line number.  I do a find on
that compiler line number and see my error.  If my source has a line
number, then I split my screen and find that source line number on my
source and I am at the line where the error was found.  If I didn't have
a compiler listing then I would have no idea where the error was. 
"Error at line 455" is meaningless if I can't find line 455.
 
>         And while I'm at it: incrementing data item level numbers by 2 or
> even 5 is archaic nonsense as well.

But it costs nothing.
```

#### ↳ BASIC Misconceptions (was: Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```

Peter van Zeeland wrote in message
<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01>...
[...]
> Line numbers are for archaic BASIC programs. GOTO 10, GOSUB 20.

The BASIC programming language never used line numbers
(or sequence numbers). The 10 and 20 in your example are
labels, the same as in FORTRAN.

Some implementors of BASIC interpreters, most notably
Microsoft, perverted the language by adding a requirement
that each line have a sequence number that also served as
a label. This did not follow the intent, or the implementations,
of the language designers, John G. Kemeny and Thomas E.
Kurtz.

[Microsoft corrected their perverse implementation of the
BASIC language in their compiled QuickBASIC and
interpreted QBASIC products.]

Kemeny and Kurtz founded a company to produce and
distribute True Basic, a product which did follow the intent
of the language designers! See < www.truebasic.com >.
Their early (1983) product only required numbers when used
as labels. Their current product is a multi-platform, compiled,
structured (modular) BASIC.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions (was: Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records)

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
On Tue, 24 Aug 1999 10:14:24 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

>
>Peter van Zeeland wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>labels, the same as in FORTRAN.

Sorry to contradict:  In 1971 I was using BASIC at the Dartmouth collegecomputer, and they
sure used line numbers.  And no stetment without a sequence number was accepted.  So did
the BASIC that came with the early microcomputers at the end of the 70ies.  (I still have
one of them in my basement)>


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions (was: Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net>`

```

Volker Bandke wrote in message <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net>...
>On Tue, 24 Aug 1999 10:14:24 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
wrote:
>
>>Peter van Zeeland wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sorry to contradict:  In 1971 I was using BASIC at the Dartmouth
collegecomputer, and they
>sure used line numbers.  And no stetment without a sequence number was
accepted.  So did
>the BASIC that came with the early microcomputers at the end of the 70ies.
(I still have
>one of them in my basement)>

This is not necesarily a contradiction. It is a difference in
the understanding of what constitutes BASIC at different
times. I am saying that BASIC never used line numbers;
but BASIC interpreters did. I am also saying that the use
of line numbers was not a requirement of the language;
but was a requirement of the interpreter. I am also saying
that by requiring line numbers, the interpreters were not
*really* implementing the BASIC programming language
as conceived by its designers.

This is similar to disagreeing as to whether implementor
extensions to COBOL are *really* COBOL. The difference
is that the COBOL standard does allow such extensions;
but there was no BASIC standard during the time in
question. The absence of a standard allowed anyone to
create anything as a language and call it BASIC.

I understood that some, if not most, BASIC interpreters,
through the 70s and early-80s, did use line numbers; but
the original BASIC from 1964 was compiled. It is my
understanding the compiled version did not use line
numbers.

I came by my understanding from a reference with a quote
from either Kemeny or Kurtz that, as I recall, specifically
pointed to line numbers as something horrible that was
done to the language.

I was unable to find that reference in a quick search.

I did find the following quote from
< http://www.digitalcentury.com/encyclo/update/BASIC.html >.

"Kemeny and Kurtz had gone on to other things and were not
actively involved in the maintenance of BASIC. In 1983, they
used a PC for the first time and were surprised at "�how
terrible were the crude 'street' versions [of BASIC] that high
school and college students all over the country had to use."
Appalled at what had happened to their language, Kurtz
became chairman of the ANSI Standard BASIC committee,
and in 1984, he and Kemeny introduced their own version,
called True BASIC."

Although this quote does not specifically point to line
numbers, it does give a sense that the originators of
BASIC were very unhappy with versions that did have
line numbers. Again, I recall seeing a comment against
line numbers.

So the problem is, "At the various times in question, what
consituted the definition of BASIC?" There may not be
a satisfactory answer, prior to 1987, since the language
was not protected. As of 1987, the language was defined
by an ANSI standard and later by an ISO standard.

Finally, the language you knew, at Dartmouth, as BASIC
may have been BASIC Sixth or some other such number.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions (was: Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q078i$1kir$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net> <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net> <RpHw3.850$dT2.31789@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>Rick Smith wrote in message <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net>...
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Talk about doubletalk!

What I am talking about is a very subtle difference between
implementations. If you like, replace the references
to "BASIC interpreters" with "Dartmouth BASIC".

In other words, BASIC never used line numbers; but
Dartmouth BASIC did.

Then, for comparison with Microsoft products, QuickBASIC
never used line numbers; but GW-BASIC did.

Now think about how the absence of line numbers, in one,
and the requirement for line numbers, in the other, make
these languages different.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pvfub$t4o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net> <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

: This is not necesarily a contradiction. It is a difference in
: the understanding of what constitutes BASIC at different
: times. I am saying that BASIC never used line numbers;
: but BASIC interpreters did. I am also saying that the use
: of line numbers was not a requirement of the language;
: but was a requirement of the interpreter. I am also saying
: that by requiring line numbers, the interpreters were not
: *really* implementing the BASIC programming language
: as conceived by its designers.

One does not get that impression from reading Kemeny and
Kurtz's 'BASIC Programming':

  "Only the discerning user will be able to detect the
difference between a BASIC system equipped with a compiler
and one equipped with an interpreter, but which were otherwise
identical."

: I understood that some, if not most, BASIC interpreters,
: through the 70s and early-80s, did use line numbers; but
: the original BASIC from 1964 was compiled. It is my
: understanding the compiled version did not use line
: numbers.

Perhaps that understanding is wrong ?

: I came by my understanding from a reference with a quote
: from either Kemeny or Kurtz that, as I recall, specifically
: pointed to line numbers as something horrible that was
: done to the language.

The use of line numbers is certainly horrible.  Mistyping
one leads to two bugs: the original line is not overwritten
and the typed one is overwritten in error and replaced
by the new line.  This does not mean that someone else
"done it to the language" as K&K seem quite happy with
this in their book.

: "Kemeny and Kurtz had gone on to other things and were not
: actively involved in the maintenance of BASIC. In 1983, they
: used a PC for the first time and were surprised at 
: terrible were the crude 'street' versions [of BASIC] that high
: school and college students all over the country had to use."
: Appalled at what had happened to their language, Kurtz
: became chairman of the ANSI Standard BASIC committee,
: and in 1984, he and Kemeny introduced their own version,
: called True BASIC."

That doesn't imply that line numbers were an addition, in
fact K&K stated that BASIC programs consisted of lines that
had a line number.

: So the problem is, "At the various times in question, what
: consituted the definition of BASIC?" There may not be
: a satisfactory answer, prior to 1987, since the language
: was not protected. As of 1987, the language was defined
: by an ANSI standard and later by an ISO standard.

Actually ANSI Basic standard was X3.60-1978 from that
date.

: Finally, the language you knew, at Dartmouth, as BASIC
: may have been BASIC Sixth or some other such number.

Dartmouth BASIC was the subject of K&K's book and was
BASIC.

There was aversion of FORTRAN called Basic (in the fundemental
sense - not related to BASIC) FORTRAN which was also an
interactive system using line numbers.  Perhaps that is where
your confusion about line numbers is coming from.
```

>P.S. to that last ... I have hated Basic with a passion ever since, strongly
>re-enforced by having to debug someone else's GL system written entirely in
>Basic floating point, so I am rather prejudiced against the idea that
>anything nice can be said about any Basic.  It's name says it all.

I agree that BASIC is not a good language for writing large programs,
but the newer versions without line numbers can be superb for small
utility programs, algorithm prototypes, and such.  Using QuickBasic,
I can usually write and execute a small program faster than in any
other language I am familiar with.  Because BASIC is 'basic', you can
learn to use it in only a few hours.

Visual Basic is a peculiar beast.  People use VB because of it's many
powerful GUI and RAD capabilities.  The language is almost incidental.

of sines for an assembler program.  I also use it on occasion to play with
ideas/graphics/ or instead of a programmable calculator. I just would not
write anything over a few pages with it, nor would I write anything for a
customer in it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL Strings 2 (was: Re: BASIC Misconceptions)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q3ahp$228d$2@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net> <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net> <7pvfub$t4o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7pvjda$nem$3@news.igs.net> <7q0dt1$1s6g$1@news.hitter.net> <37C40B98.ED7CB5C3@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37C40B98.ED7CB5C3@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>nicely.

Actually, SNOBOL4 is a character string processing language
and is probably more flexible than BASIC. Operations on strings
are defined by the language. The internal representation of strings
in SNOBOL4 is similar to Pascal.

LISP and Prolog representation is linked list; fast for some
operations, slow for others. High-level operations on these
lists are defined by the programmer, not the language.

Please, correct me if I am wrong!
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL Strings (was: Re: BASIC Misconceptions)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q3ahp$228d$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net> <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net> <7pvfub$t4o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7pvjda$nem$3@news.igs.net> <7q0dt1$1s6g$1@news.hitter.net> <7q35f0$pec$2@news.igs.net>`

```
For short strings, where the maximum length is known,
C is adequate; but then so is COBOL.

For longer strings, in C, finding the length requires,
typically, one instruction per character. Also using dynamic
memory to work with long strings is independent of the
string handling functions. This means that manipulation
of C type strings can be slower than manipulation of
BASIC type strings.

For BASIC type strings, the length is part of a control
block, so the length can be accessed quickly. Also it
should be understood that the implementation of BASIC
type strings includes the necessary dynamic memory
operations.

Some of the elementary operations for manipulating
BASIC type strings are present in the draft standard and,
that which isn't, could be implemented as user-defined
functions. I just think it would be much more useful if it
were built in to the language.
------------------
Rick Smith

donald tees wrote in message <7q35f0$pec$2@news.igs.net>...
>
>I suppose you are right on that, though I would think C better.

>Rick Smith wrote in message <7q0dt1$1s6g$1@news.hitter.net>...
[...]
>>I must say that I, too, had a similar opinion of BASIC; but
>>here is something nice about BASIC, Strings. I have yet
>>to find any language with more flexible operations on
>>strings of characters. It is perhaps the only feature of
>>BASIC that I might consider for adapting to COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions (was: Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C3EF57.AE3DE95B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net> <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> This is not necesarily a contradiction. It is a difference in
> the understanding of what constitutes BASIC at different
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> as conceived by its designers.

To me, BASIC includes the interpreter and its requirements.  There may
have been an ANSI Standard Basic (was there?), but that is one
particular subset of Basic.  If one refers to Basic, one either refers
to an implied subset of one's workplace or to the whole basic world -
unless that reference is modified down.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions (was: Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q0o4l$28bt$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <37c3e540.1690410@news2.ibm.net> <7pupa1$3027$1@news.hitter.net> <37C3EF57.AE3DE95B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37C3EF57.AE3DE95B@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>unless that reference is modified down.

BASIC Standards --------
ANSI Standard for Minimal BASIC (X3.60-1978)

ANSI Standard for Full BASIC (X3.113-1987)

ISO Standard for Minimal BASIC (ISO 6373:1984
Data processing - Programming languages -
Minimal BASIC)

ISO Standard for Full BASIC (ISO/IEC 10279:1991
Information technology - Programming languages -
Full BASIC)
----------

IBM Personal Computer BASIC 1.1 by Microsoft, 1982,
makes no reference to X3.60-1978.

Microsoft QuickBASIC 4.5, 1988, makes no reference
to X3.113-1987 or earlier standards.

After dwelling on this for far too long, I have resolved
that there is only one BASIC, that which was concieved
and implemented by Kemeny and Kurtz in 1964.
Everything else is a this-or-that BASIC.

The point, to which I responded, was that archaic
BASIC programs used line numbers. Clearly, there
is no BASIC more archaic than the original. If the
original did not require line numbers, my response was
correct, on a technicality. If the original did require line
numbers, then I was wrong (because I believed what
I read and repeated it).

I do agree that we can, and I do, use the term BASIC
in a general sense to refer to things of or related to
any BASIC.

In any case, I think I have used too much of this groups
time on this subject.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pv2tb$ote$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

: The BASIC programming language never used line numbers
: (or sequence numbers). The 10 and 20 in your example are
: labels, the same as in FORTRAN.

: Some implementors of BASIC interpreters, most notably
: Microsoft, perverted the language by adding a requirement
: that each line have a sequence number that also served as
: a label. This did not follow the intent, or the implementations,
: of the language designers, John G. Kemeny and Thomas E.
: Kurtz.

This is untrue.  I have Kemeny and Kurtz's 1967 book here. 
All the programs have line numbers (which are also used as
labels) because the Dartmouth Basic system was an interactive
language.  The way that the program was entered and edited
was by using the line number.  To quote from the book:

   "In summary, we observe that the BASIC program is made up
of statements, each of which consists of a line number followed
by an English word that gives ...."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pv7i2$ffp$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <7pv2tb$ote$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
The following is an excerpt from the chapter, "The Spirit of Basic"
from the book, "Hardcore VB". This chapter, in part, discusses
Kemeny and Kurtz book, "Back to BASIC: ...". The chapter is
on the CD, "Mastering Microsoft Visual Basic 4.0".

"Language Purification"

"Basic is an old language developed in 1964 and it still carries
a lot of baggage from the past. During its 30 years, Basic picked
up a lot of bad habits, which it has only recently begun to shed.
Over the years, Microsoft has played a large role in both the good
and the bad developments in Basic."

"If you�d like to know the history of the language you�re programming
in, try to find a copy of the book Back to BASIC: The History,
Corruption, and Future of the Language (Addison-Wesley, 1985),
by Basic inventors Kemeny and Kurtz. The book is largely a diatribe
against unnamed �Street Basics� that poisoned the language with
line numbers, unnecessary data types, and Gotos. The book mentions
no names, but anyone who programmed in the 1980s knows that
Microsoft�s GW-BASIC and IBM�s version of the same program,
called BASICA, are the worst of the Street Basics being attacked.
As a self-taught programmer who wrote his first programs with
GW-BASIC, I can confirm that these were indeed wretched
languages, richly deserving all the opprobrium piled on them by
defenders of the true faith."

I do not have a copy of "Back to BASIC" and it is out-of-print so
I can not verify whether the reference to "poisoned the language
with line numbers" is wholly accurate. If it is accurate, then Kemeny
and Kurtz never intended for BASIC to have line numbers; even if
they, or someone else, put them in for DTSS. If it is inaccurate,
then I am wrong.

Also, the above excerpt is not the reference for which I was
looking.
------------------
Rick Smith

Richard Plinston wrote in message <7pv2tb$ote$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>by an English word that gives ...."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C31F4F.604B@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <7pv2tb$ote$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> by an English word that gives ...."

Absolutely.  Back in the late 60s, when many BASIC systems were running via timeshare on 
110 baud teleprinters (i.e. 10 characters a second) the line number was NEEDED to edit a 
line. You just retyped the changed line including its number.

Also, as you note every line also had to begin with a verb.  Even calculation lines had 
to be of the form:

110 LET X=Y+1
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BASIC Misconceptions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C3EDD7.CCD0FE60@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845033E@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7ptrls$1t75$1@news.hitter.net> <7pv2tb$ote$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37C31F4F.604B@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
There were some interesting programming techniques in the old days.  We
have discussed some of them for COBOL at various times.

A technique which I have found with interpreted Basic was code in which
the first line was a go to, followed by the most frequently accessed sub
routine(s), and the body of the program at the end.  This was designed
to save time while searching for a label.

I have played with forth and with an Atari language called Action! (?)
which were based upon defining all the subroutines/definitions at the
front, then having a tiny program at the end.  This is sort of the
opposite of what we generally see in structured Cobol.  But not really -
the thought process is still structured and kind of fun.
```

On Tue, 24 Aug 1999 07:12:08 +0200, Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl> wrote:

>
>> Why on earth would one have line numbers at all?  :-)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>	Line numbers are certainly interfering.

I am not quite sure I understand what you want to say.  If the listing is not used (as the
rest of your post implies), then the line number cannot interfer.

If the listing IS used, though, then they are very helpful in associating the source line
with the compile listing line

and if you feel they clutter up your screen while editing .... why not hide them, by
scrolling 6 bytes to the side?


>
>	Those who argument that they need to synchonize with a compiler
>listing are wrong too : There should be no compiler listings.

Question:  Did you ever work in a productive invironment, with n-thousand batch and online
programs present.  ANd then, on Friday 13th, a certain program just dies on you.

Of course, you have the source file, but - which comple options have been used, which
linkage editor options, where is the data division crodd reference?  How to find offset -
verb relationship without a compile listing?  Just compile it again?  In the production
environment?  The manager says: Try that, and it's the highway...  
> Those were
>used in the good ol' days, when browsing a source from the faded-out
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>date-written, date-compiled, remarks, security)

>	not even to mention the slash in column 7 or the EJECT n directives.
Here I do agree, as compile listings don't have to be on paper, print beautifying options
are not necessary, but they don't hurt, either
>
>	Line numbers are for archaic BASIC programs. GOTO 10, GOSUB 20.
>
>	And while I'm at it: incrementing data item level numbers by 2 or
>even 5 is archaic nonsense as well.
This are strong statements, , set up to sound like rules cast in stone, but actually they
are only opinions (to which you are entitled of course, but don't belittle mine, please)

With kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)

## Thread: Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ?

- **From:** "Michael ValeCruz" <mvalecruz1@mcyber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
If the routine returns a pointer to the allocated memory block then how can
the block be relocatable?
Would the pointer be void at the point the memory is relocated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ptv9c$219n$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Michael ValeCruz wrote in message <7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>...
>If the routine returns a pointer to the allocated memory block then how can
>the block be relocatable?

The block is not relocatable! It will remain at the same
location until CBL_FREE_MEM is called using the same
pointer (value) that was returned by CBL_ALLOC_MEM.
After CBL_FREE_MEM, the block is invalid.

>Would the pointer be void at the point the memory is relocated.

The value of the pointer will be set by CBL_ALLOC_MEM.
The only (reasonable) means for changing the value of the
pointer are: SET pointer-name TO NULL, after freeing the
block, and CBL_ALLOC_MEM, for another block after
freeing the original block.

I wonder if you are confusing the phrase "dynamically
allocated memory" with "relocatable memory".

Since you mention void pointers; in C language terms,
'realloc()' may relocate a memory block; but it does not
apply to this case. The subprograms CBL_ALLOC_MEM
and CBL_FREE_MEM behave the same as the C functions
'malloc()' and 'free()'.

In (draft) COBOL terms, similar behavior is described in
the new statements, ALLOCATE and FREE; though
ALLOCATE has more functionality than CBL_ALLOC_MEM
or 'malloc()'.

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q2kel$iun$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ptv9c$219n$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
    Sorry to disagree, but the documentation for MF v3.1 for DOS says that
the memory returned by the CBL_ALLOC_MEM is by default relocatable and
nonshared. (except on Unix, OS/2, and windows, where it seems to be always
the same (ie fixed or relocatable).

    Bit 0 of "FLAGS" controls shareable, bit 1 controls relocatable.

    My guess is that there is another layer of software we have no access to
under the CBL_ALLOC_MEM, CBL_FREE_MEM, and set commands that handles
relocation.  Note that the SET sometimes (always?) has to be redone upon
returning to the program from a call, and always when returning after an
exit program  (runtime system, INT/GNT programs).

    The working storage section is also relocatable, see X"91" functions 60
& 61.


Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
news:7ptv9c$219n$1@news.hitter.net...
>
> Michael ValeCruz wrote in message
<7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>...
> >If the routine returns a pointer to the allocated memory block then how
can
> >the block be relocatable?
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q31pa$1ogl$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ptv9c$219n$1@news.hitter.net> <7q2kel$iun$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
No need to be sorry about your disagreement. I was wrong!

MF WB 3.2 allows for relocatable with either shared or
nonshared. It also allows fixed and nonshared for DOS.
This is what I have used in the past so the relocatable
option wasn't foremost in my mind.

I think the best answer for Michael is, don't worry about it
UNLESS you are using dynamic allocation in a way that
will cause severe fragmentation. If only a few small blocks
are allocated and they are all freed or freed in the reverse
order of their allocation, memory will not become severly
fragmented.

I ran some tests using various block sizes to see if I could
better understand the underlying operations. There
appears to be separate relocatable and fixed areas. Using
small blocks, all memory seems to be allocated from
the relocatable area. With larger blocks, it appears to
use the fixed area even when flagged as relocatable.

Finally, I set up a table of 256 pointers and ran the program
several times using block sizes of from 16 to 32768 bytes.
With the smaller blocks, all memory was allocated from
the relocatable area. As I increased the block size through
256 bytes, some memory was allocated from relocatable
and some from fixed. Around 4096 bytes, all memory was
from the fixed area. Even with 256 blocks of 32768 bytes,
or 8 megabytes, I could find no problem allocating or using
the blocks.

The next test would be to force the fragmenting of memory.
I chose not to do that test.

Russell Styles wrote in message
<7q2kel$iun$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...
>    Sorry to disagree, but the documentation for MF v3.1 for DOS says that
>the memory returned by the CBL_ALLOC_MEM is by default relocatable and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    My guess is that there is another layer of software we have no access
to
>under the CBL_ALLOC_MEM, CBL_FREE_MEM, and set commands that handles
>relocation.  Note that the SET sometimes (always?) has to be redone upon
>returning to the program from a call, and always when returning after an
>exit program  (runtime system, INT/GNT programs).

The MF 3.2 System Reference says that nonshared memory is
owned by the program that created it and is released when
the program is cancelled.

From what you are saying, I would call it a "bug" if control of
memory is lost in the calling program. I would call it a "bug" if
control of memory was lost in a called program that was not
cancelled.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

```
    I think that one of the major reasons to be concerned about relocatable
vs fixed would be when calling external code that can not handle relocatable
memory.  I suspect that this consideration is moot with most 32 bit
environments such as windows 95/98/NT.

    The XM memory manager for DOS even went so far as to allocate a fixed
block of memory in the lower 640K for use with DOS and BIOS interrupts, must
of which were  fairly dumb in this respect.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Queestion on MF Cobol 3.2 - CBL_ALLOC_MEM ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q5k65$1hpu$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7pu984$h0q$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ptv9c$219n$1@news.hitter.net> <7q2kel$iun$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7q31pa$1ogl$1@news.hitter.net> <7q59pe$okf$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```

Russell Styles wrote in message
<7q59pe$okf$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...
>    I think that one of the major reasons to be concerned about relocatable
>vs fixed would be when calling external code that can not handle
relocatable
>memory.  I suspect that this consideration is moot with most 32 bit
>environments such as windows 95/98/NT.

I think that the only time the MF COBOL runtime would
relocate a block of memory is during CBL_ALLOC_MEM.
It would do this as part of a memory compaction process
to create a larger block for the new allocation. During
the compaction process, it would move through chains
of pointers that contain the locations of pointers that were
set to the memory address of the blocks being moved.

As long as external code, such as a C module, does
not save the address of the relocatable block, for later
reuse, there is no problem. If each CALL passes the
address of the block, the external code will never know
the block was moved since the last CALL.

There is a potential problem with passing relocatable
blocks to other MF COBOL programs that CALL
CBL_ALLOC_MEM. Prog-1 allocates block-1 and
calls prog-2 using block-1, then prog-2 allocates
block-2. If block-1 is moved when block-2 is allocated,
the contents of block-1 could become inaccessable
in prog-2!

One other potential problem is with relocatable blocks
that contain pointers to other relocatable blocks.
If block-1 contains a pointer to block-2, then if block-1
is moved, memory management would need to adjust
its chain of pointers to pointers to block-2 to reflect
that the location of the pointer in block-1 was changed!

Fortunately, designers tend to take such potential
problems into account.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl> wrote in message
news:6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69845042B@NL-AMB-MAIL01...
 <snip>
>
> > >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Peter,
  Although I think your environment isn't everyone's environment, I can
understand why you don't like (and/or don't use) sequence numbers.  HOWEVER,
I don't understand your point on incrementing level numbers.  Are you saying
that you SHOULD use "1, 5, 10, 15" instead of "1, 3, 5, 7" - or that COBOL
should (somehow?) figure out relationships in a group without any level
numbers or something else?  I (fully) agree that ANY "incrementing" value is
purely aribtrary - and totally depends on how much "maintenance" you expect
(requiring you to "insert" intermediate levels) - or whether you like
renumbering levels each time you do maintenance.  However, I don't see any
way to support COBOL's view of levels and redefinitions without SOME form of
level numbers.

(P.S.  Related to another part of this thread, the "INSERT" "DELETE" and
"REPLACE" *verbs* are indeed COBOL "verbs" with IBM mainframe compilers.  I
assume from your note, that you were mistaking them for something else - but
they are very definitely a current part of the COBOL language - in one of
its "dialects".)
```

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> no, no, and no again: the proper sequence is 1, 2, 3, 4,...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and then you can as well change the level numbers.

The reason to increment 1,5,10 is to allow you to insert groups into
the definition later without a major resequence.

for example a fist attempt:

01  fred-struct.
  02  dd-fred pic...
  02  mm-fred...
  02  yyyy-fred....
  02  a-string-in-fred
  02 something-else-in-fred.

in order to insert a group on the dd,mm,yyyy field you need to
increment the counters on these fields.  Ok for this example but I
have had to group keys and data after the event on programs with a
very large number of fields.

Ken
```

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

My first COBOL program was written in 1969, and this was after that time
- we indented.  If we wanted to insert an intermediate level, we
indented 2 instead of 4.

At any rate, is there some cost savings in going 1, 2, 3, 4,... insted
of 01, 05, 10, 15?

Oh, I never use single character numbers for suffixes.  I tend to think
of global changes and sorts.   If I change DEBUG-1 to DEBUG-7, it will
change DEBUG-10 to DEBUG-70.  DEBUG-01 is free (now that I am not
dependent upon teletyping speeds).
```

Back in the punched card days, leaving gaps in the level numbers made sense
if it was necessary to add a new level between existing ones.  With punched
cards now in museums and serving as bookmarks,  someone can add a new level
number between contiguous ones with a few thought out substitutions with
their editor.

## Thread: Error 114 in Netexpress 3.0 — 5 msgs

### Error 114 in Netexpress 3.0

- **From:** "Enric Masdeu" <e.masdeu@atr3s.es>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7pv048$c1v3@esiami.tsai.es>`

```
Some people using my programs in Netexpress 3 are getting error 114 (Attemp
to acces item beyond bounds of memory), which I can't reproduce in my
machine.
Does anybody know about possible causes of this error?

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 114 in Netexpress 3.0

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C419DB.6C2E1D86@gfc-net.de>`
- **References:** `<7pv048$c1v3@esiami.tsai.es>`

```
Enric,

Some time ago I had a similar problem, I think it was also error 114. The docs
from Microfocus are quite confusing. I could solve it by adding several DLL
files to the customer's installation.

Most likely you will have the same problem on your machine if you rename the
directory or your Cobol system so your program will no more find their DLLs but
only the ones you deliver.

HTH

Juergen Ibelgaufts

#### ↳ Re: Error 114 in Netexpress 3.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C3632D.F4EF8D3F@home.com>`

```
Enric Masdeu wrote:
> 
> Some people using my programs in Netexpress 3 are getting error 114 (Attemp
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you.

That's a pretty broad question. Depends on what you are doing. If using
OO, you may not have declared an Object Reference. Alternatively can be
something to do with :-

Linkage section.
01 Lnk-Item		pic x.
01 ink-item2		object reference.
01 lnk-return	        pic 9.
Procedure Division using lnk-Item
			 lnk-Item2
		   returning lnk-return.

-  check your call/invoke passes all parameters
-  your Linkage shows all using/returning parameters
-  your Procedure Division shows all using/returning parameters
-  your calling/invoking program receives back correct parameters
-  ensure parameters are listed in same sequence for the above - a tip
   I always list mine alphabetically

HTH - Jimmy Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 114 in Netexpress 3.0

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C3A7FC.2FB05E20@MERANT.com>`
- **References:** `<7pv048$c1v3@esiami.tsai.es>`

```


Enric Masdeu wrote:
> 
> Some people using my programs in Netexpress 3 are getting error 114 (Attemp
> to acces item beyond bounds of memory), which I can't reproduce in my
> machine.
> Does anybody know about possible causes of this error?

Hi Enric.

While there are many potential causes of this error, as you're using
NetExpress 3 there is a way to get more information on the cause - get
the people running the program to search their hard drive for the file
'mfdebug.log' - this will be created in the current working directory
of the COBOL process when the error occurred. It details the specific
type of trap, the register set, and the COBOL call stack etc. At the
very least, it's a useful file to send to Technical Support with your
bug report, should you decide that it's not an error in your program.

The most likely ways of causing this error from COBOL code are:

Bad linkage access
Wild reference modification parameters
Bad parameters passed to CBL_ calls, Windows APIs or other non-COBOL
libraries.

Cheers, Kev.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 114 in Netexpress 3.0

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<37C3A8C6.1D4E3E94@MERANT.com>`
- **References:** `<7pv048$c1v3@esiami.tsai.es>`

```


Enric Masdeu wrote:
> 
> Some people using my programs in Netexpress 3 are getting error 114 (Attemp
> to acces item beyond bounds of memory), which I can't reproduce in my
> machine.

Hi again Enric,

I forgot to mention:

If it's a problem with referencing linkage items which have not been
passed, this will generate an error '203' in .INT code, but by default
is not checked so may generate a '114' error in GNT/OBJ/DLL. However,
if you use the LINKCHECK code-generator directive, GNT/OBJ/DLL code
will also check the linkage parameters and produce a '203'.

I don't *think* this will be your problem, as something as fundamental
as this would most likely show up on your machine also, but it's worth
trying if you don't get anywhere with other investigations.

Cheers, Kev.
```

<a id="t-3793ed7410"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress and IBM SureOne Thermal Printer — 2 msgs

### NetExpress and IBM SureOne Thermal Printer

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990824065116.08941.00003623@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I hope someone can comment on this problem because i have no idea what to do to
get this to work.  Here is my problem:

I am using NetExpress as my development system in Win9x.  I have written a
point of sale system that works on an IBM SureOne.  This terminal comes with a
thermal serial printer (Star).  My boss wants me to print a bitmap which is the
logo of a potential customer on the printer.  There are no print drivers for
this printer.  I use control codes to set the bold print, center text etc.  I
sent IBM a note asking how to accomplish the printing of the bitmap.  They told
me that the  SureOne only supports graphics printing of graphic images that are
defined
as part of the application program.  In the technical documentation there is an
example of a control code that allows you to execute normal density dot
graphics.   They then give an example of the control codes and hex values that
are sent to the printer in order to get a "train" to print on the thermal
printer.  My boss gave me a business card of our potential customer and told me
he wants that to print on the thermal printer.  I have four days to get this to
work.  Could anyone give any suggestions ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and IBM SureOne Thermal Printer

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q10b9$kls$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<19990824065116.08941.00003623@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote in message <19990824065116.08941.00003623@ng-bg1.aol.com>...
>me that the  SureOne only supports graphics printing of graphic images that
are
>defined
>as part of the application program.  In the technical documentation there
is an

This is unbelievable. Have you checked Star for a Windows printer driver? If
not, try contact the printer manufacturer, I cannot believe any printer for
PC today are delivered without windows printer driver. Anyway I have been
developing POS application myself, using both Star and Epson equipment as
POS units. And I am most certain there are printers offering windows printer
drivers.

>My boss gave me a business card of our potential customer and told me
>he wants that to print on the thermal printer.  I have four days to get
this to
>work.  Could anyone give any suggestions ?

Well fellow, you ain't got much time. Best suggestion I have depend on one
issue, is the POS application of yours a native Windows one, being capable
of loading windows printer drivers?

If so, and there really is no windows printer driver available, your best
shot is to replace the printer. There is a number of alternatives out there
which do have windows printer drivers check http://www.starmicronics.com/
and/or http://www.epson.com/

You will then have to alter you application to use windows printer driver,
and my guess, given the need for errortesting and my knowledge to POS
systems, is that 4 days is way too short.

Good luck.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 PROBOLM

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HWGw3.687$dT2.29978@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37c27df6@discussions>`

```
You already have an explanation of some of the general parameters for using
DB2 in COBOL programs, but it sounds like you want to do DYNAMIC SQL - i.e.,
the SQL statements are constructed at run-time. In your case, you are
reading the statments from a file, but makes no difference.

In pseudo-code
  Read Statement from statement file
  Move statement to area reserved for the SQL statements.
  EXEC SQL
     PREPARE statement     <<< I think?
  END-EXEC
  call SQL engine (DB2)
  Write the output to your file.


I haven't done this in a number of years, so I don't recall the exact
syntax.

Also, if you are using an IBM mainframe for the DB2, there is a batch
utility you can run where the SYSIN dataset is the SQL statement and you can
diect the output to another dataset. (This is handy if you don't want to
write a program to do the above).

Maybe someone else knows the details, but "dynamic sql" is what you are
looking to do.


Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
Nuno wrote in message <37c27df6@discussions>...
>
>Hello,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

The package contains

	a superb compiler, on the same level as COBOL for OS/390 and VM, 
	including OO extensions
	a (not so superb) Visual Builder
	a full implementation o fCICS for NT (and OS/2)
	SMARTData utilities to use MVS VSAM files

I have used this setup to write COBOL BATCH and COBOL CICS Programs.  Also, this COBOL and
CICS setup works perfectly well with DB/2 (if you have it installed on the PC as well, of
course)

All in all, I am mightily impressed......  The bad news is, the price tag is very
impressing as well



with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)

<a id="t-fbb4b45a3c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL DB2 PROBLEM — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COBOL DB2 PROBLEM

- **From:** "Calatis" <nmourapires@mail.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ptu8u$rnu$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
Hello,

How can i call, from a cobol program, an aplication that runs under DB2 to
execute my SQL statements. This aplication requires an input file (sql code)
and an output file (result of the sql).

It is possible to do this?

Thanks in advanced.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 PROBLEM

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7NUaV-mWOtB@bop99.ping.de>`
- **References:** `<7ptu8u$rnu$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
nmourapires@mail.pt meinte am 24.08.99
zum Thema "COBOL DB2 PROBLEM":

> Hello,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is possible to do this?

sure!

Your compiler must have a preprocessor or must communicate with the  
preprocessor of your db-system.

We use today the Microfocus/Merant Cobol Workbench 4.0.26 with IBM DB2 2.1.2.
Merant NetExpress 3.0 with IBM UDB 5.x works also fine and we want to use it  
in the future because the old applications are no longer supported.

The sql-statements are in the cobol-sourcecode.

example
-------
"open a cursor with declare-statement"

002517 V05-OEFFNE-EG-CURSOR SECTION.
002518*    ***********************************************************
002519*    * OeFFNET EINEN CURSOR ZUR CROSSREF-TABELLE VKA14030      *
002520*    * ES WERDEN ALLE EG"S MIT VETRAGSNUMMER SELEKTIERT        *
002521*    ***********************************************************
002522 V05A.
002523D    DISPLAY K-PROG-NAME ": V05A"
002524     MOVE "V05A"                 TO  TXT-LABEL
002525*------------------------------------- SQL STARTEN
002526     EXEC SQL
002527
002528        DECLARE EG_CURSOR CURSOR FOR
002529
002530        SELECT
002531              RFRN_INZ_VER_ID
002532             ,NUM_PNR
002533             ,CHAR(DATE_EG_ENDE,EUR)
002534
002535        FROM
002536              VKA14030
002537        WHERE
002538              RFRN_INZ_ID_VTRG = :VKA14030-HS.RFRN-INZ-ID-VTRG
002539
002540     END-EXEC.
002541*------------------------------------- CURSOR OeFFNEN
002542     EXEC SQL
002543
002550        OPEN EG_CURSOR
002560
002570     END-EXEC.
        .....

Fetch the Cursor:
-----------------
028800 V10-FETCH-EG-CURSOR SECTION.
002890*    ***********************************************************
002900*    *   LIEST ZEILEN AUS DEM CURSOR ZUR EG-TABELLE            *
002910*    *   UND KOPIERT DIE ZEILEN IN DIE HOSTSTRUKTUR.           *
002920*    *                                                         *
002930*    ***********************************************************
002940 V10A.
002950D    DISPLAY K-PROG-NAME ": V10A"
002960     MOVE "V10A"                 TO  TXT-LABEL
002970
002980*------------------------------------- SQL STARTEN
002990     EXEC SQL
003000
003010         FETCH EG_CURSOR
003020
003030         INTO
003040              :VKA14030-HS.RFRN-INZ-VER-ID
003050             ,:VKA14030-HS.NUM-PNR
003051             ,:VKA14030-HS.DATE-EG-ENDE
003060
003070     END-EXEC

In this example the sqlcode is embedded in
EXEC SQL END-EXEC.

This is the same way as you do it in a mainframe environment.
The preprocessor now have to replace this statments.
The statements are replaced by api-calls.
You have to link a db2-library (for example IBM DB2 2.1.2 db2api.lib).
At compiletime you have to set som extra directives like SQL(COMMIT=1) or
DB2(COMMIT=1).

If you use the Cobol Workbench or NetExpress you have many howtos.

The result of a sql-statement is stored in host-variables like
vka14030-hs in the example. You can use this host-variables like other
cobol-variables (for example move-statement).

So you can move the informations to an output-structure and write this
into a file.

example:
--------
002091  MOVE NUM-PNR OF VKA14030-HS
002100    TO NUM-PNR OF REC-OUTPUT(NUM-ANZAHL OF REC-OUTPUT)

example host-variable Filename: VKA14150.CPY
---------------------------------------------------------------
EXEC SQL
    DECLARE VKA14150 TABLE
   (
    RFRN_OBJ_VER_ID     CHAR(38)         NOT NULL
   ,RFRN_MAND           CHAR(2)          NOT NULL
   ,NUM_VNR             INTEGER          NOT NULL
   )
END-EXEC.
01  VKA14150-HS.
  10  RFRN-OBJ-VER-ID          PIC  X(038).
  10  RFRN-MAND                PIC  X(002).
  10  NUM-VNR                  PIC S9(009) COMP.

source-code: working-storage implemetation:
-------------------------------------------
000880*    #=========================================================#
000890*    !   VKA14030 EINZELGESCH"FTSDATEN (EGST)                  !
000900*    #=========================================================#
000910
000920     EXEC SQL
000930         INCLUDE VKA14030
000940     END-EXEC.

           instead of

           COPY VKA14030
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 PROBLEM

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990824080852.06868.00003246@ng-cm1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7ptu8u$rnu$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```
Calatis writes ...

>Hello,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm having a little problem with your English here, so let me try to get a more
precise understanding of your question. What do you mean by "call an
application"? Normally we call a program. If that program requests DB2
services, you still simply call the program.

However, any program that uses DB2 services has to go through the DB2
preprocessor that converts "EXEC DB2..." statements to CALLs to various DB2
services; and, the original "EXEC DB2..." statements are extracted and
summarized into a DBRM (Data Base Request Module) that must be BOUND into,
ultimately, a DB2 plan.

At run time, this DB2 plan must be available along with the load module
produced from the original program. So for batch this typically means running
TSO in the batch by executing IKJEFT01 (or a variation of this) and passing
some TSO commands to setup the DB2 environment (DSN command) and then to
execute the program (RUN subcommand).

This being said, it is possible to call such a program, but the calling program
must also be run in the fashion described above.

On the other hand, if you are asking if you can call DB2 services from a COBOL
program, the answer is yes. And the program must be precompiled, compiled and
link; the output from the precopmpiler (the DBRM) must be bound; and the load
module must be run as mentioned above. Depending on other issues (such as
security / ownership) there may be other concerns to be addressed.


>Thanks in advanced.
>
>

You're welcome. Hope this helps.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 PROBLEM

- **From:** "Calatis" <nmourapires@mail.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q0dla$nf7$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`
- **References:** `<7ptu8u$rnu$1@pthp35.telecom.pt>`

```

Thanks everyone!
You've been great help.

Nuno
```

<a id="t-cd1ea15bb4"></a>
## Thread: Help - Micro Focus - start another task in Unix — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help - Micro Focus - start another task in Unix

- **From:** "Niels Chr. Madsen" <ncm@memobase.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C28063.3B57F2E6@memobase.dk>`

```
Hi;

I'm porting our system from OS/2 to SUN Solaris.

In our system, we sometimes starts another task, in OS/2 we are using
the X'91' - 35 function "X"91" function 35 - Execute a Program" - but
this is  only for OS/2, DOS and Windows.

How to do this in UNIX

/Niels Chr. Madsen
```

#### ↳ Re: Help - Micro Focus - start another task in Unix

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C2960C.5B1AEF18@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37C28063.3B57F2E6@memobase.dk>`

```


"Niels Chr. Madsen" wrote:
> 
> Hi;
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> How to do this in UNIX

Hi. The easiest way to do this is:

CALL "SYSTEM" using "unix-command-line" & x"00"

(ie, pass a nul-terminated string BY REFERENCE).

There are other ways (such as opening a pipe to another process
using file handling syntax, or the CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT call on Server
Express) but use of them would depend on your requirements. Calling
'SYSTEM' (use the upper-case version, as this ensures the screeen
handling subsystem doesn't get confused if your subprocess happens to
write to the terminal) is the easiest way.

Cheers, 
Kev.
```

---

## Thread: 198 when using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT — 22 this month / 23 total

### 198 when using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT

- **From:** andersonp@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This is driving me mad! Every which way I try to use the
CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT call I get an error 198.

My current version (very hacked about, sorry) looks like this...

       01  WS-EXEC-RUN-UNIT.
           03  WS-COMMAND-LINE                  PIC X(80).
           03  WS-COMMAND-LENGTH                PIC X(4) COMP-5.
           03  WS-STACK-SIZE                    PIC X(4) COMP-5.
           03  WS-RUN-FLAGS                     PIC X(4) COMP-5.
           03  WS-TTY-COMMAND                   PIC X(80).
           03  WS-TTY-LENGTH                    PIC X(4) COMP-5.

       01  WS-RUN-UNIT-ID                       PIC X(8) COMP-5.
       01  WS-RUN-STATUS                        PIC X(4) COMP-5.

.
.
.
            STRING "BVIEW.EXE " DELIMITED BY SIZE,
                SSTR-VIEW-CODE DELIMITED BY SPACE,
                " " DELIMITED BY SIZE,
                WS-PARAMETER-FILE-NAME DELIMITED BY SPACE,
                " " DELIMITED BY SIZE,
                SSTR-FILE-NAME DELIMITED BY SPACE,
                X"00" DELIMITED BY SIZE
                INTO WS-COMMAND-LINE.

            MOVE 79 TO WS-COMMAND-LENGTH.
            MOVE 0 TO WS-STACK-SIZE.
            MOVE 1 TO WS-RUN-FLAGS.
            MOVE 0 TO WS-TTY-LENGTH.
            CALL "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" USING WS-COMMAND-LINE
                         BY VALUE     WS-COMMAND-LENGTH
                         BY REFERENCE WS-RUN-UNIT-ID
                         BY VALUE     WS-STACK-SIZE
                                      WS-RUN-FLAGS
                         BY REFERENCE WS-TTY-COMMAND
                         BY VALUE     WS-TTY-LENGTH
                         RETURNING    WS-RUN-STATUS.

I basically need to be able to run an windows 32-Bit EXE, and wait for
it to complete before continuing.

Any suggestions?

Phil


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

The run-unit handling routines routines work only with .int files and .gnt
files. They do not work with .exe files or .dll files.

been removed - you can now CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT executable files (whether
they contain COBOL or not).

Cheers, Kev.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 198 when using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q3b6g$dh5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mQYw3.32878$C6.389784@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <37C52322.1601865@MERANT.com> <T5ax3.33714$C6.412802@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
This is copied from NE online Help...Don't see any mention of the fact that
it is only used for .int or .gnt...



Creates a synchronous or asynchronous run-unit.

call "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" using        command-line

                         by value     command-line-len
                         by reference run-unit-id
                         by value     stack-size
                                      flags
                         by reference tty-cmd
                         by value     tty-cmd-len
                         returning    status-code

Parameters:

command-line pic x(n).

command-line-len pic x(4) comp-5.
run-unit-id pic x(8) comp-5. Must be level 01.
stack-size pic x(4) comp-5.
flags pic x(4) comp-5.
tty-cmd pic x(n)
tty-cmd-len pic x(4) comp-5.
status-code See Key

On Entry:

command-line The command passed to the new run-unit. This should be the
program-name followed by any parameters.

line-length The length of the command line.
stack-size This parameter is ignored.
flags A 32-bit word indicating how the new run-unit is created as follows:

Bit Value Meaning
0 0 CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT returns to the caller immediately after creating the
child process.
1 CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT waits for the new run-unit to complete before returning
to the caller.
1 0 The newly created run-unit inherits all COBOL and run-time switches,
environment variables and open libraries as they exist in the caller at the
time of creation. See notes.
1 The newly created run-unit only inherits all environment variables as they
exist in the caller at the time of creation. See notes.
2 0 The newly created run-unit will inherit the caller's console for screen
I/O. Only one member of the coru should access this console at a time,
otherwise unpredictable results may be encountered.
1 The newly created run-unit has a new console for terminal I/O allocated to
it. This console is independent from the parent and can be used for screen
I/O.If this flag is specified then the tty-cmd and tty-cmd-len parameters
must also be specified.
3-31 0 Reserved - must be set to 0.
tty-cmd This parameter is needed only if bit 2 of flags is set. tty-cmd
identifies the console to allocate to the new run-unit or provides a command
to create a console for that new run-unit. This parameter should be a dummy
value, as this extra specification is not required by the operating system.
tty-cmd-len The length of the tty-cmd buffer. The length should always be
set to 0, as this extra specification is not required by Windows 95.

On Exit:

run-unit-id The unique handle identifying the new run-unit.

status-code Status of operation:
0  Success
157 Out of memory
181 Invalid parameter
200 Internal logic error

Comments:

The run-unit that makes the call is known as the parent, while the run-unit
created is known as the child. When the child is created it inherits certain
attributes from its parent. The attributes inherited by the child are copies
of the attributes in the parent at the exact time of the call; any changes
made to the attributes of the parent after the call are not reflected in the
child's attributes. Similarly, any changes made to the child's attributes
are not reflected in the parent's attributes.

Bit 1 flag controls which attributes of the parent are inherited by the
child.

The set of run-units containing the initial run-unit and all run-units
created from it is known as the coru.

You should be aware of the following when using run-units:

All COBOL programs in the child are in their initial state.
 Data is not shared between run-units unless explicitly specified using
CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR, CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR or CBL_ALLOC_MEM.
 Open files and heaps are never inherited from the parent.
```

Ken Mullins <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:7q3b6g$dh5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
> This is copied from NE online Help...Don't see any mention of the fact
that
> it is only used for .int or .gnt...

<<SNIP>>
>
This is copied from the NE online help under the heading "Run-unit Handling
Routines":

The multiple run-unit routines enable you to make use of the Concurrency
support provided with this system.
The routines work only with .int files and .gnt files. They do not work with
.exe files or .dll files.

CBL_ABORT_RUN_UNIT Abort the current run-unit irrespective of the state of
existing threads
CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT Create run-unit
CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR Read named value
CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR Create/update named value
CBL_YIELD_RUN_UNIT Yield the current run-unit

Now, quit calling me a liar. ;o)
```

#### ↳ Re: 198 when using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<935618444.10219.0.nnrp-07.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

<andersonp@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> This is driving me mad! Every which way I try to use the
> CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT call I get an error 198.
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> Phil

Phil,

You could try the win32 API method instead ie: this code im using atm
and works a treat.

You will need the various win* copybook files to use this though or you
could write
your own.
If you need them i will send them.

      $IF INTCODE=1
           CALL "cob32api" end-call
      $else
           set myproc to entry "kernel32.dll"
      $end
           move StartupInfoSize to cb of StartupInfo

      * To hide the contents of process use...
      *    move SW-HIDE to wshowwindow of StartupInfo

           move SW-NORMAL to wshowwindow of StartupInfo

           move z"eoedit unicalc.ini" to executable
      * We create the process
           call WinAPI CreateProcess using
               by value 0 size 4,
               by reference executable,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value CREATE-DEFAULT-ERROR-MODE,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by reference StartupInfo,
               by reference ProcessINFO
               returning ws-Okay
           end-call

      *wait for end of process
           if ws-Okay equals isTRUE
               call WINAPI WaitForSingleObject using
                    by value hProcess of ProcessInfo,
                    by value INFINITE
                    returning ws-Okay
               end-call

               if ws-Okay equals WAIT-FAILED
                     invoke self "MessageBox" using msgCouldNotOpenEditor

no-Bitmap
               else
                     call WINAPI GetExitCodeProcess using
                          by value hProcess of ProcessInfo,
                          by reference ws-AppReturnCode
                          returning ws-Okay
                     end-call
               end-if
           else
             invoke self "MessageBox" using msgCouldNotOpenEditor
                                                              no-Bitmap
           end-if


Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

doc's etc. are part of NetExpress. I think it would not be correct to
give away copyrighted materials. And also, because it is very
proprietary, it wouldn't work in other environments: you either already
have it or you don't get it to work.

IM(h)O

Frog.

these copybooks should work using any flavor of cobol
using the correct call-convention, hexidecimal is hexidecimal in any cobol.

#### ↳ Re: 198 when using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c8ce38@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

I read the thread and it seems nobody bothered to help solve your problem,
instead it developed into an argument over the NetExpress
documentation...curious...

The call fails because the Call _By_Name routine is receiving incorrect
parameters.

I checked what you wrote and it looks OK. You have done what they say is
required. (There is a slight discrepancy in that you have defined the
command line to be 80 characters, then set the length to 79. As the command
line is passed by reference, this shouldn't matter; only the address is
passed, via the stack. I think it is unlikely that this is causing your
problem...) So, why doesn't it work? Unfortunately, I no longer have a
MicroFocus environment available with which to test the suggestion below,
but I remember spending many hours trying to get API calls to work passing
parameters by value and reference and found that the suggested method
invariably works.

First, some background...

Although MicroFocus COBOL specifically allows the contents of a dataname to
be passed in the "By Value" clause, this is not how "By Value" is usually
implemented.
If you say "By Value" you should specify a literal. Like:

By Value    2
By Value    'A'

In other words you are passing the actual value to the called program.(This
value will be placed on the stack instead of a pointer)

When you say "By Reference" you must specify a dataname.

By Reference MY-PARAMETERS [Note that this is the default and can be
omitted]

This is because the calling program pushes the ADDRESS of the parameter onto
the stack, from whence it is popped by the called program. That's why you
have different "Calling Conventions" In MF COBOL; the conventions decide
(among other things) whether the stack is cleaned up by the calling or
called program, and the sequence in which the parameters are pushed on it.
"By Reference" means what it says; a "reference" to the parameter is being
passed.

In the NetExpress documentation where it says BY VALUE, try substituting the
actual value as a literal, NOT the dataname. So you need:

>            CALL "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" USING WS-COMMAND-LINE
>                         BY VALUE     80
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>                         RETURNING    WS-RUN-STATUS.

Finally, when you use STRING it is good practice to clear the receiving
field BEFORE stringing into it (unless you are ABSOLUTELY certain that the
strung values will ALWAYS fill it EXACTLY). I know in this case you will
create an ASCIIZ  string (x'00' on the end) so it doesn't really matter, but
it is another chance for errors to occur...

Thanks for posting a full code sample, it always helps, and please don't
infer any criticism from any of my comments. ;-)

HTH,

Pete.

andersonp@my-deja.com wrote in message <7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>This is driving me mad! Every which way I try to use the
>CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT call I get an error 198.
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Using BY VALUE (was: Re: 198 when using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qbaj6$1pre$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7q114p$tp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37c8ce38@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```

peter dashwood wrote in message <37c8ce38@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
[...]
>Although MicroFocus COBOL specifically allows the contents of a dataname to
>be passed in the "By Value" clause, this is not how "By Value" is usually
>implemented.

[Implemented in Micro Focus COBOL or other languages?
I will assume the latter from context; but discuss both.]

"BY VALUE identifier" is XOPEN, not Micro Focus specific.

>If you say "By Value" you should specify a literal. Like:
>
>By Value    2
>By Value    'A'

"BY VALUE integer" is a Micro Focus extension. As far
as I can determine, "BY VALUE (character) literal" is not
valid with MicroFocus.

Even in languages or implementations that permit values
to be passed directly, an identifier should be used to
provide meaning to the value. Generally, "BY VALUE 2"
has less meaning than "BY VALUE a-well-chosen-name".

The exception occurs when a procedure (function, program,
etc.) name or interface name is so specific that the meaning
of the parameter value may be correctly inferred from the context.
Many C functions have that characteristic. Most APIs do not.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C3F5C5.D2877F82@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698450487@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
Peter van Zeeland wrote:
> 
> > in a production environment, I work in an IBM OS/390 environment,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Animator). But......you convinced me for your case.

We have interactive debuggers (which I don't use).  They can be useful
in development, but when your program aborts in the middle of the night
using transient production data, sometimes all you have to work with is
a snapshot or less.  Also, turning on a debugger can change how a
program works (not often, but it does happen).

I remember a story about a program which didn't work correctly, except
with debugging statements in it.  After lots of work they discovered a
HARDWARE error!  Slowing down the program by putting in debug statements
was enough to change some of the races to make it work.  

Sometimes I see a recommendation to change a compiler option to solve a
problem.  But too many environments need to have a migration procedure
which uses the same compiler options for all programs.  That makes
debugging production programs easier.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: prettyCBL - was Variable Length Records

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CA6B14.1CE8E4AD@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698450487@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <37C3F5C5.D2877F82@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> We have interactive debuggers (which I don't use).  They can be useful
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> makes debugging production programs easier.

Firstly, I have come across two 'bugs' in the cobol compiler for MVS
over the last ten plus years.  The first is the handling of intrinsic
functions (compile time COMPUTE only), the second is that you cannot
recursively perform (documentation will be changed).

The ONLY time I have seen a development compile work and not
production it was still an application bug.  It was hard to track down
but SSRANGE fixed that (checks for memory overrun).

I firmly believe in turning on every option you can to check that your
code is correct in development but turning it off in production (it
slows it down to much).  Therefore I cannot agree with your last
statement.  SSRANGE is the perfect example, slows it down by a lot but
it picks up that elusive bug very easily.

Regarding interactive debugging,  use it.  Use it to test your
programs and force them into the error conditions.  I am surprised how
many production programs actually have not had their error handling
tested.  In one job the error handling actually put the program into a
loop.

Ken
```

## Thread: problem linking cobol dll — 3 msgs

### problem linking cobol dll

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q1fi5$9bj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 Using Microfocus Cobol 4.0.07 on Windows NT 4.0 ��

I have a pgm that currently is compiled and linked as a DLL and is
called by Powerbuilder and VisualBasic apps.  Now I would like to
separate one of the functions into its own pgm, to be called by the
orginal as well as by other PB, VB, and Cobol pgms.

If I started with this:

Program-id. IRMP0332
Perform do-some-work
Perform get-a-value
Perform do-more work.


I now have this:
Program-id. IRMP0332.
Perform do-some-work
*     	call "nmmcrypt.dll"   *> make api available when animating
     	call APIENTRY "decrypt" using
             by reference wrk-lex-strg
        Perform do-more work.

Program-id. NMMCRYPT.
       Entry decrypt.
       Perform get-a-value.

But when I try to link IRMP0332, the link fails with these msgs:
irmp0332.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol "_decrypt@4"
irmp0332.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol "_nmmcrypt.dll"

I am compiling and linking with

CBLLINK -v -d -k -l -o IRMP0332 IRMP0332.cbl+cobintfn.obj advapi32.lib

And have LITLINK set in the source code.

Any ideas on what I need to do to make this work?
```

#### ↳ Re: problem linking cobol dll

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JXYw3.32956$C6.389847@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7q1fi5$9bj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Upgrade to MF COBOL 4.0.38. Merant fixed CBLLINK and the way they mangle
names 'round about 4.0.26. Also, your link statement doesn't seem to include
nmmcrypt.lib or a .DEF file with some import statements so the linker knows
where to find the missing entrypoints.


Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com> wrote in message
news:7q1fi5$9bj$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Using Microfocus Cobol 4.0.07 on Windows NT 4.0 ..
 >
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

```
OK.  We have another workgroup working with NetExpress, and they were
able to get clean links, but I don't understand one of the things they
had to do.  The call from pgm-a to pgm-b was changed from

          call APIENTRY "decrypt" using ....

to
          call APIENTRY "decrypt.dll" using ....

What's confusing is that "decrypt" is the entry point (one of several)
within the DLL.  The name of the DLL itself is NMMCRYPT.DLL.  The pgm
is working, but there is no reference at all to the name of the DLL
file, so I'm not understanding how the entry point is located.  Is the
system having to search every DLL in the path?
```
