##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q1cej$csd$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990825123811.20828.00000813@ng-fs1.aol.com> <gBVw3.1114$SC5.50102@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
DEFINE HEX-STORAGE-FIELD AS PIC X(1) COMP-X

MOVE 15 TO HEX-STORAGE-FIELD
MOVE MATH-FIELD TO HEX-STORAGE-FIELD

ETC
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30Ww3.113$wv2.8197@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<19990825123811.20828.00000813@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote in message <19990825123811.20828.00000813@ng-fs1.aol.com>...

>But how do I move a value after some math functions have been done for
>instance:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>HEX-STORAGE-FIELD.


You might want to redefine the MATH-FIELD so you would have access to the
low order byte of that field.  Reference modification might also work, e.g.,
MOVE MATH-FIELD(5:1) TO HEX-STORAGE-FIELD.

HTH
```

```
e.g.,

```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q114r$2iqd$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<19990825123811.20828.00000813@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 wrote in message <19990825123811.20828.00000813@ng-fs1.aol.com>...
[...]
>But how do I move a value after some math functions have been done for
>instance:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>HEX-STORAGE-FIELD.

Depending upon whether you are using Byte or Word
storage mode, the contents of MATH-FIELD will be, either
'0F 00 00'  or  '0F 00 00 00', due to the COMP-5 usage
and the picture clause.

The instruction
MOVE MATH-FIELD (1:1) TO HEX-STORAGE-FIELD
should do what you need.

You could also use
MOVE FUNCTION CHAR (MATH-FIELD + 1)
    TO HEX-STORAGE-FIELD
provided you are not also using an ALPHABET clause.

The first option depends upon the precise definition of
MATH-FIELD; but is fast.

The second option does not depend upon the precise
definition of MATH-FIELD (as long as it is numeric) and
is less likely to break during maintenance; but it may
be slow.

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c4d1fb.11034482@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990825123811.20828.00000813@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

Make it a COMP-X field.

01  Field-Name Pic X COMP-X.

Move 15 to field-name will get you X"0F" in Field-Name.

Remove all the intermediate steps.


On 25 Aug 1999 16:38:11 GMT, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Good Afternoon,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey
```

## Thread: Set Address of linkage to Address of WS? — 2 msgs

### Set Address of linkage to Address of WS?

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B0160C12C@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
I am now using IBM COBOL for MVS and VM and I can't seem to do what I did
using MF on HPUnix.

I was able to "SET ADDRESS OF an 01 dataname in Linkage TO ADDRESS OF a non
01 dataname in Working Storage.  Now I can't even set it to an 01 dataname.

Is this correct?

Did I lose the "dsect-ability"?

Bill

wthompson at okc dot disa dot mil


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Set Address of linkage to Address of WS?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c43283.6870268@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B0160C12C@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
On Wed, 25 Aug 1999 10:01:53 -0500, "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil> wrote:

>I am now using IBM COBOL for MVS and VM and I can't seem to do what I did
>using MF on HPUnix.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bill, 

the COBOL for this and that does not allow ADDRESS OF working-storage-item.  But you can,
of course, use a small cobol program to overcome this problem:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
  Program-ID. GetAddr IS INITIAL.
 DATA DIVISION.
  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  Linkage Section.
   01 Parm1               PIC X.
   01 Parm1-address       POINTER.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Parm1 Parm1-address.
     SET Parm1-address TO ADDRESS OF Parm1
     GOBACK
     .


You use it like this

       Identification Division.
        Program-ID. AskAddr.
       Data Division.
        Working-Storage Section.
         01 Pgm-name              PIC X(8).
         01 Item-pointer          Pointer.
         01 Some-item.
            05 Some-sub-item  PIC X(13).
        Linkage Section.
         01 Some-Sub-Item-Dsect1.
            05 A PIC X(4).
            05 B PIC S9(4) BINARY.
            05 C PIC X(7).
         01 Some-Sub-Item-Dsect2.
            05 A PIC X(7).
            05 B PIC S9(4) BINARY.
            05 C PIC X(4).
       Procedure Division.
           MOVE 'Volker Bandke' TO Some-Sub-Item
           MOVE 'GETADDR' TO Pgm-name
           CALL Pgm-name USING some-sub-item, Item-pointer
           Set ADDRESS OF SOME-SUB-ITEM-DSECT1 TO Item-Pointer
           DISPLAY A OF Some-Sub-Item-DSECT1
           DISPLAY B OF Some-Sub-Item-DSECT1
           DISPLAY C OF Some-Sub-Item-DSECT1
           Set ADDRESS OF SOME-SUB-ITEM-DSECT2 TO Item-Pointer
           DISPLAY A OF Some-Sub-Item-DSECT2
           DISPLAY B OF Some-Sub-Item-DSECT2
           DISPLAY C OF Some-Sub-Item-DSECT2
           GOBACK
           .

Now you have DSECTing back.  I tested the program on the PC, using Visual Age COBOL for
NT.  It SHOULD therefore work on MVS as well


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

## Thread: Help! Updating Access Database from MF COBOL 3.0 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! Updating Access Database from MF COBOL 3.0

- **From:** rlritchi <monsters@fuse.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C3D3EA.1A238C51@fuse.net>`

```
I need help with updating an access database from MF COBOL 3.0. The
program can be invoked from either DOS or Windows to execute the update.
If it is possible I would appreciate an example or simply some
direction. I have tried several methods (sequential, indexed, etc) with
no success.

Thanks for your Help,

Rick Ritchie
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Updating Access Database from MF COBOL 3.0

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<935598039.16439.0.nnrp-03.c1edc1b5@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37C3D3EA.1A238C51@fuse.net>`

```

rlritchi <monsters@fuse.net> wrote in message
news:37C3D3EA.1A238C51@fuse.net...
> I need help with updating an access database from MF COBOL 3.0. The
> program can be invoked from either DOS or Windows to execute the update.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You need to connect to the Access database using ODBC(Open Database
Connectivity)
Simply opening the file using traditional cobol methods will not work.
You say Mf cobol 3.0 is that netexpress?, if so then NetExpress supports
ESQL(embeded simple query language, i think! :) ), very simple SQL calls
to access your data, if you have not got this feature ie: not NetExpress
then you can use the Windows API, using call-convention 2 for 32-bit
this will work for any cobol compiler.

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

<a id="t-ad03fb4d46"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Freeware Utility — 1 msgs

### COBOL Freeware Utility

- **From:** "editor" <editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2NUw3.887$WM.2738@client>`

```
Over the last several months, I have had many COBOLers ask me for one or
more of the following items that I have provided for free to others.  I came
across your name on the Internet and thought you might be interested.

1.  Free Utility Download: SIZER (No More Counting Lines of COBOL Code)
Check out this free utility download for COBOL and cut the hassle of
calculating the size of COBOL records.  It's called COBOL Record Sizer.
You copy any COBOL record layout to the clipboard and this application will
calculate "the size in bytes" of the (largest) record found.

2.  Free Internet Newsletter aboutlegacycoding.com
Is a new Internet newsletter for IT professionals who work with legacy code.
The content focuses on management and integration of enterprise data written
in COBOL, Fortran, C,  ADA, PL1, RPG and other languages.  It's a newsletter
that is designed to provide the facts and spare you the corporate fluff.

3. Government IV&V Definition Statement:  Many companies are requiring  IV&V
(Independent Verification and Validation) evaluation of the COBOL code.  We
have been asked by many of the IS Directors to provide them an IV&V
definition.

If you are interested in either, send me a reply and I will provide the
web-site.
 If you aren't interested or don't work with legacy code, no need to reply,
I won't e-mail you again.  Have a great day.

Sincerely,

Barry Ewell
bje@progeni.com
```

## Thread: Set Address of linkage to Address of WS? -Reply — 1 msgs

### Set Address of linkage to Address of WS? -Reply

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7c3f16f.073@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```


>>> Thompson, William 08/25 11:01 am >>>

 
I am now using IBM COBOL for MVS and VM and I can't seem to do what I did
using MF on HPUnix.

I was able to "SET ADDRESS OF an 01 dataname in Linkage TO ADDRESS OF a non
01 dataname in Working Storage.  Now I can't even set it to an 01 dataname.

Is this correct?

Did I lose the "dsect-ability"?

Bill

wthompson at okc dot disa dot mil


Welp, the only I've ever did a SET ADDRESS was in a CICS program many moons ago so it sounds like to me what y'all want to do is:

MOVE item-in-linkage TO item-in-working-storage.

Doesn't matter if the WS item is an 01 or not.  All you have to worry about is if the types are the same (PIC X to PIC X - PIC 9 to PIC 9 etc) and the lengths are the same.  Not the same lengths you'll have to worry about high or low order elephantnosation.
```

```
wrote:
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol II alternative

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q2pc4$6ur$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<aF_w3.1867$vy.164126@weber.videotron.net>`

```
In article <aF_w3.1867$vy.164126@weber.videotron.net>,
  tan_phat@pop.videotron.ca (crispan) wrote:
> Hi!
> I'm looking for other languages that are either similar or more
performant than
> cobol II. The only critieria is that it must run on a mainframe
environment.
>
> Thx.
>

Mainframe? Do you think about IBM OS/390? If so, you may choose between
the IBM's languages C/C++, FORTRAN, PL/I, COBOL, PASCAL, ASSEMBLER, CSP,
Clist, REXX (and probably some other I don't know about). Do you
consider SQL as a 'language'?
If Unix System Services is activated, AFIK you could use JAVA and
Smalltalk (maybe these two are less 'performant', if you think about CPU
usage).
There are several third party products of 3GL and 4GL level like
Easytrieve, Mantis, Ideal, SAS/C and some dozen more.

All these languages mentioned above are installed on the 'mainframe' on
which I am currently working. Most of them in several releases. And all
of them are about 'similar ore more performant' as VS COBOL II release
4.

Maybe, you should be more specific with your question.
```

## Thread: JCL question — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL question

- **From:** "M. Lenz" <Mogens.Lentz@sas.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
Why does the variable not substitude the filenames in the delete-step of
this JCL. : Is it because it is a sysin-satement or what? Does anyone know?

//*
//SETRDATE  SET RDATE=R199903
//*
//**=======================================
//** SCRATCH THE OLD FILES
//**=======================================
//*
//STEP10    EXEC PGM=AASCR,PARM=NOCOND
//SYSPRINT  DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSIN     DD *
DEL DSN=CPHDBIC.ICSELON.IN.&RDATE
DEL DSN=CPHDBIC.ICSELON.ICW03UD.&RDATE
........
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL question

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q326t$diq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
In article <7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk>,
  "M. Lenz" <Mogens.Lentz@sas.dk> wrote:
> Why does the variable not substitude the filenames in the delete-step
of
> this JCL. : Is it because it is a sysin-satement or what? Does anyone
know?
>
> //*
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It's not a SYSIN statement, it's not a JCL-Statement, it's just data in
this case. You could code //SYSIN DD DSN=.... as well and you will
understand that in this case, the JES interpreter is not allowed to
change your data, just because there is a '&' character in it.
Instream data is treated in exactly the same way as external data, so
you canot use symbolic parameters.
You could write your own little program which receives the name as
parameter and writes the 'DEL ...' statements on a file. Take this file
and use it as input to your delete-step:

//S1       EXEC PGM=yourpgm,PARM=&RDATE
//MYFILE   DD DISP=(NEW,PASS),SPACE=(TRK,1),
//            DCB=(RECFM=F,LRECL=80)
//*
//STEP10   EXEC PGM=AASCR,PARM=NOCOND
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSIN    DD DSN=*.S1.MYFILE,DISP=OLD
```

```
you cannot put DD * into a procedure.  (has this been changed lately
and dated me?)

And the basic premise if it wont translate DD * in mainstream JCL why
would it do it in a procedure.

By the way:  who uses '// SET RDATE=1234' in their JCL.  Makes for
simple changes.  You simply don't use any parameter statement in your
procs and it flows through to them.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CB13D6.6360F17E@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk> <37c54515.3190168@news.speedfactory.net> <37CA6EFC.71617A60@zip.com.au> <37CA89F0.76CA2CEF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
You can now put data cards inside a PROC.  There is a religious
war about its merits.

JCL has some new commands, SET to set a variable and IF THEN ELSE
ENDIF to conditionally execute job steps. The IF is not real
flexible (most notably, if can't compare the value of a symbol to
a constant or another symbol value), but you can now test for
ABENDs, the ABEND code, and if a job step ran.  Some system
symbols are also available such as the RACF userid, which is nice
for generating data set names.

The precedence of setting a symbol value is a little difficult to
express, understanding that it can be changed by a SET statement
inside or outside a PROC, and when the value on an EXEC statement,
or a PROC statement is not overridden.  Nested procedures are the
complicating factor.

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm not familiar with this, could you explain some more?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CBD84D.A7ED97E8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk> <37c54515.3190168@news.speedfactory.net> <37CA6EFC.71617A60@zip.com.au> <37CA89F0.76CA2CEF@NOSPAMhome.com> <37CB13D6.6360F17E@techie.com>`

```
Jared Thomas wrote:
> 
> You can now put data cards inside a PROC.  There is a religious
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> for generating data set names.

I stress the word STEP here.  You cannot conditionally change a single
DD (I keep hoping).

> 
> The precedence of setting a symbol value is a little difficult to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> complicating factor.

Let's try an example:

// job
//....
//  set prefix=PROD
//js1  exec pgm=cobol1
//dd1  dd dsn=&prefix..some.dataset,disp=shr
//js2  exec proc1

...
If proc1 is defined as (don't trust the syntax I don't do this
anymore):

//proc1 proc  prefix=dev
// ....

Prefix will equal dev inside the procedure but :

//proc1 proc
//...

prefix will equal the mainline SET value.

This means that you can have your jcl 'variables' defined in one spot
at the top of the JCL and it automatically passes through the JCL.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2051E101A92CF376.0FE1A3FA3A872543.1DD40F3429F170E1@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk> <37c54515.3190168@news.speedfactory.net> <37CA6EFC.71617A60@zip.com.au> <37CA89F0.76CA2CEF@NOSPAMhome.com> <37CB13D6.6360F17E@techie.com> <37CBD9FB.3E3063B2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Tue, 31 Aug 1999 07:34:51 -0600, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> enlightened us:

>Jared Thomas wrote:
>> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>more recent one to find out about the SET command.

F.Y.I., the syntax of the SET command is:

SET parameter=value,[parameter=value,.....]

parameter = The name of a symbolic parameter you want to assign a
value to.
value = The value you want assigned.

The advantage of the SET statement is that you don't have to use it in
conjunction with procedures at all.  For example, suppose you have a
job that you frequently use with only minor variations.  If you use a
SET statement at the beginning of the job to set a symbolic parameter
that's used throughout the job, you can use a text editor to change
the SET statement each time you submit the job.  There's one
peculiarity of the SET statement you need to be aware of:  It's not
subject to the conditional processing imposed by IF, ELSE, or ENDIF
statements.  If you enclose a SET statement within an IF/ELSE/ENDIF
construct, the SET statement will be executed regardless of the
results of the IF statement's condition tests.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I finally got my head together, now my body is falling apart.


 Steve
```

```
to a proc, whre the proc would just do an IEFBR14 with
DISP=(MOD,DELETE,DELETE) for the DDnames.
```

```
M. Lenz wrote:
> 
> Why does the variable not substitude the filenames in the delete-step of
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> ........

I've never tried using a &name in data cards.  When I used IDCAMS
delete, I used DD statements for that purpose.

But now my favorite delete JCL is as follows:
//TBEB       EXEC PGM=IEFBR14
//TRANSIN    DD DSN=CPHDBIC.ICSELON.ICW03UD.&RDATE,
//             DISP=(MOD,DELETE),SPACE=(TRK,0)
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL question

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k1dx3.141$Yp.7777@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
M. Lenz wrote in message <7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk>...
>Why does the variable not substitude the filenames in the delete-step of
>this JCL. : Is it because it is a sysin-satement or what? Does anyone know?
>


You already know that the data in a SYSIN statment is just data, not subject
to symbolic parameter substitution.


However, if all you want to do is delete any existing datasets, you can use
something like...

>//SETRDATE  SET RDATE=R199903
 KILL EXEC PGM=IEBBR14
 DD1 DSN=CPHDBIC.ICSELON.IN.&RDATE, DISP=(MOD,DELETE,DELETE),
                 UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(TRK,(1,1),RLSE)


.. Adjust for your installation (SPACE and UNIT may or may not be required)

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL question

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YeDx3.248$mo.15784@viper>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
Since your question has been answered, I'll make some other suggestions:

- Use IEFBR14 to delete flat files, PDS's, or single generations of a GDG
    //delete exec pgm=iefbr14
    //deldd    dd dsn=node1.node2.node3.&rdate,
    //            disp=(mod,delete,delete),space=(1,(1,1),rlse)

- Use the TSO delete command to delete GDG bases and VSAM files:
    //delete exec pgm=ikjeft01,
    //  parm='delete ''node1.node2.node3.&rdate'' '
****not sure of exact syntax to send " ' " to IKJEFT01, you'll have to
experiment, but I know it works.

- Wirte a COBOL or other program to accept a PARM with symbolics and write
to a control card, then use the control card in your example:
    //subst    exec pgm=cobchng,parm='DEL DSN=CPHDBIC.ICSELON.IN.&RDATE'
    //ctlcard  dd dsn=&&ctlcard,
    //  disp=(new,pass,keep),
    //  dcb=(recfm=fb,lrecl=80,blksize=0),
    //  space=(80,1,rlse)
    //STEP10    EXEC PGM=AASCR,PARM=NOCOND
    //SYSPRINT  DD SYSOUT=*
    //SYSIN     DD dsn=&&ctlcard,disp=(shr,delete,delete)

- or possibly a more complicated answer would be to do similar to the above,
but have it read in a control file with the symbolics in them. Then have the
program manually scan the control cards making the appropriate substitutions
writing out to yet another control card file. This would better allow for
multi-line control cards.


hope this helps
```

<a id="t-265d8a165d"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Decimal to Hex — 3 msgs

### RE: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698498433@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
> >But how do I move a value after some math functions have been done for
> >instance:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
	Try this:

	       01  MATH-FIELD           PIC S9(04) COMP.
	       01  FILLER REDEFINES MATH-FIELD.
	             02 HEX-FIELD         PIC X(01).
	             02 FILLER              PIC X(01).


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l1dx3.142$Yp.7777@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698498433@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```

Peter van Zeeland wrote in message
<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698498433@NL-AMB-MAIL01>...
>>
> Try this:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


If ANSI standard COBOL, you need to reverse the positions of HEX-FIELD and
FILLER. COMP data is stored with the MSBs at the lowest address (i.e.,
"leftmost").

YMMV, as COMP is often "implementor (re-)defined."


MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Decimal to Hex

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q41gg$lgd@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698498433@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <l1dx3.142$Yp.7777@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:l1dx3.142$Yp.7777@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
>
> Peter van Zeeland wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Michael, Michael, Michael,

 In "ANSI standard COBOL" a COMP field can be ANYTHING the vendor wants.
Even a USAGE BINARY field can be stored in any way that the implementor
defines.  "Little-endian/big-endian" and almost everything else is allowed.
As (often) stated in this NG, there are even implementations where USAGE
COMP is stored as "packed-decimal" or "display" -  all of this is ENTIRELY
ANSI conforming.
```

```
> >	Do yourself a favour, and consider the alternatives.
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
	As TIME equals MONEY in our trade, if anywhere, this should not be
hard to prove.
	I, for one, would gladly pay for a laptop, a compiler, and a piece
of cable myself to escape from something that is called "IKFCBL00".
Seriously.

	You don't like traffic jams either, do you ? 

	Have you ever wondered why terminals are considerably larger than 1
line by 1 column ? (e.g. 25 x 80)
	This is because we do not want to observe the world through a
keyhole.

	Not only do we dislike traffic jams and keyholes, we move faster and
are more observant without them.
	Translate this to the beancounters as 'more productive'.

	The alternaltive: minimize all open windows NOW, and continue with
your work. You'll never notice the difference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: prgrammers environment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
DD,
  If you are actually concerned about "bean-counters" and IKFCBL00 (aka
OS/VS COBOL - aka an IBM mainframe compiler for which support was dropped
about 5 years ago), I think that IBM *does* have some White Papers on this.
Ignoring the fact that OS/VS COBOL is AMODE(24)/RMODE(24) only (for its
object code), I think there are some other "cost justification" case
studies.  If you (or more importantly your customers - because I know that
you already know all of this) are seriously interested in any of this, send
me private email - and I can get you some references.

HAVING SAID THAT, I do understand the "if it ain't broke and it takes more
that 2 cents to upgrade - why upgrade" philosophy that almost ALL of us have
run into at one time or another. My guess is that your clients wouldn't
read/study anything that didn't promise them an
easy/free/over-night/100%-perfect conversion.

P.S. Randall (or other UNISYS people),
   I know that I keep reading in this NG about the same type of "upgrade"
problems for UNISYS people.  Where the "upgrade" to a COBOL '85 compiler is
actually a "downgrade" in performance.  Is there (generally available)
UNISYS case studies or white papers on "justifying" an upgrade?  I probably
should add both the IBM and Unisys stuff (if it exists) to the FAQ.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ORB Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C8BFC4.B34E8B2B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37C69E63.8C43B00A@sib.fr> <37c85781.241859357@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Fofgive my ignorance, but what is an ORB?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

ORB(Object Request Broker)

From the Micro Focus on-line glossary :-

ORBIX is a CORBA based middleware platform from Iona on which NetExpress
developers can create and run distributed applications. Orbix
applications can be written in a variety of different languages, such as
COBOL, C++, or Java, and interact together in a transparent manner at an
enterprise level across a broad range of different platforms. For
additional information about ORBIX, refer to the Iona ORBIX home page at
http://www.iona.com.

If you're reading this Warren - more $10 words !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ORB Cobol

- **From:** "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CA7A1D.68D7F41B@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<37C69E63.8C43B00A@sib.fr> <37c85781.241859357@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Forgive my ignorance, but what is an ORB?
> 

Object Request Broker. Best thing since bread and butter. :-)
You define object interfaces (assume that by this time everybody at
least has heard of objects - myself have only theoretical knowledge).
You use the interfaces methods (used to call them procedures) to do
things. The broker finds out on which machine, and in which language,
the object is implemented, and makes the connection. If there is a
result, you get it back. Your program can be written in COBOL, C, C++,
Java, Smalltalk, ADA (I think), and probabley some others. The object
itself also can be written in any of these languages, and run anywhere
on a network. You don't need to know where. You just talk to the
interface. There is a a IDL (interface definition language) which is
universal - it's something like C++, and a compiler which translates the
IDL to the particular language used.
CORBA means Common ORB Arquitecture. You can get more information at
www.omg.org. Definitely worth looking into.
Disclaimer: I don't have any practical experience with this. Just read a
bit.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ORB Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cb246f.43156192@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37C69E63.8C43B00A@sib.fr> <37c85781.241859357@news1.ibm.net> <37CA7A1D.68D7F41B@conexion.com.py>`

```
This whole topic intrigued me, so I found a source that explains it
ALL.

http://www.omb.org

It's very interesting stuff.

You can see by the data types that the broker uses, however, that is
is a bit "anti" COBOL.



On Mon, 30 Aug 1999 08:33:33 -0400, "Albert Ratzlaff R."
<albert@conexion.com.py> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ORB Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CBB889.CEEE0CB7@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<37C69E63.8C43B00A@sib.fr> <37c85781.241859357@news1.ibm.net> <37CA7A1D.68D7F41B@conexion.com.py> <37cb246f.43156192@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> This whole topic intrigued me, so I found a source that explains it
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> is a bit "anti" COBOL.

For one, you need pointers. That's not posible with the COBOL I use (RM
on Unix). But you can write a C server process that talks to a COBOL
program, so that would be an indirect way for interfacing to a "legacy"
system (or is "heritage" now the politicaly correct term? :).

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ORB Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cc43fd@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37C69E63.8C43B00A@sib.fr> <37c85781.241859357@news1.ibm.net> <37CA7A1D.68D7F41B@conexion.com.py> <37cb246f.43156192@news1.ibm.net>`

```
CORBA is the OMG Request Broker. MicroSoft's Common Object Model (COM) does
the same job and is the basis for .OCX and ActiveX controls. These controls,
if developed according to the COM standard, are language independent anyway
and remove the need for a CORBA type function. ActiveX controls can be
developed in ANY language and do NOT require a "broker" as such. It is
interesting that the OMG is about to embrace the COM model with CORBA (not
vice/versa). There has been some competition between the 2 standards for
some time and it is good to see it being resolved.

You can develop COM (and therefore, soon to be CORBA) compliant ActiveX
controls in Fujitsu PowerCOBOL which supports the COM model. This allows you
to produce re-usable pluggable controls with defined methods and properties.
These controls can be placed on your GUI windows just like any other
"button" and can be visible or invisible to the user. Fujitsu provide
standard ones for Database access (very VB like...), node lists, timers,
floating toolbars, and a whole plethora too numerous to mention. They claim
you can freely mix these with 3rd Party ActiveX controls (which may have
been written in any language) as long as they are COM compliant, including
controls you may have developed yourself. I thought this was a bit too good
to be true, so I tried it.... Guess What?

It works perfectly with no hassles.

I now have a re-usable scheduling engine which allocates resources and staff
to appointments for ANY application which requires an appointment book (you
have to set up a relational database with structures to support whatever
ACTIVITIES, STAFF, and RESOURCES you need, but this can easily be done in
co-operation with the User. There is no coding, just a data analysis and
entry. The scheduling (ActiveX) control does the rest (including all the DB
access and updating via the appropriate methods). Ideal for Hospitals,
Doctors, Dentists, even Restaurants or any professional service which needs
to have allocated appointments.) I have similar controls for handling
Clients, entering dates (all millennium compliant for the next 26,000
years...), interfacing to Crystal Reports, and so on...

Our systems no longer use COBOL access methods (apart from LINE Sequential)
no more ISAM or Relational access. Everything is via ODBC using re-usable
modules  (for mainframe and PC) or ActiveX controls (PC only). Once the
methods are written and de-bugged that's it. EVERYTHING is designed to be
low-maintenance and platform independent.

The net result is that we can "tailor" a system to a client very quickly.
It's like assembling cars from sub-components.

As you will have gleaned from the above, I am completely "sold" on the
object model for system development.

COBOL is an ideal language for developing commercial components. Fujitsu are
to be commended for providing a development environment which actively
encourages the use of COM compliant modules.

Pete.




Thane Hubbell wrote in message <37cb246f.43156192@news1.ibm.net>...
>This whole topic intrigued me, so I found a source that explains it
>ALL.
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-ff7c655851"></a>
## Thread: Need Advice: Graduate School Worthwhile? — 7 this month / 8 total

### Need Advice: Graduate School Worthwhile?

- **From:** "Cosmic Knight" <cosmicknight@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aoGx3.11704$ok3.3305@news.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
I have been considering a Master's degree in the C.S. field, either
e-commerce, or straight C.S.  In your opinion, is an advanced degree a
worthwhile pursuit?

I am currently a programmer with a B.A. and additional schooling through
certificate programs, which my company paid for.

My boss feels that advanced C.S. degrees are worthless, and that an M.B.A.
is much more beneficial in the I.T. area, as it gives the student practical
business knowledge.  He feels that most University C.S. programs are a joke,
and that everything in this business can be learned on the job.

Thoughts?

Advice from people in positions where they actually *HIRE* people is greatly
appreciated.  What do you look for in a candidate, and do advanced degrees
give a prospective employee an advantage over a similarly qualified
non-master's candidate?

Thanks-
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Advice: Graduate School Worthwhile?

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ClJx3.465$vl2.48965@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<aoGx3.11704$ok3.3305@news.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```

Cosmic Knight <cosmicknight@home.com> wrote in message
news:aoGx3.11704$ok3.3305@news.rdc1.il.home.com...
> I have been considering a Master's degree in the C.S. field, either
> e-commerce, or straight C.S.  In your opinion, is an advanced degree
a
> worthwhile pursuit?

An advanced degree is a plus. Which degree depends on you. With a
Masters in Computing Science you may have more intellectual
stimulation and the ability to teach at the community college level.

If you want money, an MBA is worth a lot more. A hell of a lot more.
As much as twice more. Plus, you can still teach.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need Advice: Graduate School Worthwhile?

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I've been in the position of working with people with joint first class
honours degrees in Computing Science and Mathematics and, on the other
hand, people with no prior knowledge of computing and a couple of GCE
'A' Levels (a UK qualification). I would not touch anyone with a Maths
or CS degree with a barge pole, especially if it is a first class
degree. Give me the lower (GCE 'A' level ) qualification anytime.

However, take your bosses advice and get an MBA. I would value that more
highly and am considering going for the same myself.
```

```
What are your goals?  An M.B.A. is definitely the way to go if you want
to get promoted.  The better you understand your business, the more
valuable you will be.  But if you want to persuade your bosses to let
you work on the new Java project, a CIS degree may be better for that.  
Getting practice in OO and the current language of the day may prepare
you to acquire skills you want to keep your present level of work within
a changing industry.
```

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>

```
and that VB or C(++) are the languages to learn.  Although I believe that VB
and the C(++) languages are important and worth while learning, I DO NOT
believe that COBOL is dead.  (esp. with OO COBOL and the new GUI
interfaces.)

Does anyone know any facts, figures, strengths and weaknesses to help my
battle?

Thanks, Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

- **From:** andydecepida@programmer.net (Andy Decepida)
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
This doesn't explain why most of the lead designers/programmers at
Borland/Inprise were recruited by Microsoft.

Speaking as a fundamentally COBOL programmer who's done VB, C/C++ and
Delphi in a mult-platform, multi-language environment I would say that
Delphi happens to provide the most robust of error-handling and
capabilities of the more modern languages -- matching and in some
instances exceeding COBOL's.

I am not disputing that it has not been marketed as much as it can be
but Delphi has contributed much to computer technology if only because
it keeps being emulated and imitated by its competitors and peers --
obviously without much acknowledgement of progeny.  But that's as
things are in life, the better things aren't always the most
successful.
```

```
So says someone that do not know.

I dear to say so, I have experience with a number of languages, and I would
say that few, if any are more general purpose than Delphi. The way Delphi
combines low level entry to first time users and at the same time offers
power to the extent of assembly is rather unique.

If Delphi is gone in 5 years, it is because the marketing power once again
has ruled like it did when OS/2 died, like it did when Beta lost to VHS, for
those who remember the dawn of home video.

When that is said, as I have stated before in this ng, most languages has
their pros'n cons. Most have their congregation. I have never understood
those, who force this debate. If there are a programming language and it is
being used by someone, let there be the language.

I have my preferences, depending on the task to be done. I let others have
their preferences. If a programming language dies that is regulated easily
by itself, and do not need anyone to push it by distributing rumours.

Any discussion about a language's ability is any way doomed to be biased.
Who could possibly claim to be unbiased?

Cheesle
```

```
Personally, all of my development is done using cobol and native windows
API's...I doubt you can tell the difference in the resulting executable, as
to whether it is C, C++, VB, etc...Other than having to understand C to
decode the API calls, it is fairly straight forward...I have programmed in
cobol for 25 years or so, and will be sticking with it...I may not stick
with MicroFocus, but will stick with cobol...I don't think cobol is dead at
all...The compiler vendors could do more to help it's rep...

kenmullins
```

#### ↳ Reports of COBOL's demise are vastly exaggerated

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bef0c6$2f4b2fc0$0100007f@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<zAyx3.184$3s2.745@news.flash.net>`

```
Best answer I've found is tell 'em: I remember when the news 
was COBOL and FORTRAN were dead.  Everybody would be
coding in Algol, then PL/I (I really liked PL/I), then Pascal, even Lisp
(fer cryin' out loud! Lisp (and, yeah, I've programmed in Lisp, too))

I first heard the COBOL is dead rant over 30 years ago, most of the
suggested replacement languages are long gone.   Sic transit gloria FAD.

As for ammunition to debate the matter, why waste your time, you will not
convince bigots of anything, even with incontrovertible proof.  And anyone
who comes off with "COBOL is dead" expressed with a severe attitude is
a bigot.   Better to debate the matter with a babbling brook, at least you
can relax outside doing that.

That said, learning other computer languages can be very good.  Each has
a different way of looking at things, and some are more suited to certain
tasks than others.  But don't bother with rantings and flame.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7NfiIzQWOtB@bop99.ping.de>`
- **References:** `<zAyx3.184$3s2.745@news.flash.net>`

```
senseisk@hotmail.com meinte am 27.08.99
zum Thema "COBOL isn't a dead language...  is it?":

> Can anyone help me?  many people I talk to say that COBOL is a dead
> language and that VB or C(++) are the languages to learn.  Although I
> believe that VB and the C(++) languages are important and worth while
> learning, I DO NOT believe that COBOL is dead.  (esp. with OO COBOL and
> the new GUI interfaces.)

Cobol is a business language. There are many cobol programs out there.
Many students (in germany) learn c++ or pascal. Many windows programmers use  
c++ or delphi. Some private people using visual basic.

In the mainframe environment, i think, cobol isn't dead.
A great problem is that no oo-standard is out.

If you ask me i like the way delphi supports the gui-programming.
I think cobols role in the compilerworld is changed. Today cobol is the  
language to build server-solutions in a client/server environment where the  
server is one of the big mainframes.

It's not a failure to learn c++ because it's the windows-, linux/unix and  
technical-problem standardsolution to solve problems.

I'm working in a german insurance company and our mainframe environment uses  
more than 90 percent cobolcode. Our clients are build in c++ or java.

> Because I don't know VB etc. I don't have ammo to counter such
> statements. Does anyone know any facts, figures, strengths and
> weaknesses to help my battle?

Visual Basic, Delphi and C++ are all born in the same way with the same  
concept. But there plattform is different (Basic, Pascal, C).
IMHO the oo-solutions are looking in a same way in all this languages.
The differences between this languages aren't great.

I think it's very important to understand the concepts like oo.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c8aaae@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<zAyx3.184$3s2.745@news.flash.net> <7NfiIzQWOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

Juergen Vetter wrote in message <7NfiIzQWOtB@bop99.ping.de>...
>senseisk@hotmail.com meinte am 27.08.99
>zum Thema "COBOL isn't a dead language...  is it?":
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


This is so close to another conversation I participated in recently, that
I'm posting it here again....

Frank Y wrote in message ...
>Don't bother looking for work in Utah. Lots of age discrimination and you
>are too old, and so am i. Besides Cobol is really dead now that the Y2K
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> programming many applications.And that was ten yrs back.. was just
curious
>> to know if i want to migrate to the US or Canada do i stand any chance ?
>>
>> In a nut shell i have worked on DEC's PDP 11 mostly.And right now am
doing
>> ladder programming for PLC's.
>> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
Response from Dashwood:>>>

Well, COBOL may be dead in Utah but it is alive and well in much of the USA
(and the rest of the World)

Y2K was NOT the death knell for COBOL.

Many projects which were shelved while people tackled Y2K (and much time and
resource was wasted running around in circles...) will require
implementation in the new Millennium. I have seen Business Studies which
estimate an average of at least 4 years work waiting to be done. (Of course,
these studies are notoriously inaccurate and I have seen no estimates of
average "man years" per organisation, but it is a fair bet that COBOL
development will continue well into the new century. I am talking here
"Mainframe COBOL" (mainly batch).

If we look at the situation with Client/Server or PC Networking, the picture
is not so encouraging as other tools are often preferred over COBOL.

In my experience this is often due to COBOL's "bad" image and a lack of
Management understanding about the relative merits of various languages. VB
and C guys are consistently amazed when I produce GUI based modules written
in COBOL and the usual reaction is "I didn't know you could do that in
COBOL...!!!"

ActiveX  (also known as .OCX) controls are particularly appropriate as they
can be plugged
directly onto forms (for both Windows or the Web) and the language they are
written in doesn't matter as, due to encapsulation, the only parts which are
visible are the properties
(variables) and methods (procedures).

I find Fujitsu COBOL extremely useful for doing this as they have a fine
environment for custom controls, but MicroFocus is also very good. Being
able to independently produce re-usable controls (and gradually build
libraries of them) has saved several projects
I have managed, from slipping. It is like having an extra resource (in this
case, me...<grin>) up your sleeve and this is the stuff that Project
Managers' dreams are made of....

I have written elsewhere in this NG about my belief that COBOL's days are
numbered. It wasn't ONLY COBOL (I include all procedural languages in this;
programming as we understand it will/must undergo a sea change), and the
number is STILL a fairly large
one...I reckon 15 years.

There is still a huge market for COBOL skills. The only difference is that,
whereas 30 years ago this market seemed interminable, it now appears to be
finite. (I agree it is a matter of opinion and before you all flame me or
suggest templates at dawn, let me stress again, that this is my OPINION. I
like to think it is an informed opinion but that is also subject to debate
<grin>)

Part of the future growth path for COBOL has to be in moving legacy code to
newer platforms. My company has already done a fair bit of this and I see no
diminishing of the requirement. We usually insist on setting up a pilot
project using the client's own COBOL programmers and converting them (both
evangelically and practically :-) ) to the merits of using the "new" COBOL
(GUI). This makes sound dollars and "sense". It re-motivates the COBOL team
who are often very worried about their futures, and it leverages their
existing skill into state of the art technology. The company (our client) is
happy, the programmers are happy, and I can afford to take long vacations
and go away and just think about stuff that I otherwise wouldn't get time
to...everybody wins, the best kind of game.

To Frank: I've never been to Utah. If it is REALLY as bad as you say, why
are you living there? (I know, 6 wives and 18 kids need a lot of looking
after...and the desert is beautiful in the Summer...;-) )

To Patrick: Your experience is still valuable. As for your age, I am much
older than you and still working (I insist on keeping my hand in at
programming and never let my other duties prevent me from doing so...Also
take a keen interest in technical problems encountered by the team and enjoy
the challenge of getting things working.) In fact, I'm planning stuff beyond
the next 15 years... If you can't find an employer who can see your value,
screw 'em! Go freelance. You can't be any worse off and you may find you
enjoy it. (I did for over 20 years).

It is interesting that a recent survey here in the U.K. found that older
workers are usually more reliable, have fewer days off sick, and provide
better value for money. (OK, I know there are loads of good young people,
I'm just telling you what I heard on the radio...)

Pete.

END of response from Dashwood<<<

So, if we are counting votes, my vote says that COBOL is far from dead.
(IMHO, "computer programming" by specialists (i.e. career programmers) is an
activity with a limited lifetime, but that's another story...).

Having said that, it DOES need a bit of assistance. I would expect COBOL
programmers on every corporate site to be honing their skills and looking at
opportunities to convert legacy systems to modern User-Friendly front ends
(let's get rid of "green screens" forever...). At the same time, Management
should be informed that the juicy new development they are about to
outsource for programming in C, could easily be done using in-house people
and GUI COBOL for much less money, with consequent positive benefits on
morale, maintainability, and project control. If they don't believe you,
knock out some GUI screens and show 'em...(I did this once and a certain
company lost a major contract....but a lot of other people kept their
jobs...and acquired some new skills which made them more valuable). If you
intend to keep working as a COBOL programmer, support the language. Talk to
VB guys (not in an argumentative or competitive way, but with genuine
interest in what they are doing and how they are doing it...you could learn
something). Find out what they have trouble with, then see if you can do it
in COBOL. (Offer them an ActiveX control to animate the Company Logo on a
Form, for example...that one worked for me).

The bottom line is that unless the corporate image of COBOL is changed, it
WILL die much sooner than need otherwise be the case. This is NOT an
impossible task. There are some fabulous COBOL tools now available and they
won't break the bank. (Certainly not the corporate bank.) COBOL had a slow
start on PCs because COBOL compilers have traditionally been 5 or 6 times
the cost of a VB or C compiler and that did not encourage students of
computer science and young people wanting to get into programming to
purchase them. That is no longer the case.

OK, this has been a long post but we care about the language, right? (see
"Who Cares?" elsewhere in this NG).

If so, then DO something about it!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C962C0.6A55@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<zAyx3.184$3s2.745@news.flash.net> <7NfiIzQWOtB@bop99.ping.de> <37c8aaae@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37c95c0f.308571163@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Sun, 29 Aug 1999 04:29:32 +0100, "peter dashwood"
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> use has been batch mainframe?

Maslow said "If your only tool is a hammer, every problem looks like a nail". There 
aren't too many people who have both the mainframe COBOL skills and the PC skills to 
combine them (or even think of doing so). So the people working on it  use only what 
they know.

My first client-server foray (so new at the time it was covered in a 1991 article in 
Computing Canada) used a CSP ("COBOL with lantern slides") SQL/DS mainframe program 
interfacing with the PC via IND$FILE transfer of data (too much info to handle with a 
background "screen scraper").  Did it that way because back then it was pretty much the 
ONLY way to to what we wanted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CA8482.4F551971@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<zAyx3.184$3s2.745@news.flash.net> <7NfiIzQWOtB@bop99.ping.de> <37c8aaae@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37c95c0f.308571163@news1.ibm.net> <37C962C0.6A55@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
Ed Guy wrote:

> Maslow said "If your only tool is a hammer, every problem looks like a nail". There
> aren't too many people who have both the mainframe COBOL skills and the PC skills to
> combine them (or even think of doing so). So the people working on it  use only what
> they know.

But this kind of fusion IS the future, along with data communications
and data management (warehouses, etc.).  Don't be a cobol programmer, or
a C programmer or a Java programmer.  Be a programmer - with specialties
such as business or engineering.  Learn the appropriate tools.  Not tool
- what kind of craftsman uses one tool?

Learn your business.  Your business is not programming.  

I see kids wanting to get paid for walking around a lake (for charity)
and it bothers me.  Let them get paid for building for the needy instead
- let them see cause and effect of their efforts.   Programmers can
sometimes have this same blind spot.  Get paid for how hard their
programming is - not for how much that programming contributes to the
objectives of their organization. 

In other fields, the machine tools operator has learned CAD-CAM over the
years or he has lost his job.  The Cobol mainframe programmer should
learn sockets, databases, PC's, GUI's, and other languages - but
especially, his business environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

- **From:** dnalor@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qdkpj$dlb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<zAyx3.184$3s2.745@news.flash.net>`

```
Hi!
1st of all: COBOL is "starving" for a longer period, than other
languages even exited!!

Okay, to ur Question:
You are REALLY asking the WRONG question!
It is not a question of COBOL or NOT COBOL but of the APPLICATION!
If u have to write a service, u will use C or C++, if u have a WEB-
Client, u might use JAVA, if u have some inference Problems u might go
and do it in Prolog. If u have tricky ADT's COBOL is really the wrong
way, on the other hand, COBOL is pretty easy to learn, and pretty nice
to create great applications pretty fast, running on a wide range of
platforms, if u consider a few important details.

As u can see, it is a matter of the purpose a Software is designed for.
And for this reaseon there are Software-Engineers (like myself) who
decide which language, what platforms, what interfaces and the complete
technical background, the software-specifications, the
EntityRelationModels, the ObjectOrientedAnalysis, the
ObjedtOrientedDesign, the Structured Analysis Design Technique-Diagram,
and all that stuff, that is called: Softwareengineering.

And as a SE you have to make a GOOD decision, which language the coders
have to use. In my case, I work in a pretty small department, where I
do the SE and the pretty tricky codings (that is ADT-design, the
appropriate algorithms and that stuff). By the way: in MF-Cobol u can
implement recursive functions (it is a heck but it works)!

Hope this helps!
Ah! Warning! Do not run into a religious war!!! :-))
Greetings,
Roland.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it?

```
Dear Steve,

Don't let these people fool you -- COBOL is not dead, and won't be until
probably after they are dead.

Just think that more than 75% of all the programs (business programs) in just
the United States are written in Cobol.  There are Cobol Compilers written for
the client/server environment as well as for the PC environment.  There are 3rd
party packages written to interface Cobol programs with SQL databases.  There
are 3rd party packages available to interface Cobol programs with Windows-style
user-interface techniques.

And why do you think that so many Cobol programmers are making top dollar today
because of the Y2K bug???  Because Cobol is dead -- NO!!! because it is alive
and well, and living in everybodies backyard.  It's still the easiest language
to write business applications in -- and it's cheaper to hire a Cobol consultant
than it is to hire one for an assembler, C, Java, Pascal, Algol, Jovial, etc.

Think about it -- if one of these guys who have said Cobol is dead, came along
and told you that sex was dead -- would you believe him and never have sex
again???  NO, I think not...

Regards,

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: Newbie: how to create a function — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** "Fabio Pagano" <fpagano@apsystem.it>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Sorry for the question (i am not experienced!). How can i create a function
that receives a parameter and changes its value?

Program example:

======

...Program
Perform ChgVar(MyVar).
...Program

ChgVar(MyParm).
   Add 1 To MyParm.
   Exit.
======

I dont' know if i can do this with the Perform. I use Cobol (ANSI 85) on Ibm
As/400.

I am a Visual Basic developer, so sorry for the ignorance.

Thanks in advance.

Fabio Pagano
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q6ib6$ssb@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
The normal way in COBOL is to use CALL USING.  IBM on OS/390 and  the
Workstation has an extension that allows the "RETURNING" phrase on a CALL
statement - or when doing a GOBACK.  However, I am not certain that this
same extension is available (yet) in COBOL/400.
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dPCx3.1261$Or6.18499@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Unless there is something real specific about your compiler, user-defined
functions are not possible in ANSI COBOL 85. (I think Mr Klein said
something about UDFs being in the draft for the next standard).

Closest you can come is to write a nested program, something like...

PROGRAM-ID. Main.
....
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  MyVar       PIC S9(09) COMP.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     MOVE +10 TO MYVAR
     DISPLAY MyVAR
     CALL "MyFUNC" USING MyVar
     DISPLAY MYVAR.

    STOP RUN.

END PROGRAM MAIN.
PROGRAM-ID. MyFUNC.
...
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 DATA-IN     PIC S9(09) COMP.

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING DATA-IN.

     ADD +1 TO DATA-IN
    GOBACK.

END PROGRAM MyFUNC.

(BY REFERENCE assumed for CALL and USING).
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7NfiKgolOtB@bop99.ping.de>`
- **References:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
fpagano@apsystem.it meinte am 27.08.99
zum Thema "Newbie: how to create a function":
>
> I dont' know if i can do this with the Perform. I use Cobol (ANSI 85)
> on Ibm As/400.
>
> I am a Visual Basic developer, so sorry for the ignorance.

Standard cobol (not oo-cobol) does not have the concept of functions or  
procedures with local and global variables.

The only way is to build a subprogram.

mainprogram:
------------

working-storage section

01  parm
  05  feld  pic x(001).   <-------+
...                               !
                                  !
procedure division.               !
                                  !
call subpgm using parm            !
                                  !
                                  !
subpgm:                           !
-------                           !
                                  !
working-storage section.          !
                                  !
01  feld2  pic  x(001).   --------------> local variable
...                               !
linkage section.                  !
01  parm                          !
  05  feld pic x(001).    --------+  is a pointer of this adress

procedure division using parm.

...
move "1" to feld         ---------> changes the value
move 0   to return-code  ---------> tells the mainprogram, that there
                                    is no error / optional
goback                   ---------> back to mainprogram


some code like that does not work:
==================================
....
procedure division.

main section.
  perform some
  stop run.

some section.

01  feld pic x(001) <---- must be defined global in the working-storage
                          or is a part of a parameter in the linkage
                          All definitions in the working-storage or
                          linkage are known at any part of the program.


    move "1" to feld
    exit.

a c-language constuct:
----------------------
 +--------- return datatype
 !     +--- function name
 !     !                 +------ input type / call by reference
 !     !                 !               (makes a temp. copy in storage)
 !     !            +----+---+
 v     v            v        v
int functionexample(int a, int b)
{
    int c = 2;           <------ local definition / set value / temp. life
    return (a+b)/c;      <------ return value
}

To do this in cobol you have to write a subprogram.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q7p61$2i1@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it> <7NfiKgolOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```
Juergen Vetter <Juergen@bop99.ping.de> wrote in message
news:7NfiKgolOtB@bop99.ping.de...
> fpagano@apsystem.it meinte am 27.08.99
> zum Thema "Newbie: how to create a function":
> >
<snip>
>
> Standard cobol (not oo-cobol) does not have the concept of functions or
> procedures with local and global variables.
>

Actually, since the '85 Standard, COBOL *does* fully support "nested
programs" which does allow you to have "local" and "global" variables (as
well as EXTERNAL items which available across  non-nested programs).

Having said that, if you are interested in "user-defined functions" in terms
of referencing a "procedure" and getting back a "returned value" - then it
is true that Standard COBOL doesn't inlcude that yet - although many
implementations do already provide it.  (For example - going back to the
original question - IBM does support RETURNING values on S/390 and on
Workstations - but not on OS/400.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** "Cosmic Knight" <cosmicknight@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nMHx3.11773$ok3.3220@news.rdc1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Hey Fabio-

COBOL doesn't really have functions like other languages, so you really
don't have to pass a value to a paragraph in COBOL like you do in C, for
example.  In essence, all the variables are 'global', to use an
object-oriented refenrence.

Example:

WORKING-STORAGE-SECTION.
01   MY-NUMBER      PIC 9(02)  VALUE 0.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

000-MAIN.

        PERFORM 100-INIT.
        PERFORM 200-LOOP UNTIL MY-NUMBER = 10.
        PERFORM 300-END.

        STOP-RUN.

100-INIT.
        DISPLAY MY-NUMBER.

200-LOOP.

        IF MY-NUMBER < 10
            PERFORM 250-ADD
        END-IF.

250-ADD.

        ADD 1 TO MY-NUMBER.

300-END.

        DISPLAY MY-NUMBER.

So. the 250-ADD paragraph acts like a function, in that it manipulates a
variable in a way that is useful to the other paragraphs.

Hope this helps-

Fabio Pagano wrote in message <7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it>...
>Sorry for the question (i am not experienced!). How can i create a function
>that receives a parameter and changes its value?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>I dont' know if i can do this with the Perform. I use Cobol (ANSI 85) on
Ibm
>As/400.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie: how to create a function

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CA70F5.84A3D0CC@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7q6adn$adf$1@nslave1.tin.it> <nMHx3.11773$ok3.3220@news.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Cosmic Knight wrote:
> 
> Hey Fabio-
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Global is definitely **NOT** object oriented.  C has it and it is
considered extremely bad form to use it.  Explicitly passed
information makes code much more understandable.  A C++ programmer
would be shot for doing this.

It goes against common practice but break up your large programs into
smaller called routines and make them modular and information hiding.

Ken
```

<a id="t-d537f2a48f"></a>
## Thread: JCL question — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: JCL question

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rPEx3.287$mo.17390@viper>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk> <YeDx3.248$mo.15784@viper> <37C7067C.CCC31B54@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>What is the advantage of this over an IDCAMS delete?  IDCAMS delete is
>simple, but passes a return code of 4 if the file is already deleted.
>I've never tried a TSO command from JCL.

IEFBR14 uses less resources and does not require a control card. I don't
believe it can delete an entire VSAM file, leaving CLUSTER and INDEX while
deleting the root.

If you need to know if the DSN was there, then IDCAMS would probably work
just as well. Further, using IDCAMS ... GDG PURGE option allows for deleting
a GDG, not sure about the generations. Plus I believe it can also delete an
entire VSAM file, cluster and index as well, with one command.

I also believe they are the same command. If I remember correctly they use
the exact same syntax. But using IKJEFT01 and the PARM allows for symbolic
substiutution and no control cards.

Try TSO HELP DELETE at the COMMAND line to see all options.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL question

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C720B8.ACF57C41@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk> <YeDx3.248$mo.15784@viper> <37C7067C.CCC31B54@NOSPAMhome.com> <rPEx3.287$mo.17390@viper>`

```
 One disadvantage is that IEFBR14 will do a recall of a migrated  file. IDCAMS
does not do a recall.
IEFBR14 can be used to delete  VSAM clusters and entire GDG generations too:

// EXEC PGM=IEFBR14
//DD00   DD   DSN=your.vsam,DISP=(OLD,DELETE)  <<delete VSAM
//DD01    DD   DSN=your.gdg,DISP=(OLD,DELETE)    <<delete all GDG generations.

When deleting a data set IDCAMS returns a CC of 8 if the data set is not found.
If you want a zero return code you can always do:
    IF LASTCC = 0 THEN    SET MAXCC = 0

Gumbo wrote:

> >What is the advantage of this over an IDCAMS delete?  IDCAMS delete is
> >simple, but passes a return code of 4 if the file is already deleted.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Try TSO HELP DELETE at the COMMAND line to see all options.
```

```
I never tried either of these.  It's not IEFBR14 that does the delete
though, any program can do it as long as the DD is set up.

I do IEFBR14 primarily to do miscellaneous cleanup with
DISP=(MOD,DELETE).  If I know it exists I generally delete in the step
which wants it deleted.
 
> // EXEC PGM=IEFBR14
> //DD00   DD   DSN=your.vsam,DISP=(OLD,DELETE)  <<delete VSAM
> //DD01    DD   DSN=your.gdg,DISP=(OLD,DELETE)    <<delete all GDG generations.

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

> > just as well. Further, using IDCAMS ... GDG PURGE option allows for deleting
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL question

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1nzy3.970$wv2.39927@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7q2nla$33q$1@news.dknet.dk> <YeDx3.248$mo.15784@viper> <37C7067C.CCC31B54@NOSPAMhome.com> <rPEx3.287$mo.17390@viper> <37C720B8.ACF57C41@ix.netcom.com> <37CA896B.A8E11014@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37CA896B.A8E11014@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>John Trifon wrote:
>>
>>  One disadvantage is that IEFBR14 will do a recall of a migrated  file.
IDCAMS
>> does not do a recall.
>
>Useful to know
>
>> IEFBR14 can be used to delete  VSAM clusters and entire GDG generations
too:
>
>I never tried either of these.  It's not IEFBR14 that does the delete
>though, any program can do it as long as the DD is set up.


True.  IEFBR14s consist of only two lines of assembler code:
     SR 15,15
     BR 14

hence it's name.  After zeroing the entry point address and return-code
register
(15) it returns to the caller.  The COBOL equivalent of only STOP RUN in the
procedure division.  Needless to say it does nothing with any file referred
to, everything is done by the JCL.


>I do IEFBR14 primarily to do miscellaneous cleanup with
>DISP=(MOD,DELETE).  If I know it exists I generally delete in the step
>which wants it deleted.
```

#### ↳ Re: OOCobol and OLE

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qg8vv$jt3$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37C677C4.5AF52498@baeurer.de> <37C6DA59.CAF4D6FD@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>Frank Pohlmann wrote:
>> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>> me?

Hi Frank,
The example you quote from uses vocabularies, which is a way of defining 
your own syntax in COBOL. OLEREP.CBL doesn't uses the vocabularies - the 
example that does is OLEREPV.CBL (which is just the same program 
demonstrating their use) in the same project.

OLEREP.CBL, as someone else mentions, uses the Word95 Word interface 
("Word.Basic"). While this is perfectly acceptable to Word97, Word97 now 
only documents how to call the old methods using its newer interface, and 
not the old interface. You might want to look at using the new 
"Word.Application" interface.

>Hopefully Merant will jump in and answer your query - I'll also email
>you direct.

Thanks Jimmy.
I've been away, so missed the original post. I have a step-by-step 
Object COBOL/Word97 demo - for Net Express 3.0, unfortunately - but if 
you're interested, e-mail me (and I'm going to regret posting this... ;-)

Steve.
```

<a id="t-1af340ae91"></a>
## Thread: WINNT 4.0, RM/Cobol, Netware — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: WINNT 4.0, RM/Cobol, Netware

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.os.ms-windows.nt.software.compatibility,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q68r0$fft$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37C6413C.13ECB229@netcom.ca>`

```
Brant Telfer wrote in message <37C6413C.13ECB229@netcom.ca>...
>Have an RM/Cobol (v2.2 for PC Network) application running under
>DOS(v6.22) Application resides on a Netware 4.11 server. Now "upgrading"
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any suggestion ?

I have had similar experiences many times, most often I have been able to
get rid of it by createing a shortcut to the application, rightclick the
icon, choose properties and open up the Memory Tab.

From here, forget about Conventional memory

If Expanded (EMS) Memory is not already set tp None, set it to none. EMS is
an outdated memory protocol, which is rarely used anyway.

Extended (XMS) memory could be left as is in most cases, however, to be sure
set it to 0, this one is more likely that could be requested though.

MS-DOS protected-mode (DPMI) memory is usually the one that messes up for
you, by some reason, Microsoft set this one to *auto* by default, many
applications can't deal with that. Unfortunately there is no way to turn it
of, but set it to a fixed number uses to fix the problem, set it for
instance to 3096.

Let me know if it helps.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: rebuilding an indexed file — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### rebuilding an indexed file

- **From:** daniel cargile <trvlr5@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C75DE3.A62943FE@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In the programming class that I'm taking, a question came up about what,
if anything, can be done when the file status of an indexed file is 47.

I would like to know if there is some way to 'rebuild' or 'reorganize'
the file from within the program. We are using Microfocus' compiler in
NT, if that makes a difference.

I would like to emphasize that this is not a homework assignment. I'm
perfectly capable of doing my own work and wouldn't try to trick anyone
into helping me.

Thanks,
Dan
```

#### ↳ Re: rebuilding an indexed file

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iiJx3.464$vl2.48628@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<37C75DE3.A62943FE@worldnet.att.net>`

```

daniel cargile <trvlr5@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:37C75DE3.A62943FE@worldnet.att.net...
> In the programming class that I'm taking, a question came up about
what,
> if anything, can be done when the file status of an indexed file is
47.

A file status of 47 means an I-O operation was attempted against an
unopened file.
```
