<a id="t-df1300d57a"></a>
## Thread: Please Help - Panic setting in !!! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Please Help - Panic setting in !!!

- **From:** ruth9266@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q5vjs$ehf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I hope somebody is kind to help me. I've written a Cobol assignment for
college and I'm really stuck.  Below is the program so far. It's a
student record program, the functions I have to include are:

1. 	Maintain by insert, amend and delete student records.
	Student records are deleted by changing status field to W.
        Records are not deleted from the file at this stage.
2. 	View student's details on screen
3. 	List all student's details on file to the printer
4. 	Update records when a student is withdrawn by placing a W in the
        status field.
5. 	List current students according to required course code. List to
        printer
6. 	Delete all withdrawn students when required.
7.   Reports should have headings

The only function that's working is the Insert. I can't do anymore.

Thanks v.v.v. much - Ruth

PROGRAM:

Identification Division.
       Program-id. Student.
       Author. Ruth. Wingstedt.
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
       Source-Computer. Viglen.
       Object-Computer. Viglen.
       Input-Output Section.
       File-Control.
           Select student-file assign "A:assign1"
           Organization is indexed
           Access is dynamic
           record key is Enrolment-No
           File status is wsfs.
           Select Pr-file assign "A:1print".
       Data Division.
       File Section.
       FD student-file.
           01 student-rec.
               03 Enrolment-No                 PIC XX999999.
               03 Course-Code                  PIC 99999.
               03 Student-Surname            PIC X(15).
               03 Student-Initials             PIC XX.
               03 Date-of-Birth                PIC 9(6).
               03 Address-Line-1               PIC X(20).
               03 Address-Line-2               PIC X(20).
               03 Address-Line-3               PIC X(20).
               03 Student-Status               PIC X9.
               03 Misc                         PIC X(15).

       FD Pr-file.
           01 Pr-rec.
               03 Pr-Student-In                PIC XX.
               03 Pr-Student-Sur               PIC X(15).

       Working-Storage Section.
           01 Ws-rec.
               03 Ws-Enrolment-No              PIC XX999999.
               03 Ws-Course-Code               PIC 99999.
               03 Ws-Student-Surname           PIC X(15).
               03 Ws-Student-Initials          PIC XX.
               03 Ws-Date-of-Birth             PIC 9(6).
               03 Ws-Address-Line-1            PIC X(20).
               03 Ws-Address-Line-2            PIC X(20).
               03 Ws-Address-Line-3            PIC X(20).
               03 Ws-Student-Status            PIC X9.
               03 Ws-misc                      PIC X(15).


       77 Wsfs                                 PIC 99.
       77 Option                               PIC X value space.
           88 coderange values 'I', 'A', 'D', 'X'.
       77 Option-check                         PIC X value space.
           88 menurange values 'M', 'V', 'L', 'X'.
       77 Response                             PIC X value space.
       77 eof                                  PIC X value space.
           88 valid-eof value 'E'.

       Procedure Division.
       Main.
           Perform initialise.
           Perform Process.
           Perform finish.
           STOP RUN.

       Initialise.
           Open i-o student-file.
           move space to ws-rec.

       Process.
           Perform process-body until option-CHECK equal "X".

       Process-body.
           Perform Menu1.
           Perform select-option1.

       Menu1.
           Perform with test after until menurange
               Display space upon crt
               Display '(M)aintain'   at 1010
               Display '(V)iew-student' at 1210
               Display '(L)ist-All'   at 1310
               Display 'E(X)it'       at 1410
               Display 'Please enter option ' at  1610
               Accept Option-CHECK
               end-accept
           End-perform.

       Select-option1.
           Evaluate OptioN-CHECK
               When 'M' perform Maintain-routine
               When 'V' perform View-student-routine
               When 'L' perform List-All-routine
               When 'X' Next sentence

           End-evaluate.

       Maintain-routine.
           Perform with test after until coderange
               Display space upon crt
               Display '(I)nsert' at 1010
               Display '(A)mend'  at 1210
               Display '(D)lete'  at 1410
               Display 'E(X)it'   at 1610
               Display 'Please enter option ' at 1810
               Accept option end-accept

           End-perform.
               perform Select-option2.
       Select-option2.
           Evaluate option
               When 'I' perform insert-routine
               When 'A' perform amend-routine
               When 'D' perform delete-routine
               When 'X' next sentence
           End-evaluate.

       Insert-Routine.

           Perform init-insert.
           Perform until response = 'N'
               Perform Get-details
               Perform write-it
               Perform re-anymore
           End-perform.

       Amend-Routine.

           Move " " to response.
           Perform init-amend.
           Perform until response = 'N'
               Perform amend-details
               Perform re-write-it
               Perform re-anymore
           End-perform.

       init-amend.
           move " " to response.
           Close student-file.
           OPEN I-O student-file.

       amend-details.

           Display space upon crt.
           Display 'Please enter Enrolment-No '      at 1010
           Accept ws-Enrolment-No.
           Display 'Please enter new Course-Code '     at 1210
           Accept ws-Course-Code.
           Display 'Please enter new Student-Surname '    at 1310
           Accept ws-Student-Surname.
           Display 'Please enter new Student-Initials '   at 1410
           Accept ws-Student-Initials.
           Display 'Please enter new Date-of-Birth '       at 1510
           Accept ws-Date-of-Birth.
           Display 'Please enter new Address-Line-1 '      at 1610
           Accept ws-Address-Line-1.
           Display 'Please enter new Address-Line-2 '      at 1710
           Accept ws-Address-Line-2.
           Display 'Please enter new Address-Line-3 '      at 1810
           Accept ws-Address-Line-3.
           Display 'Please enter new Student-Status '  at 1910
           Accept ws-Student-Status.


       re-write-it.
           move ws-rec to student-rec.
           REWRITE student-rec.
           Close student-file.

       re-anymore.
           Display ' Any more records Y/N ?' at 2410.
           Accept response.

       Delete-Routine.
           Perform init-delete.
           Perform del-details until response = "N".

       Init-delete.
           Close student-file.
           OPEN I-O student-file.

       del-details.
           Display space upon crt.
           Display ' Please enter Enrolment no for deleting' at 1010.
           Accept Ws-Enrolment-No.
           Move ws-Enrolment-No to Enrolment-No.
           Read student-file key is Enrolment-no.
           Display "Student Record to be deleted" Enrolment-No at 1210.
           Delete student-file invalid key
           Display 'Invalid key' at 1210.
           Display "Press any key " at 3310.
           Accept response.
           Move "D" to ws-misc.
           Perform re-anymore2.
           Exit.

           Delete student-file invalid key
           Display 'Invalid key' at 1210.
           Display "Press any key " at 3310.
           Accept response.
           Perform re-anymore2.
           Exit.


       re-anymore2.
           Display ' Any more records to delete Y/N ?' at 2410.
           Accept response.

       view-student-routine.
           Perform init-view.

	view-details.
           Display space upon crt.
           Display ' Please enter Enrolment no for viewing' at 1010.
           Accept Ws-Enrolment-No.
           Move ws-Enrolment-No to Enrolment-No.
           Read student-file key is Enrolment-No.
           Display space upon crt.
           Display "Student Details " at 1010.
           Display "Enrolment Number is " Enrolment-no at 1210.
           Display "Course Code is " Course-Code at 1410.
           Display "Student Surname is " Student-surname at 1510.
           Display "Student Initials are " Student-initials at 1610.
           Display "Date of Birth is " date-of-birth at 1810.
           Display "1st Line of Address is " address-line-1 at 2010.
           Display "2nd Line of Address is " address-line-2 at 2210.
           Display "3rd Line of Address is " address-line-3 at 2410.
           Display "Student Status = " Student-Status at 2610.
           Display "Press any key " at 3310.
           Move "V" to ws-misc.
           Perform re-anymore3.
           Exit.

       re-anymore3.
           Display ' Any more records to view Y/N ?' at 2410.
           Accept response.

       List-All-routine.
           Perform init-list-all.
           Perform list-all-details until response = "N".

       Init-list-all.
           Close student-file.
           Open I-O student-file.


       list-all-details.
           Display space upon crt.
           Display ' Please enter Enrolment no for viewing' at 1010.
           Accept Ws-Enrolment-No.
           Move ws-Enrolment-No to Enrolment-No.
           Read student-file key is Enrolment-No.
           Display space upon crt.
           Display "Student Details " at 1010.
           Display "Enrolment Number is " Enrolment-no at 1210.
           Display "Course Code is " Course-Code at 1410.
           Display "Student Surname is " Student-surname at 1510.
           Display "Student Initials are " Student-initials at 1610.
           Display "Date of Birth is " date-of-birth at 1810.
           Display "1st Line of Address is " address-line-1 at 2010.
           Display "2nd Line of Address is " address-line-2 at 2210.
           Display "3rd Line of Address is " address-line-3 at 2410.
           Display "Student Status = " Student-Status at 2610.
           Display "Press any key " at 3310.
           Move "L" to ws-misc.
           Perform re-anymore3.
           Exit.

       re-anymore4.
           Display ' Any more records to list Y/N ?' at 2410.
           Accept response.

       Init-insert.
           Move space to response.
           Move space to ws-rec.

       Get-details.
           Display space upon crt.
           Display 'Please enter Enrolment-No '      at 1010
           Accept ws-Enrolment-No.
           Display 'Please enter Course-Code '         at 1210
           Accept ws-Course-Code.
           Display 'Please enter Student-Surname '    at 1310
           Accept ws-Student-Surname.
           Display 'Please enter Student-Initials '   at 1410
           Accept ws-Student-Initials.
           Display 'Please enter Date-of-Birth '       at 1510
           Accept ws-Date-of-Birth.
           Display 'Please enter Address-Line-1 '      at 1610
           Accept ws-Address-Line-1.
           Display 'Please enter Address-Line-2 '      at 1710
           Accept ws-Address-Line-2.
           Display 'Please enter Address-Line-3 '      at 1810
           Accept ws-Address-Line-3.
           Display 'Please enter Student-Status '  at 1910
           Accept ws-Student-Status.

       Write-it.
           Move ws-rec to student-rec.
           Write student-rec INVALID KEY perform operation-error.

       operation-error.
           evaluate wsfs
            when 22 Display 'Duplicate key record not added' at 2210
            when 23 Display 'Record not found' at 2210
           End-evaluate.
          Display 'Hit C to continue ' at 2310.
          Move space to response.
          Accept response at 3210.

       INIT-Anymore.
           Display ' Any more records to insert Y/N ?' at 2410.
           Accept response.
       Print-all.
           Perform init-print.
           Perform print-it.

       init-print.
           Close student-file.
           Open i-o student-file.
           open output pr-file.
           move spaces to pr-rec.
           Read student-file next record at end move 'E' to eof.
       Print-it.
           perform print-out until valid-eof.

       print-out.
           Move student-rec to pr-rec.
           Write pr-rec after 1.
           Read student-file next record at end move 'E' to eof.

       Finish.
           Close student-file.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

### help--text editor for student

- **From:** stevekh1@aol.com (Stevekh1)
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990827021701.04622.00000669@ng-cg1.aol.com>`

```
I hope i am not asking a question that has been answered numerous times here
but i have been monitoring the group for about a month and checked the FAQ and
havn't seen any mention of this. I am a student just learning cobol our school
is using MFCobol 2.0 for dos. I am much more comfortable writing code in a
windows  environment and have been using notepad for this purpose. I am
wondering if there is a text editor geared to cobol that would do the job more
```

#### ↳ Re: help--text editor for student

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<J_vx3.9280$e3.90343@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990827021701.04622.00000669@ng-cg1.aol.com>`

```
Stevekh1 wrote:
>I am wondering if there is a text editor geared to cobol that would
>do the job more effeciently for me.

There are a number of good text editors out there.  Multi-Edit is very
powerful, supports COBOL 'templates' (among many others), emulates some
other powerful editors, allows easy keyboard reconfiguration, and is
inexpensive.  You can download an evaluation copy or order it online
at a discount ($129 US) at: http://www.amcyber.com/
```

<a id="t-503a943874"></a>
## Thread: COBOL WINDOWS — 4 msgs

### COBOL WINDOWS

- **From:** "Ronny Martin" <ronny.martin@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q7abg$l45@news3.euro.net>`

```
I used Fujitsu COBOL for my finals at college this year. COBOL dead? Normal
courses use an old Compiler (IBM 370 and Digital VAX) but our group got the
permission to experiment with Windows and COBOL.
We used PowerCOBOL 3.0 as supplied with COBOL for Dummies, ISDG Books
(sorry, but this way the compiler was affordable). There is a lot of
difference in legacy COBOL and we wasted a lot of time experimenting and
searching because we were completely on our own.
Now Windows COBOL is a major attraction on my curriculum. I want to help the
other students by combining some Fujitsu COBOL tips in a small written
course. Perhaps even some unofficial training if interest is high enough.
I ask for the readers to know regular COBOL. Most have no experience in
Visual Basic or anything.

*     If anyone has suggestions about the contents, please send them to my
email (I am not on-line that often to follow newsgroups)
*    The course will be in Dutch but if someone wants to read it (still in
very early stages)
*    Job offers in Belgium are also welcome :)
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL WINDOWS

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CA868C.71D587B1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7q7abg$l45@news3.euro.net>`

```
Ronny Martin wrote:
> 
> I used Fujitsu COBOL for my finals at college this year. COBOL dead? Normal
> courses use an old Compiler (IBM 370 and Digital VAX) but our group got the
> permission to experiment with Windows and COBOL.

All languages are dead, just as we all are.  (obviously I mean this in
the same way as you are asking if Cobol is dead - we & Cobol are
productive today, but someday will die).  Sure Cobol will die out.  I
would be hard pressed to pick a language that will last longer than
Cobol will though.  It wasn't too long ago when colleges taught Pascal
as the language of the future.  The environments which Visual Basic and
Java have been designed to fit into are changing rapidly.  If the
environments are changing, then it seems likely that the tools won't fit
as well in the future.

Learn to like whatever tool (programming language) you can get.  If you
learn to be a good Cobol programmer, your biggest challenge may be to
get your employer to allow you to work on the Java project coming up as
well.  You want to be skilled in a variety of tools - and more
importantly, have a good picture at what you want to accomplish with
those tools (solutions which help your organization achieve its
objectives)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL WINDOWS

- **From:** "Ronny Martin" <ronny.martin@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qhekg$2thf$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`
- **References:** `<7q7abg$l45@news3.euro.net> <37CA868C.71D587B1@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The msg wasn't exactly clear I guess. I don't believe COBOL is really dead.
At college they only teach the older variants, real 'consol like' COBOL.
I try to make a bit publicity at my (now ex-)college to show that new tools
do exist. In several points, I believe it is even better to teach a decent
'visual' COBOL than Visual Basic. Last product's basics are simple enough to
learn on your own...
Students just get the wrong idea when facing these artifacts :)
```

```

COBOL.
```

### What do you think of Realia II and Realia JCL for PC

- **From:** "john smith" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fwKx3.4639$9I.117646@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
I have a package of Realia II and Realia JCL for the PC/WIN/OS2 in front of
me and was wondering if anyone had any experience with it.
Can it honestly mimick mainframe apps and CICS?

We have an inhouse app we use that I love but i cant find it for PC its
called WAAPDSUT, has anyone made a PC version of that?
```

#### ↳ Re: What do you think of Realia II and Realia JCL for PC

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c859ef.242481477@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<fwKx3.4639$9I.117646@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
On Sat, 28 Aug 1999 00:33:24 -0500, "john smith"
<someone@microsoft.com> wrote:

>I have a package of Realia II and Realia JCL for the PC/WIN/OS2 in front of
>me and was wondering if anyone had any experience with it.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

GAG.   One of the biggest hassles we had in converting some business
we bought was in converting the "Wapps".  It's a product developed for
the Navy by EDS and then sold for a short time.  No longer obtainable
or purchaseable.  It's like Interpreted Assembler with a standard
logic loop like RPG.  I understand it, and we converted the WAAPS to
ASM programs.  

To say the least, if there is a PC version, EDS would be the place to
look, but they would not license it to us for the conversion, so I
think you are out of luck.
```

### Micro Focus data files

- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7q8qp8$ag9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm not a programmer (in any language). I use an accounting package that
 uses Micro Focus Cobol. I have all these "dat, idx, gnt, int" files but
I can't seem to open or use them in any way.

I know my data is in there somewhere - I just can't seem to get it out.

I would eventually get the data into Microsoft's Access.

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks, Bill


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus data files

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C82E93.23CF@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<7q8qp8$ag9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
medacman@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I'm not a programmer (in any language). I use an accounting package that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Try a generic parsing engine like ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm).  
It (and its competition) can extract data from text or binary files, allow for 
EBCDIC/ASCII, BigEndian/LittleEndian variations etc. and put out the results as fields 
in fixed, delimited, dBase files etc.
```

```
ACTUALLY, according to the original post they do have an ".INT" file.  If
they do (also) happen to have an ".IDY" file, then they CAN get the file
layout - even if they don't have the original ".CBL" file.  However, I do
agree that you MUST have some clue of what your file layout is - to make
"real" use out of COBOL created files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus data files

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C89800.5EA2@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<7q8qp8$ag9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37C82E93.23CF@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <37c85acd.242703487@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Sat, 28 Aug 1999 11:46:43 -0700, Ed Guy
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> IF you have the software vendors cooperation and can get the layout.


I don't usually respond to insults (actually I don't usually receive any to respond to), 
but clearly you have never done any forensic work.  Having the layouts obviously makes 
it easier, but it is far from impossible without them.  Over the last 35 years I've had 
to do it many times in circumstances where the layouts just plain weren't available. You 
can't just tell the client "I'm sorry but you can't get there from here".

I well remember an early assignment (1969) where I was told "Our COBOL experts have told 
us it can't be done".  They meant that they didn't want the hassle of doing it, and 
should have said so. Their credibility suffered when I finished it.

You (or the client) usually have some indication of what is in the record and by 
examining a few records you can determine the layout - the parsers help you do that by 
providing various views.  

Of course, as you note, if you have the original program and can generate reports that 
also makes it much easier.  You can re-direct the reports to a print file and turn the 
parser loose on that.   This is often the only route when the system designer has 
deliberately obfuscated the data files to keep those quarterly usage fees coming in - as 
with one library package that I had to rescue the catalog data from.

Typical example (couple of weeks ago - client identifying data removed):
=================================================================================
First email to me:
I am attempting to import my data files from an old program named "****"
made in 1987.  I believe the process I should be using is a binary
defenition, however the data is not ouputting properly.  I have attached the
data files and a text version of the form it is entered in.  My hope is that
you may have some suggestions so I may export it to a standard dBase file
importable by Microsoft Excel.

My response:
Since ParseRat will extract a maximum of 99 fields from a binary file in one pass, it 
would take two passes to extract all of the fields.  The way that works would be to 
extract the first batch into a dBase table including a record number - then the second 
batch would be extracted into another also with the record number.  Then the two tables 
would be joined based on the record number into one resulting table. (This way records 
with ANY number of fields can be extracted).  You'd create a PRZ preference file for 
each batch.

Your records contain a combination of what today would likely go into different tables 
anyway, since they combine customer data, order data and item data.  A modern database 
would likely keep them in three tables so that:
1. You wouldn't need to keep entering the customer data for every order.
2. You wouldn't be limited to ten items per order.
2. You would be able to run reports by item type and customer.

Some other notes:
The text data will extract fine (as in the example I sent).
The numeric data will extract fine (the figures are include in binary fields as cents 
and I know they are right because the arithmetic works out properly).
The dates appear non-standard in format - they might be coded as a number of days after 
a datum date but I can't tell because I don't know what the values should be.

The above notes, together with the sample should allow your local database guru to do 
all that you need.

Second email to me:
Thank you VERY much for the information and quick response.  I am analyzing
the data now and adjust the settings of the .prz to read our other .dat
formats.  Thanks again!
=================================================================================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus data files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37c95b1a.308325988@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7q8qp8$ag9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37C82E93.23CF@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <37c85acd.242703487@news1.ibm.net> <37C89800.5EA2@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
On Sat, 28 Aug 1999 19:16:32 -0700, Ed Guy
<ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My apologies.  You obviously can do the necessary magic, and if I run
into any such queires in the future, I'll forwared them to you
(seriously).  Thanks for the good explanation of your process.

I do have a query - how do you hadle compressed data?  With one system
I wrote, all the microfocus data files used compression.  Can you
handle that?  (You can Decompress with rebuild - an interesting by
product of the -D switch).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus data files

_(thread depth: 5)_

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Sat, 28 Aug 1999 19:16:32 -0700, Ed Guy
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> (seriously).  Thanks for the good explanation of your process.

Thane, you are a gentleman.

> I do have a query - how do you hadle compressed data?  With one system
> I wrote, all the microfocus data files used compression.  Can you
> handle that?  (You can Decompress with rebuild - an interesting by
> product of the -D switch).

I can't handle compressed files.  There are so many compression algorithms.
In fact, I've only just added the ability to unscramble EBCDIC - somebody asked for it.

ParseRat started as a tool for my own use, I put a more "cosmetic" front end on it and 
marketed it after several people suggested that I should.

Each version since has resulted from a request by somebody that I add the ability to 
handle a different kind of file.
```

```
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Bill,

Your data will be held (according to those extensions above) in the .dat
files.

You dont need to worry about the .gnt generated code, and .int intermediate
code
files.
It looks to me that your data is held in indexed file, so if you open them
up in any
other program like a text editor then you wont be able to read them.
What i sugest is you get someone to write a very basic MF program to simply
convert --> .dat indexed files to .txt line sequential text based flat
files, then open thse
in Excel, then choose convert to Microsoft Access data base within Excel,
then play with
the data put them into different tables as you disire, Access is quite user
friendly, a non
technical kind of approch.

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

```
Our data2flat converter can read MF data files. You just need to have
SELECT and FD statements for the file or create the corresponding
FDD and RDD descriptor files manually. The trial version of data2flat
can be downloaded at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus data files

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ca9fc7.6223430@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7q8qp8$ag9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Bill,

Most commercial accounting packages come with export utilities that
allow you to export data files into fixed-field ascii files.

You will need to contact your vendor or the software company to learn:
1. How to run the export programs
2. What the layout of the data is
3. What the fields mean (IE Code fields)
4. The inter-relationships between the files.

Paul

words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

## Thread: RM COBOL 6: Data extraction help request! — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM COBOL 6: Data extraction help request!

- **From:** "PHead" <jhutter@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jONx3.8669$7d2.41929@news4.atl>`

```
I need to know if there is some program out there to convert RM Cobol 6
index and relative files to a flat file?  Please email me with response.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: RM COBOL 6: Data extraction help request!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z4by3.2367$z85.53417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<jONx3.8669$7d2.41929@news4.atl>`

```
PHead wrote in message ...
>I need to know if there is some program out there to convert RM Cobol 6
>index and relative files to a flat file?  Please email me with response.


Yes. You need an RM COBOL compiler and the File Description of the files.
Then you write a program to read your files and rewrite the data as a "flat"
(whatever that means: sequential, non-keyed, non-indexed?) file.

Otherwise, you get to hunt through the *.dat files, attempting to figure out
what's in them and divining how they are laid out. This is not easy.
Assuming you get this far, now you write a program to read what's in there,
and write it out to a "flat" file. This part, too, is not not easy.

Better you should use the RM COBOL product to reformat the data. Much
easier.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: RM COBOL 6: Data extraction help request!

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37C9DE47.EE68A4FB@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<jONx3.8669$7d2.41929@news4.atl>`

```
PHead wrote:
> 
> I need to know if there is some program out there to convert RM Cobol 6
> index and relative files to a flat file?  Please email me with response.
> 
> Thanks

Dear PHead,

Our data2flat converter http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/
can read MF and FSC data files. We have not tried it on 
RM index files yet, but it might work for RM sequential files. 

If you send your files and the program to us 
at info@siber.com, we can try to read your data.
Our experiences shows that we can usually figure out
how to read a new format fairly quickly.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

## Thread: Change then name of Wrun32.exe. — 2 msgs

### Change then name of Wrun32.exe.

- **From:** "Oscar Jofre" <jofjimos@erste.eupmt.es>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qb7fv$3ip$1@talia.mad.ttd.net>`

```
I'm working with AcuCobol and Windows Nt. Some one knows how to change the
name of the wrun32 with the name of my application. I'd like to see in the
process that are running in the system then name that I want.

If I run:   wrun32 CANE I'd like to see the process CANE.exe running in the
system


We add new function with C++ in then Wrun32.

                Thanks for all ! Oscar
```

#### ↳ Re: Change then name of Wrun32.exe.

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-08-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A7AhtAAoway3IwPL@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7qb7fv$3ip$1@talia.mad.ttd.net>`

```
Hi Oscar,

To get the name of your application to appear in the task list on NT
all you need to do  set the window title. There are various ways to
do this, but probably the simplest two are:

If you explicitly declare a window, e.g.

        DISPLAY STANDARD WINDOW.

you just need to add a TITLE to that statement
        
        DISPLAY STANDARD WINDOW TITLE "CANE".

If you let the runtime create the window, then you can do:

        DISPLAY "CANE" UPON GLOBAL WINDOW TITLE.

Either should get you what you need.

Best regards

Nigel


In article <7qb7fv$3ip$1@talia.mad.ttd.net>, Oscar Jofre <jofjimos
@erste.eupmt.es> writes
>I'm working with AcuCobol and Windows Nt. Some one knows how to 
>change the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>system
```

```
Curt Neuman wrote:
>Is this a joke?  I remember carrying my Algol programs in a tray of IBM
>cards to the card reader for the UNIVAC in college at Case Western Reserve
>back in the sixties.  But I've had no opportunity to program in Algol since
>nor have I heard of anyone seeking experienced Algol programmers in the last
>30 years.  Do they really exist?

The Unisys A Series computers have no assembly language compilers; the
system software is written in ALGOL, and the WFL language (analogous to
JCL, but *infinitely* friendlier) is very ALGOL-like.
```

<a id="t-5042db43b8"></a>
## Thread: SYSIN DD Missing without abend? — 10 this month / 16 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** zberger@ldl.net
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I had a coworker ask me recently if it was possible to use an "ACCEPT
FROM SYSIN" statement in a program that did not have "SYSIN" in the JCL.
 They were claiming that it worked fine on previous releases but was
failing since we went to the combination of COBOL/MVS and LE.

I know it can be done for SYSOUT using the CBLDYA(on|off) option, and I
vaguely remember it possibly working that way at one time, but I'm not
sure if that was an IBM created option or if it was something we brewed
up in house.

Does anyone know if I can do "ACCEPTS" without "SYSIN" in the
JCL?

   TIA,

   Joe Zitzelberger



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cadb25.8157019@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 30 Aug 1999 18:53:10 GMT, zberger@ldl.net wrote:

>I had a coworker ask me recently if it was possible to use an "ACCEPT
>FROM SYSIN" statement in a program that did not have "SYSIN" in the JCL.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>JCL?

 If there is no SYSIN, how should the ACCEPT work?  Where should it ACCEPT its data from?

If no SYSIN is part of the JCL, then the runtime system will issue an error message and
terminate

If SYSIN DD DUMMY or SYSIN DD DSN=NULLFILE is specified, the ACCEPT will get an End Of
File Condition on first read


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<7qelkh$bud@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I guess this (partially?) depends on what you mean by "working" and what you
expect/want to happen.  Do you want to use another DDNAME *rather* than
SYSIN for doing your ACCEPTs?  Do you want to get an "at end" type results
if no SYSIN is allocated?  Or do you want something else to happen?  Most of
these can be accomplished - but they require different approaches (depending
on exactly what you WANT to happen).
```

#### ↳ Re: SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<dVFy3.2652$mo.69097@viper>`
- **References:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> They were claiming that it worked fine on previous releases but was
>failing since we went to the combination of COBOL/MVS and LE.

Sounds like you were calling a proc to run the COBOL which did the exec. The
PROC had the SYSIN defined within it. Now someone has changed the PROC. If
your ACCEPT is failing it is because the SYSIN is not there.Doing an ACCEPT
without a SYSIN DD on purpose is foolhardly and definitely unpredictable
depending on how the compiler is designed to handle it. If you changed the
compiler then reran a previously functioning JCL with an undefined SYSIN
then the JCL was written incorrectly erroneously and incompletely.

Just because something worked in once doesn't mean we should do it.

To prove your co-workers claim, recompile the program under your old
compiler and re-run the JCL. Recompile with new compiler and rerun. Compare
results.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37CB6DFF.271FC4AF@att.net>`
- **References:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dVFy3.2652$mo.69097@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
> 
> > They were claiming that it worked fine on previous releases but was
> >failing since we went to the combination of COBOL/MVS and LE.

Not clear to me exactly what's happening here, but the following might
be useful/applicable. It's not necessary to code a "//SYSIN DD *" in
your JCL in front of your SYSIN data, MVS does that when it encounters a
statement which does not begin with "//". It's done this since the
1975-78 time frame.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<37CBDA14.CC4688CF@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
zberger@ldl.net wrote:
> 
> I had a coworker ask me recently if it was possible to use an "ACCEPT
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The dynamic allocate option (which I think is bad) only works for
output.

I would bet that Bill has hit the nail on the head, there has been a
JCL clean up and that strange line has been cleaned up by a well
meaning programmer / support person.

I vaguely recall that there was a way to manipulate the SYSIN DD name
to be something else by the compiler options, might be worth a look.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SYSIN DD Missing without abend?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<7qgud7$qhf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7qejug$4s7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37CBDA14.CC4688CF@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <37CBDA14.CC4688CF@zip.com.au>,
  Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
<snip>
> I vaguely recall that there was a way to manipulate the SYSIN DD name
> to be something else by the compiler options, might be worth a look.
>
> Ken
>

No, you can manipulate the DDNAME of the message file (the file where
your DISPLAYs will go to), but not the DDNAME of SYSIN. The option ist
OUTDD, the default value OUTDD(SYSOUT).
You can also redirect the Language Environment messages to another
DDNAME with the runtime option MSGFILE. The IBM default setting is
MSGFILE=((SYSOUT,FBA,121,0),OVR).

You might ACCEPT FROM xxx, I never tried that.
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> >Grep and 3.14 (mainframe speak) hammer the disk and don't reliably
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> access to the code?

Sorry, the false hits are more of an issue.

Other products will tell you where they are defined, where they are
accessed (read) and where they are updated.  Very nice, very fast.

3.14 about 10 minutes to search.  Data Investigator about 2 minutes to
find all references have a look at all the code and determine the
changes required.  The 'build' process happened every evening about 3
am.  Very nice tool for complex systems rather than simple systems.

Ken
```

```
help from Word (description of Word Basic is in Word 6.0 help files). If you
have any questions, mail me.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<37cb2f9a.3569859@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Hi All,

I am considering a move to the USA and would like to know where I can
find some information about salaries and contract rates. 
I am a South African (Black) who has been working in the UK and is now
contemplating a move to the states.
I have approx. 10 years experience - m/frame COBOL, IDMS, some IMS,
DB2, Natural/ADABAS, Microfocus COBOL  etc.
What kind of salary/rate should I be looking at? I do not want to
undersell or oversell myself.

Many Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Coming to America!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00

- **References:** `<37cb2f9a.3569859@news.freeserve.net>`

```
It will vary depending on what part of the USA you move to.  I recommend
that you let a recruiter sell you to start off with.  You will get
higher pay with less security with a recruiter, and you will have time
to find out how to sell yourself.

COBOL Refugee wrote:
```

```
Hi group!

I need to scan the keyboard directly from a RM-Cobol program, I used to
do that with an assembly routine by means of the BIOS int 16h services
but under RM-Cobol for Windows no Bios services nor assembly routines
can be used.
I've wrote a DLL using c++ and I've succeeded in communicating it with
my cobol program but I can't read any key. I'm using the PeekMessage
and GetMessage functions in order of finding queued KEYB messages but,
with the exception of ctrl-c, the message queue always appears empty. I
suppose that RM-Cobol has a way of intercepting those messages before
they can reach the queue.

I'd appreciate any kind of help.

Greetings from  Spain.
Juan Maria.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
exported functions with a leading "?". This happens, when porting from
16 to 32bit. Exactly: it happens if the function starts witjh an
underscore, as 16bit compilers required it.
eg.:
old function:  _func @24
new function:  ?_func @24

how can I avoid this?
Thanks a lot!
Roland.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>`

```
Through various threads, contributors have said that where necessary
they will use other languages (C, C++, Java etc.) to do things more
effectively than in COBOL.

I don't dispute these claims - but am curious - could you  please give
examples of where you do this in preference to using COBOL, (presuambly
caused by deficiencies in the latter ?)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cad439.6386282@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>`

```
On Mon, 30 Aug 1999 17:56:51 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>Through various threads, contributors have said that where necessary
>they will use other languages (C, C++, Java etc.) to do things more
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

System related high performance functions:  Assembler (S/390)

Processing of arrays comes to mind, it can be way more efficient in PL/I than in COBOL

Also, dynamically growing and shrinking linked lists - it is much easier in Assembler, or
C, or PL/I (in this order, but that is a matter of taste)


Java? Don't know whats good in Java, much less what's better... <eg>

C++?  Oh, come on - I wouldn't write a GUI type OS in COBOL, that's probably where C++
comes in, but given VA COBOL I can write GUI apps in COBOL, and don't have to touch that
ugly sad excuse of a programming language

Interfacing to existing mathematical, statistical, and engineering packages: Fortran,
possibly PL/I

With Kind Regards
Volekr Bandke
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

<a id="t-f8acf745d7"></a>
## Thread: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it? -Reply — 1 this month / 2 total

### Re: COBOL isn't a dead language... is it? -Reply

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7ca9041.047@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Not exactly sure who gave me this but it is worth a groan in this topic:

There was once a COBOL programmer who was getting burned out with all the Y2K conversions taking place.  He was so stressed out that he finally arrainged to have himself put in cryostasis until after Jan 1,2000.

Time passed and he awoke to find himself in a white room with lots of people rushing around.  A voice from behind his head spoke in an awed whisper "He's awake!"  Activity in the room stopped as all turned to look at him.  He was a bit nervous and asked if everything had gone ok - was he in good health and so on.  A nurse assured him he was ok, the doctors declared all his tests showed him to be in excellent health.  He was given a sumpious meal and all his wishes were immediately fulfilled.  

While all this attention pleased him it also made him a bit nervous.  He kept asking what day it was and he was told to never mind that all was well.  Finally about a week after he awakened a very official man walked into the room and sat in the chair near his bed.  He told the programmer that the Galaxy Leader wanted to speak with him.  A screen came down from the ceiling and a man appeared who had a eerie resemblence to Bill Gates, he spoke....

"I have some bad news for you.  You were scheduled to be awakened on 8/1/2000 but there was a glitch in the programming and it is now 8/1/9999.  You have nothing to worry about, all desease and war has been eliminated and the world lives in peace and harmony.  We hope to continue to live this way but to do so we need your help....

We understand you know COBOL......"


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

---

## Thread: Micro Focus User-Written File Handler Problems — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus User-Written File Handler Problems

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cae3a8@news1.us.ibm.net>`

```
To anyone that can help,

I have written a callable file handler for use with Micro Focus Workbench
and Netexpress.

The problem I am having is getting it to work correctly when compiled to a
.DLL.  When I animate through the code (source calling the file handler and
the file handler itself) it works fine.  I am using a CBL_ERROR_PROC call in
the file handler, but do have the priority flag set to 200.  The error that
I receive is "200 - Internal Logic Error".

I am currently testing with Cobol Workbench 4.0.26, using "cob.exe
filehand.cbl -x:csd" command-line to create a .DLL that utilizes the MF
Dynamic Run-Time System environment.

Does anyone have any ideas?

If so could you please e-mail me at Todd.Lucas@ibm.net, as I do not check
this message board very often...

Thanks - Todd
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus User-Written File Handler Problems

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qeqi1$cnf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37cae3a8@news1.us.ibm.net>`

```

check
.....this message board very often...

.....Thanks - Todd

MF has a problem with being able to handle duplicate entry points within a
module and a dll being called by that module...Is the normal MF file handler
loaded also?

I discovered the problem when trying to call a DLL from a program when both
modules had a duplicate entry point...for ex the root called a calc routine
and the dll calls the same calc routine...I received internal logic
error...The runtime doesn't know what do do in this case...

kenmullins
```

<a id="t-cc4dfdd92d"></a>
## Thread: Help with MF Cobol being very slow under OS/2 — 3 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with MF Cobol being very slow under OS/2

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CBDCCE.C773215E@gfc-net.de>`

```
Hi,

I ran into a serious runtime problem under OS2. I have Microfocus Cobol 4.0.36
for Win32 and for OS2. I have one application that makes extensive use (120
calls per transaction) of calling a function from a DLL (created with cbllink
-d).

While working under Win32, the program is not as fast as it should, but it is
OK.

Under OS2, the response time counts up to five times the Win32 response time.

The source codes are identical.

I found out that a simple call into an entry inside the DLL takes about 10
milliseconds on NT and 30-40 milliseconds on OS2 that steals more than 3 seconds
for one transaction.

What's going wrong here ?

Any suggestions highly welcome.

Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with MF Cobol being very slow under OS/2

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qgk6p$80f$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37CBDCCE.C773215E@gfc-net.de>`

```
>>I ran into a serious runtime problem under OS2. I have Microfocus Cobol
4.0.36
for Win32 and for OS2. I have one application that makes extensive use (120
calls per transaction) of calling a function from a DLL (created with
cbllink
-d).

Try making the function(s) in the dll a subroutine(s) in the main program
and eliminate the dll...Compile the module that is currently a dll as an
.obj and include the .obj in the link for the main program...Should be very
little source changes required...

kenmullins
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with MF Cobol being very slow under OS/2

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CBEC2A.399434C7@gfc-net.de>`
- **References:** `<37CBDCCE.C773215E@gfc-net.de> <7qgk6p$80f$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken,

many thanks for your hint, but, I'm sorry to say, the response time is exactly
the same (differs about 20 milliseconds for a whole transaction with about 100
calls).

Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cc14a9.19668506@news.enter.net>`

```
"Yarema Mazuryk" <mazuryk@litech.net> wrote:

>Where can I find some stuff to learn cobol?
>And whre can I get compilers?
>thanks
>
>

The best place to get a free compiler is to buy Thane Hubbell's book,
"Teach Yourself COBOL In 24 Hours!"

It not only includes a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit
versions of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's
book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
There appears to be a 'FREEZE' going on at a lot of companies. That
is  'No new developement until after the dreaded year 2000 date change'
consequently a lot of programmers (contractors) have been laid off.
There are a few jobs still out there but the rates have dropped quite a
bit also to make up for the glut of out-of-contract contractors.!.
If you are a Canadian citizen then I would recommend that you check out
the contract possibilities in the UK.
try news group < uk.jobs.contract >  then check the agencies web sites.
If you have a four year degree and lots of exp. then check out
the euro jobs also I've still seen some y2k work over there! (egads) it
so late in the game for some!.!  Or just sit it out it will pick up
again in January!!

Ken


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
I agree with your view of the situation 100%!

I did company training on application programming in an OS/390
environment last winter.  When I returned from that there were no more
consultants going out to such sites.  Y2K stuff dried up.  I have been
placed (have not yet started) at a place that is now looking at clearing
their user request and application modification plates now.

After companies are certain that they are okay with the new year then
there will probably be work for people to work up more permanent
fixes for all the Y2K kludges and in a similar vein to do routine
program maintenence and upgrading.
```

```
It probably was and it is probably now abending.  Your co-workers are not crazy.

Eileen (who spent many a night on the phone coding missing DD statements).
```

<a id="t-3172d30bdd"></a>
## Thread: Getting rid of trailing spaces — 3 this month / 12 total

### Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** raider4967@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Can someone tell me how to get rid of trailing
spaces in cobol.  I can't seem to find any answers
in any of my books.  Let say i have a file that
the fields are enclosed by "" and each field is
comma delimited.  It looks like this:

Cust#   Address                 Zip

"1997","123 South Ave        ","12345"
"1998","1276 North Ave       ","65455"

And i want the file to look like this:

"1997","123 South Ave","12345"
"1998","1276 North Ave","65455"

This would no doubt turn the file into a varible
length record, but isint there a keyword or
something to do this kinda like rtrim in oracle??
Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CC4015.83DA9F06@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Look up STRING

raider4967@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Can someone tell me how to get rid of trailing
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f7Yy3.3203$mo.83808@viper>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>in any of my books.  Let say i have a file that
>the fields are enclosed by "" and each field is
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>"1998","1276 North Ave","65455"


If your file already exists in the space-filled format then a simple move of
each field to the output file would suffice. Using the STRING command or
reference modification for the variable fields. Typically COBOL does not
input comma separated files, although STRING will help with this if there is
no other way.

The long way:

Set up each field as a varible field in WS.
move fields from fixed to variable
determine individual lengths and set appropriate ODO length indicator
STRING delimited by size into V length output record
set output record length
write record

In other words, do your homework.
```

---

```
Can someone help me get rid of trailing spaces in COBOL.  I have a file
that the fields are enclosed in quotes and is comma delimited.  And
example is as follows:


Cust#   Address                  Zip

"3534","123 So Ave            ","53436"
"6235","4321 Sesame St        ","84674"

And i want it to look like this:

"3534","123 So Ave","53436"
"6235","4321 Sesame St","84674"


This would no doubt change this to a varible length record, but i can't
seem to find out anything on this in any of my books.  Isint there a
keyword i can use like in Oracle you would use 'RTRIM(field)'  and that
would get rid of the spaces.  Is there anything i can do like that in
COBOL.  Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks!
```

<a id="t-324f3743bb"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Freeware — 1 msgs

### COBOL Freeware

- **From:** "editor" <editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvYy3.659$Ws2.6467@client>`

```
Freeware Utility Download: SIZER (No More Counting Lines of COBOL Code)
Check out this free utility download for COBOL and cut the hassle of
calculating the size of COBOL records.  It's called COBOL Record Sizer.
You copy any COBOL record layout to the clipboard and this application will
calculate "the size in bytes" of the (largest) record found.
www.aboutlegacycoding.com
```

<a id="t-1e850a547b"></a>
## Thread: Some Introductory Cobol Info... — 1 this month / 12 total

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-1e850a547b)._

#### ↳ Re: Some Introductory Cobol Info...

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-08-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GV%y3.590$1I2.46061@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.3.95.990901012326.25486A-100000@holyrood.ed.ac.uk>`

```

Nicola Shields <njs@holyrood.ed.ac.uk> wrote in message

## Well, you certainly came to the right place.

> I guess I'm really looking for some general info about Cobol.
> My current understanding (I'm sure I'll be corrected) is that it is
> largely used within the financial sector,

## "Business" sector would be more accurate. This encompasses
financial (of course), but lots of other areas too (marketing,
advertising - i.e., direct mail - inventory control, bill of
materials,
client/shareholder relations, law, medicine, insurance, etc.
If its a help, most COBOL programmers wear wingtips and
work 9 to 5.

>that a lot of old programs,
> originally written in Cobol, are still in use.
## You bet. There are more programs and lines of code in COBOL
in daily use than any other language. Much of the fuss over Y2K
issues involves COBOL programs written thirty years ago that are
still running.

>I get the impression that
> much of the work involving Cobol is related to maintenance work but
that
> 'new systems' are now being written in other langs.
## Yes and no. Yes there's a lot of maintenance, and no, not all new
systems written in other languages. And cosider a "new" application
that has to interact with 5,500 in-house COBOL programs and a staff
of 100 COBOL programmers. What language would you use?
>
> As certain langs may be better for certain types of tasks (eg Java
useful
> for web-based tasks), is Cobol better for certain types of tasks
than
> others?
## Sure. For example, while exceptions abound, COBOL is the hands-
down winner for ease of maintenance. If you intend to write a program
with an expected lifetime of more than two weeks, many times COBOL
is an excellent choice.
>
>
> Any recommendations for useful books and/or informative web sites
would be
> much appreciated.
## There are NOT a LOT of websites - a search for "COBOL" will find
most.
And what kind of books? History? Elementary programming? Advanced
programming? Language standards?
```
